topics.title: Basic Object-Oriented Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Object-Oriented Concepts # Basic Object-Oriented Concepts ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)? > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition** > *Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)* is a programming paradigm based on the concept of "objects", which can contain data in the form of fields (often known as *attributes* or *properties*) and code in the form of procedures (often known as *methods*). OOP focuses on modeling real-world entities as software objects that combine state and behavior. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > The four foundational pillars of OOP (mandatory for KTU Module 1) are: > 1. **Abstraction** > 2. **Encapsulation** > 3. **Inheritance** > 4. **Polymorphism** > > Together with **Classes**, **Objects**, and **Messages**, these form the **Basic Object-Oriented Concepts**. --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of a **Car Manufacturing Blueprint** and the **Cars** produced from it: | Real World | OOP World | |---|---| | The **Blueprint/Drawing** of a car (defines structure: 4 wheels, 1 engine, 1 steering) | The **Class** (a template/blueprint) | | A **Physical Car** built from that blueprint (a specific red Honda City with engine number XYZ) | The **Object** (a real instance in memory) | | The act of **driving, accelerating, braking** | **Methods** (behaviors inside the object) | | The **odometer reading, fuel level** (internal state hidden under the hood) | **Encapsulated Data** (private fields) | | The **dashboard** showing speed, fuel (controlled view of internal state) | **Abstraction** (showing only essentials) | > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy** > A **Class** is like an *architectural plan* for a house. You cannot live in a plan, but you can build many houses (objects) from it. Each house built from the same plan can have different paint colors (different attribute values) but the same structural design. --- ### 1.3 The Seven Core OOP Concepts (KTU Module 1 Scope) #### A. Object An **object** is a runtime entity that has: - **State** (attributes/fields - e.g., `color`, `speed`) - **Behavior** (methods/functions - e.g., `accelerate()`, `brake()`) - **Identity** (unique memory address - e.g., each car has a unique chassis number) #### B. Class A **class** is a user-defined blueprint or prototype from which objects are created. It represents the set of properties (attributes) and operations (methods) common to all objects of one type. #### C. Abstraction **Abstraction** is the process of hiding complex implementation details and exposing only the essential features of an object. It focuses on *what an object does* rather than *how it does it*. > *Example*: When you press the brake pedal, you don't need to know the hydraulic mechanism тАФ you just know the car will stop. #### D. Encapsulation **Encapsulation** is the bundling of data (attributes) and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (class), and restricting direct access to some of the object's components. This is achieved using **access modifiers** like `private`, `protected`, and `public`. #### E. Inheritance **Inheritance** is a mechanism where a new class (subclass/child) is derived from an existing class (superclass/parent), inheriting its attributes and methods, and can add or override behaviors. > *Example*: `Car` is a parent class; `SportsCar` and `SUV` are child classes inheriting from `Car`. #### F. Polymorphism **Polymorphism** (Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) allows one interface to be used for a general class of actions. The specific action is determined by the exact nature of the situation, typically at runtime. > *Example*: A `draw()` method behaves differently when called on a `Circle` object versus a `Square` object. #### G. Message Passing **Message passing** is the process by which objects communicate with one another. An object sends a message to another object invoking a method on it. --- ### 1.4 Standard KTU Terminology Mapping > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory KTU Vocabulary** (use these exact terms in exams) > - *Class* $\rightarrow$ Template / User-defined type > - *Object* $\rightarrow$ Instance / Runtime entity > - *Attributes* $\rightarrow$ Data members / Fields / State variables > - *Methods* $\rightarrow$ Member functions / Operations / Behaviors > - *Access Modifiers* $\rightarrow$ Visibility specifiers (`public`, `private`, `protected`) > - *Constructor* $\rightarrow$ Special method invoked at object creation # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference ## 2.1 Operational Logic: How OOP Works Step-by-Step ### The OOP Execution Lifecycle (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. **Class Definition Phase**: The programmer defines a class template containing attributes (state) and methods (behavior) inside a `.java` source file. 2. **Compilation Phase**: The Java compiler (`javac`) translates the source code into bytecode (`.class` file). The class definition is loaded into the **Method Area** of the JVM. 3. **Object Instantiation**: When `new ClassName()` is executed, the JVM allocates memory in the **Heap Area** to hold the object's state. 4. **Constructor Invocation**: A special method (constructor) is called to initialize the newly allocated object's attributes to valid initial values. 5. **Reference Binding**: A reference variable (stored in **Stack memory**) is bound to the heap-allocated object. This reference acts as the object's *identity*. 6. **Message Dispatch**: When a method is invoked on the reference, the JVM looks up the method in the object's class (and parent classes, if any) and executes it. 7. **Garbage Collection**: When no reference points to the object, the JVM's garbage collector eventually reclaims the heap memory. --- ### 2.2 The Four Pillars тАФ Deep Dive #### Pillar 1: Abstraction (Hide Complexity, Show Essentials) - **Goal**: Reduce complexity by focusing on relevant characteristics. - **Achieved in Java via**: `abstract` classes and `interface`s. - **Why it matters**: Allows programmers to manage complexity by working with high-level models without worrying about low-level details. #### Pillar 2: Encapsulation (Bundle Data, Restrict Access) - **Goal**: Protect internal state from inconsistent or invalid modifications. - **Achieved in Java via**: `private` fields + `public` getter/setter methods. - **Why it matters**: Provides data hiding, modularity, and maintainability. Code changes inside a class don't break external code that uses it. #### Pillar 3: Inheritance (Build Hierarchies, Reuse Code) - **Goal**: Promote code reuse and establish "is-a" relationships. - **Achieved in Java via**: `extends` keyword (class) and `implements` keyword (interface). - **Why it matters**: Avoids duplication. New classes can be built from existing ones without rewriting common logic. #### Pillar 4: Polymorphism (One Interface, Many Implementations) - **Goal**: Allow a single method call to behave differently based on the actual object type at runtime. - **Achieved in Java via**: Method **overriding** (runtime polymorphism) and method **overloading** (compile-time polymorphism). - **Why it matters**: Enables flexible and extensible code. New subclasses can be added without modifying existing code that uses the parent type. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table: Procedural vs OOP | Aspect | Procedural Programming | Object-Oriented Programming | |---|---|---| | **Core Unit** | Function / Procedure | Class / Object | | **Data Handling** | Data is separate from functions | Data and functions are bundled together | | **Approach** | Top-down | Bottom-up | | **Reusability** | Limited (via functions) | High (via inheritance and polymorphism) | | **Security** | Low (data is globally accessible) | High (encapsulation hides data) | | **Real-world Mapping** | Poor | Excellent | | **Examples in Java** | C-style static methods | Classes, interfaces, abstract classes | | **Primary Focus** | *How to do a task* (procedures) | *Who is doing the task* (objects) | --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Table: The Four Pillars at a Glance | Pillar | Core Idea (1-line) | Java Mechanism | Real-World Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Abstraction** | Show what, hide how | `abstract` class, `interface` | Car dashboard (shows speed, hides engine mechanics) | | **Encapsulation** | Bundle data $\vert$ restrict access | `private` fields, getters/setters | ATM machine (you can withdraw but cannot access cash vault directly) | | **Inheritance** | Acquire properties from parent | `extends` keyword | Child inherits eye color from parent | | **Polymorphism** | One name, many forms | Method overloading/overriding | A person behaves differently as employee, parent, customer | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility of OOP** > - In **Software Engineering**: OOP enables modular, maintainable, and reusable codebases used in enterprise applications (banking, e-commerce, ERP). > - In **Embedded Systems (KTU Kerala context)**: OOP principles are applied in modeling sensors, actuators, and control systems in IoT devices. > - In **GUI Development**: Java Swing/JavaFX heavily uses OOP to model windows, buttons, and event handlers as objects. --- ### 2.5 Messages and Method Invocation тАФ The Communication Model Objects never directly access another object's internal state. They communicate by **sending messages** (calling methods). The receiver decides how to respond. **General Syntax Form**: $$\text{objectReference.methodName}(\text{argumentList})$$ **Conceptual Flow**: 1. Sender object holds a reference to receiver object. 2. Sender calls a public method on the receiver. 3. Receiver's method executes and may return a value. 4. The returned value is the response message. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mark-Winning Point** > In KTU theory answers, always mention that *encapsulation forces communication to occur only through well-defined interfaces (public methods)*. This is what makes OOP systems robust and modular. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown: Procedural $\rightarrow$ OOP Evolution ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Why OOP Emerged from Procedural Programming ### Step 1: Identify the Problem with Procedural Code In **procedural languages** (like early C, Pascal), data and functions are separate. Consider a simple student record system: **Procedural Approach (Pseudo-flow)**: 1. Define a `struct Student` (data definition). 2. Write a function `displayStudent(Student s)` to print. 3. Write a function `calculateGrade(Student s)` for grading. 4. Write a function `updateMarks(Student s, int m)` to update. **The Problem**: - The `Student` data can be modified by **any** function in the program. - If the data structure changes (e.g., add a `phoneNumber` field), all related functions must be updated. - There is no enforcement of "valid" state transitions. --- ### Step 2: Apply the Encapsulation Principle Move the data and the functions that operate on it *inside* a single unit. This unit becomes a **class**. **Step-by-Step Transformation**: **Step 2.1**: Group the data (attributes) inside a class. $$ \text{Class Definition: } \texttt{class Student} \begin{cases} \text{Attributes: } \texttt{rollNo, name, marks} \\ \text{Methods: } \texttt{display(), calculateGrade(), updateMarks()} \end{cases} $$ **Step 2.2**: Apply access modifiers to enforce data hiding. $$ \text{Visibility Rule: } \texttt{private} \leq \text{default} \leq \texttt{protected} \leq \texttt{public} $$ > [!NOTE] > In Java, the access hierarchy is: **`private` (most restrictive) $\rightarrow$ default/package-private $\rightarrow$ `protected` $\rightarrow$ `public` (least restrictive)**. **Step 2.3**: Provide controlled access via public methods (getters/setters). This is the **Encapsulation** pattern. --- ### Step 3: Apply the Inheritance Principle Observe that `Student` and `Teacher` share common properties: `name`, `age`, `address`. **Step 3.1**: Extract common properties into a parent class `Person`. $$ \texttt{class Person} = \{ \texttt{name}, \texttt{age}, \texttt{address}, \texttt{display()} \} $$ **Step 3.2**: Derive `Student` and `Teacher` from `Person`. $$ \texttt{Student} \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \texttt{Person} \quad ; \quad \texttt{Teacher} \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \texttt{Person} $$ **Resulting Hierarchy**: - `Person` (parent) $\rightarrow$ contains `name`, `age`, `address` - `Student` (child) $\rightarrow$ inherits from `Person`, adds `rollNo`, `marks` - `Teacher` (child) $\rightarrow$ inherits from `Person`, adds `subject`, `salary` **Inheritance Mathematical Notation**: $$ \text{If } B \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} A, \text{ then } B \supseteq A \text{ in terms of properties and methods} $$ --- ### Step 4: Apply the Polymorphism Principle Suppose both `Student` and `Teacher` need a `getRole()` method, but the returned role description differs. **Step 4.1**: Declare a common method signature in the parent. $$ \texttt{class Person} \{ \texttt{String getRole() \{ return "Person"; \}} \} $$ **Step 4.2**: Override the method in each child class. $$ \texttt{Student.getRole()} \rightarrow \text{"Student"} $$ $$ \texttt{Teacher.getRole()} \rightarrow \text{"Teacher"} $$ **Step 4.3**: Use a single reference type to invoke the method. A reference of type `Person` can hold a `Student` object or a `Teacher` object. When `getRole()` is called, Java's **dynamic method dispatch** (runtime polymorphism) invokes the correct overridden version based on the actual object type. **Mathematical Representation of Polymorphism**: $$ \text{Let } p : \texttt{Person} $$ $$ \text{If } p \rightarrow \texttt{Student object}, \text{ then } p.\texttt{getRole()} \rightarrow \text{"Student"} $$ $$ \text{If } p \rightarrow \texttt{Teacher object}, \text{ then } p.\texttt{getRole()} \rightarrow \text{"Teacher"} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight** > The key to scoring full marks in OOP theory questions is to demonstrate the *flow* from problem identification (Step 1) to the application of each pillar (Steps 2-4). Examiners reward answers that show *why* each principle is applied, not just *what* it is. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Pillar | Symptom in Code | OOP Pillar to Apply | Resulting Benefit | |---|---|---| | Complex internal logic exposed to user | **Abstraction** | User interacts with a simple interface | | Data being modified from many places, causing bugs | **Encapsulation** | Single point of controlled access | | Duplicate code in multiple classes | **Inheritance** | Common code defined once in parent | | Same method name used for different behaviors | **Polymorphism** | Flexible, extensible code | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Class-Object Relationship Model ```mermaid flowchart TB classA["CLASS DEFINITION
Blueprint Template"]:::blueNode objA["OBJECT 1
Identity: 0xABC
State: red, 60kmph"]:::greenNode objB["OBJECT 2
Identity: 0xDEF
State: blue, 80kmph"]:::greenNode objC["OBJECT 3
Identity: 0xGHI
State: black, 100kmph"]:::greenNode methodA["METHODS
accelerate brake honk"]:::yellowNode attrA["ATTRIBUTES
color speed fuelLevel"]:::yellowNode classA -->|"instantiates"| objA classA -->|"instantiates"| objB classA -->|"instantiates"| objC classA -.->|"contains definition of"| methodA classA -.->|"contains definition of"| attrA objA ---|"shares structure"| objB objB ---|"shares structure"| objC classDef blueNode fill:#1E3A8A,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef greenNode fill:#065F46,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef yellowNode fill:#92400E,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` **Diagram Reading Guide**: - The **CLASS** node is the single blueprint. - **Three OBJECT** nodes are independent instances, each with a unique identity (memory address) and state. - **METHODS** and **ATTRIBUTES** are defined *inside* the class and *shared* by all objects (but each object has its own copy of attribute values). --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The Four Pillars of OOP and Their Interconnection ```mermaid flowchart LR pillars["OOP FOUR PILLARS"]:::centerNode abs["ABSTRACTION
Show what hide how"]:::pillarA enc["ENCAPSULATION
Bundle data restrict access"]:::pillarB inh["INHERITANCE
Acquire reuse extend"]:::pillarC poly["POLYMORPHISM
One interface many forms"]:::pillarD pillars --> abs pillars --> enc pillars --> inh pillars --> poly enc -->|"Achieves data hiding used in"| abs inh -->|"Uses abstraction of parent class"| abs poly -->|"Often implemented via method overriding in inherited classes"| inh abs -->|"Public abstract methods enable"| poly classDef centerNode fill:#581C87,stroke:#6B21A8,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF classDef pillarA fill:#7C2D12,stroke:#9A3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef pillarB fill:#134E4A,stroke:#0F766E,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef pillarC fill:#1E3A8A,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef pillarD fill:#4C1D95,stroke:#5B21B6,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` **Diagram Reading Guide**: - All four pillars branch from a central concept. - The connecting arrows show **interdependence**: - Encapsulation supports Abstraction (you cannot abstract what you haven't encapsulated). - Inheritance leverages Abstraction (child classes inherit abstract behavior of parent). - Polymorphism relies on Inheritance (overriding requires an inherited method). - Abstraction enables Polymorphism (abstract methods force polymorphic implementation). --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Inheritance Hierarchy (Person-Student-Teacher Example) ```mermaid flowchart TB personCls["PERSON CLASS
name age address
display getRole"]:::parentNode studentCls["STUDENT CLASS
rollNo marks
calculateGrade"]:::childNode1 teacherCls["TEACHER CLASS
subject salary
conductClass"]:::childNode2 personCls -->|"extends keyword"| studentCls personCls -->|"extends keyword"| teacherCls noteA["Student inherits name age address display getRole from Person"]:::noteStyle noteB["Teacher inherits name age address display getRole from Person"]:::noteStyle studentCls -.-> noteA teacherCls -.-> noteB classDef parentNode fill:#1E3A8A,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF classDef childNode1 fill:#065F46,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef childNode2 fill:#92400E,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef noteStyle fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#F59E0B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 ``` **Diagram Reading Guide**: - The arrows labeled **"extends"** represent Java's inheritance relationship. - The dotted annotations show what each child class inherits automatically. - This is a classic **KTU 14-mark theory question** setup. Memorize the structure. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** ### Q1. Define Object-Oriented Programming. List and briefly explain the four basic features of OOP. **Model Answer (3 marks)**: **Definition (1 mark)**: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a programming methodology that organizes software design around data (objects) rather than functions and logic. An object is a self-contained entity that bundles together attributes (data) and methods (behavior). **Four Features (2 marks тАФ 0.5 each)**: 1. **Abstraction**: Hiding complex implementation details and exposing only essential features. 2. **Encapsulation**: Wrapping data and methods into a single unit and restricting direct access to data. 3. **Inheritance**: Mechanism by which a class acquires properties and behaviors of another class. 4. **Polymorphism**: Ability of a message or method to take multiple forms based on the object type. --- > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]** ### Q2. Differentiate between a Class and an Object with a suitable example. **Model Answer (3 marks)**: | Basis | Class | Object | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | A blueprint/template that defines properties and behaviors | A runtime instance of a class | | **Memory** | No memory allocated at definition time | Memory is allocated when instantiated using `new` | | **Declaration** | Declared once using `class` keyword | Can be created multiple times | | **Example** | `class Car { ... }` | `Car myCar = new Car();` | | **Keyword** | Template | Instance | **Example (1 mark)**: `Car` is a class defining that all cars have a color, model, and an `accelerate()` method. `myCar = new Car()` is an object representing one specific car with a specific color and model. --- ## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Paper]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the four basic concepts of Object-Oriented Programming in detail. (7 marks) **(b)** With a suitable example, explain the concept of encapsulation. Write a Java program to demonstrate encapsulation. (7 marks) --- ### Model Answer for Q.A(a) тАФ Four OOP Concepts (7 marks) **1. Abstraction (1.75 marks)**: Abstraction is the process of representing essential features without including background details. It focuses on the external behavior of an object, separating *what* an object does from *how* it does it. *Example*: When sending an email, the user provides recipient, subject, and body. The internal process of connecting to a mail server and transmitting data packets is hidden from the user. In Java, abstraction is achieved using `abstract` classes and `interfaces`. **2. Encapsulation (1.75 marks)**: Encapsulation is the wrapping up of data and methods into a single unit (class) and protecting the data from outside interference. Access to data is controlled through well-defined public methods (getters and setters). *Example*: A bank account class hides the balance variable as `private` and provides `deposit()` and `withdraw()` methods to modify it safely. **3. Inheritance (1.75 marks)**: Inheritance is the mechanism by which one class (subclass/child) acquires the properties and methods of another class (superclass/parent). It promotes code reuse and establishes a hierarchical relationship between classes. *Example*: A `Vehicle` parent class is extended by `Car` and `Bike` child classes. Both children inherit `speed` and `fuelType` from `Vehicle` but can have their own additional features. In Java, inheritance is achieved using the `extends` keyword. **4. Polymorphism (1.75 marks)**: Polymorphism allows the same interface (method name) to be used for different underlying forms (data types or classes). It enables a single method call to perform different tasks based on the object that invokes it. *Types*: - **Compile-time polymorphism**: Method overloading тАФ same method name, different parameter lists. - **Run-time polymorphism**: Method overriding тАФ child class redefines a parent class method. *Example*: A `Shape` class has a `draw()` method. The `Circle` subclass overrides `draw()` to draw a circle, while the `Square` subclass overrides it to draw a square. --- ### Model Answer for Q.A(b) тАФ Encapsulation with Java Program (7 marks) **Theoretical Explanation (3 marks)**: Encapsulation is the process of binding data (attributes) and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit called a class. It also restricts direct access to some of the object's components, which is known as **data hiding**. This is typically done by declaring variables as `private` and providing `public` getter and setter methods to access and modify them. **Benefits of Encapsulation**: 1. Data hiding prevents unauthorized access. 2. The class can have total control over its data. 3. Code becomes flexible, maintainable, and reusable. 4. Fields can be made read-only (no setter) or write-only (no getter). **Java Program Demonstrating Encapsulation (4 marks)**: ```java class Student { // Step 1: Private data members (data hiding) private String name; private int rollNo; private double marks; // Step 2: Public getter method for name public String getName() { return name; } // Step 3: Public setter method for name with validation public void setName(String name) { if (name != null && !name.isEmpty()) { this.name = name; } else { System.out.println("Invalid name"); } } // Getter and setter for rollNo public int getRollNo() { return rollNo; } public void setRollNo(int rollNo) { if (rollNo > 0) { this.rollNo = rollNo; } } // Getter and setter for marks public double getMarks() { return marks; } public void setMarks(double marks) { if (marks >= 0 && marks <= 100) { this.marks = marks; } } } public class EncapsulationDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Student s1 = new Student(); // Accessing data only through public methods s1.setName("Arjun"); s1.setRollNo(45); s1.setMarks(89.5); System.out.println("Name: " + s1.getName()); System.out.println("Roll No: " + s1.getRollNo()); System.out.println("Marks: " + s1.getMarks()); // s1.marks = 200; // ERROR: marks has private access } } ``` **Output**: ``` Name: Arjun Roll No: 45 Marks: 89.5 ``` **Explanation of Code (1 mark)**: - All three fields (`name`, `rollNo`, `marks`) are declared `private`, so they cannot be accessed directly from outside the class. - Each field has a corresponding `public` getter and setter method. - The setters contain validation logic to ensure only valid values are assigned. - In `main()`, the fields are accessed only via the public methods, demonstrating controlled access. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) тАФ ALTERNATIVE CHOICE **(a)** Define the following terms with examples: (i) Class, (ii) Object, (iii) Message Passing. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain the concept of inheritance in Java with a suitable program demonstrating single inheritance. (7 marks) --- ### Model Answer for Q.B(a) тАФ Class, Object, Message Passing (7 marks) **(i) Class (2.5 marks)**: A class is a user-defined data type that acts as a template or blueprint for creating objects. It defines the attributes (data members) and methods (member functions) that the objects of that class will have. **Syntax**: ```java class ClassName { // data members // methods } ``` **Example**: ```java class Dog { String breed; int age; void bark() { System.out.println("Woof!"); } } ``` **(ii) Object (2.5 marks)**: An object is a runtime instance of a class. It represents a real-world entity with state, behavior, and identity. Objects are created using the `new` keyword. **Example**: ```java Dog myDog = new Dog(); // creating an object myDog.breed = "Labrador"; // setting attribute myDog.bark(); // calling method ``` A class can be used to create multiple objects. Each object has its own copy of attributes but shares the same method definitions. **(iii) Message Passing (2 marks)**: Message passing is the means by which objects communicate with one another in an OOP system. An object sends a message to another object by invoking one of its public methods, possibly passing arguments. **Example**: ```java Calculator calc = new Calculator(); int result = calc.add(10, 20); // calc receives 'add' message with args 10, 20 ``` **Components of a message**: 1. The receiver object (e.g., `calc`) 2. The method name to be invoked (e.g., `add`) 3. Any parameters passed (e.g., `10, 20`) The receiver processes the message and may return a response. --- ### Model Answer for Q.B(b) тАФ Inheritance in Java (7 marks) **Theoretical Explanation (3 marks)**: Inheritance is a mechanism in Java by which one class (called the *subclass* or *child class*) can inherit the attributes and methods of another class (called the *superclass* or *parent class*). In Java, inheritance is achieved using the `extends` keyword. **Syntax**: ```java class Parent { // parent members } class Child extends Parent { // child members - can use parent's members } ``` **Types of Inheritance in Java**: - Single inheritance (one parent, one child) - Multilevel inheritance (grandparent тЖТ parent тЖТ child) - Hierarchical inheritance (one parent, multiple children) - *Note*: Multiple inheritance (one child, multiple parents) is **not supported** with classes in Java (achieved via interfaces). **Java Program Demonstrating Single Inheritance (4 marks)**: ```java // Parent class class Animal { String name; void eat() { System.out.println(name + " is eating."); } void sleep() { System.out.println(name + " is sleeping."); } } // Child class inheriting from Animal class Dog extends Animal { String breed; // New method specific to Dog void bark() { System.out.println(name + " says: Woof Woof!"); } // Overriding the eat() method @Override void eat() { System.out.println(name + " the " + breed + " is eating dog food."); } } // Main class public class InheritanceDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Dog d = new Dog(); d.name = "Bruno"; d.breed = "Labrador"; // Inherited methods from Animal d.sleep(); // Overridden method d.eat(); // Child's own method d.bark(); } } ``` **Output**: ``` Bruno is sleeping. Bruno the Labrador is eating dog food. Bruno says: Woof Woof! ``` **Explanation (1 mark)**: - `Dog` is the child class and `Animal` is the parent class. - `Dog` inherits `name`, `eat()`, and `sleep()` from `Animal`. - `Dog` adds its own attribute `breed` and method `bark()`. - `Dog` overrides the inherited `eat()` method to provide a more specific implementation. - This demonstrates **code reuse** (no need to redefine `name` and `sleep()` in `Dog`) and **method overriding** (a key polymorphism mechanism). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Confusing Class and Object**: A common mistake is writing *"Object is a program and Class is a function"*. Correct phrasing: *Class is a blueprint, Object is an instance of a class*. > > 2. **Writing only the definition, no example**: In KTU theory, every concept MUST be followed by an example. A definition without an example typically receives only 50% marks for that section. > > 3. **Forgetting to mention `extends` keyword in Inheritance**: Java uses `extends` (not `:`, as in C++). Writing C++ syntax in a Java exam will cost marks. > > 4. **Polymorphism without distinguishing types**: Always explicitly mention **compile-time (overloading)** and **run-time (overriding)**. Examiners expect this distinction. > > 5. **Abstraction conflated with Encapsulation**: Remember тАФ *Encapsulation is about data hiding (HOW)*, *Abstraction is about interface simplification (WHAT)*. Confusing these two is a top reason for partial marking. > > 6. **In code, missing `public class Main`**: Java requires the file name to match the public class. Writing code without a `main()` method means zero execution marks. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary тАФ Read This the Night Before the Exam** - [ ] **OOP** = programming paradigm based on objects that bundle data and behavior. - [ ] **Class** = blueprint / template; defined once; no memory at definition. - [ ] **Object** = instance of a class; created with `new`; has state, behavior, identity. - [ ] **State** = attribute values; **Behavior** = methods; **Identity** = unique memory reference. - [ ] **Abstraction** = show essentials, hide details; achieved via `abstract` class and `interface`. - [ ] **Encapsulation** = bundle data + methods, restrict access; achieved via `private` + public getters/setters. - [ ] **Inheritance** = child acquires parent's properties; achieved via `extends` keyword; promotes reuse. - [ ] **Polymorphism** = same interface, different behavior; achieved via overloading (compile-time) and overriding (run-time). - [ ] **Message Passing** = objects communicate by invoking each other's public methods. - [ ] **Access Modifiers Hierarchy**: `private` < default < `protected` < `public` (most to least restrictive). - [ ] **OOP vs Procedural**: OOP = bottom-up, data-centric, secure. Procedural = top-down, function-centric. - [ ] **Always pair definitions with real-world examples** in KTU answers (car, ATM, animal, bank account). - [ ] **Java-specific**: Multiple inheritance not allowed with classes; use `interface` for it. - [ ] **Code in Java exam**: always include `class Main` with `public static void main(String[] args)`. - [ ] **Method Overriding** requires: same name, same parameters, IS-A relationship, access modifier not more restrictive. - [ ] **Keywords to remember**: `class`, `new`, `extends`, `private`, `public`, `protected`, `abstract`, `interface`, `implements`, `super`, `this`.

topics.title: Introduction to Java - Java Programming and Runtime Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Java - Java Programming and Runtime Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Java** is a **high-level, object-oriented, platform-independent, robust, secure, and multithreaded** general-purpose programming language developed by **James Gosling** and his team at **Sun Microsystems** in **1991** (initially called the *Oak* project, later renamed *Java* in 1995, and now maintained by **Oracle Corporation**).

Java is officially defined by the *Java Language Specification (JLS)*, and its design philosophy is captured in the famous eleven (originally twelve) goals of the Java project, of which the most important is **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Platform Independence in Java** is achieved because Java source code is **NOT compiled into machine-specific native code**. Instead, it is compiled into an intermediate binary representation called **Bytecode (`.class` file)**, which is executed by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** тАФ a software-based virtual machine that acts as an abstraction layer between the bytecode and the underlying hardware/OS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **world-class chef (your Java program)** who writes recipes in a single universal language (Java source code).

* **Traditional Languages (C, C++)** тЖТ You must re-write and re-translate your recipe into the local language of every country (Windows, Linux, Mac). A different translator is needed for each.
* **Java's Approach** тЖТ You write the recipe in **English (Java source code)**. A universal translating machine (the **JVM**, customized per country) reads the English and produces the local dish. You write the recipe **once**; the JVM handles the local language.

That translating machine (JVM) is platform-specific, but the **recipe (bytecode)** is universal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The JVM is **platform-dependent**, but the **bytecode is platform-independent**. This is the single most testable fact in KTU exams on this module.

## 1.3 The Java Platform Family

| Acronym | Full Form | Role |
|---------|-----------|------|
| **JDK** | Java Development Kit | Toolset for **developers** to write, compile, and debug Java programs |
| **JRE** | Java Runtime Environment | Environment required to **run** a Java program (already compiled) |
| **JVM** | Java Virtual Machine | The actual **engine** that executes bytecode line by line |
| **JIT** | Just-In-Time Compiler | A component of the JVM that compiles bytecode to native code **at runtime** for speed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy to remember for exams:**
> $$\text{JDK} \supset \text{JRE} \supset \text{JVM}$$
> If JDK is installed, JRE and JVM come along. If JRE is installed, you can *run* programs but cannot *develop* them.

## 1.4 Salient Features of Java (Board-Favourite List)

1. **Simple** тАУ No pointers, no operator overloading, automatic garbage collection.
2. **Object-Oriented** тАУ Pure OOP language (except primitive types).
3. **Platform Independent** тАУ Bytecode runs on any JVM.
4. **Secured** тАУ No explicit pointer; runs inside the sandbox/JVM.
5. **Robust** тАУ Strong memory management, exception handling, type checking.
6. **Architecture Neutral** тАУ No implementation-dependent features (sizes are fixed).
7. **Portable** тАУ Same bytecode, no platform-specific features.
8. **Multithreaded** тАУ Built-in `Thread` class; multiple tasks concurrently.
9. **Interpreted + Compiled** тАУ Source тЖТ Bytecode (compiled) тЖТ Executed (JIT interpretation).
10. **High Performance** тАУ JIT compiler brings near-native speed.
11. **Distributed** тАУ Supports RMI, EJB for distributed apps.
12. **Dynamic** тАУ Classes are loaded on demand at runtime.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture: JDK vs JRE vs JVM

The **JDK** is the superset package installed on a developer's machine. It contains:

1. **Development Tools** тАУ `javac` (compiler), `java` (launcher), `jdb` (debugger), `jar` (archiver), `javadoc` (doc generator).
2. **JRE (Java Runtime Environment)** тАУ which contains:
   * The **JVM** (Java Virtual Machine).
   * **Core libraries / rt.jar** (runtime classes like `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`).
   * **Supporting files** (property files, fonts, etc.).

The **JVM** itself has three logical sub-systems:

| Sub-system | Function |
|------------|----------|
| **ClassLoader Subsystem** | Loads, links, and initializes `.class` files on demand. |
| **Runtime Data Areas (Memory)** | Allocates memory for method area, heap, stack, PC registers, native stacks. |
| **Execution Engine** | Interprets bytecode OR uses the **JIT compiler** to convert hot-code to native machine code; also includes the **Garbage Collector (GC)**. |

## 2.2 The Java Program Execution Life-Cycle (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most repeated KTU question**. Memorize the order: *Source тЖТ Compiler тЖТ Bytecode тЖТ ClassLoader тЖТ Bytecode Verifier тЖТ Interpreter/JIT тЖТ Native Code тЖТ Execution.*

**Step 1 тАФ Source Code Creation:** The programmer writes `MyProgram.java` in any text editor/IDE.

**Step 2 тАФ Compilation:** The Java compiler `javac` compiles the source into `MyProgram.class` containing **bytecode** (instructions for the JVM, NOT the CPU).

**Step 3 тАФ Class Loading:** The JVM's **ClassLoader Subsystem** reads the `.class` file into memory (Loading тЖТ Linking тЖТ Initialization).

**Step 4 тАФ Bytecode Verification:** The **Bytecode Verifier** checks for illegal code (stack overflow, illegal data type conversions) тАФ a security mechanism.

**Step 5 тАФ Execution:** The **Execution Engine** processes bytecode. The **Interpreter** executes it line by line. The **JIT compiler** detects "hot" methods (called many times) and compiles them to native machine code for performance.

**Step 6 тАФ Runtime Memory:** While executing, the JVM partitions memory into **Method Area, Heap, Stack, PC Registers, and Native Method Stack**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Memory Areas Cheat Sheet

| Memory Area | Stores | Thread Scope |
|-------------|--------|--------------|
| **Method Area** | Class-level data: static variables, method bytecode, constant pool, field data | Shared (1 per JVM) |
| **Heap Area** | All **objects** and instance variables (via `new` keyword) | Shared (1 per JVM) |
| **Stack Area** | Local variables, partial results, method-call frames (LIFO) | Per thread |
| **PC Register** | Address of the currently executing JVM instruction | Per thread |
| **Native Method Stack** | Holds native (C/C++) method references for JNI calls | Per thread |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two memory areas shared by ALL threads:** Method Area and Heap. The other three are **per-thread**. This distinction is a 3-mark favorite.

## 2.4 Java Editions (Frequently Asked in 2-Mark Questions)

| Edition | Full Form | Use Case |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| **JSE / J2SE** | Java Standard Edition | Desktop & standalone applications (the topic of PEERT416) |
| **JEE / J2EE** | Java Enterprise Edition | Web, distributed, server-side enterprise apps |
| **JME / J2ME** | Java Micro Edition | Mobile, embedded, IoT devices |
| **JavaFX** | JavaFX | Rich GUI desktop applications |

## 2.5 Why Java is Both Compiled AND Interpreted

This is a conceptual question worth 3 marks. The answer structure is:

* **Compilation phase:** `javac` translates `.java` тЖТ `.class` (bytecode). The bytecode is **not machine code**; it is instruction set for the JVM.
* **Interpretation phase:** The JVM's interpreter reads bytecode line by line and executes it.
* **JIT phase:** For performance, the JIT compiles hot bytecode into native machine code at runtime so subsequent calls are executed directly by the CPU.

Hence Java is **both compiled (to bytecode) and interpreted (by the JVM)**, with JIT optimization layered on top.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Compiling and Running a Java Program

Consider the canonical first program:

**Step 1 тАФ Write the source file `Hello.java`:**

```java
public class Hello {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello, KTU!");
    }
}
```

**Step 2 тАФ Invoke the compiler `javac`:**

```
javac Hello.java
```

**Step 3 тАФ Output produced:**

The compiler emits `Hello.class` in the same directory. This file contains **bytecode** тАФ binary instructions for the JVM, NOT for x86/ARM.

**Step 4 тАФ Invoke the JVM launcher `java`:**

```
java Hello
```

**Step 5 тАФ JVM internal actions (in sequence):**

1. **ClassLoader** loads `Hello.class` into the **Method Area**.
2. JVM locates the `main(String[])` method signature.
3. **Bytecode Verifier** validates the loaded class.
4. The **Interpreter** begins executing bytecode for `main`.
5. The call to `System.out.println` triggers loading of `java.lang.System` and `java.io.PrintStream` (lazy loading).
6. The string `"Hello, KTU!"` is created in the **Heap**; reference stored in the **Stack** frame of `main`.
7. The JIT compiler may eventually compile the `println` method to native code for speed.
8. Output: `Hello, KTU!` appears on the console.

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: Memory Allocation for a Simple Program

Consider:

```java
class Demo {
    static int count = 0;     // static variable
    int id;                   // instance variable

    void show() {
        int x = 10;           // local variable
        count++;
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step memory mapping at runtime when `main` calls `new Demo().show()`:**

| Variable | Type | Allocated In | Reason |
|----------|------|--------------|--------|
| `count` | static | **Method Area** | Class-level data, shared across all objects |
| `id` | instance | **Heap** (inside the `Demo` object) | Belongs to the object created via `new` |
| `x` | local | **Stack** (inside `show()`'s frame) | Local to the method call, destroyed after `show()` returns |
| Reference to `Demo` object | тАФ | **Stack** (in `main`'s frame) | Holds the heap address of the object |

**Logic of allocation:**

$$
\text{Static data} \rightarrow \text{Method Area} \quad \text{Objects} \rightarrow \text{Heap} \quad \text{Locals} \rightarrow \text{Stack}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough: Why Java is Platform-Independent тАФ A Math-Style Proof

**Given:**
* The Java compiler `javac` produces a `.class` file containing bytecode.
* Bytecode is a standardized instruction set defined by the *Java Virtual Machine Specification*.
* A JVM is implemented for every target platform (Windows, Linux, macOS, Android, etc.).

**To Prove:** A single `.class` file runs unchanged on all platforms.

**Reasoning:**

1. `javac` is part of the JDK and is itself **platform-specific**, but it always emits the *same* standardized bytecode (as long as the source is the same).

2. Let $B$ = bytecode (universal, standardized).
   Let $JVM_W$, $JVM_L$, $JVM_M$ = JVM implementations for Windows, Linux, Mac.
   Let $H_W$, $H_L$, $H_M$ = hardware/OS below each JVM.

3. The execution on Windows is:
$$
B \xrightarrow{JVM_W} \text{Native code for } H_W
$$
On Linux:
$$
B \xrightarrow{JVM_L} \text{Native code for } H_L
$$
On Mac:
$$
B \xrightarrow{JVM_M} \text{Native code for } H_M
$$

4. The bytecode $B$ is identical in all three cases тАФ the source was the same. Therefore the **same `.class` file** runs on all three without recompilation. Hence, **Java is platform-independent.**

**Conclusion:** Platform independence is a property of the *bytecode*, not of the *JVM*. The JVM is platform-dependent; the bytecode is not.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Compilers vs Interpreter vs JIT

| Property | Compiler (javac) | Interpreter | JIT Compiler |
|----------|------------------|-------------|--------------|
| **Input** | `.java` source | bytecode | bytecode (hot methods) |
| **Output** | `.class` bytecode | immediate execution | native machine code |
| **When** | Before execution | During execution (line by line) | During execution (on demand) |
| **Speed** | Fast compile | Slow execution | Fast execution |
| **Part of JDK/JVM?** | JDK | JVM | JVM |
| **Happens once?** | Yes (per source change) | Repeatedly | Once per hot method |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: JDK тКГ JRE тКГ JVM Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    jdkBox["JDK - Java Development Kit"]
    jreBox["JRE - Java Runtime Environment"]
    jvmBox["JVM - Java Virtual Machine"]
    tools["Development Tools: javac, java, jdb, jar, javadoc"]
    libs["Core Libraries - rt.jar with java.lang, java.util, java.io"]
    classLoader["ClassLoader Subsystem"]
    bytecodeVerifier["Bytecode Verifier"]
    rtMem["Runtime Data Areas: Method Area, Heap, Stack, PC Register, Native Stack"]
    execEngine["Execution Engine: Interpreter, JIT Compiler, Garbage Collector"]
    jit["JIT Compiler - Just In Time"]
    interp["Interpreter"]
    gc["Garbage Collector"]

    jdkBox --> tools
    jdkBox --> jreBox
    jreBox --> libs
    jreBox --> jvmBox
    jvmBox --> classLoader
    jvmBox --> bytecodeVerifier
    jvmBox --> rtMem
    jvmBox --> execEngine
    execEngine --> interp
    execEngine --> jit
    execEngine --> gc
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Java Program Execution Life-Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[".java source file"] -- "javac compiles" --> byte[".class bytecode file"]
    byte -- "ClassLoader loads" --> mem["Runtime Data Areas"]
    mem -- "Bytecode Verifier checks" --> verified["Verified Bytecode"]
    verified -- "Interpreter executes line by line" --> exec1["Direct execution"]
    verified -- "JIT compiles hot methods" --> native["Native machine code"]
    native -- "CPU executes directly" --> exec2["Fast execution"]
    exec1 --> output["Program Output"]
    exec2 --> output
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: JVM Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cls["ClassLoader Subsystem"]
    rtda["Runtime Data Areas"]
    ee["Execution Engine"]
    jni["JNI and Native Method Libraries"]

    subgraph s1[" "]
        loading["Loading - reads .class file"]
        linking["Linking - verifies, prepares, resolves symbolic references"]
        init["Initialization - runs static initializers"]
        cls --> loading --> linking --> init
    end

    subgraph s2[" "]
        method["Method Area - class metadata, static vars, constant pool"]
        heap["Heap Area - all objects and instance variables"]
        stack["Stack Area - per-thread method frames, local variables"]
        pc["PC Registers - per-thread current instruction address"]
        nms["Native Method Stack - native C/C++ method calls"]
    end

    subgraph s3[" "]
        interp["Interpreter - reads bytecode line by line"]
        jit["JIT Compiler - compiles hot bytecode to native code"]
        gc["Garbage Collector - reclaims unreferenced heap objects"]
    end

    init --> method
    init --> heap
    ee --> interp
    ee --> jit
    ee --> gc
    ee --> jni
    jni --> nms
```

## 4.4 Comparison Diagram: C/C++ Execution vs Java Execution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cpp["C or C++ Execution - Platform Dependent"]
        cSrc[".c source"] -- "gcc/clang" --> cObj[".o object file machine specific"]
        cObj -- "linker" --> cExe["Native executable for one OS only"]
    end

    subgraph java["Java Execution - Platform Independent"]
        jSrc[".java source"] -- "javac" --> jByte[".class bytecode universal"]
        jByte -- "any JVM" --> jExe["Native execution on host OS"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the features of Java that make it platform-independent and robust.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **Platform Independence:** Java source code is compiled by `javac` into **bytecode** (intermediate code) stored in a `.class` file. This bytecode is executed by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**, which is available for every platform (Windows, Linux, Mac). Since the bytecode is universal and only the JVM is platform-specific, the same `.class` file runs on any platform тАФ this is the **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** principle.
* **Robustness:** Java achieves robustness through **(a)** strong type-checking at compile time, **(b)** exception handling for runtime errors, **(c)** automatic garbage collection (no manual `free()` like C++), and **(d)** absence of explicit pointers, eliminating illegal memory access.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "Java uses bytecode" and stop. You **must** mention the role of the JVM and WORA to score full marks.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between JDK, JRE, and JVM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | JDK | JRE | JVM |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Full Form** | Java Development Kit | Java Runtime Environment | Java Virtual Machine |
| **Purpose** | Develop + run Java programs | Run already-compiled Java programs | Execute bytecode |
| **Contains** | JRE + dev tools (`javac`, `jdb`, `jar`) | JVM + runtime libraries (`rt.jar`) | ClassLoader, Bytecode Verifier, Execution Engine |
| **Who needs it?** | Programmers | End users | Internal part of JRE |
| **Hierarchy** | JDK тКГ JRE тКГ JVM | тАФ | тАФ |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) with a neat block diagram. Describe its major subsystems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the step-by-step procedure for compiling and executing a Java program. Explain the role of `javac` and the `java` launcher.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a) тАФ JVM Architecture**

The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is the cornerstone of Java's platform independence. It is a software-based execution engine that loads, verifies, and executes Java bytecode. The JVM architecture is divided into **three major subsystems**:

**1. ClassLoader Subsystem** тАФ Responsible for loading `.class` files into memory. It performs three phases:
* **Loading:** Reads the `.class` file using `Bootstrap`, `Extension`, and `Application` class loaders.
* **Linking:** Combines the bytecode with the JVM runtime тАФ performs **Verification** (checks for stack overflow, type safety), **Preparation** (allocates memory for static variables), and **Resolution** (replaces symbolic references with direct references).
* **Initialization:** Executes static initializers and assigns values to static variables.

**2. Runtime Data Areas (Memory)** тАФ The JVM partitions memory into five logical areas:
* **Method Area** тАФ Stores class-level metadata, static variables, method bytecode, and runtime constant pool. Shared across all threads.
* **Heap Area** тАФ Stores all objects and instance variables created via the `new` keyword. Shared across all threads and is the primary target of the **Garbage Collector**.
* **Stack Area** тАФ Each thread has its own stack, holding method-call frames with local variables and partial results. LIFO structure.
* **PC Register** тАФ Each thread has a Program Counter holding the address of the currently executing JVM instruction.
* **Native Method Stack** тАФ Holds references to native (C/C++) methods invoked through JNI.

**3. Execution Engine** тАФ The actual unit that runs the bytecode. It contains:
* **Interpreter** тАФ Reads bytecode line by line and executes. Slow but immediate.
* **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler** тАФ Detects "hot" methods (frequently executed) and compiles them to native machine code for faster subsequent execution.
* **Garbage Collector (GC)** тАФ Automatically reclaims memory of objects no longer referenced, preventing memory leaks.

**Block Diagram (textual representation for answer sheet):**

```
                JVM Architecture
   тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
   тФВ  ClassLoader Subsystem               тФВ
   тФВ  (Loading тЖТ Linking тЖТ Initialization)тФВ
   тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФмтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
                  тЖУ
   тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
   тФВ  Runtime Data Areas (Memory)         тФВ
   тФВ  Method Area тФВ Heap тФВ Stack тФВ PC тФВ NMSтФВ
   тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФмтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
                  тЖУ
   тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
   тФВ  Execution Engine                    тФВ
   тФВ  Interpreter тФВ JIT Compiler тФВ GC     тФВ
   тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students only draw the diagram without naming the three subsystems and their internal components. **Naming + brief description = full marks.**

---

#### **Model Solution to (b) тАФ Compilation and Execution Steps**

**Step 1 тАФ Source Code Creation:** The programmer writes a Java program and saves it as `Hello.java` using any text editor or IDE (Eclipse, IntelliJ, NetBeans).

**Step 2 тАФ Compilation:** The Java compiler **`javac`** (provided by JDK) is invoked from the command line:

```
javac Hello.java
```

The compiler performs lexical analysis, syntax checking, semantic analysis, and bytecode generation. If there are no compile-time errors, it produces a file called **`Hello.class`** in the same directory. This file contains **bytecode** тАФ instructions for the JVM, not for the CPU.

**Step 3 тАФ Execution / Running:** The Java launcher **`java`** is invoked:

```
java Hello
```

Note: the `.class` extension is **not** typed. The `java` command:
* Loads `Hello.class` into the JVM's **ClassLoader Subsystem**.
* Performs **Bytecode Verification** for security.
* Locates the `public static void main(String[] args)` method.
* The **Interpreter** begins executing bytecode instructions.
* The **JIT Compiler** may compile frequently executed methods to native code for performance.
* Output is displayed on the console.

**Role Summary:**

* **`javac`:** Converts `.java` source тЖТ `.class` bytecode. **Part of the JDK. Platform-independent output.**
* **`java`:** Launches the JVM, loads the `.class` file, and executes it. **Platform-specific launcher, but the bytecode it runs is universal.**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the features of Java. Why is Java considered both compiled and interpreted?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the different memory areas of the JVM. Explain which areas are shared between threads and which are thread-private.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a) тАФ Features of Java & Compiled-Interpreted Nature**

Java provides twelve key features, summarized below:

1. **Simple:** No pointers, no operator overloading, no preprocessor тАФ easier to learn than C++.
2. **Object-Oriented:** Everything (except primitives) is an object; supports encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction.
3. **Platform Independent:** Bytecode runs on any JVM тАФ the WORA principle.
4. **Secured:** No explicit pointer, security manager, bytecode verification, sandbox model.
5. **Robust:** Strong type-checking, exception handling, automatic garbage collection.
6. **Architecture Neutral:** Fixed sizes for all primitive types (e.g., `int` is always 32-bit, unlike C where it varies).
7. **Portable:** Same bytecode, same behavior on all platforms.
8. **Multithreaded:** Built-in `Thread` class тАФ multiple threads share the same memory.
9. **Interpreted + Compiled:** See below.
10. **High Performance:** JIT compiler brings near-native speed.
11. **Distributed:** Supports RMI, EJB, sockets for distributed computing.
12. **Dynamic:** Classes are loaded on demand using reflection.

**Why Java is Both Compiled AND Interpreted:**

* **Compilation stage:** The Java compiler `javac` compiles `.java` source code into **bytecode** stored in `.class` files. This compilation happens **once** (when the source changes). The output is NOT machine code тАФ it is an intermediate representation.
* **Interpretation stage:** The JVM's **interpreter** reads the bytecode **line by line** at runtime and executes it. This is similar to how Python works, except the bytecode already passed through a compile step.
* **JIT optimization:** To overcome the slowness of pure interpretation, the **Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler** inside the JVM monitors execution. When it identifies "hot" methods (executed many times), it compiles them into **native machine code** for that specific platform. Subsequent calls to that method run directly on the CPU, bypassing interpretation.

Hence, Java undergoes **compilation to bytecode (offline)** followed by **interpretation + JIT compilation (online at runtime)**. This hybrid model is what gives Java both portability and high performance.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b) тАФ JVM Memory Areas**

The JVM divides its runtime memory into **five logical areas**, which can be classified as **shared** or **thread-private**:

**Shared Memory Areas (one per JVM):**

1. **Method Area:**
   * Stores class-level metadata: class structure, method bytecode, field data, runtime constant pool.
   * Stores **static variables**.
   * Created at JVM startup.
   * Logically part of the heap in some JVM implementations.

2. **Heap Area:**
   * Stores **all objects** and their **instance variables** (created via `new`).
   * Shared across all threads тАФ therefore requires synchronization for thread-safe code.
   * Managed by the **Garbage Collector**, which reclaims memory of unreferenced objects.
   * Divided into Young Generation, Old Generation, and Permanent/Metaspace (in modern JVMs).

**Thread-Private Memory Areas (one per thread):**

3. **Stack Area:**
   * Each thread has its own JVM stack, created when the thread starts.
   * Stores **stack frames** тАФ one frame per active method call.
   * Each frame contains: local variables, operand stack, frame data, return address.
   * **LIFO** structure: frames are pushed on method entry, popped on method exit.
   * Stores **local variables** and **partial results**.

4. **PC (Program Counter) Register:**
   * Each thread has its own PC register.
   * Holds the address of the **current JVM instruction** being executed.
   * For native methods, the value of the PC register is **undefined**.

5. **Native Method Stack:**
   * Each thread has its own native method stack.
   * Used to invoke **native (C/C++) methods** via the Java Native Interface (JNI).
   * Not used for regular Java methods.

**Summary Table:**

| Memory Area | Shared / Private | Stores |
|-------------|------------------|--------|
| Method Area | Shared | Class metadata, static variables, constant pool |
| Heap Area | Shared | Objects, instance variables |
| Stack Area | Per-thread | Method frames, local variables |
| PC Register | Per-thread | Current instruction address |
| Native Method Stack | Per-thread | Native method calls |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is stating that the **Stack is shared**. It is **NOT** тАФ each thread has its own stack. Only the **Method Area and Heap** are shared. Mixing this up costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist тАФ Module 1: Introduction to Java**

* **Inventor:** James Gosling, Sun Microsystems, 1991 (Oak) тЖТ 1995 (Java) тЖТ Oracle (current).
* **Latest LTS Version:** Java 21 (as of 2024 scheme).
* **Motto:** "Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA).
* **Hierarchy:** $\text{JDK} \supset \text{JRE} \supset \text{JVM}$.
* **JDK** is for *developers*; **JRE** is for *end-users*; **JVM** is the *engine* that runs bytecode.
* **File extensions:** `.java` (source) тЖТ `.class` (bytecode).
* **Compilation command:** `javac FileName.java`
* **Execution command:** `java ClassName` (no `.class` extension).
* **Bytecode** is **platform-independent**; **JVM** is **platform-dependent**.
* **JVM 3 Subsystems:** ClassLoader, Runtime Data Areas, Execution Engine.
* **5 Memory Areas:** Method Area, Heap (shared) тАФ Stack, PC Register, Native Method Stack (per-thread).
* **Static variables** тЖТ Method Area; **Objects/Instance vars** тЖТ Heap; **Local vars** тЖТ Stack.
* **Java is both compiled and interpreted:** `javac` compiles to bytecode; JVM interprets (with JIT for performance).
* **JIT** = Just-In-Time compiler inside JVM; converts hot bytecode to native code at runtime.
* **GC** = Garbage Collector inside JVM; reclaims unreferenced heap memory automatically.
* **Java Editions:** JSE (desktop), JEE (enterprise/web), JME (mobile/embedded).
* **Why Java is robust:** strong typing, exception handling, no pointers, GC.
* **Why Java is secure:** bytecode verification, sandbox, no explicit pointers, security manager.
* **Why Java is multithreaded:** built-in `Thread` class, `synchronized` keyword.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development Platforms - Java Virtual Machine (JVM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Development Platforms - Java Virtual Machine (JVM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is an abstract computing machine, runtime engine, and specification that enables a computer to run a Java program. It is the cornerstone of the Java platform, responsible for converting **Java bytecode** (intermediate representation) into native machine instructions at runtime, thereby providing the celebrated **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** portability.

The JVM performs three primary functions:
1. **Loads** code (via the ClassLoader subsystem)
2. **Verifies** code (bytecode verifier)
3. **Executes** code (via the execution engine and runtime data areas)

> [!NOTE]
> The JVM is **platform-dependent** (a separate JVM exists for Windows, Linux, macOS), but the **bytecode** it consumes is **platform-independent**. This asymmetry is the secret of Java's cross-platform nature.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of the JVM as a **Universal Power Adapter** used during international travel:

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Java Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Your Laptop | Java Source Code (`.java`) | High-level human-readable instructions |
| The Charger Tip (Type-C, Lightning) | Platform-Specific Hardware | Different OS/CPU architectures |
| The Universal Adapter | **JVM** | Standardized converter |
| Wall Socket (US/UK/EU) | Native Machine Code | Hardware-executable binary (0s and 1s) |

Just as the universal adapter converts varying wall-socket voltages into the single standard your laptop accepts, the JVM converts the same standardized Java bytecode into the unique machine code required by Windows, Linux, or macOS. **One adapter (JVM), many sockets (platforms), one device (your Java program).**

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **JVM Specification Origin:** Originally developed by **Sun Microsystems** (1995); currently maintained under the **Java Community Process (JCP)** as **JSR-924** (for Java 11+ JVMs).
> - **Bytecode Size:** Each instruction is a **single byte** (hence the name *bytecode*) тАФ the opcode is **1 byte**, operands can be 0 to 4 bytes.
> - **Stack-Based Architecture:** JVM is a **0-operand (stack-based) machine**, in contrast to register-based CPUs.
> - **Memory Unit:** **1 KB = 1024 bytes**, default heap sizes typically **256 MB тАУ 4 GB** depending on JVM tuning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of the JVM Architecture

The JVM is logically partitioned into three cooperating subsystems. Understanding these is **mandatory** for KTU 14-mark questions on JVM internals.

#### Pillar 1: ClassLoader Subsystem (The Delivery System)
- **Responsibility:** Locates, reads, and loads `.class` files into JVM memory.
- **Three Built-in Loaders (Parent-Del delegation model):**
  1. **Bootstrap ClassLoader** тАФ Loads core Java API classes from `rt.jar` / `jrt-fs` (written in native C/C++).
  2. **Platform ClassLoader (Extension ClassLoader)** тАФ Loads extension classes from `jre/lib/ext` or module path.
  3. **Application ClassLoader (System ClassLoader)** тАФ Loads classes from the application's classpath (`CLASSPATH` env variable / `-cp` flag).
- **Phases of Loading:** *Loading тЖТ Linking (Verify, Prepare, Resolve) тЖТ Initialization*

#### Pillar 2: Runtime Data Areas (The Memory Blueprint)
The JVM partitions memory into five logical regions:

| Memory Area | Thread Scope | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Method Area** | Shared (per JVM) | Stores class metadata, static variables, constant pool, method bytecode |
| **Heap Area** | Shared (per JVM) | Stores all **objects** and instance variables (GC-managed) |
| **Stack Area** | Per-thread | Stores `frames` for method calls (local variables, operand stack, frame data) |
| **PC Registers** | Per-thread | Holds address of currently executing JVM instruction |
| **Native Method Stack** | Per-thread | Supports native (C/C++) methods invoked via JNI |

#### Pillar 3: Execution Engine (The CPU of JVM)
- **Interpreter:** Reads bytecode line-by-line and executes (fast startup, slow steady-state).
- **Just-In-Time (JIT) Compiler:** Compiles hot bytecode paths to native machine code at runtime (slow startup, fast steady-state). Examples: **C1 (Client Compiler)**, **C2 (Server Compiler)**, and **Graal JIT** (Java 11+).
- **Garbage Collector (GC):** Automatic memory reclamation. Common algorithms: **Serial GC, Parallel GC, G1 GC (default since Java 9), ZGC, Shenandoah**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **must-memorize** facts and formulas for the KTU board exam. The vertical bar `\vert` is used inside math expressions to prevent table-syntax breakage.

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Default Value / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $N_{methods}$ | Number of stack frames = $N_{active\,methods}$ | One frame per method call |
| $S_{operand}$ | Operand stack depth per frame | Max 65535 slots |
| $S_{local}$ | Local variables array size per frame | Max 65536 entries |
| $T_{exec}$ | Total execution time | $T_{exec} = T_{interpret} + T_{JIT}$ |
| $M_{heap}$ | Heap memory size | Configurable via `-Xms` and `-Xmx` flags |
| $GC_{pauses}$ | Stop-The-World pause time | G1 GC target: $\le 10$ ms |
| $N_{bytecode}$ | Total bytecode size | Sum of all method bytecodes in `.class` |
| $P_{WORA}$ | Portability Metric | $P_{WORA} = 1$ if JVM exists for platform, else $0$ |
| $B_{verifier}$ | Bytecode verification complexity | $O(N_{bytecode})$ linear pass |
| $\vert x \vert$ | Absolute address offset within method | Signed 32-bit branch offset |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The JVM is **not limited to Java**! Many modern languages target the JVM and reuse its mature ecosystem:
> - **Kotlin** (Official Android language) тАФ compiles to JVM bytecode.
> - **Scala** тАФ functional/OOP hybrid on JVM.
> - **Clojure** тАФ Lisp dialect on JVM.
> - **Groovy** тАФ dynamic scripting on JVM.
> - **JavaScript via GraalVM** тАФ polyglot execution engine.
>
> This makes the JVM arguably the **most widely deployed virtual machine in the world**, powering **~35+ billion** active JVMs (devices, servers, Android phones, embedded cards).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Process Analysis & Execution Matrix

### 3.1 The Complete JVM Execution Pipeline

The following derivation presents the **end-to-end execution flow** of a Java program. Each transition is mathematically/logically explicit so a KTU student can reproduce it in any descriptive question.

**Given:** A Java source file `Hello.java` and any compliant platform (Windows/Linux/macOS).

**Step 1 тАФ Source Compilation (offline, by `javac`)**

$$
\text{Hello.java} \xrightarrow{\,javac\,} \text{Hello.class}
$$

The `javac` compiler (part of the JDK) translates source code into **bytecode**, a platform-neutral instruction set. The output file `Hello.class` is the **unit of deployment** in Java.

**Step 2 тАФ Class Loading (online, by ClassLoader Subsystem)**

The JVM invokes the **Application ClassLoader**, which delegates upward to the **Platform ClassLoader**, which in turn delegates to the **Bootstrap ClassLoader**. The chain returns the class if found; otherwise a `ClassNotFoundException` is thrown.

$$
\text{Bootstrap} \rightarrow \text{Platform} \rightarrow \text{Application} \rightarrow \text{CustomUserLoader}
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Linking (Verify тЖТ Prepare тЖТ Resolve)**

- **Verify:** Bytecode verifier performs a linear pass $O(N_{bytecode})$ to ensure no stack overflow, no illegal type casts, and proper branch targets. Failure here produces `VerifyError`.
- **Prepare:** Static fields are allocated in the **Method Area** and initialized to **default zero values** (0, false, null).
- **Resolve:** Symbolic references (e.g., `java/lang/Object`) are replaced with direct references (memory pointers).

**Step 4 тАФ Initialization**

The `<clinit>` (class initializer) method executes, assigning declared values to static variables in source-code order. This is the only step where user-defined static logic actually runs.

**Step 5 тАФ Object Instantiation & Method Invocation**

When `main()` is called, the JVM:

1. Allocates a `main` **frame** on the **JVM Stack** of the main thread.
2. Loads the operand stack and local variables array of the frame.
3. The **Execution Engine** either interprets bytecode or JIT-compiles it.

Let us formalize the execution time decomposition. Let $I$ be the total number of bytecode instructions in `Hello.class`, $t_{int}$ be the average interpretation time per instruction, and $H$ be the hot-spot threshold (call count beyond which JIT triggers).

$$
T_{exec} = \underbrace{(I - H) \cdot t_{int}}_{\text{interpreted tail}} \;+\; \underbrace{H \cdot t_{JIT}}_{\text{compiled hotspot}} \;+\; \underbrace{T_{GC}}_{\text{garbage collection overhead}}
$$

Here, $H$ is determined dynamically by the JVM's **hotspot detection counters** (default threshold $\approx 10{,}000$ invocations per method, configurable via `-XX:CompileThreshold`).

**Step 6 тАФ Garbage Collection Sweep**

The **Garbage Collector** periodically identifies unreachable objects (no live references from any GC root) and reclaims their heap memory. Reachability is defined as:

$$
\text{Reachable}(o) \;=\; \exists \,g \in \text{GC Roots} \;\mid\; g \overset{*}{\rightarrow} o
$$

where $\overset{*}{\rightarrow}$ denotes the transitive closure of reference pointers, and **GC Roots** include local variables on stacks, active threads, and static fields.

**Step 7 тАФ Program Termination**

The `main()` frame is popped, the main thread terminates, and if it was a non-daemon thread, the JVM exits, releasing all runtime data areas.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Memory Layout for a Sample Execution

Consider a snippet:
```java
class Demo {
    static int counter = 0;     // тЖТ Method Area
    String name;                // тЖТ Heap (per object)
    void greet() {              // тЖТ Method Area (code), Stack (frame on call)
        int x = 10;             // тЖТ Stack frame (local variable)
    }
    public static void main(String[] a) {
        Demo d = new Demo();    // d тЖТ Stack; new Demo() тЖТ Heap
    }
}
```

| Variable / Object | Stored In | Lifecycle |
|---|---|---|
| `counter` (static) | Method Area | Until class unloaded |
| `name` (instance) | Heap | Until object GC'd |
| `d` (local reference) | Stack (`main` frame) | Until method returns |
| `greet()` bytecode | Method Area | Until class unloaded |
| `x` (local primitive) | Stack (`greet` frame) | Until `greet` returns |

### 3.3 KTU Analytical Traps to Avoid

> [!WARNING]
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Saying "JVM is platform-independent." **Wrong.** The **JVM itself is platform-dependent**; only the **bytecode** is platform-independent.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **JDK**, **JRE**, and **JVM**. *JDK тКГ JRE тКГ JVM*. The **JDK** is for developers, **JRE** is for end-users, **JVM** is the runtime engine.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Believing Java is "purely interpreted." Modern JVMs use **mixed-mode execution** (Interpreter + JIT).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level JVM Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end JVM architecture** and the flow of a Java program from `.class` file to native execution. All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed, and labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[CLASS FILE .class] --> A1[CLASS LOADER SUBSYSTEM]
    A1 --> A2[BOOTSTRAP CLASS LOADER]
    A1 --> A3[PLATFORM CLASS LOADER]
    A1 --> A4[APPLICATION CLASS LOADER]
    A1 --> A5[CUSTOM USER CLASS LOADER]
    A2 --> B1[RUNTIME DATA AREAS]
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    A5 --> B1
    B1 --> B2[METHOD AREA]
    B1 --> B3[HEAP AREA]
    B1 --> B4[STACK AREA PER THREAD]
    B1 --> B5[PC REGISTERS]
    B1 --> B6[NATIVE METHOD STACK]
    B2 --> C1[EXECUTION ENGINE]
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[INTERPRETER]
    C1 --> C3[JIT COMPILER C1 C2 GRAAL]
    C1 --> C4[GARBAGE COLLECTOR G1 ZGC SHENANDOAH]
    C1 --> D1[JNI NATIVE METHOD LIBRARIES]
    D1 --> E1[NATIVE OPERATING SYSTEM WINDOWS LINUX MACOS]
    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> F1[PROGRAM OUTPUT]
    C3 --> F1
    C4 --> F1
```

### 4.2 ClassLoader Delegation Model (Parent-Del Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    req[APPLICATION REQUESTS CLASS X] --> app[APPLICATION CLASS LOADER]
    app --> plat{FOUND IN PLATFORM}
    plat -->|NO| plat2[PLATFORM CLASS LOADER]
    plat2 --> boot{FOUND IN BOOTSTRAP}
    boot -->|NO| boot2[BOOTSTRAP CLASS LOADER RT JAR]
    boot2 -->|NOT FOUND| exc[CLASSNOTFOUND EXCEPTION]
    boot -->|YES| ret1[RETURN CLASS TO PLATFORM]
    plat -->|YES| ret2[RETURN CLASS TO APPLICATION]
    plat2 -->|YES| ret3[RETURN CLASS TO APPLICATION]
    ret1 --> ret2
    ret2 --> use[LOAD CLASS X INTO JVM MEMORY]
    ret3 --> use
```

### 4.3 JIT Compilation vs Interpretation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s0[BYTECODE ENTERS EXECUTION ENGINE] --> s1{METHOD HOTSPOT THRESHOLD REACHED}
    s1 -->|NO| s2[INTERPRETER EXECUTES LINE BY LINE]
    s1 -->|YES| s3[JIT COMPILER TRANSLATES TO NATIVE MACHINE CODE]
    s3 --> s4[METHOD CACHE NATIVE CODE STORAGE]
    s4 --> s5[RE EXECUTE FROM CACHE FAST PATH]
    s2 --> s1
    s5 --> s1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Trace any class-loading request from `APPLICATION REQUESTS CLASS X` downward тАФ the request **bubbles up** to the highest-priority loader first (parent-delegation), and the **class itself bubbles back down** if found. This prevents user code from spoofing core Java classes (a critical security feature).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
> **Define Java Virtual Machine. Explain how it achieves platform independence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is an abstract runtime engine that loads, verifies, and executes Java bytecode. Platform independence (WORA тАФ *Write Once, Run Anywhere*) is achieved through a **two-tier translation model**:
1. The **Java compiler (`javac`)** converts source code into **platform-neutral bytecode** (`.class` file).
2. A **platform-specific JVM** (separate implementations for Windows, Linux, macOS) installed on the target machine translates this bytecode into native machine instructions at runtime.

Thus, the **bytecode is portable**, while the **JVM adapts it** to the underlying hardware. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for the two tiers.)*

---

> **Question 2. [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024 Pattern]**
> **Differentiate between JDK, JRE, and JVM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **JDK** | **JRE** | **JVM** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Java Development Kit | Java Runtime Environment | Java Virtual Machine |
| Purpose | Develop + Run | Run only | Execute bytecode |
| Contains | JRE + `javac`, `jdb`, etc. | Libraries + JVM | Runtime engine |
| Audience | Developers | End-users | Internal component |
| Contains JVM? | Yes (transitively) | Yes | Itself |
| Contains `javac`? | Yes | No | No |

**Key relation:** `JDK тКГ JRE тКГ JVM` *(3 marks: 1 for diagram/relation, 2 for the table.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023 / Model Paper, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of the Java Virtual Machine with a neat block diagram. Discuss the functions of each subsystem. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the ClassLoader subsystem in detail. Explain the delegation model with an example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (a) [7 marks]

The **JVM architecture** is logically divided into **three major subsystems**:

1. **ClassLoader Subsystem (Loading Mechanism):**
   Responsible for loading `.class` files into JVM memory. Performs three activities: *Loading*, *Linking* (Verify, Prepare, Resolve), and *Initialization*. Uses the **Parent-Delegation Model** for security and uniqueness.

2. **Runtime Data Areas (Memory Blueprint):**
   Five logical memory regions exist:
   - **Method Area** тАФ class metadata, static variables, constant pool *(shared)*.
   - **Heap Area** тАФ all objects and instance variables *(shared, GC-managed)*.
   - **Stack Area** тАФ one frame per active method, per thread.
   - **PC Registers** тАФ current instruction address, per thread.
   - **Native Method Stack** тАФ supports JNI native methods.

3. **Execution Engine (The CPU):**
