The following are **four complete, compile-tested Java programs** demonstrating the final variable rules. Each program is annotated with the KTU cognitive level (L1тАУL6, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy) it tests.

---

### ЁЯУЛ Program 1 тАФ Final Local Variable (L3: Apply)

```java
public class FinalLocalDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final int MAX_USERS = 100;     // Declaration + initialization
        System.out.println("Max users allowed: " + MAX_USERS);

        // MAX_USERS = 200;  // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR: cannot assign a value to final variable
        // The compiler produces:
        // error: cannot assign a value to final variable MAX_USERS

        // Blank final local тАФ legal if assigned before use
        final double PI;
        PI = 3.14159;
        System.out.println("Value of PI: " + PI);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. The compiler allocates stack memory for `MAX_USERS` and binds it to the value `100`.
2. Any statement attempting to reassign `MAX_USERS` triggers `javac` error: *"cannot assign a value to final variable"*.
3. The blank final `PI` is assigned `3.14159` before its first read, satisfying R1.

---

### ЁЯУЛ Program 2 тАФ Final Instance Variable (L4: Analyze)

```java
public class FinalInstanceDemo {
    final int serialNumber;          // Blank final instance variable
    final String model = "KTU-X1";   // Initialized at declaration

    public FinalInstanceDemo(int serial) {
        this.serialNumber = serial;  // Must be assigned in EVERY constructor
        System.out.println("Serial: " + serialNumber + ", Model: " + model);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        FinalInstanceDemo obj = new FinalInstanceDemo(2024);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. `serialNumber` is a **blank final instance variable** тАФ declared but not initialized at the point of declaration.
2. According to **Rule R2**, every constructor in the class must explicitly assign it a value **before the constructor completes**.
3. The compiler inserts an implicit check: *"Final field serialNumber may not have been initialized"*.
4. If a second constructor existed that did not assign `serialNumber`, compilation would fail.

---

### ЁЯУЛ Program 3 тАФ Final Static Variable (L5: Evaluate)

```java
public class FinalStaticDemo {
    public static final int MAX_RETRY = 5;          // Compile-time constant
    public static final String APP_NAME;            // Blank final static

    static {
        APP_NAME = "KTU Java Module 2";             // Initialized in static block
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println(APP_NAME + " allows " + MAX_RETRY + " retries.");
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. `MAX_RETRY` is initialized at declaration тЖТ it becomes a **compile-time constant**. The compiler **inlines** its value at every call site during bytecode generation (the class file contains the literal `5`, not a reference).
2. `APP_NAME` is a **blank final static variable** тАФ it must be assigned in a `static` initializer block (or at declaration), executed **once** when the class is loaded by the ClassLoader.
3. The order of static blocks matters тАФ if `APP_NAME` were accessed before the static block, it would throw `NoClassDefFoundError` or have its default `null` value visible (an anti-pattern).

---

### ЁЯУЛ Program 4 тАФ Final Reference vs Final Primitive (L4: Analyze) тнР KTU Favourite

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

public class FinalReferenceDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final List<String> cities = new ArrayList<>();
        cities.add("Kochi");
        cities.add("Trivandrum");
        cities.add("Kozhikode");
        System.out.println("Cities: " + cities);

        // cities = new ArrayList<>();  // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR: cannot reassign reference
        // But cities.add("Thrissur");  // тЬЕ ALLOWED тАФ internal state mutates

        final int marks = 95;
        // marks = 100;  // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. The reference variable `cities` is **locked** to the first `ArrayList` object created.
2. Operations on that object (`add`, `remove`, `clear`) are perfectly legal тАФ `final` only restricts **reference reassignment**, not **object mutation**.
3. For the primitive `marks`, the value `95` is locked in the stack frame.

---

## ЁЯзо Mathematical Model of "Effective Final"

Java defines an **effectively final** variable using the following formal rule:

$$
\text{EffectivelyFinal}(v) \iff \big(\forall \text{ assignment } a \text{ to } v \text{ after declaration}\big) \, a \text{ is the initialization}
$$

In plain terms: a local variable is *effectively final* if **only one assignment** to it ever occurs (the initialization). Even without the `final` keyword, such variables can be captured by **lambdas** and **anonymous inner classes**.

```java
int counter = 0;       // Initialization тАФ assignment #1
// counter = 5;        // If uncommented, counter would NO LONGER be effectively final
Runnable r = () -> System.out.println(counter);  // тЬЕ Works
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** If you reassign the local variable even once, the lambda that captures it will **fail to compile** with the error: *"local variables referenced from a lambda expression must be final or effectively final"*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## ЁЯЧ║я╕П Diagram 1 тАФ Final Variable Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Final Variables in Java] --> B[Final Local Variable]
    A --> C[Final Instance Variable]
    A --> D[Final Static Variable]
    A --> E[Final Parameter]

    B --> B1[Declared inside method or block]
    B --> B2[Must be initialized before use]
    B --> B3[Stored on STACK memory]

    C --> C1[Declared at class level, non static]
    C --> C2[Initialized at declaration OR in constructor]
    C --> C3[Stored on HEAP memory per object]
    C --> C4[May be BLANK FINAL]

    D --> D1[Declared as static final]
    D --> D2[Initialized at declaration OR in static block]
    D --> D3[Stored in METHOD AREA constant pool]
    D --> D4[True compile time constant if primitive]

    E --> E1[Passed as method argument]
    E --> E2[Cannot be reassigned inside method body]
    E --> E3[Used to preserve original value for lambdas]
```

---

## ЁЯЧ║я╕П Diagram 2 тАФ Memory Architecture for Final Variables

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph StackThread[Stack Frame of main method]
        L1[final int MAX = 100]
        L2[final double PI = 3.14]
    end

    subgraph HeapObj[Heap Object: FinalInstanceDemo]
        I1[final int serialNumber = 2024]
        I2[final String model = KTU X1]
    end

    subgraph MethodArea[JVM Method Area / Metaspace]
        S1[static final int MAX_RETRY = 5]
        S2[static final String APP_NAME]
        S3[Constant Pool entry for MAX_RETRY]
    end

    StackThread -. reference .-> HeapObj
    HeapObj -. reads .-> MethodArea
```

---

## ЁЯЧ║я╕П Diagram 3 тАФ Final Reference vs Mutable Object Internal State

```mermaid
graph TD
    RefVar[final List cities = new ArrayList] -->|locked reference| Obj1[ArrayList Object on Heap]
    Obj1 -->|element 0| E0[Kochi]
    Obj1 -->|element 1| E1[Trivandrum]
    Obj1 -->|element 2| E2[Kozhikode]
    Obj1 -.->|allowed mutation| AddOp[list add Thrissur]
    RefVar -.->|REJECTED| NewRef[new ArrayList assignment]
```

---

## ЁЯУР Decision Matrix тАФ When to Use Which Final Variable Type

| Scenario | Best Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical constant (PI, MAX_SIZE) | `static final` | One copy for entire class, inlined |
| Per-object immutable ID (serial, UUID) | `final instance` | Unique per object, set in constructor |
| Loop counter that should not change inside loop | `final local` | Prevents accidental modification |
| Method argument that must remain unchanged | `final parameter` | Defensive programming |
| Value needed in lambda or anonymous class | `final` or *effectively final* | JMM requirement for closures |
| Configuration that is loaded at runtime | `static final` in static block | Cannot use compile-time constant |
| Database connection string | `static final String` | Single source of truth, thread-safe read |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ЁЯУЭ Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a `final` variable and a `static final` variable in Java. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `final` Variable | `static final` Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword combination | `final` only | `static final` together |
| Memory location | Each object has its own copy (heap) | Single copy shared across all objects (method area) |
| Initialization | At declaration or in constructor | At declaration or in static block |
| Number of copies | One per instance | Exactly one per class |
| Use case | Per-object immutable fields | True class-level constants |

```java
class Example {
    final int serialNo = 1;                // final instance
    static final double PI = 3.14159;      // static final constant
}
```

**Mark distribution:** Definition (1) + Difference table (1) + Code example (1) = **3 marks**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]**
**What is a blank final variable in Java? Why must it be initialized in every constructor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **blank final variable** is a `final` variable that is **declared but not initialized** at the point of declaration. The Java compiler enforces that such a variable **must be definitely assigned exactly once** before its first read or before the constructor/object initialization completes.

**Why every constructor?** Because every constructor is a separate code path that creates an independent object. The compiler cannot assume a value from one constructor will carry over to another. Initializing in **every** constructor ensures that **no matter which constructor is invoked, the final field has a definite value before the object becomes accessible to the rest of the program**.

```java
class Student {
    final int rollNo;           // blank final
    final String name;          // blank final

    Student(int r, String n) {
        rollNo = r;
        name = n;
    }

    Student(int r) {            // also must assign name
        rollNo = r;
        name = "Unknown";
    }
}
```

**Mark distribution:** Definition (1) + Reason (1) + Example (1) = **3 marks**

---

## ЁЯУЭ Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### тнР Question A (Choice 1) тАФ **[KTU University Exam тАФ July 2024]**

**a)** Explain the different types of `final` variables in Java with suitable examples. Discuss the rules for initializing each type. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With a neat diagram and code, explain how `final` reference variables differ from `final` primitive variables. Why is this distinction important? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ЁЯУЦ Model Solution for Q-A(a) тАФ 7 Marks

The Java language defines **three** primary types of final variables, plus a special category for parameters:

**1. Final Local Variable** (1.5 marks)
A variable declared inside a method, constructor, or block, prefixed with `final`. It must be initialized **before its first use** in the method body. The compiler performs *definite assignment analysis* to verify this.

```java
void calculate() {
    final int LIMIT = 50;          // Initialized at declaration
    final int total;                // Blank final local
    total = LIMIT * 2;              // Initialized before use тЬЕ
    System.out.println(total);
}
```

**2. Final Instance Variable** (1.5 marks)
Declared at the class level, **non-static**, prefixed with `final`. Must be initialized at:
- the point of declaration, **OR**
- in **every** constructor of the class.

```java
class Account {
    final long accountNumber;
    final String holderName = "Default";
    Account(long accNo) { accountNumber = accNo; }
}
```

**3. Final Static Variable** (2 marks)
Declared as `static final` at the class level. Belongs to the class, not instances. Must be initialized at:
- the point of declaration, **OR**
- in a `static` initializer block.

If initialized with a **compile-time constant expression** (literal or `final` primitive), it is *inlined* by the compiler into the constant pool.

```java
class Config {
    static final int MAX_USERS = 1000;                  // Inlined
    static final String DB_URL;                          // Blank final static
    static { DB_URL = "jdbc:mysql://localhost/ktu"; }   // Static block init
}
```

**4. Final Parameter** (2 marks)
Method/constructor parameter prefixed with `final`. The method body cannot reassign it.

```java
void greet(final String name) {
    // name = "New";  тЭМ Compile error
    System.out.println("Hello, " + name);
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Initialization Rules Summary (must be memorized for KTU):**
> - **Local** тЖТ Initialize before use.
> - **Instance** тЖТ Declaration OR every constructor.
> - **Static** тЖТ Declaration OR static block.
> - **Parameter** тЖТ Initialized by caller, cannot be reassigned.

---

#### ЁЯУЦ Model Solution for Q-A(b) тАФ 7 Marks

**Final Primitive Variable** (3.5 marks)
For primitives (`int`, `double`, `boolean`, etc.), the `final` keyword locks the **value** stored in the memory cell. Any attempt to reassign is a compile-time error.

```java
final int age = 21;
// age = 22;  тЭМ Compilation fails
```

**Final Reference Variable** (3.5 marks)
For object references, `final` locks the **reference (memory address)**, not the **internal state** of the object. You cannot make the variable point to a different object, but you may call mutator methods on the existing object.

```java
final StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("KTU");
sb.append(" 2024");           // тЬЕ Modifies internal buffer
// sb = new StringBuilder();   тЭМ Compile error
```

**Memory Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[final int age = 21] -->|value 21 locked| M1[Stack: age cell]
    R1[final StringBuilder sb] -->|reference locked| M2[Heap: StringBuilder object]
    M2 -->|mutable char array| C1[KTU 2024]
    R1 -.->|REJECTED| M3[Heap: New StringBuilder object]
```

**Why the Distinction Matters:**
- Allows **selective immutability** тАФ protect object identity while permitting controlled mutation.
- Essential for **collection APIs** (`final List`, `final Map` parameters).
- Critical for **lambda capture rules** тАФ effectively final references can be used, but the object itself can still mutate.
- Aligns with OOP principle of **encapsulation** тАФ you control what is locked and what is flexible.

---

### тнР Question B (Choice 2) тАФ **[KTU University Exam тАФ May 2023]**

**a)** What is the significance of the `final` keyword in Java? Explain with reference to:
   (i) Final classes, (ii) Final methods, (iii) Final variables. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of `static final` variables, blank final variables, and final parameters. Explain the output. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ЁЯУЦ Model Solution for Q-B(a) тАФ 7 Marks

The `final` keyword in Java is a **non-access modifier** used to restrict modification. It applies to **classes, methods, and variables**:

**(i) Final Classes** (2.5 marks)
A class declared as `final` **cannot be subclassed (extended)**. This is Java's way of preventing inheritance for security and design reasons.

```java
final class ImmutablePoint {
    private final int x, y;
    ImmutablePoint(int x, int y) { this.x = x; this.y = y; }
}
// class ExtendedPoint extends ImmutablePoint {}  тЭМ Compile error
```

*Examples:* `java.lang.String`, `java.lang.Math`, all wrapper classes (`Integer`, `Double`).

**(ii) Final Methods** (2.5 marks)
A `final` method **cannot be overridden** in any subclass. Used to prevent polymorphic alteration of critical behavior.

```java
class Parent {
    final void display() { System.out.println("Cannot override"); }
}
class Child extends Parent {
    // void display() {}  тЭМ Compile error
}
```

**(iii) Final Variables** (2 marks)
A `final` variable is a **constant** тАФ assigned once, never reassigned. Provides immutability, thread safety, and design clarity.

```java
final int MAX = 100;
```

**Unified Significance:**
- **Security:** Prevents malicious or accidental modification.
- **Design:** Communicates immutable design intent.
- **Optimization:** Enables compiler inlining and JIT optimizations.
- **Concurrency:** Guarantees safe publication across threads.

---

#### ЁЯУЦ Model Solution for Q-B(b) тАФ 7 Marks

```java
public class FinalVariableDemo {
    static final int MAX_ATTEMPTS = 3;       // static final
    static final String INSTITUTION;          // blank final static
    final int studentId;                       // blank final instance

    static {
        INSTITUTION = "APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University";
    }

    FinalVariableDemo(int id) {
        this.studentId = id;                  // initialized in constructor
    }

    void attemptQuiz(final String topic) {    // final parameter
        System.out.println("Topic: " + topic);
        for (int i = 1; i <= MAX_ATTEMPTS; i++) {
            System.out.println("Attempt " + i + " by student " + studentId);
        }
        // topic = "Maths";  тЭМ Cannot reassign final parameter
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        FinalVariableDemo s = new FinalVariableDemo(416);
        s.attemptQuiz("Object Oriented Programming using Java");
        System.out.println("Institution: " + INSTITUTION);
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Topic: Object Oriented Programming using Java
Attempt 1 by student 416
Attempt 2 by student 416
Attempt 3 by student 416
Institution: APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University
```

**Explanation:**
- `MAX_ATTEMPTS` is a true compile-time constant; the loop uses its inlined value `3`.
- `INSTITUTION` is assigned in the static block when the class is loaded.
- `studentId` is a blank final instance variable, assigned via constructor.
- `topic` is a final parameter; attempting to reassign it would cause a compile error.
- This program demonstrates all four categories of final variables in a single class тАФ a typical KTU board expectation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **ЁЯЪи KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Confusing `final` with `finally` or `finalize()`** тАФ these are three different concepts. Marks are deducted if mixed up.
> 2. **Writing `final` class incorrectly as `Final class`** тАФ Java is case-sensitive.
> 3. **Believing `final` makes objects immutable** тАФ `final` locks the reference, NOT the object's internal state. This is the **#1 mark-losing mistake** in KTU exams.
> 4. **Initializing a static final variable inside a constructor** тАФ this is a compile error. Always use a static block.
> 5. **Forgetting to initialize a blank final instance variable in every constructor** тАФ if even one constructor omits it, compilation fails.
> 6. **Using `final` with abstract methods** тАФ illegal combination. `final` prevents overriding, `abstract` requires it.
> 7. **Confusing `final int x;` (legal blank final) with `static int x;` inside a method (illegal тАФ methods cannot have static locals)**.

---

## ЁЯза Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **ЁЯОп Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam**

- тЬЕ `final` variable = **single-assignment lock** (NOT a C-style `#define` constant).
- тЬЕ Three primary types: **final local**, **final instance**, **final static**.
- тЬЕ Plus **final parameter** and **blank final** as special categories.
- тЬЕ Final **primitive** тЖТ value locked.
- тЬЕ Final **reference** тЖТ reference locked, **object state still mutable**.
- тЬЕ Static final with compile-time constant expression тЖТ **inlined into constant pool**.
- тЬЕ Blank final instance тЖТ **must be assigned in EVERY constructor**.
- тЬЕ Blank final static тЖТ **must be assigned in static block** (or at declaration).
- тЬЕ Interface fields are **implicitly `public static final`**.
- тЬЕ Lambda/anonymous class capture requires **final or effectively final** local variables.
- тЬЕ Naming convention: **UPPER_SNAKE_CASE** for constants.
- тЬЕ `final` does **NOT** make objects immutable тАФ use immutable class design (private final fields, no setters, defensive copies).
- тЬЕ Real-world examples: `Math.PI`, `Integer.MAX_VALUE`, `String` class itself, `LocalDate.MAX`.
- тЬЕ KTU marks weightage: typically **6тАУ8 marks** in Part A and a full **14-mark** Part B question.
- тЬЕ Always distinguish `final` (Java keyword) from `finally` (exception handling) and `finalize()` (Object class method) тАФ they are unrelated concepts.

> **ЁЯОУ Closing Note for KTU 2024 Students:** Final variables are the foundation of Java's **immutability contract** тАФ once mastered, they unlock understanding of immutable classes (like `String`), the `final` keyword's application to classes and methods, and Java's concurrency safety model. This is **high-yield territory** for both Module 2 internal assessments and university ESE papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inner Classes, Inheritance - Super Class
Sub Class
The Keyword super
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** The Keyword super ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) In the Java programming language, **`super`** is a **reserved keyword** that functions as a **reference variable** referring to the **immediate parent class object**. It is implicitly created by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) when an object of a subclass is instantiated, providing a direct, type-safe pathway for the subclass to access members (fields, methods) and constructors of its **superclass** (parent class). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"`super` keyword is used to refer to the immediate parent class object. It is used to access parent class constructors, methods, and instance variables when there is a name conflict or when subclass overrides a method inherited from the parent class."* The keyword `super` is part of the Java language's **inheritance access mechanism**, residing in the same family of keywords as `this` and `final`, but uniquely oriented **upward** in the class hierarchy rather than referring to the current object. ### 1.2 The Three Primary Use-Cases of `super` The `super` keyword has exactly three official, syllabus-defined applications in Java: 1. **`super()`** тАФ Invoking the **parent class constructor** from a subclass constructor. 2. **`super.memberVariable`** тАФ Accessing a **parent class instance variable** hidden by a same-named subclass field. 3. **`super.memberMethod()`** тАФ Invoking a **parent class method** that has been **overridden** by the subclass. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy тАФ The Family Tree Passport** > > Imagine you are a citizen of a country (the **subclass**), but your citizenship was originally granted through your parent (the **superclass**). When you need to access certain "ancestral privileges" тАФ like a family surname, a family property deed, or an ancestral skill тАФ you do not reinvent them. Instead, you present a special "passport" that says, *"I am acting on behalf of my parent."* That passport is `super`. > > - `super.method()` тЖТ "Dad, please teach me your original recipe." > - `super.field` тЖТ "Show me the family surname, not my nickname." > - `super()` тЖТ "First, let my parent set things up before I customize my room." The `super` keyword is essentially Java's built-in **upward navigation tool** in the inheritance chain. ### 1.4 Position in the KTU 2024 Module 2 Hierarchy ``` Module 2 тАФ Inheritance & Polymorphism тФЬтФАтФА Inheritance Basics тФЬтФАтФА Types of Inheritance тФЬтФАтФА The Keyword super тЧДтФБтФБ THIS TOPIC тФЬтФАтФА Method Overriding тФЬтФАтФА Dynamic Method Dispatch тФЬтФАтФА Abstract Classes тФФтФАтФА Interfaces ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Mastering `super` is **prerequisite knowledge** for understanding **Method Overriding**, **Dynamic Method Dispatch**, and **Abstract Class** design тАФ all upcoming Module 2 topics. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown тАФ The Three Modes of `super` #### **Mode 1: `super()` тАФ Constructor Invocation** When a subclass object is created, **Java automatically inserts an implicit call** `super();` as the **first statement** of every constructor (if the programmer does not write one explicitly). **Logic Flow:** 1. JVM encounters `new SubClass()`. 2. Execution enters the SubClass constructor. 3. The very first executable statement (whether implicit or explicit) **must** be a call to a parent constructor via `super(...)`. 4. Control transfers to the matching parent constructor. 5. After parent initialization completes, control returns to continue the subclass constructor body. **Syntax Variations:** | Invocation | Meaning | When Used | |------------|---------|-----------| | `super();` | Calls the parent's **no-argument** constructor | Default, automatic insertion | | `super(args);` | Calls the parent's **parameterized** constructor | When parent has no default constructor | | `super(arg1, arg2);` | Calls matching multi-arg parent constructor | Multi-level parameterized inheritance | > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** `super()` **must be the first statement** inside a constructor. Placing it anywhere else causes a **compile-time error**: *"call to super must be first statement in constructor"*. #### **Mode 2: `super.variableName` тАФ Field Access** When a subclass declares a field with the **same name** as one in the parent class, the subclass field **hides** the parent's field. To explicitly access the hidden parent field, the `super` reference is used. **Logic Flow:** 1. Compiler resolves field references in **nearest-scope-first** order (subclass first). 2. To bypass subclass scope and reach parent scope, prefix with `super.`. 3. Useful for **field shadowing** scenarios. #### **Mode 3: `super.methodName()` тАФ Method Access (Overriding Context)** When a subclass **overrides** a method from its parent, the parent's version becomes **hidden** under normal subclass references. To explicitly invoke the parent's version (typically to **extend** rather than completely **replace** behavior), `super.methodName()` is used. **Logic Flow:** 1. Subclass overrides `display()` from parent. 2. Inside subclass `display()`, the programmer wishes to run parent logic first, then add new logic. 3. `super.display();` executes the parent's implementation. 4. Subclass-specific code follows after. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet **Summary Table тАФ All `super` Applications:** | Aspect | `super()` | `super.field` | `super.method()` | |--------|-----------|---------------|------------------| | **Purpose** | Invoke parent constructor | Access parent class field | Invoke parent class method | | **Position Rule** | Must be **first statement** in constructor | Anywhere within instance context | Anywhere within instance context | | **Static Context?** | тЭМ Not allowed in `static` block | тЭМ Cannot use in `static` context | тЭМ Cannot use in `static` context | | **Required When?** | Parent has only parameterized constructor | Field name collision (shadowing) | Method overriding scenario | | **Auto-Inserted?** | тЬЕ Yes (no-arg form) if not written | тЭМ Never auto-inserted | тЭМ Never auto-inserted | | **Constructor Chaining?** | тЬЕ Yes (multi-level possible) | тЭМ No | тЭМ No | **Comparison Table тАФ `this` vs `super` (High-Yield for KTU):** | Feature | `this` | `super` | |---------|--------|---------| | **Refers to** | Current class object | Immediate parent class object | | **Constructor call** | `this()` calls another constructor in **same** class | `super()` calls a constructor of the **parent** class | | **Used for** | Distinguishing instance vars from local/param vars | Accessing overridden/hidden parent members | | **Direction in hierarchy** | Horizontal / self | Vertical / upward | | **Static context** | тЭМ Not allowed | тЭМ Not allowed | | **Position in constructor** | Must be **first statement** (mutually exclusive with `super()`) | Must be **first statement** (mutually exclusive with `this()`) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hard Rule for KTU Exams:** A constructor can use either `this()` OR `super()` as its first statement, **never both**. This is a frequently tested 3-mark question. ### 2.3 Constructor Chaining Mechanism **Constructor chaining** is the process where a chain of constructors is invoked sequentially starting from the topmost superclass down to the subclass. This is achieved through a coordinated sequence of `super()` calls. **Rules Governing Chaining:** - The chain **always starts from the Object class** (the root of the Java hierarchy). - Each constructor in the chain (except `Object`) calls its parent's constructor as the first statement. - If no explicit `super()` is written, the compiler **auto-inserts** `super();` (the no-arg form). - If the parent has **no no-arg constructor**, the compiler raises an error, forcing the programmer to write an explicit `super(args)`. **Mathematical Representation of Chaining Depth:** $$ D_{chain} = N_{classes} - 1 $$ Where: - $D_{chain}$ = Depth of constructor chain - $N_{classes}$ = Number of classes in the inheritance hierarchy For a 3-level hierarchy (`A тЖТ B тЖТ C`), $D_{chain} = 3 - 1 = 2$ explicit/implicit `super()` calls occur during `new C()`. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The `super` keyword is fundamental in: - **GUI Frameworks (Swing/JavaFX):** Extending `JFrame` and invoking `super(title)` to set window titles. - **Exception Handling Hierarchy:** Custom exceptions calling `super(message)` to leverage `Throwable` initialization. - **Servlet Programming:** `HttpServlet` subclasses calling `super()` for proper servlet lifecycle. - **Software Design Patterns:** Template Method pattern relies heavily on `super.method()` to invoke template steps. - **Framework Development:** Spring, Hibernate, and Android SDK base classes are designed assuming subclasses will use `super` correctly. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Code Derivations ### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough тАФ `super()` Constructor Invocation **Problem Scenario:** Given a multi-level inheritance chain, trace the exact order of constructor execution. **Class Hierarchy Definition:** ```java class A { A() { System.out.println("A's constructor"); } } class B extends A { B() { System.out.println("B's constructor"); } } class C extends B { C() { System.out.println("C's constructor"); } } ``` **Step-by-Step Execution Trace:** **Step 1:** Programmer writes `new C();` **Step 2:** JVM allocates memory for object of class `C` on the heap. **Step 3:** Constructor `C()` begins execution. **Step 4:** Java compiler had **auto-inserted** `super();` (which calls `B()`) as the first statement of `C()`. **Step 5:** Control enters `B()`. Compiler had auto-inserted `super();` (which calls `A()`) as the first statement of `B()`. **Step 6:** Control enters `A()`. Compiler had auto-inserted `super();` (which calls `Object()` тАФ implicit root). The print statement executes: prints **"A's constructor"**. **Step 7:** Control returns to `B()`. The print statement executes: prints **"B's constructor"**. **Step 8:** Control returns to `C()`. The print statement executes: prints **"C's constructor"**. **Final Output Order:** $$ \text{Output} = \text{"A's constructor"} \rightarrow \text{"B's constructor"} \rightarrow \text{"C's constructor"} $$ **Visualization of Constructor Chaining:** $$ \text{new C()} \xrightarrow{\text{calls}} C() \xrightarrow{\text{auto super()}} B() \xrightarrow{\text{auto super()}} A() \xrightarrow{\text{auto super()}} Object() $$ ### 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough тАФ `super.field` Variable Shadowing Resolution **Problem Scenario:** A subclass field shadows a parent field. Demonstrate how `super` resolves the ambiguity. **Class Definitions:** ```java class Vehicle { int maxSpeed = 120; } class Car extends Vehicle { int maxSpeed = 180; // shadows parent's maxSpeed void displaySpeeds() { System.out.println("Car maxSpeed (subclass): " + maxSpeed); System.out.println("Vehicle maxSpeed (parent): " + super.maxSpeed); } } ``` **Analytical Breakdown:** **Step 1:** When `new Car().displaySpeeds();` is called, the method executes. **Step 2:** The first `println` references `maxSpeed` (no prefix). Java's scope resolution rule searches: - Local scope of `displaySpeeds()` тЖТ not found - Current class (`Car`) тЖТ **found (180)** - Resolution complete: prints **180** **Step 3:** The second `println` references `super.maxSpeed`. Java interprets `super.` as a directive to bypass the current class scope and search the parent class: - Current class (`Car`) тЖТ bypassed - Parent class (`Vehicle`) тЖТ **found (120)** - Resolution complete: prints **120** **Symbolic Representation of Scope Search:** $$ \text{Resolve}(\text{field}) = \begin{cases} \text{Local Scope} & \text{if found, use it} \\ \text{Current Class Scope} & \text{else if found, use it} \\ \text{Parent Scope via super} & \text{if prefixed with super.} \\ \text{Compile Error} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ ### 3.3 Exhaustive Walkthrough тАФ `super.method()` Overriding Extension **Problem Scenario:** A subclass overrides a parent method but wishes to **augment** (not fully replace) the parent's behavior. **Class Definitions:** ```java class Animal { void speak() { System.out.println("Animal makes a sound"); } } class Dog extends Animal { @Override void speak() { super.speak(); // invoke parent's version first System.out.println("Dog barks"); } } ``` **Step-by-Step Execution:** **Step 1:** `new Dog().speak();` invokes the overridden `speak()` in `Dog`. **Step 2:** The first executable statement `super.speak();` calls the parent `Animal`'s `speak()`. Prints **"Animal makes a sound"**. **Step 3:** Control returns to `Dog.speak()`. The next line executes. Prints **"Dog barks"**. **Final Output:** $$ \text{Output} = \text{"Animal makes a sound"} \rightarrow \text{"Dog barks"} $$ **Conceptual Equation of Behavioral Extension:** $$ \text{Behavior}_{Dog} = \text{Behavior}_{Parent} \oplus \text{Behavior}_{Subclass Addition} $$ Where $\oplus$ denotes behavioral concatenation, achieved mechanically through `super.method()` invocation. ### 3.4 Exhaustive Walkthrough тАФ Parameterized `super(args)` with No Default Parent Constructor **Problem Scenario:** Parent class has **no no-arg constructor**. Subclass must explicitly call the parameterized parent constructor. **Class Definitions:** ```java class Employee { String name; int id; Employee(String name, int id) { // No default constructor exists this.name = name; this.id = id; } } class Manager extends Employee { String department; Manager(String name, int id, String department) { super(name, id); // MANDATORY тАФ no default in parent this.department = department; } } ``` **Step-by-Step Logical Analysis:** **Step 1:** If the programmer **omits** `super(name, id);` and writes only: ```java Manager(String name, int id, String department) { this.department = department; } ``` The compiler attempts to auto-insert `super();` (no-arg form). However, `Employee` has **no no-arg constructor**, so compilation **fails** with: *"error: no suitable constructor found for Employee()"* **Step 2:** Correct version: `super(name, id);` explicitly invokes the parameterized `Employee` constructor. **Step 3:** Parent initializes `name` and `id`. Then subclass initializes `department`. **Symbolic Rule of Constructor Selection:** $$ \text{super}(\underbrace{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n}_{n \text{ args}}) \xrightarrow{\text{matches}} \text{Parent}(\underbrace{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n}_{\text{same signature}}) $$ The argument list of `super(...)` must match **exactly** one parent constructor's parameter list in both **type** and **arity**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1 тАФ The `super` Reference in Memory Architecture The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how `super` and `this` references coexist within a single subclass object in heap memory. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph HEAP["JAVA HEAP MEMORY"] OBJ["SUBCLASS OBJECT
Reference: this"] SUPER["SUPER REFERENCE
Reference: super"] OBJ --- FIELDS1["Subclass Fields
department
bonus"] OBJ --- METHODS1["Subclass Methods
calculateSalary
display"] OBJ --- THISPTR["this pointer
points to OBJ"] SUPER --- FIELDS2["Parent Fields
name
id
baseSalary"] SUPER --- METHODS2["Parent Methods
getName
getId"] THISPTR -.-> OBJ SUPER -.-> OBJ end STACK["STACK MEMORY
empRef (reference variable)"] --> OBJ ``` **Architectural Explanation:** - The **heap object** contains all fields and methods of both the parent and subclass (the subclass object is a **superset** containing parent data). - `this` is an implicit reference pointing to the **entire object** (start of allocation). - `super` is an implicit reference pointing to the **parent portion** of the same object (offset by parent class size). ### 4.2 Diagram 2 тАФ Constructor Chaining Flow in Multi-Level Inheritance ```mermaid flowchart TD A["new SubClassB()"] --> B["SubClassB() constructor begins"] B --> C{"super() explicit
or implicit?"} C -->|Implicit super| D["SuperClassA() invoked"] C -->|Explicit super args| E["Matching parent constructor invoked"] D --> F["Object() constructor invoked
(root of hierarchy)"] E --> F F --> G["Object initialization
completes"] G --> H["SuperClassA initialization
completes"] H --> I["SubClassB initialization
completes"] I --> J["Object fully constructed
and returned to caller"] ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3 тАФ The Three Use-Cases Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Need to use super keyword"] --> Q1{"What do you
want to access?"} Q1 -->|Parent Constructor| C1["Use super() or super args"] C1 --> C1A["Position: FIRST statement
of subclass constructor"] C1A --> C1B["Required when parent has
no default constructor"] Q1 -->|Parent Field| C2["Use super.fieldName"] C2 --> C2A["Needed when subclass field
shadows parent field"] Q1 -->|Parent Method| C3["Use super.methodName"] C3 --> C3A["Needed when subclass overrides
parent method but wants to
invoke original version"] Q1 -->|Static member| C4["ERROR: Cannot use super
in static context"] C4 --> C4A["Use ClassName.memberName
instead"] ``` ### 4.4 Diagram 4 тАФ Block-Level Functional Architecture of `super` Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph COMPILATION["COMPILATION PHASE"] C1["Source Code
subclass.java"] --> C2["Compiler checks
parent class
members exist"] C2 --> C3{"Parent field/method
accessible?"} C3 -->|No| C4["COMPILE ERROR
cannot find symbol"] C3 -->|Yes| C5["super reference
resolved at compile time"] C5 --> C6["Bytecode generated
with invokespecial
or getfield instruction"] end subgraph RUNTIME["RUNTIME PHASE"] C6 --> R1["JVM loads class"] R1 --> R2["Object created
in heap memory"] R2 --> R3["super reference
initialized by JVM"] R3 --> R4["super method call
dispatches to
parent implementation"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Compilation Note:** All `super` references are **statically bound** at compile time (the compiler knows exactly which parent class member to target). This is in contrast to **dynamic method dispatch** (virtual method invocation) which is resolved at runtime. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024 Model]** *Explain any three uses of the `super` keyword in Java with suitable examples.* **Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):** The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable that refers to the immediate parent class object. It has three primary uses: **Use 1 тАФ `super()` to invoke parent class constructor (1 mark):** The `super()` call is used to invoke the constructor of the parent class from a subclass constructor. It must be the first statement in the subclass constructor. ```java class Parent { Parent() { System.out.println("Parent constructor"); } } class Child extends Parent { Child() { super(); // calls Parent constructor System.out.println("Child constructor"); } } ``` **Use 2 тАФ `super.variableName` to access parent class instance variables (1 mark):** When a subclass declares a variable with the same name as the parent class, the parent's variable is hidden. `super.variableName` is used to access the hidden parent variable. ```java class Parent { int x = 10; } class Child extends Parent { int x = 20; void show() { System.out.println(super.x); // accesses Parent's x = 10 System.out.println(x); // accesses Child's x = 20 } } ``` **Use 3 тАФ `super.methodName()` to invoke parent class method (1 mark):** When a subclass overrides a method from the parent, `super.methodName()` is used to invoke the overridden parent method. ```java class Parent { void display() { System.out.println("Parent display"); } } class Child extends Parent { void display() { super.display(); // calls Parent's overridden method System.out.println("Child display"); } } ``` --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023 Model]** *Differentiate between `this` and `super` keywords in Java. Can both be used as the first statement in the same constructor? Justify your answer.* **Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):** | Feature | `this` | `super` | |---------|--------|---------| | Refers to | Current class object | Immediate parent class object | | Used for | Differentiating instance variables from local/parameter variables | Accessing parent class members (fields, methods, constructors) | | Constructor call | `this()` invokes another constructor of **same** class | `super()` invokes constructor of **parent** class | | Scope direction | Within current class (self-reference) | Upward to parent class | **Justification (1 mark):** **No**, both `this()` and `super()` **cannot be used as the first statement in the same constructor**. This is because: - The first statement rule in Java constructors demands that the call to either `this()` or `super()` must be the very first executable statement. - Having both would be syntactically invalid as only one statement can occupy the "first" position. - The compiler raises the error: *"call to this must be first statement in constructor"* or *"call to super must be first statement in constructor"*. ### 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) тАФ Option 1** **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** Explain the concept of constructor chaining in Java with a suitable example demonstrating a three-level inheritance hierarchy. Show the output and explain the order of execution. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What happens when a parent class has only a parameterized constructor and the subclass does not explicitly invoke it using `super()`? Illustrate with a code example and explain the compiler behavior. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### Part (a) тАФ Constructor Chaining (7 Marks) **Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):** Constructor chaining in Java is the mechanism by which a chain of constructors is invoked in sequence, starting from the topmost superclass (Object) down to the subclass that is being instantiated. This ensures that all parent class fields are properly initialized before the subclass's own initialization logic executes. The chaining is facilitated by the `super()` call inserted (explicitly or implicitly) as the first statement in each constructor. **Code Example (3 marks):** ```java class GrandParent { GrandParent() { System.out.println("GrandParent constructor invoked"); } } class Parent extends GrandParent { Parent() { System.out.println("Parent constructor invoked"); } } class Child extends Parent { Child() { System.out.println("Child constructor invoked"); } } public class ChainDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Child c = new Child(); } } ``` **Execution Trace (1 mark):** 1. `new Child()` is called. 2. Compiler had auto-inserted `super();` as first statement in `Child()`, invoking `Parent()`. 3. Compiler had auto-inserted `super();` as first statement in `Parent()`, invoking `GrandParent()`. 4. Compiler had auto-inserted `super();` as first statement in `GrandParent()`, invoking `Object()`. 5. `Object()` executes (no output), then `GrandParent()` prints its message. 6. Control returns to `Parent()` which prints its message. 7. Control returns to `Child()` which prints its message. **Output (1 mark):** ``` GrandParent constructor invoked Parent constructor invoked Child constructor invoked ``` --- #### Part (b) тАФ Missing Explicit `super()` with Parameterized Parent (7 Marks) **Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):** If a parent class defines **only** a parameterized constructor and **no** no-argument (default) constructor, then the Java compiler **cannot** auto-insert the implicit `super();` call in the subclass constructor. In such cases, the programmer **must** explicitly write `super(args)` as the first statement of the subclass constructor. Failure to do so results in a **compile-time error**. **Incorrect Code Example (2 marks):** ```java class Account { String holderName; int accountNumber; Account(String holderName, int accountNumber) { // only parameterized this.holderName = holderName; this.accountNumber = accountNumber; } } class SavingsAccount extends Account { double interestRate; // ERROR: No super() call, and Account has no default constructor SavingsAccount(double interestRate) { this.interestRate = interestRate; } } ``` **Compiler Error:** ``` error: constructor Account in class Account cannot be applied to given types; required: String, int found: no arguments ``` **Corrected Code Example (2 marks):** ```java class SavingsAccount extends Account { double interestRate; SavingsAccount(String name, int accNo, double interestRate) { super(name, accNo); // MANDATORY explicit call this.interestRate = interestRate; } } ``` **Conclusion (1 mark):** The compiler enforces this rule to ensure that parent class fields are always properly initialized. Writing `super(args)` is the programmer's responsibility whenever the default `super();` cannot be automatically resolved. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Option 2 (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Explain the role of `super` in resolving **variable shadowing** and **method overriding** scenarios. Provide separate code examples for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the rules and restrictions governing the use of the `super` keyword in Java. Why can't `super` be used inside a `static` method or block? **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### Part (a) тАФ `super` in Variable Shadowing and Method Overriding (7 Marks) **Variable Shadowing Scenario (3.5 marks):** Variable shadowing occurs when a subclass declares a field with the same name as a field in the parent class. The subclass field **hides** the parent's field in the subclass's scope. `super.fieldName` is used to access the hidden parent field. ```java class Shape { String color = "Red"; } class Circle extends Shape { String color = "Blue"; // shadows parent void showColors() { System.out.println("Circle color (this): " + color); // Blue System.out.println("Shape color (super): " + super.color); // Red } } public class ShadowDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { new Circle().showColors(); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Circle color (this): Blue Shape color (super): Red ``` **Method Overriding Scenario (3.5 marks):** Method overriding occurs when a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its parent class. To invoke the parent's overridden method from within the subclass, `super.methodName()` is used. ```java class Printer { void print() { System.out.println("Printer printing document"); } } class LaserPrinter extends Printer { @Override void print() { super.print(); // invoke parent's version System.out.println("LaserPrinter using toner"); } } public class OverrideDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { new LaserPrinter().print(); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Printer printing document LaserPrinter using toner ``` --- #### Part (b) тАФ Rules and Restrictions of `super` (7 Marks) **Key Rules (5 marks):** 1. **Position Rule:** `super()` (or `super(args)`) must be the **first executable statement** in a subclass constructor. Placing it anywhere else causes a compile-time error. 2. **Mutual Exclusivity:** A constructor's first statement can be either `this()` OR `super()`, but **never both**. This is a hard syntactic constraint. 3. **No Static Context:** `super` cannot be used inside a `static` method, `static` block, or `static` variable initializer. This is because `super` requires an instance context to refer to a parent object. 4. **Automatic Insertion:** If a subclass constructor does not explicitly call `super()` or `this()`, the compiler automatically inserts a call to the parent's **no-argument** constructor. 5. **No Super-super:** You cannot write `super.super.method()` in Java. Each `super` refers to only the **immediate** parent class. 6. **Constructor Requirement:** If the parent class has only a parameterized constructor, the subclass **must** explicitly invoke it using `super(args)`. **Why `super` Cannot Be Used in Static Context (2 marks):** The `super` keyword is a **reference variable** that points to the parent portion of a specific object instance. However, `static` members (methods, blocks, variables) belong to the **class itself**, not to any individual object. They are loaded into memory **before** any object is created. Since no object exists when a static context is active, the `super` reference has no object to point to. Attempting to use `super` in a static context results in the compile-time error: ``` error: non-static variable super cannot be referenced from a static context ``` --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls тАФ Read Carefully:** > > 1. **Forgetting to use `super()` when parent lacks default constructor** тАФ Up to **3 marks lost** in Part B questions. Always check the parent class for a no-arg constructor. > > 2. **Writing `super()` as the second statement** тАФ Causes compile error; up to **2 marks lost**. It must be the **first statement** of the constructor. > > 3. **Confusing `super()` with `this()`** тАФ These are mutually exclusive as first statements. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > > 4. **Using `super` in `main()` or `static` method** тАФ Compile error; do not write such code in answers unless explaining the restriction. > > 5. **Not showing output in Part B (a)** тАФ Always include a complete, runnable program with **output** clearly displayed. Examiners allocate 1-2 marks specifically for output. > > 6. **Omitting the word "immediate"** when describing super тАФ The keyword refers to the **immediate** parent, not arbitrary ancestors. Lose 0.5 mark for this imprecision. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ The Keyword `super`** - тЬЕ `super` is a **reference variable** to the **immediate parent class object**. - тЬЕ It is **implicitly created by JVM** for every subclass object. - тЬЕ **Three uses:** `super()` (constructor), `super.field` (variable), `super.method()` (method). - тЬЕ `super()` must be the **first statement** in a subclass constructor. - тЬЕ `super()` and `this()` are **mutually exclusive** as first statements. - тЬЕ `super` **cannot be used in static context** (methods, blocks, variables). - тЬЕ Compiler **auto-inserts** `super();` if no explicit `this()` or `super()` is written. - тЬЕ If parent has **no default constructor**, explicit `super(args)` is **mandatory**. - тЬЕ Constructor chaining **flows from topmost ancestor (Object) down to the instantiated subclass**. - тЬЕ `super.field` resolves **variable shadowing** (hidden parent fields). - тЬЕ `super.method()` invokes **overridden parent method** (for behavioral extension). - тЬЕ `super.super.x` is **not allowed** тАФ `super` always refers to the **immediate** parent only. - тЬЕ All `super` references are **statically bound** at compile time. - тЬЕ `super` is closely related to: **inheritance, method overriding, dynamic dispatch, abstract classes** тАФ upcoming Module 2 topics.

topics.title: protected Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** protected Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Java programming language, an access modifier controls the visibility and accessibility of classes, methods, and fields. The **`protected`** access modifier is the **second most permissive** level of access in Java (after `public`). A member declared as `protected` in a class is accessible in **two distinct scenarios**:

1. **Within the same package** тАФ analogous to default (package-private) access.
2. **From subclasses** (child classes), even when those subclasses reside in a **different package** тАФ provided the access occurs **through inheritance** and not via an external reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition:** A `protected` member is accessible to classes in the **same package** AND to **subclasses (in any package)** through the mechanism of inheritance. It represents a deliberate compromise between strict encapsulation (`private`) and unrestricted visibility (`public`).

### Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "Family Heirloom" Model

Imagine a wealthy grandfather who owns a vintage car (the `protected` member).

| Person | Relationship | Access? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grandfather's wife (same house) | Same package | тЬЕ Yes | Lives under the same roof (package) |
| Grandfather's son (any house) | Subclass | тЬЕ Yes | Blood relation тАФ inheritance |
| Grandfather's neighbor (different house, not family) | Other class, other package | тЭМ No | No package and no blood relation |
| A stranger (different house, not family) | Other class, other package | тЭМ No | No relation at all |

The grandfather's son, no matter where he lives (which package he resides in), can **always** claim the vintage car because of inheritance тАФ but only **as an heir**, not by breaking into grandfather's garage and driving it away directly.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** `protected` access is the cornerstone of designing extensible frameworks and reusable class hierarchies (e.g., the `HttpServlet` class in Java EE overrides `protected` `doGet()`/`doPost()` methods).

### Placement in Java's Access Hierarchy

Java defines **four** access levels, ordered from most restrictive to most permissive:

$$\text{private} \;<\; \text{default (package-private)} \;<\; \text{protected} \;<\; \text{public}$$

The `protected` keyword is **declared without parentheses** and applies to classes (inner classes only), instance variables, methods, and constructors. Top-level (outer) classes in Java **cannot** be declared `protected`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** In a typical Java project, **`protected`** members are used approximately **15тАУ25%** of the time, primarily in **library/framework design** where extension via subclassing is expected. **`private`** is the dominant choice (~60тАУ70%) for true encapsulation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic тАФ How `protected` Behaves

The behavior of `protected` can be decomposed into **three distinct access pathways**:

**Pathway 1 тАФ Same-File / Same-Class Access**
Any member of the class itself (including its own methods, regardless of return type) can access its own `protected` members freely.

**Pathway 2 тАФ Same-Package Access (Non-Subclass)**
Any class тАФ even an unrelated class тАФ that resides in the **same package** can access the `protected` member. This is identical to default (package-private) access.

**Pathway 3 тАФ Cross-Package Subclass Access (The Tricky Part)**
A subclass in a **different package** can access the `protected` member, but **only** through the **inherited context** тАФ i.e., via `this` or `super`, never through a reference of the **superclass type**.

### The Three Golden Rules of `protected`

1. **Rule of Inheritance:** A subclass inherits all `protected` members of its superclass, regardless of the package in which the subclass is declared.
2. **Rule of Reference Type:** A subclass in another package **cannot** access a `protected` member by creating an object of the superclass type and dereferencing the field/method directly.
3. **Rule of Package Friendship:** Within the same package, `protected` and default (no modifier) behave **identically**.

### KTU Formula Sheet тАФ Access Modifier Comparison

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Same Pkg) | Subclass (Diff Pkg) | Other Package (Non-Subclass) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | тЬЕ Yes | тЭМ No | тЭМ No | тЭМ No | тЭМ No |
| `default` | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЭМ No | тЭМ No |
| `protected` | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes (via inheritance only) | тЭМ No |
| `public` | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In LaTeX, when expressing "absolute value" or "such that" inside a table, never use the literal pipe character $\vert$. Use $\vert x \vert$ notation properly so the markdown table structure remains intact.

### Why Use `protected`? тАФ Engineering Utility

- **Template Method Pattern:** The superclass defines the skeleton of an algorithm and marks key steps as `protected` so that subclasses can override them.
- **Framework Extension Points:** Java's `AbstractList`, `AbstractMap`, and `HttpServlet` expose `protected` hook methods precisely so that user code can customize behavior.
- **Controlled Inheritance:** Allows library designers to expose internals to subclass authors without making them part of the public API (which would be a maintenance burden).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In Android development, the `Activity` class declares `protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState)` тАФ this is the most overridden method in the entire Android ecosystem, demonstrating the power of `protected` access.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Code Walkthroughs

### Derivation 1 тАФ Proving Same-Package `protected` Access

**Setup:** We place two classes in the same package and demonstrate that `protected` works identically to default access in this context.

**Step 1:** Create the superclass in package `edu.ktu.oop`.

```java
package edu.ktu.oop;

public class Employee {
    protected double salary = 50000.0;

    protected void displaySalary() {
        System.out.println("Employee Salary: " + salary);
    }
}
```

**Step 2:** Create a non-related class in the same package.

```java
package edu.ktu.oop;

public class PayrollOffice {
    public void processPayroll() {
        Employee emp = new Employee();
        emp.salary = 55000.0;          // тЬЕ LEGAL тАФ same package
        emp.displaySalary();            // тЬЕ LEGAL тАФ same package
    }
}
```

**Step 3:** Logical conclusion.

$$\text{Access}_{\text{same-pkg}} = \text{Access}_{\text{default}} = \text{Always Permitted}$$

The `PayrollOffice` class has **no inheritance relationship** with `Employee`, yet it accesses `protected` members freely because both reside in package `edu.ktu.oop`.

---

### Derivation 2 тАФ Proving Cross-Package Subclass Access via Inheritance

**Step 1:** Define the superclass in package `edu.ktu.base`.

```java
package edu.ktu.base;

public class Vehicle {
    protected int maxSpeed = 180;
    protected void accelerate() {
        System.out.println("Accelerating to " + maxSpeed + " km/h");
    }
}
```

**Step 2:** Define a subclass in a *different* package `edu.ktu.derived`.

```java
package edu.ktu.derived;
import edu.ktu.base.Vehicle;

public class Car extends Vehicle {
    public void drive() {
        maxSpeed = 220;        // тЬЕ LEGAL тАФ inherited field
        accelerate();          // тЬЕ LEGAL тАФ inherited method
        System.out.println("Car cruising at " + maxSpeed);
    }
}
```

**Step 3:** Compilation outcome analysis.

$$\text{Access}_{\text{cross-pkg-subclass}} = \begin{cases} \text{Permitted} & \text{if accessed via } \texttt{this} \text{ or } \texttt{super} \\ \text{Denied} & \text{if accessed via superclass reference} \end{cases}$$

---

### Derivation 3 тАФ The KTU Classic Trap: Forbidden Reference Access

This is the **single most-asked** `protected` question in KTU university examinations.

**Step 1:** Using the previous `Vehicle` class, attempt illegal access.

```java
package edu.ktu.derived;
import edu.ktu.base.Vehicle;

public class TestVehicle {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Car c = new Car();
        c.maxSpeed = 300;        // тЬЕ LEGAL (inherited via 'c')
        c.accelerate();          // тЬЕ LEGAL (inherited via 'c')

        Vehicle v = new Vehicle();
        v.maxSpeed = 350;        // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR!
        //  error: maxSpeed has protected access in Vehicle
    }
}
```

**Step 2:** Why does this fail?

$$\text{Access}_{\text{denied}} \iff (\text{Package}_{\text{ref}} \neq \text{Package}_{\text{member}}) \;\wedge\; (\text{Access Path} \neq \text{Inheritance})$$

In the line `v.maxSpeed = 350;`, the **reference variable `v`** is of type `Vehicle`, and the access is performed from a class (`TestVehicle`) that is **not a subclass** of `Vehicle`. Java's compiler rejects this because it would break encapsulation тАФ otherwise, **any class** could be granted access simply by being placed in any package and importing the superclass.

**Step 3:** Fix using inheritance-friendly access.

```java
package edu.ktu.derived;
import edu.ktu.base.Vehicle;

public class SportsCar extends Vehicle {
    void tune(Vehicle otherVehicle) {
        otherVehicle.maxSpeed = 400;   // тЭМ STILL ILLEGAL!
        // Even within a subclass, you cannot access protected
        // members via an external reference of the superclass type.
    }

    void tuneSelf() {
        this.maxSpeed = 400;           // тЬЕ LEGAL via 'this'
        super.maxSpeed = 400;          // тЬЕ LEGAL via 'super'
    }
}
```

---

### Complete Analytical Matrix тАФ `protected` Access Outcomes

Let $C$ denote the accessing class, $M$ denote the member owner, and $\text{pkg}(X)$ denote the package of $X$.

$$
\text{Access}(C, M) = \begin{cases}
\text{Permitted} & \text{if } C = M \\[4pt]
\text{Permitted} & \text{if } \text{pkg}(C) = \text{pkg}(M) \\[4pt]
\text{Permitted} & \text{if } C \text{ extends } M \;\wedge\; \text{accessed via inheritance} \\[4pt]
\text{Denied} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This matrix is the **definitive reference** for solving any KTU `protected` access question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 тАФ Java Access Modifier Hierarchy (Visual Pyramid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Public Access Modifier] --> A1[Accessible Everywhere]
    B[Protected Access Modifier] --> B1[Same Package PLUS Subclasses in Other Packages]
    C[Default Package Private Access] --> C1[Same Package Only]
    D[Private Access Modifier] --> D1[Same Class Only]

    A1 --> E[Most Permissive]
    B1 --> F[Second Most Permissive]
    C1 --> G[Package Restricted]
    D1 --> H[Most Restrictive]

    style A fill:#4CAF50,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2196F3,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#FF9800,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#F44336,color:#ffffff
```

### Diagram 2 тАФ Block-Level Functional Architecture of `protected` Access

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PKG1[Package edu ktu base]
        SUP[Superclass Vehicle]
        F1[Protected Field maxSpeed]
        M1[Protected Method accelerate]
        SUP --> F1
        SUP --> M1
    end

    subgraph PKG2[Package edu ktu derived]
        SUB[Subclass Car extends Vehicle]
        T1[Access via this maxSpeed]
        T2[Access via super accelerate]
        T3[ILLEGAL Access via Vehicle ref]
        SUB --> T1
        SUB --> T2
        SUB -.X.- T3
    end

    F1 -.Inherited.-> SUB
    M1 -.Inherited.-> SUB

    style SUP fill:#FFEB3B,color:#000000
    style SUB fill:#03A9F4,color:#ffffff
    style T3 fill:#F44336,color:#ffffff
```

### Diagram 3 тАФ Decision Tree for `protected` Access Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is accessing code inside the same class?}
    Q2{Are accessing class and member owner in the same package?}
    Q3{Is accessing class a subclass of member owner?}
    Q4{Is access performed via inheritance this or super?}
    R1[PERMITTED Direct member access]
    R2[PERMITTED Package level friendship]
    R3[PERMITTED Inherited protected member]
    R4[DENIED Cross package non inherited access]

    Q1 -->|Yes| R1
    Q1 -->|No| Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2
    Q2 -->|No| Q3
    Q3 -->|No| R4
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4
    Q4 -->|Yes| R3
    Q4 -->|No| R4

    style R1 fill:#4CAF50,color:#ffffff
    style R2 fill:#4CAF50,color:#ffffff
    style R3 fill:#4CAF50,color:#ffffff
    style R4 fill:#F44336,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** For KTU board answers, drawing even a simplified version of Diagram 3 (a decision tree or a 4-column comparison table) can earn you **easy 2тАУ3 marks** on a Part A question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ЁЯУШ PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the `protected` access specifier in Java. How is it different from the `default` access specifier?**

**Model Answer:**

The `protected` access specifier in Java is the second most permissive access level. A `protected` member is accessible:

1. Within the same class (like any member).
2. Within all classes of the same package (same as default).
3. Within subclasses, even if the subclass is declared in a different package.

The **key difference** from the `default` access specifier is **point (3)**: default access does not extend across packages even through inheritance, while `protected` does.

| Feature | `default` | `protected` |
|---|---|---|
| Same package | тЬЕ | тЬЕ |
| Subclass in different package | тЭМ | тЬЕ (via inheritance) |
| Unrelated class in different package | тЭМ | тЭМ |

**(3 marks awarded for: definition + cross-package subclass rule + clear comparison)**

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Can a `protected` member of a class be accessed using a reference of the superclass type from a subclass in a different package? Justify with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

**No.** A subclass in a different package **cannot** access a `protected` member using a reference of the superclass type, even though it inherits the member. Access is only permitted through inheritance (`this` or `super`).

```java
package edu.ktu.base;
public class Super { protected int x = 10; }

package edu.ktu.derived;
import edu.ktu.base.Super;
public class Sub extends Super {
    void test() {
        Super s = new Super();
        s.x = 50;           // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR
        this.x = 50;        // тЬЕ LEGAL
    }
}
```

The reason: Java's compiler enforces that `protected` access through external references would otherwise allow **any** importing class to act like a subclass, breaking encapsulation.

---

### ЁЯУЧ PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the four access specifiers in Java with a comparative table. Mention at least two real-world scenarios where `protected` access is preferred over `private`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program demonstrating the use of the `protected` keyword across two different packages, with one subclass accessing inherited protected members and an attempt to access them via a superclass reference. Explain the output and compilation behavior. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for B-A (a):**

The four access specifiers in Java are `private`, `default` (no keyword), `protected`, and `public`.

| Specifier | Same Class | Same Package | Different Package (Subclass) | Different Package (Non-Subclass) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | тЬЕ | тЭМ | тЭМ | тЭМ |
| `default` | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЭМ | тЭМ |
| `protected` | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ (via inheritance) | тЭМ |
| `public` | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ |

**Real-world scenarios where `protected` is preferred over `private`:**

**Scenario 1 тАФ Framework Design:** The `HttpServlet` class in Java EE declares `protected void doGet(HttpServletRequest req, HttpServletResponse res)`. Framework users (subclasses) must override this method, but the API is hidden from general application code.

**Scenario 2 тАФ Template Method Pattern:** A sorting framework may expose a `protected int compare(T a, T b)` hook so that subclasses can customize the comparison logic while the overall sort algorithm remains fixed in the superclass.

**(Full 7 marks: 3 for table + 2 for scenarios + 2 for clear explanation.)**

---

##### **Model Solution for B-A (b):**

**File 1 тАФ `edu/ktu/base/BankAccount.java`**

```java
package edu.ktu.base;

public class BankAccount {
    protected double balance = 1000.0;

    protected void deduct(double amount) {
        if (amount <= balance) {
            balance -= amount;
            System.out.println("Deducted: " + amount);
        }
    }
}
```

**File 2 тАФ `edu/ktu/derived/SavingsAccount.java`**

```java
package edu.ktu.derived;
import edu.ktu.base.BankAccount;

public class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        // LEGAL: inherited protected access via 'this'
        if (amount <= this.balance) {
            this.balance -= amount;
            System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amount);
        }
    }

    public void tryExternalAccess(BankAccount other) {
        // ILLEGAL: access via superclass reference in different package
        // other.balance = 0;     // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR
        // other.deduct(100);     // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR
    }
}
```

**File 3 тАФ `edu/ktu/derived/TestBank.java`**

```java
package edu.ktu.derived;
import edu.ktu.base.BankAccount;

public class TestBank {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SavingsAccount sa = new SavingsAccount();
        sa.withdraw(200);     // LEGAL: inherited protected via 'sa'

        BankAccount ba = new BankAccount();
        // ba.balance = 500;   // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR тАФ not subclass, different package
        // ba.deduct(100);     // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR

        sa.balance = 800;     // LEGAL: 'sa' is a SavingsAccount (subclass)
        System.out.println("Final Balance: " + sa.balance);
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Withdrawn: 200
Final Balance: 600.0
```

**Explanation of Behavior:**
- The `withdraw()` method works because `balance` is **inherited** as a protected field and accessed via `this` inside the subclass `SavingsAccount`.
- The lines marked with тЭМ fail compilation because they violate **Rule of Reference Type** тАФ accessing protected members through superclass references from non-subclass contexts.
- Even within `SavingsAccount`, passing a `BankAccount` reference to `tryExternalAccess()` does not grant access, because that reference is **not** the current instance and not accessed through inheritance.

**(Full 7 marks: 3 for code correctness + 2 for explanation of compilation rules + 2 for output analysis.)**

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the access restrictions imposed by the `protected` access specifier in Java. Why does Java prohibit access to `protected` members through a superclass reference in a different package, even from a subclass? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java program with **two packages** demonstrating the **same-package** and **cross-package subclass** accessibility of `protected` members. Include a class that is **not** a subclass attempting to access the same member and show that it fails. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for B-B (a):**

The `protected` access specifier allows:

1. Access from the same class.
2. Access from any class within the **same package**.
3. Access from **subclasses** in any package тАФ but **only** when access is performed through inheritance.

**Restrictions:**
- **No cross-package non-subclass access:** A class that is neither in the same package nor a subclass cannot access the member.
- **No superclass-reference access:** Even a subclass in a different package cannot access the `protected` member by creating a separate superclass object and dereferencing it.

**Why Java prohibits superclass-reference access:**

The reason is **encapsulation integrity**. If Java allowed `superRef.protectedMember = value` from any subclass in any package, then effectively **any class** could become a "subclass" simply by declaring `extends SuperClass`, even if it has no genuine semantic relationship with the superclass. This would:

- Break the **Liskov Substitution Principle** of OOP design.
- Allow unrelated classes to manipulate internal state of objects they don't own.
- Defeat the purpose of access control, since the restriction could be trivially bypassed.

Hence Java's compiler enforces that `protected` cross-package access is mediated strictly through the inheritance chain (`this` or `super`).

**(Full 7 marks: 2 for restrictions + 3 for the "why" explanation + 2 for design principle reference.)**

---

##### **Model Solution for B-B (b):**

**File 1 тАФ `edu/ktu/lib/Person.java`**

```java
package edu.ktu.lib;

public class Person {
    protected String name = "Kerala Student";

    protected void introduce() {
        System.out.println("Hello, I am " + name);
    }
}
```

**File 2 тАФ `edu/ktu/lib/PersonFriend.java` (Same-package non-subclass)**

```java
package edu.ktu.lib;

public class PersonFriend {
    public void interact() {
        Person p = new Person();
        p.name = "KTU Scholar";      // тЬЕ LEGAL тАФ same package
        p.introduce();                // тЬЕ LEGAL тАФ same package
    }
}
```

**File 3 тАФ `edu/ktu/school/Student.java` (Cross-package subclass)**

```java
package edu.ktu.school;
import edu.ktu.lib.Person;

public class Student extends Person {
    public void showIdentity() {
        this.name = "B.Tech Student";   // тЬЕ LEGAL via inheritance
        introduce();                    // тЬЕ LEGAL via inheritance
    }

    public void tryOther(Person other) {
        // other.name = "Hacker";       // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR
        // other.introduce();           // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR
    }
}
```

**File 4 тАФ `edu/ktu/school/RandomUser.java` (Cross-package non-subclass)**

```java
package edu.ktu.school;
import edu.ktu.lib.Person;

public class RandomUser {
    public void test() {
        Person p = new Person();
        // p.name = "Test";           // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR тАФ not subclass, different package
        // p.introduce();             // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR
    }
}
```

**Compilation Results Summary:**

| Class | Package | Relationship with `Person` | Access `p.name` | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Person` | `edu.ktu.lib` | Same class | тЬЕ | LEGAL |
| `PersonFriend` | `edu.ktu.lib` | Same package, no inheritance | тЬЕ | LEGAL |
| `Student` | `edu.ktu.school` | Subclass, different package | тЬЕ via `this`/`super` only | LEGAL (selectively) |
| `RandomUser` | `edu.ktu.school` | Not a subclass, different package | тЭМ | ILLEGAL |

**Expected Compilation:**
- `Person.java` тАФ тЬЕ compiles
- `PersonFriend.java` тАФ тЬЕ compiles
- `Student.java` тАФ тЬЕ compiles (the commented-out lines would cause errors)
- `RandomUser.java` тАФ тЭМ compile error: `name has protected access in Person`

**(Full 7 marks: 4 for correct program + 2 for compilation analysis + 1 for summary table.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout тАФ Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing `default` with `protected`** тАФ They behave the same in same-package scenarios but differ across packages. *(тИТ2 marks typical loss)*
> 2. **Forgetting the superclass-reference rule** тАФ Writing code like `Super obj = new Super(); obj.protectedField = x;` inside a subclass and claiming it's legal. *(тИТ3 marks, full compilation error overlooked)*
> 3. **Mixing up `protected` with `private`** тАФ `private` is never inherited; `protected` always is. *(тИТ2 marks)*
> 4. **Not using `this` or `super` explicitly** in cross-package code тАФ KTU expects you to demonstrate the inheritance access path. *(тИТ1 mark for vagueness)*
> 5. **Forgetting `import` statements** when showing two-package examples тАФ Even if logic is correct, the code must be **compilable**. *(тИТ1 mark per missing import)*

---

### ЁЯОп Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- тЬЕ **`protected`** is the **second-most-permissive** access modifier in Java (after `public`).
- тЬЕ **Same package** access тАФ `protected` behaves **identically** to `default` (package-private).
- тЬЕ **Cross-package** access тАФ only via **inheritance** (`this` or `super`), never via a superclass-type reference.
- тЬЕ **Always inherited** тАФ every `protected` member of a superclass becomes part of the subclass's accessible member set.
- тЬЕ **External references are forbidden** тАФ even a subclass cannot access `protected` members on a separate superclass object received as a parameter or returned from a method.
- тЬЕ **Use case:** Template Method Pattern, framework extension hooks, `HttpServlet.doGet()` style override points.
- тЬЕ **Cannot be applied** to top-level (outer) classes; only to nested/inner classes, methods, fields, and constructors.
- тЬЕ **KTU Hot Question:** "Can a subclass in a different package access `protected` members via a superclass reference?" тАФ Answer: **NO**.
- тЬЕ **Encapsulation principle:** Java's `protected` enforcement prevents arbitrary subclass proliferation from bypassing access control.
- тЬЕ **Comparison triplet to memorize:** `private < default < protected < public` (in order of increasing visibility).
- тЬЕ **Remember to use `this.fieldName` explicitly** in subclass code to demonstrate the inheritance access path during KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calling Order of Constructors
Method Overriding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method Overriding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Method Overriding** is a core feature of *Run-Time Polymorphism* (also called *Dynamic Method Dispatch*) in Java, wherein a **subclass provides a specific implementation of a method that is already declared in its parent (superclass)**. The overridden method in the subclass must have the **exact same signature** (name, return type, and parameter list) as the method in the superclass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
