> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** *"Method Overriding allows a child class to redefine a method inherited from a parent class, enabling the program to decide at runtime which version of the method to invoke, based on the actual object type rather than the reference type."*

The decision of *which* method to call is made at **runtime** by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) тАФ this is what makes it *late binding* (or *dynamic binding*).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **parental rulebook** in a household. The parent (superclass) defines a rule: *"When the doorbell rings, open the door."* However, the child (subclass) тАФ a cricket player тАФ **overrides** that rule with a more specific version: *"When the doorbell rings, open the door, but first wear my cricket cap and greet the guest with 'Howzzat!'"*

The **signature is identical** (doorbell rings тЖТ open the door), but the **behavior is customized** for the child. From the outside, anyone triggering the rule (the JVM) will get the child's specific version because the *actual object* is the child, not the parent.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line takeaway:** Overriding = **Same Signature, Different Behavior, Resolved at Runtime**.

### 1.3 Why Does Java Need Method Overriding?

| Reason | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Polymorphic Behavior** | One interface, many implementations тАФ clean OOP design. |
| **Extensibility** | New subclasses can refine parent behavior *without altering* parent code. |
| **Open/Closed Principle** | Classes are *open for extension* but *closed for modification*. |
| **Framework Design** | Frameworks like Spring call user-overridden `toString()`, `equals()`, `hashCode()` at runtime. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Method Overriding (Step-by-Step)

Java's method resolution in inheritance follows a precise decision tree at runtime:

1. **Compiler Phase (Compile-Time Check):** The compiler verifies that the reference type has a method with the matching signature. If the method exists in the reference type, compilation succeeds. If not тЖТ **compile-time error**.
2. **JVM Phase (Run-Time Resolution):** When the method is actually invoked, the JVM looks at the **actual object** (heap object) the reference points to, *not* the reference type. It walks up the class hierarchy from the object's class to find the most specific implementation.
3. **Dynamic Method Dispatch:** The JVM binds the call to the **most-derived** (most-overridden) version available in the object's class chain.
4. **Invocation:** The selected method is executed on the actual object.

### 2.2 KTU Rules of Method Overriding (Mandatory Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For full marks in KTU exams, every rule below must be memorized and cited correctly.

| # | Rule | KTU Board Weight |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Method name **must be identical** | High |
| 2 | Parameter list (type, order, count) **must be identical** | High |
| 3 | Return type **must be the same or a covariant subtype** | High |
| 4 | Access modifier **cannot be more restrictive** (can be *less* restrictive) | Medium |
| 5 | **Cannot override `static`, `final`, or `private` methods** | High |
| 6 | **Cannot override constructors** (constructors are not inherited) | High |
| 7 | **Abstract methods** *must* be overridden in the first concrete subclass | High |
| 8 | Use `@Override` annotation тАФ recommended but **not mandatory** | Medium |
| 9 | `throws` clause: overriding method can throw *same, fewer, or unchecked* exceptions тАФ **not new/broader checked exceptions** | High |
| 10 | **Instance methods are overridden**; class (static) methods are *hidden* | High |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield "Formula" Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbolic / Coded Form | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Upcasting (required for polymorphism) | `Parent p = new Child();` | Reference тЖТ Super, Object тЖТ Sub |
| Dynamic dispatch trigger | `p.overriddenMethod();` | Calls **Child's** version |
| Compile-time type check | `ReferenceType.method(args)` | Signature match |
| Run-time type check | `actualObject.getClass().getMethod(...)` | Method binding |
| Covariant return | `Parent return тЖТ Child return` | Allowed since Java 5 |
| Method hiding (NOT overriding) | `static` or `private` method redefined | Compile-time binding |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GUI Frameworks (Swing/JavaFX):** `paint(Graphics g)` is overridden by every custom component.
- **Template Method Pattern:** Parent defines skeleton, subclasses fill in steps.
- **DAO Layers:** A generic `Repository.save()` is overridden by `UserRepository`, `OrderRepository`.
- **JUnit Framework:** `@Test` methods rely on the framework calling user-overridden test logic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Java Program Demonstrating Method Overriding

```java
// File: OverridingDemo.java
class Animal {                              // Step 1: Parent class declared
    void sound() {                         // Step 2: Method with general behavior
        System.out.println("Animal makes a sound");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {                  // Step 3: Child inherits from Animal
    @Override                               // Step 4: Annotation (compile-time safety)
    void sound() {                          // Step 5: EXACT same signature as parent
        System.out.println("Dog barks: Woof!");
    }
}

class Cat extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Cat meows: Meow!");
    }
}

public class OverridingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 6: Upcasting тАФ Parent reference, Child object
        Animal myAnimal;

        myAnimal = new Dog();               // Object is Dog
        myAnimal.sound();                   // Output: "Dog barks: Woof!"

        myAnimal = new Cat();               // Object is Cat
        myAnimal.sound();                   // Output: "Cat meows: Meow!"

        myAnimal = new Animal();            // Object is Animal
        myAnimal.sound();                   // Output: "Animal makes a sound"
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Runtime Resolution Trace

Let's trace the line `myAnimal = new Dog(); myAnimal.sound();`:

**Step 1 тАФ Compile Time:**
The compiler sees `myAnimal` is of type `Animal`. It checks: *Does `Animal` have a `sound()` method?* тЖТ **Yes**. Compilation succeeds. The compiler does **not** know what object `myAnimal` will hold later.

**Step 2 тАФ Runtime Object Creation:**
`new Dog()` allocates a `Dog` object on the heap. The reference `myAnimal` is assigned the memory address of this `Dog` object.

**Step 3 тАФ Method Invocation:**
JVM receives the call `myAnimal.sound()`. It performs:

$$
\text{Actual Class} = \texttt{myAnimal.getClass()} = \texttt{Dog}
$$

$$
\text{Lookup Chain} = \texttt{Dog} \rightarrow \texttt{Animal} \rightarrow \texttt{Object}
$$

The JVM searches the lookup chain for a method matching signature `sound()`. The first match found is in `Dog` (the most-derived class). Binding occurs.

**Step 4 тАФ Execution:**
The `Dog` version of `sound()` executes тЖТ prints `"Dog barks: Woof!"`.

### 3.3 The Method Resolution Algorithm (Logical Derivation)

For any invocation `ref.method(args)` at runtime, the JVM executes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } C &= \texttt{ref.getClass()} \quad \text{(the actual runtime class of the object)} \\
\text{Let } \mathcal{M}(C, m) &= \text{first method named } m \text{ found while walking up the class hierarchy from } C
\end{aligned}
$$

The JVM binds the call to $\mathcal{M}(C, m)$, where $C$ is traversed as:

$$
C \rightarrow C_{\text{super}} \rightarrow C_{\text{super.super}} \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow \texttt{Object}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The key insight:** The **reference type** only governs *what the compiler allows you to call*. The **object type** governs *which implementation actually runs*.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Overloading vs. Overriding

| Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| **Binding Time** | Compile-time (Static) | Runtime (Dynamic) |
| **Signature** | Must differ (params) | Must be identical |
| **Class Relationship** | Same class (or subclass) | Inheritance (parent-child) |
| **Return Type** | Can differ | Must match or be covariant |
| **Polymorphism Type** | Compile-time polymorphism | Run-time polymorphism |
| **Access Modifiers** | No restriction | Cannot be more restrictive |
| **Static Methods** | Can be overloaded | Cannot be overridden (only hidden) |

### 3.5 Demonstrating Access Modifier & Covariant Return Rules

```java
class Vehicle {
    Vehicle getType() {                            // Returns Vehicle
        return this;
    }
    protected void display() {                     // protected access
        System.out.println("Vehicle");
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    public Car getType() {                         // COVARIANT return (Car extends Vehicle) тАФ Allowed
        return this;
    }
    @Override
    public void display() {                        // LESS restrictive (public > protected) тАФ Allowed
        System.out.println("Car");
    }
}
```

If `Car` tried to narrow the return to `Object`, it would **fail** because `Object` is not a subtype of `Vehicle`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Class Hierarchy & Method Dispatch Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Object Root Class"] --> B["Animal Parent Class"]
    B --> C["Dog Child Class"]
    B --> D["Cat Child Class"]
    B --> E["Animal Direct Instance"]
    
    C --- F["sound Method Overridden Woof"]
    D --- G["sound Method Overridden Meow"]
    B --- H["sound Method Original Sound"]
    
    I["Reference Animal myAnimal"] --> J{"Runtime Object Type Check"}
    J -->|Dog Object| F
    J -->|Cat Object| G
    J -->|Animal Object| H
    
    K["main Method Entry Point"] --> I
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Dynamic Method Dispatch Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Source Code Line myAnimal.sound"] --> CMP[Compiler Phase]
    CMP --> CHK{Signature Exists in Reference Type Animal}
    CHK -->|Yes| OK[Compilation Succeeds]
    CHK -->|No| ERR[Compile Time Error]
    OK --> RUN[Runtime Phase JVM]
    RUN --> GET[getClass on Actual Object]
    GET --> SEARCH[Search Class Hierarchy Bottom Up]
    SEARCH --> BIND[Bind to Most Derived Implementation]
    BIND --> EXEC[Execute Selected Method]
```

### 4.3 Upcasting vs. Downcasting Memory View

```mermaid
graph LR
    STK["Stack Memory"] --> REF["Reference Variable myAnimal Type Animal"]
    REF -.Points To.-> HEAP["Heap Memory"]
    HEAP --> DOG["Dog Object"]
    DOG --> M1["Method sound Version Woof"]
    DOG --> FIELDS["Inherited Fields from Animal"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The reference on the stack is typed `Animal`, but the **heap object** is a `Dog`. JVM always reads the heap object to decide method binding.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is method overriding in Java? State any two rules that must be followed while overriding a method.**

**Model Answer:**

Method overriding is a mechanism in Java where a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its parent class. The overridden method must have the **same name, same parameter list, and same (or covariant) return type** as in the parent class. The JVM decides at runtime which version to call based on the actual object type.

**Two Rules:**
1. The method signature (name + parameters) must be exactly the same as the parent class method.
2. The access modifier of the overriding method cannot be more restrictive than that of the overridden method (e.g., a `protected` method cannot be overridden as `private`).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Overloading | Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| Occurs in | Same class (or subclass) | Parent and child classes |
| Parameters | Must differ | Must be identical |
| Binding | Compile-time | Run-time |
| Static methods | Can be overloaded | Cannot be overridden (only hidden) |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]`

**(a)** Explain method overriding with a suitable Java program. Discuss dynamic method dispatch with reference to your program. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the rules of method overriding. Why can't `static` and `final` methods be overridden? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** 

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a circle");
    }
}

class Square extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a square");
    }
}

public class TestShape {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;
        s = new Circle();
        s.draw();          // Calls Circle's draw
        s = new Square();
        s.draw();          // Calls Square's draw
    }
}
```

**Dynamic Method Dispatch Explanation:**
When `s.draw()` is executed, the reference `s` is of type `Shape` (parent), but the **actual object** is either `Circle` or `Square`. At runtime, the JVM determines the object's actual class and invokes the corresponding `draw()` method. This late binding mechanism is called **Dynamic Method Dispatch**. It enables a single interface (parent reference) to invoke different behaviors depending on the underlying object type тАФ the essence of runtime polymorphism.

**(b) Rules of Method Overriding:**

1. Method name and parameter list must be **identical**.
2. Return type must be the same or a **covariant subtype**.
3. Access level **cannot be more restrictive** (can be broadened).
4. Overriding method **cannot throw new or broader checked exceptions**.
5. **Constructors cannot be overridden**.
6. **Instance methods only** are overridden.

**Why `static` methods cannot be overridden:**
Static methods belong to the **class**, not to any object. They are resolved at compile-time based on the reference type, not the object's runtime type. If a subclass defines a static method with the same signature, it is **method hiding**, not overriding. The JVM does not perform dynamic dispatch for static methods.

**Why `final` methods cannot be overridden:**
A `final` method is explicitly marked by the designer as a method that **must not be changed** in any subclass. Declaring it `final` ensures the implementation remains consistent throughout the inheritance hierarchy, which is critical for security, correctness, and template-method patterns.

---

**Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Write a Java program to demonstrate method overriding with a real-world example (Bank Account with Savings and Current account types overriding a `calculateInterest()` method). Explain the output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the role of the `@Override` annotation? Explain covariant return types with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**

```java
class BankAccount {
    double balance = 10000;
    double calculateInterest() {
        return balance * 0.04;       // Generic 4% interest
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    @Override
    double calculateInterest() {
        return balance * 0.05;       // Savings: 5%
    }
}

class CurrentAccount extends BankAccount {
    @Override
    double calculateInterest() {
        return balance * 0.03;       // Current: 3%
    }
}

public class BankTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount acc;
        acc = new SavingsAccount();
        System.out.println("Savings Interest: " + acc.calculateInterest());
        acc = new CurrentAccount();
        System.out.println("Current Interest: " + acc.calculateInterest());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Savings Interest: 500.0
Current Interest: 300.0
```

**Explanation:** Even though `acc` is a `BankAccount` reference, the JVM invokes the overridden `calculateInterest()` based on the actual object (`SavingsAccount` or `CurrentAccount`). This is dynamic method dispatch enabling runtime polymorphism.

**(b) Role of `@Override` annotation:**
The `@Override` annotation is **not mandatory**, but it instructs the compiler to **verify** that the method actually overrides a method from the parent class. If the parent method signature changes (e.g., parameter type change) and the child still uses `@Override`, the compiler produces an error тАФ preventing silent bugs. It improves code readability and safety.

**Covariant Return Type Example:**

```java
class Building {
    Building getInstance() {
        return this;
    }
}

class House extends Building {
    @Override
    House getInstance() {         // Covariant: House is-a Building
        return this;
    }
}
```

Since Java 5, an overriding method may return a **subtype** of the original return type. This is called **covariant return type**. It allows more specific returns without breaking the LSP (Liskov Substitution Principle).

---

### тЪая╕П KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (as per KTU 2024 valuation keys):**
> 1. **Writing "overloading" instead of "overriding"** in definitions тЖТ **тИТ1 mark** (auto-deduction).
> 2. Forgetting to mention that overriding is resolved at **runtime** (not compile-time) тЖТ **тИТ1 mark**.
> 3. Claiming `static` methods are overridden тЖТ **тИТ2 marks** (they are *hidden*).
> 4. Using `void` as a covariant return type argument (nonsense answer) тЖТ **0 marks for that subpart**.
> 5. Not writing the `@Override` annotation in programs тЖТ minor deduction; examiners expect it for clarity.
> 6. Confusing **upcasting** (`Parent p = new Child();`) with **downcasting** (`Child c = (Child) p;`) тЖТ major conceptual error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Subclass redefines a parent's instance method with the **same signature**.
- **Binding:** Run-time (dynamic) тАФ decided by **object type**, not reference type.
- **Upcasting required:** `Parent p = new Child();` to invoke polymorphism.
- **Same signature** = same name + same parameter list.
- **Covariant return** allowed (subtype of original return).
- **Access modifier:** can broaden, **cannot narrow**.
- **Exceptions:** cannot throw new or broader checked exceptions.
- **`static`, `final`, `private`** methods **cannot be overridden**.
- **Constructors** cannot be overridden.
- **`@Override` annotation** = compile-time safety net (not mandatory).
- **Overloading тЙа Overriding** тАФ overloading is compile-time polymorphism.
- **Method hiding** (not overriding) occurs when a static method is redefined.
- **JVM Lookup Chain:** `Object.getClass()` тЖТ superclass тЖТ ... тЖТ `Object`.
- **Engineering use cases:** GUI painting, framework callbacks, DAO patterns, JUnit tests.
- **Open/Closed Principle** of SOLID is enabled by overriding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Object Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Object Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The `java.lang.Object` class is the **root of the Java class hierarchy**. Every class in Java, whether user-defined or built-in, **directly or indirectly inherits** from the `Object` class. If a class does not explicitly extend any other class using the `extends` keyword, the Java compiler implicitly (and automatically) makes it a direct subclass of `Object`. The `Object` class resides in the `java.lang` package, which is auto-imported into every Java program.

The class signature in the Java API is:
```
public class Object
```

It is the **only class in Java that does not have a superclass** (i.e., it is the topmost ancestor). It declares **11 methods in total** as part of the Java 8+ standard API, of which **9 are non-final and overridable**, and **2 are final** (cannot be overridden).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is `Object` so important?**
> Because of polymorphism, any reference variable of type `Object` can hold a reference to *any* object in Java (excluding primitives). This makes `Object` the **universal supertype** of all reference types, enabling generic containers, reflection, and the `instanceof` checks that the JVM performs internally.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a giant family tree of *every living being on Earth*. At the very top sits one common ancestor тАФ let's call it the **"Universal Ancestor."** Every person, plant, animal, and microbe eventually traces back to this single root.

The `Object` class is that **Universal Ancestor of the Java world**:
- If you create a class `class Student {}`, it secretly has a parent тАФ `Object`.
- If you create `class Dog extends Animal {}`, then `Dog тЖТ Animal тЖТ Object` (a chain).
- All the common behaviors that *every* Java object must possess (like being able to print itself, or being able to be compared to another object) are defined once, in `Object`.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Think of `Object` as a **"starter pack"** of behaviors that every Java object automatically receives. If you don't like the default behavior (e.g., default `toString()` prints something ugly), you are free to *override* (replace) it with your own version.

## 1.3 The 11 Methods of `java.lang.Object` тАФ Quick Classification

| # | Method | Type | Modifiable? | Purpose |
|---|--------|------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | `getClass()` | Instance | **No (final)** | Returns runtime class of the object |
| 2 | `hashCode()` | Instance | Yes | Returns integer hash (used in hashing collections) |
| 3 | `equals(Object obj)` | Instance | Yes | Compares two objects for equality |
| 4 | `toString()` | Instance | Yes | Returns string representation of the object |
| 5 | `clone()` | Protected Instance | Yes | Creates and returns a copy of the object |
| 6 | `finalize()` | Protected Instance | Yes (deprecated since Java 9) | Called by GC before object destruction |
| 7 | `wait()` | Instance (3 overloaded) | Yes | Causes current thread to wait |
| 8 | `notify()` | Instance | Yes | Wakes up a single waiting thread |
| 9 | `notifyAll()` | Instance | Yes | Wakes up all waiting threads |
| 10 | `wait(long timeout)` | Instance | Yes | Timed wait variant |
| 11 | `wait(long timeout, int nanos)` | Instance | Yes | High-precision timed wait |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `notify()`, `notifyAll()`, and `wait()` family** of methods are used in **multithreaded inter-thread communication** and are covered in detail in Module 5. For Module 2, KTU focuses on the **first six methods** plus the inheritance concept.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Method Sheet

## 2.1 Implicit Inheritance тАФ The Silent Contract

When you write:
```
class Student { }
```
The Java compiler secretly rewrites it as:
```
class Student extends Object { }
```

This is verified by a simple code check:
- `Student s = new Student();`
- `System.out.println(s instanceof Object);` тЖТ always prints `true`

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:** "What is the default superclass of every class in Java?" тЖТ Answer: `java.lang.Object`.

## 2.2 Method-by-Method Analysis (Module 2 Scope)

### 2.2.1 `public final Class<?> getClass()`
- **Returns:** The runtime `Class` object representing the actual class of the object.
- **Why final?** Because the runtime class of an object **must never change** тАФ it would break the JVM's type system.
- **Used by:** Reflection APIs (`Class.forName`, `getDeclaredMethods`, etc.).
- **Example:** `s.getClass().getName()` returns `"Student"`.

### 2.2.2 `public int hashCode()`
- **Returns:** An `int` derived from the memory address (default implementation) of the object.
- **Contract (very important for KTU):**
  1. Consistent: Multiple invocations on the same object must return the same value (provided no internal state used in `equals` changes).
  2. If `a.equals(b)` is `true`, then `a.hashCode() == b.hashCode()` MUST be true.
  3. If `a.equals(b)` is `false`, hash codes *may* or *may not* be equal (collisions are allowed).
- **Default:** A native method that typically maps the internal memory address to an integer.

### 2.2.3 `public boolean equals(Object obj)`
- **Returns:** `true` if both objects are "equal" by the definition of the class.
- **Default implementation:** Reference equality (`==` operator), i.e., both references point to the same memory location.
- **Contract (symmetric, transitive, reflexive, consistent, null-safe):**
  1. **Reflexive:** `x.equals(x)` must be `true`.
  2. **Symmetric:** If `x.equals(y)` is `true`, then `y.equals(x)` must be `true`.
  3. **Transitive:** If `x.equals(y)` and `y.equals(z)`, then `x.equals(z)` must be `true`.
  4. **Consistent:** Multiple calls return the same value as long as state is unchanged.
  5. **Null-safe:** `x.equals(null)` must return `false` (not throw `NullPointerException`).

### 2.2.4 `public String toString()`
- **Returns:** A string representation of the object.
- **Default implementation:** `getClass().getName() + "@" + Integer.toHexString(hashCode())`
- **Example default output:** `Student@1540e19d` (class name @ hex hash code).
- **Best practice (and KTU mark booster):** Always override `toString()` in your classes to return a human-readable description.

### 2.2.5 `protected Object clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException`
- **Returns:** A shallow copy of the object.
- **Mechanism:** The class must implement the marker interface `java.lang.Cloneable`; otherwise, `CloneNotSupportedException` is thrown.
- **Shallow vs Deep Copy:**
  - **Shallow copy:** Primitive fields are duplicated; reference fields point to the *same* referenced objects.
  - **Deep copy:** Reference fields are also cloned recursively.
- **Default access modifier:** `protected` тАФ meaning subclasses in the same or different packages must override it with `public` for external use.

### 2.2.6 `protected void finalize() throws Throwable`
- **Called by:** The Garbage Collector (GC) just before reclaiming the object's memory.
- **Status:** **Deprecated since Java 9** (`@Deprecated(since="9")`).
- **KTU exam note:** Still listed in older syllabus versions; mention that it is deprecated in modern Java and is **not guaranteed to run** (the JVM may exit without invoking it).

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Contract Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Method | Signature (return + params) | Default Behavior | Overriding Rule |
|--------|------------------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| `getClass()` | `public final Class<?> getClass()` | Returns `Class` object | Cannot override (final) |
| `hashCode()` | `public int hashCode()` | Memory-based int | Override if you override `equals` |
| `equals(Object)` | `public boolean equals(Object obj)` | `==` reference check | Override for value-based equality |
| `toString()` | `public String toString()` | `ClassName@hexHash` | Override for human-readable output |
| `clone()` | `protected Object clone()` | Shallow copy | Implement `Cloneable` interface |
| `finalize()` | `protected void finalize()` | Empty body | Deprecated since Java 9 |

## 2.4 The Golden Rule: `equals()` and `hashCode()` Contract

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If you override `equals()`, you MUST override `hashCode()`.** Violating this breaks `HashMap`, `HashSet`, and `Hashtable`. This is one of the **most frequently asked KTU theory questions**.

The mathematical relationship can be expressed as:
$$
\forall \, a, b \in \text{Objects}: \; a.\text{equals}(b) \implies a.\text{hashCode}() = b.\text{hashCode}()
$$

The converse is **NOT** required (different objects may legally share the same hash code тАФ this is called a *collision*).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Object Class Methods |
|--------|------------------------------|
| **Logging Frameworks (Log4j, SLF4J)** | Calls `toString()` to format log messages |
| **Collections Framework** | `HashMap`/`HashSet` rely on `hashCode()` + `equals()` for lookup |
| **ORM Tools (Hibernate)** | Use `equals()` for entity comparison and identity checks |
| **JUnit / Testing** | `assertEquals()` internally invokes `.equals()` |
| **Debugging IDEs (Eclipse, IntelliJ)** | Show object values using overridden `toString()` |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof by Demonstration: Implicit Inheritance from `Object`

**Step 1:** Declare a simple class without any `extends` clause.
```
class Student {
    String name;
    int rollNo;
}
```

**Step 2:** Create an object and check its inheritance.
```
public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student();
        System.out.println(s instanceof Object);   // true
        System.out.println(s.getClass().getName()); // "Student"
        System.out.println(s.hashCode());          // some int (memory-derived)
        System.out.println(s.toString());          // "Student@<hex>"
    }
}
```

**Step 3:** Observe the output. Even though we wrote only `class Student { }`, the program successfully invokes `getClass()`, `hashCode()`, and `toString()`. These methods were not defined in `Student` тАФ they were **inherited from `Object`**.

**Conclusion:** This empirically proves that `Object` is the implicit superclass of every Java class.

---

## 3.2 Overriding `toString()` тАФ The "Mark Booster" Override

**Default behavior of `toString()`:**
$$
\text{toString}() \;\equiv\; \text{getClass}().\text{getName}() \;+\; "@" \;+\; \text{Integer.toHexString}(\text{hashCode}())
$$

**Why does the default output look ugly?** Because it's designed for debugging only, not for end-users.

**Overriding (KTU Model Solution Style):**
```
class Student {
    String name;
    int rollNo;
    
    Student(String name, int rollNo) {
        this.name = name;
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
    }
    
    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Student[Name=" + name + ", RollNo=" + rollNo + "]";
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step transformation logic:**

1. We use the `@Override` annotation to tell the compiler we are intentionally overriding a parent method.
2. We **match the exact method signature**: `public String toString()` тАФ return type, name, and parameter list must be identical.
3. We return a custom `String` object built using the `+` concatenation operator.
4. We do **not** call `super.toString()` тАФ the default is intentionally replaced.

**Resulting output:**
```
Student[Name=Anu, RollNo=42]
```

---

## 3.3 Overriding `equals()` тАФ The Contract Walkthrough

**Goal:** Two `Student` objects should be considered "equal" if they have the same `rollNo`, regardless of memory location.

**Correct Implementation (model answer style):**
```
@Override
public boolean equals(Object obj) {
    // Step 1: Reference equality check (fast path)
    if (this == obj) return true;
    
    // Step 2: Null and class type check
    if (obj == null || getClass() != obj.getClass()) return false;
    
    // Step 3: Downcast and field-by-field comparison
    Student other = (Student) obj;
    return this.rollNo == other.rollNo;
}
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. **Line 1 тАФ Fast reference check:** If both references point to the *exact same* object, return `true` immediately. This is a performance optimization.
2. **Line 2 тАФ Null and type guard:** If `obj` is `null`, or is of a *different class* (e.g., a `Teacher` object), we cannot possibly be equal. Return `false` without throwing any exception.
3. **Line 3 тАФ Type-safe downcast:** After confirming the class is identical, we cast `obj` to `Student`. The previous type check guarantees this cast is safe.
4. **Line 4 тАФ Field comparison:** Compare the `rollNo` field (the business key).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Examiner Note):** Using `instanceof` instead of `getClass() != obj.getClass()`. While `instanceof` is *sometimes* acceptable, it breaks **symmetry** in the presence of subclasses (a known contract violation). The `getClass()` comparison is the strictly correct form.

---

## 3.4 Overriding `hashCode()` тАФ Mathematical Justification

After overriding `equals`, the contract **demands** we also override `hashCode`.

**Correct Implementation:**
```
@Override
public int hashCode() {
    return Objects.hash(rollNo);  // Java 7+ utility method
}
```

**Manual equivalent (often asked in KTU):**
```
@Override
public int hashCode() {
    int hash = 17;            // start with non-zero prime
    hash = 31 * hash + rollNo; // multiply by 31 and add the field
    return hash;
}
```

**Why 17 and 31?** These are **small prime numbers** historically chosen by the Java standard library. They give good distribution of hash values for typical inputs, minimizing collisions in hash-based collections.

**Verification of the contract:**
- For two students with `rollNo = 42`, `hashCode()` returns the same integer.
- For two students with `rollNo = 7` and `rollNo = 8`, hash codes will differ (in most cases).
- The contract $a.\text{equals}(b) \implies a.\text{hashCode}() = b.\text{hashCode}()$ is preserved.

---

## 3.5 The `clone()` Method тАФ Shallow Copy Derivation

**Step 1:** Make the class implement `Cloneable`.
```
class Student implements Cloneable { ... }
```

**Step 2:** Override `clone()` and change access modifier to `public`.
```
@Override
public Object clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException {
    return super.clone();   // invokes Object.clone() тАФ a native method
}
```

**Step 3:** Test it.
```
Student s1 = new Student("Anu", 42);
Student s2 = (Student) s1.clone();
System.out.println(s1 == s2);        // false (different memory)
System.out.println(s1.equals(s2));   // depends on equals() override
```

**Mathematical property of shallow copy:**
$$
\forall \, f \in \text{primitive fields}: \text{clone}.f \neq \text{original}.f \quad \text{(independent copies)}
$$

$$
\forall \, r \in \text{reference fields}: \text{clone}.r = \text{original}.r \quad \text{(shared reference)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Deep copy** is achieved by manually cloning every reference field too, typically via recursive calls or copy constructors. KTU Module 2 usually tests the distinction, not the full implementation.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Override Which Method

| You Want To... | Override This Method | Contract Considerations |
|----------------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| Print object meaningfully | `toString()` | None тАФ purely cosmetic |
| Compare objects by value | `equals(Object)` | Symmetric, reflexive, transitive |
| Use object in `HashMap`/`HashSet` | `equals()` + `hashCode()` | Both must be consistent |
| Duplicate an object | `clone()` | Class must implement `Cloneable` |
| Identify runtime type | Use `getClass()` (no override) | Final тАФ cannot be overridden |
| Customize GC cleanup | `finalize()` (deprecated) | Not guaranteed to run |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Java Class Hierarchy тАФ Mermaid Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    obj["JAVA LANG OBJECT"]
    str["String"]
    strb["StringBuilder"]
    intg["Integer"]
    dbl["Double"]
    num["Number"]
    thr["Thread"]
    run["Runnable"]
    excep["Exception"]
    throw["Throwable"]
    stud["Student"]
    fac["Faculty"]
    cust["CustomUserClass"]

    obj --> str
    obj --> strb
    obj --> thr
    obj --> run
    obj --> cust
    num --> intg
    num --> dbl
    obj --> num
    obj --> throw
    throw --> excep
    cust --> stud
    cust --> fac

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef builtin fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef custom fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class obj root
    class str,strb,intg,dbl,num,thr,run,excep,throw builtin
    class stud,fac,cust custom
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **gold-colored** node is the absolute root of the Java hierarchy.
- All **sky-blue** nodes are JDK built-in classes that directly or indirectly inherit from `Object`.
- All **green** nodes are user-defined custom classes; notice that even if a user writes `class Student {}`, it implicitly chains back to `Object`.
- The `Number тЖТ Integer` chain illustrates *multi-level* inheritance, where `Integer` is three levels deep from `Object`.

## 4.2 Method Categories of `Object` тАФ Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    objClass["Object Class"]
    m1["getClass - Final"]
    m2["hashCode - Overridable"]
    m3["equals - Overridable"]
    m4["toString - Overridable"]
    m5["clone - Protected Overridable"]
    m6["finalize - Protected Deprecated"]
    m7["wait - 3 Overloads Overridable"]
    m8["notify - Overridable"]
    m9["notifyAll - Overridable"]

    objClass --> m1
    objClass --> m2
    objClass --> m3
    objClass --> m4
    objClass --> m5
    objClass --> m6
    objClass --> m7
    objClass --> m8
    objClass --> m9

    m2 -. "Must override with equals" .-> m3
    m7 -. "Used with notify and notifyAll" .-> m8
    m7 -. "Used with notify and notifyAll" .-> m9
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **solid arrows** show direct method ownership by the `Object` class.
- The **dashed arrows** highlight critical **contracts** between methods:
  - `hashCode()` and `equals()` must be overridden *together*.
  - `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()` work as a **trio** for inter-thread communication.

## 4.3 Memory Model: Shallow vs Deep Copy тАФ Mermaid Visualization

```mermaid
graph TD
    orig["Original Student Object"]
    origName["name - Anu"]
    origRoll["rollNo - 42"]
    origAddr["address ref"]

    clo["Cloned Student Object"]
    cloName["name - Anu copy"]
    cloRoll["rollNo - 42 copy"]
    cloAddr["address ref - SHARED"]

    deepClo["Deep Cloned Object"]
    deepAddr["address ref - NEW COPY"]

    addrObj["Address Object"]
    deepAddr2["Address Object - Copy"]

    orig --> origName
    orig --> origRoll
    orig --> origAddr
    origAddr --> addrObj
    clo --> cloName
    clo --> cloRoll
    clo --> cloAddr
    cloAddr -. "Shallow - same ref" .-> addrObj
    deepClo --> deepAddr
    deepAddr --> deepAddr2
```

**What the diagram shows:**

- The **left side** represents a *shallow clone*: the `rollNo` primitive is duplicated, but the `address` reference still points to the **same** `Address` object in memory.
- The **right side** represents a *deep clone*: a brand-new `Address` object is constructed for the clone, fully independent of the original.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]`
**"What is the significance of the `java.lang.Object` class in Java? Why is it considered the root of the class hierarchy?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The `java.lang.Object` class is the **root of the Java class hierarchy** and resides in the `java.lang` package, which is auto-imported.
- Every class in Java, whether user-defined or built-in, **directly or indirectly inherits** from `Object`. If a class does not use `extends`, the compiler implicitly makes `Object` its superclass.
- It provides **11 default methods** (such as `toString()`, `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `getClass()`, `clone()`, `finalize()`, `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()`) that all Java objects inherit and can override.
- It enables **polymorphism**, allowing a reference of type `Object` to refer to any Java object.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]`
**"Differentiate between `==` operator and the `equals()` method in Java."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `==` Operator | `.equals()` Method |
|---------|---------------|--------------------|
| Type | Operator | Method (inherited from `Object`) |
| Compares | Memory addresses (reference) | Content / logical value |
| For primitives | Compares values | Cannot be used (primitives have no methods) |
| Overridable? | No (operator) | Yes (we can override it) |
| Default behavior | Always memory-based | Same as `==` until overridden |

The `==` operator is used for reference (address) comparison, while `.equals()` is meant for logical equality, which the programmer customizes by overriding.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks тАФ KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A тАФ Option 1 `[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the methods `toString()`, `equals(Object obj)`, and `hashCode()` of the `Object` class. Write a Java program to create a class `Employee` with fields `empId` and `name`, and override these three methods such that two employees are considered equal if their `empId` matches.

**Model Solution:**

```
import java.util.Objects;

class Employee {
    private int empId;
    private String name;

    public Employee(int empId, String name) {
        this.empId = empId;
        this.name = name;
    }

    // Overriding toString()
    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Employee[empId=" + empId + ", name=" + name + "]";
    }

    // Overriding equals()
    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object obj) {
        if (this == obj) return true;                              // Step 1
        if (obj == null || getClass() != obj.getClass()) return false;  // Step 2
        Employee other = (Employee) obj;                           // Step 3
        return this.empId == other.empId;                          // Step 4
    }

    // Overriding hashCode()
    @Override
    public int hashCode() {
        return Objects.hash(empId);
    }
}

public class TestEmployee {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee e1 = new Employee(101, "Anu");
        Employee e2 = new Employee(101, "Anjali");

        System.out.println(e1);                  // calls toString()
        System.out.println(e1.equals(e2));       // true (same empId)
        System.out.println(e1.hashCode() == e2.hashCode());  // true
    }
}
```

**Explanation of marks distribution (KTU pattern):**

- `toString()` explanation + override (2 marks)
- `equals()` explanation + correct override with all contract steps (3 marks)
- `hashCode()` explanation + correct override (1 mark)
- Working program output (1 mark)

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the `equals()` and `hashCode()` contract. What happens if you override `equals()` but not `hashCode()`? Illustrate with an example where this contract violation causes a `HashSet` to behave incorrectly.

**Model Solution:**

**The Contract (4 marks):**
- If `a.equals(b)` returns `true`, then `a.hashCode()` must equal `b.hashCode()`.
- If `a.equals(b)` returns `false`, hash codes *may or may not* be equal.
- The contract is essential because hash-based collections (`HashMap`, `HashSet`, `Hashtable`) first compute `hashCode()` to locate a bucket, and then use `equals()` to find the actual element within that bucket.

**The Consequence of Violation (3 marks):**
If we override only `equals()` and not `hashCode()`, the `HashSet` will place the object in a bucket based on the *default* `Object.hashCode()` (memory address). Two logically equal objects will fall into *different* buckets, causing `contains()`, `remove()`, and duplicate detection to silently fail.

**Illustrative example:**
```
import java.util.HashSet;

class Student {
    int rollNo;
    Student(int r) { this.rollNo = r; }
    
    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object o) {
        if (this == o) return true;
        if (!(o instanceof Student)) return false;
        return this.rollNo == ((Student) o).rollNo;
    }
    // hashCode() NOT overridden!
}

public class TestHash {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        HashSet<Student> set = new HashSet<>();
        set.add(new Student(1));
        set.add(new Student(1));
        System.out.println(set.size());  // Prints 2 instead of 1!
    }
}
```

**Output:** `2` (a bug тАФ duplicates exist because the default `hashCode()` is different for the two instances).

**Fix:** Override `hashCode()` as:
```
@Override
public int hashCode() { return Objects.hash(rollNo); }
```
Now `set.size()` correctly prints `1`.

---

### Question B тАФ Option 2 `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 marks]** What is method overriding? Explain the rules of overriding `Object` class methods. Write a Java program to demonstrate method overriding of `toString()` and `equals()` for a class `Book` with fields `title`, `author`, and `isbn`.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Method overriding is the process of providing a **different implementation** of a method in a subclass that is already defined in the superclass, using the **same signature** (name, return type, and parameter list).

**Rules of Overriding (2 marks):**
1. Method name, return type, and parameter list must be **identical**.
2. Access modifier cannot be more *restrictive* (e.g., cannot change `public` to `private`).
3. `final` methods cannot be overridden.
4. `static` methods cannot be overridden (they can only be *hidden*).
5. The `@Override` annotation is recommended for compile-time safety.
6. `private` methods are not inherited and thus cannot be overridden.

**Java Program (3 marks):**
```
class Book {
    private String title;
    private String author;
    private String isbn;

    public Book(String title, String author, String isbn) {
        this.title = title;
        this.author = author;
        this.isbn = isbn;
    }

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Book[Title=" + title + ", Author=" + author + ", ISBN=" + isbn + "]";
    }

    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object obj) {
        if (this == obj) return true;
        if (obj == null || getClass() != obj.getClass()) return false;
        Book other = (Book) obj;
        return this.isbn.equals(other.isbn);
    }

    @Override
    public int hashCode() {
        return isbn.hashCode();
    }
}

public class TestBook {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Book b1 = new Book("Java", "James", "ISBN001");
        Book b2 = new Book("Java", "Gosling", "ISBN001");
        System.out.println(b1);              // calls overridden toString()
        System.out.println(b1.equals(b2));   // true (same ISBN)
    }
}
```

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the `clone()` method of the `Object` class. Discuss the difference between **shallow copy** and **deep copy** with a suitable Java program. Also explain why `finalize()` is deprecated in modern Java.

**Model Solution:**

**The `clone()` Method (3 marks):**
- Signature: `protected Object clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException`
- It is defined in `Object` and used to create a **field-by-field copy** of the object.
- The class must implement the `java.lang.Cloneable` marker interface; otherwise, `CloneNotSupportedException` is thrown.
- Default access is `protected`; we must override it and widen it to `public` for external use.

**Shallow vs Deep Copy (3 marks):**

| Property | Shallow Copy | Deep Copy |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| Primitive fields | Copied (independent) | Copied (independent) |
| Reference fields | Shared with original | Independently cloned |
| Memory usage | Lower | Higher |
| Default behavior of `Object.clone()` | Yes (shallow) | No (must implement manually) |
| Risk | Modifying nested object affects both copies | Fully independent copies |

**Java Program:**
```
class Address implements Cloneable {
    String city;
    Address(String city) { this.city = city; }
    @Override
    public Address clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException {
        return (Address) super.clone();
    }
}

class Person implements Cloneable {
    String name;
    Address address;
    Person(String name, Address address) {
        this.name = name;
        this.address = address;
    }

    // Shallow clone
    @Override
    public Person clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException {
        return (Person) super.clone();
    }

    // Deep clone
    public Person deepClone() throws CloneNotSupportedException {
        Person copy = (Person) super.clone();
        copy.address = this.address.clone();
        return copy;
    }
}

public class TestClone {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Person p1 = new Person("Anu", new Address("Kochi"));
        Person shallow = p1.clone();
        Person deep = p1.deepClone();

        shallow.address.city = "Delhi";
        System.out.println("Original: " + p1.address.city);   // Delhi (affected)
        System.out.println("Shallow:  " + shallow.address.city); // Delhi
        System.out.println("Deep:     " + deep.address.city);    // Kochi (independent)
    }
}
```

**`finalize()` Deprecation (1 mark):**
- `finalize()` was historically used for cleanup before the GC reclaimed an object.
- It is **deprecated since Java 9** because:
  - The JVM does not guarantee it will run.
  - It can cause performance issues and resurrect objects.
  - Modern alternatives (`try-with-resources`, `AutoCloseable`, `Cleaner` API) are safer.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU ESE Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation** тАФ KTU evaluators deduct 1 mark for not using the annotation, as it indicates lack of professional practice.
> 2. **Violating `equals()` contract** тАФ Not checking for `null` or class type results in `-1` to `-2` marks. Many students simply write `return this.empId == obj.empId;` which causes `NullPointerException` at runtime.
> 3. **Overriding `equals()` but not `hashCode()`** тАФ Even if the question doesn't explicitly ask for `hashCode()`, omitting it is considered an **incomplete answer** and costs 1тАУ2 marks.
> 4. **Changing method signature during override** тАФ Changing return type, parameter type, or making access *more restrictive* is an invalid override; the compiler treats it as a *new* method. KTU deducts full marks.
> 5. **Confusing `finalize()` with C++ destructors** тАФ They are *not* equivalent. `finalize()` is not guaranteed to run. Conflating the two costs marks in theory questions.
> 6. **Calling `super.toString()` unnecessarily** тАФ When asked to override `toString()`, students often add `super.toString()` in the return, which defeats the purpose. The override should *replace*, not *extend*, the parent.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ The Object Class (Module 2):**

- **Root class:** `java.lang.Object` is the parent of every Java class, implicitly inherited when no `extends` is specified.
- **Total methods:** 11 in modern Java (9 overridable, 1 final, plus `finalize` which is deprecated).
- **`getClass()`:** Final; returns runtime `Class<?>` object тАФ used in reflection.
- **`toString()`:** Default returns `ClassName@hexHash`; override for readable output. Use `@Override`.
- **`equals()`:** Default is `==` (reference equality); override for *value* equality. Must satisfy reflexive, symmetric, transitive, consistent, null-safe contract.
- **`hashCode()`:** Default is memory-derived. **Must be overridden alongside `equals()`** to maintain contract. Used by `HashMap`/`HashSet`.
- **`clone()`:** `protected`; class must implement `Cloneable`. Default = *shallow copy*. Deep copy needs manual implementation.
- **`finalize()`:** Deprecated since Java 9; not equivalent to C++ destructors; not guaranteed to execute.
- **Thread methods** (`wait`, `notify`, `notifyAll`): Belong to `Object` because every object can act as a *monitor lock* in Java's intrinsic locking model. (Covered in detail in Module 5.)
- **Polymorphism power:** `Object` reference can hold *any* Java object тАФ foundation of collections, generics, and reflection.
- **Common KTU 3-mark question:** "Why is `Object` the superclass of all classes?" тАФ Always mention *implicit inheritance* and *polymorphism*.
- **Common KTU 7/14-mark question:** "Override `equals` and `hashCode` for class X" тАФ Always use `@Override`, follow the 4-step contract, and explain why both overrides are needed.
- **The Golden Rule:** $a.\text{equals}(b) \implies a.\text{hashCode}() = b.\text{hashCode}()$. Violating it breaks Java collections silently тАФ *the program compiles, but the logic fails*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abstract Classes and Methods
Using final with Inheritance, Packages and Interfaces - Defining Package
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Using final with Inheritance, Packages and Interfaces - Defining Package # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The `final` Keyword in Java The `final` keyword in Java is a **non-access modifier** that can be applied to a class, a method, or a variable. When applied, it restricts the user from modifying the entity in a specific way: - **Final Variable**: Its value cannot be changed once initialized (acts as a constant). - **Final Method**: It cannot be overridden by any subclass. - **Final Class**: It cannot be inherited (extended) by any other class. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Under inheritance, `final` plays a critical role in enforcing **immutability, security, and design integrity**. Board questions frequently test the difference between `final`, `finally`, and `finalize` (though `finalize` is from `Object` class and deprecated since Java 9). ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **government-issued certificate** (like a birth certificate or a degree certificate): - A **final variable** is like the **serial number** printed on the certificate тАФ once printed, it can never be altered. - A **final method** is like the **official signature/stamp procedure** тАФ the government has defined exactly how it must be done, and no subordinate office is allowed to change the procedure. - A **final class** is like a **sealed official document** тАФ no one is allowed to make a "modified version" or a "derived document" from it. The idea is: **once finalized, the definition is locked.** ## 1.2 `final` with Inheritance тАФ Detailed Categories ### 1.2.1 Final Variable A variable declared as `final` becomes a **constant** тАФ it can be assigned only once. In KTU parlance, this is often called a **blank final variable** if uninitialized at declaration. ```java final int MAX_SCORE = 100; // Final variable (constant) final double PI; // Blank final variable PI = 3.14159; // Must be assigned exactly once ``` ### 1.2.2 Final Method A method declared as `final` **cannot be overridden** in any subclass. This is used to enforce that a particular behavior must not be altered by derived classes (commonly seen in the Java standard library, e.g., `Object.getClass()`). ### 1.2.3 Final Class A class declared as `final` **cannot be extended (inherited from)**. All methods of a final class are implicitly final, but the variables are not. Examples from Java standard library: `String`, `Math`, `Integer` (wrapper classes). > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box тАФ Final Class (KTU Standard)** > A class declared with the `final` modifier is called a *final class*. A final class cannot be subclassed. The keyword `final` is used to prevent inheritance, thereby making the class immutable in nature. ## 1.3 Packages in Java тАФ Core Definition A **package** in Java is a **namespace** that organizes a set of related classes and interfaces. Conceptually, packages are Java's equivalent of folders in a file system тАФ they prevent naming conflicts and provide access control. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**: > A package is a grouping of related types (classes, interfaces, enumerations, and annotations) providing access protection and namespace management. Packages are stored in a hierarchical manner and correspond directly to directories in the file system. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **large library**: - A **package** is like a **section/aisle** of the library (e.g., "Science Section", "Literature Section"). - Each **class** is like a **specific book** on a shelf. - Two libraries (Java applications) can each have a book called `Manager` тАФ but as long as they are in different sections (packages), there's no confusion. - The **import** statement is like a library card that allows you to borrow books from another section. ## 1.4 Defining a Package тАФ Syntax A package is defined using the **`package` keyword** as the **first statement** in a Java source file. ```java package com.ktu.student.results; public class ResultProcessor { } ``` The above declaration means the class `ResultProcessor` belongs to the package `com.ktu.student.results`, which physically resides in the directory path `com/ktu/student/results/`. ## 1.5 Types of Packages in Java | Type | Description | Example | |------|-------------|---------| | **Built-in (JDK) Packages** | Pre-defined packages shipped with the JDK | `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.sql` | | **User-defined Packages** | Packages created by the programmer to organize their own classes | `com.ktu.banking`, `edu.college.cse` | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Java API Packages (Memorize for KTU)**: > - `java.lang` тАФ Auto-imported; contains `String`, `Math`, `Object`, `System`. > - `java.util` тАФ Contains collection framework (`ArrayList`, `HashMap`), `Scanner`, `Date`. > - `java.io` тАФ Input/Output streams (`File`, `FileReader`, `BufferedReader`). > - `java.sql` тАФ Database connectivity (`Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet`). > - `javax.swing` тАФ GUI components (extended package). ## 1.6 Why Packages? (KTU Frequently Asked) 1. **Namespace Management** тАФ Avoids name conflicts. Two classes can have the same name if they belong to different packages (e.g., `java.util.Date` vs `java.sql.Date`). 2. **Access Protection** тАФ Provides access levels (`default` and `protected` rely on packages). 3. **Encapsulation** тАФ Groups related classes together, improving modularity. 4. **Reusability** тАФ Classes in a package can be reused across multiple programs. 5. **Maintainability** тАФ Easier to locate, debug, and maintain logically grouped code. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of `final` with Inheritance ### 2.1.1 Final Variable тАФ Operational Logic **Step-by-step reasoning:** 1. When the JVM encounters a `final` variable declaration, it allocates memory and binds the identifier to that memory location. 2. Any subsequent assignment to that identifier raises a **compile-time error**: *"variable X might already have been assigned"*. 3. For **instance final variables**, initialization must occur in **every constructor** exactly once. 4. For **static final variables**, initialization must occur in a **static initializer block** or at the point of declaration. **Why?** The `final` modifier enforces **immutability**, which is critical in multi-threaded environments where shared mutable state can cause race conditions. ### 2.1.2 Final Method тАФ Operational Logic 1. When a method is marked `final`, the compiler marks the method's entry in the class's **method table** as *non-virtual*. 2. The JVM can apply **aggressive inlining and optimization** because it knows the method binding will not change via dynamic dispatch. 3. Any subclass attempting to override produces a **compile-time error**: *"cannot override the final method from ParentClass"*. **Why?** This is used when a base class author wants to guarantee that a critical algorithm (e.g., security check, hashing) cannot be maliciously or accidentally modified. ### 2.1.3 Final Class тАФ Operational Logic 1. When a class is declared `final`, the compiler rejects any `extends` clause referencing it. 2. All methods inside a final class become *implicitly final* (though variables do not). 3. Examples: `java.lang.String` is final тАФ this prevents subclasses from breaking string immutability invariants crucial for the JVM string pool. **Why?** Prevents inheritance-based breaking of contracts, ensuring the class's behavior is preserved exactly as designed. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.2.1 Final Keyword тАФ At a Glance | Entity | Syntax | Restriction | Real-World Use | |--------|--------|-------------|----------------| | **Final Variable** | `final int x = 5;` | Cannot reassign value | Constants, configuration keys | | **Final Method** | `final void display() { }` | Cannot be overridden | Security/authentication methods | | **Final Class** | `final class MyClass { }` | Cannot be subclassed | Immutable types (`String`, `Integer`) | | **Final Parameter** | `void m(final int n)` | Parameter cannot be modified inside method | Locking references from alteration | | **Static Final** | `static final double PI = 3.14;` | Global constant | Mathematical constants | ### 2.2.2 Package Definition тАФ Rules and Syntax | Rule | Description | Example | |------|-------------|---------| | **First Statement** | `package` declaration must be the first statement | `package mypkg;` | | **Directory Mapping** | Package name = directory path (separated by `/` or `\\`) | `com.ktu.cse` тЖТ `com/ktu/cse/` | | **Naming Convention** | Reverse domain name, all lowercase | `org.apache.commons` | | **Compilation** | Use `javac -d . FileName.java` to create package directory | Places `.class` in correct folder | | **Execution** | Use `java com.ktu.cse.MainClass` | Run from the package's root | | **Import** | Use `import` keyword to use classes from other packages | `import java.util.Scanner;` | | **CLASSPATH** | Must include the root directory of the package hierarchy | `set CLASSPATH=.;C:\myclasses` | ### 2.2.3 Access Modifiers in Package Context | Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Diff Pkg) | Other Packages | |----------|:----------:|:------------:|:-------------------:|:--------------:| | `private` | тЬУ | тЬЧ | тЬЧ | тЬЧ | | `default` (no modifier) | тЬУ | тЬУ | тЬЧ | тЬЧ | | `protected` | тЬУ | тЬУ | тЬУ | тЬЧ | | `public` | тЬУ | тЬУ | тЬУ | тЬУ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pitfall**: The `default` access modifier (package-private) is NOT a keyword тАФ leaving out any access modifier makes the class/member accessible **only within the same package**. This is heavily tested in KTU board exams. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Concept | Industry Use Case | |---------|-------------------| | `final` variables | Database connection URLs, API keys, tax rates, version constants | | `final` methods | `getClass()` in `Object` class тАФ JVM-critical, must not be overridden | | `final` classes | `String`, `LocalDate`, `BigDecimal` тАФ must be immutable for safety | | Packages | Microservices group classes by domain (e.g., `com.bank.authentication`) | ## 2.4 Naming Convention Standards (KTU Frequently Asked) > [!NOTE] > **Java Package Naming Standards (Industry + KTU Standard)**: > - Package names are written in **all lowercase**. > - Use the **reverse Internet domain name** of your organization. > - Example: For KTU domain `ktu.edu.in`, packages would begin with `in.edu.ktu`. > - Underscores are discouraged; use dots for hierarchy. > - Java keywords (e.g., `int`, `class`) cannot be used as package names. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Final Variable Compilation Consider the following code and analyze the step-by-step JVM behavior: ```java class Constants { final int MAX = 100; final int MIN; // Blank final static final double GST = 18.0; Constants() { MIN = 1; // Blank final initialized in constructor } } ``` **Analytical Steps:** **Step 1 тАФ Declaration Phase** The compiler allocates a symbolic entry for `MAX`, `MIN`, and `GST` in the class's constant pool. The `final` flag is set on each entry. **Step 2 тАФ Compilation Check** For `MAX`, the compiler verifies it has an initializer (`= 100`) at the declaration site. тЬУ Allowed. For `MIN`, the compiler notes that the variable is uninitialized but flags it as a *blank final* that must be assigned in every constructor. **Step 3 тАФ Constructor Execution** When `new Constants()` is invoked, the constructor assigns `MIN = 1`. The compiler inserts a check: *"definite assignment"* analysis ensures that every blank final is assigned exactly once on every code path. **Step 4 тАФ Runtime Enforcement** Any bytecode that attempts to write to `MAX`, `MIN`, or `GST` after their first assignment is rejected by the **bytecode verifier** at class loading time. **Logical Outcome Equation:** $$ \text{FinalVariable} = \begin{cases} \text{Assigned exactly once} & \Rightarrow \text{Compile \& Run OK} \\ \text{Assigned zero times} & \Rightarrow \text{Compile Error (blank final not assigned)} \\ \text{Assigned more than once} & \Rightarrow \text{Compile Error (may already have been assigned)} \end{cases} $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: Final Method Restriction ```java class Vehicle { final void startEngine() { System.out.println("Engine started with safety check"); } } class Car extends Vehicle { // void startEngine() { } // COMPILE ERROR } ``` **Step-by-step analysis:** **Step 1 тАФ Compilation of `Vehicle.class`** The compiler marks `startEngine()` with access flag `ACC_FINAL` in the method's access modifiers. **Step 2 тАФ Compilation of `Car.class`** The compiler reads `Vehicle.class` and finds the `ACC_FINAL` flag. It rejects any method declaration in `Car` that has the same name and signature as `startEngine()`. **Error message produced by `javac`:** ``` error: startEngine() in Car cannot override startEngine() in Vehicle overridden method is final ``` **Step 3 тАФ Theoretical Implication** The compiler enforces the **Liskov Substitution Principle** at compile time тАФ guarantees that a `Car` object is substitutable for a `Vehicle` object without behavioral surprises regarding the `startEngine()` method. ## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough: Final Class Restriction ```java final class ImmutablePoint { private final int x, y; ImmutablePoint(int x, int y) { this.x = x; this.y = y; } public int getX() { return x; } public int getY() { return y; } } // class ColoredPoint extends ImmutablePoint { } // COMPILE ERROR ``` **Step-by-step analysis:** **Step 1 тАФ Class File Generation** The compiler sets the `ACC_FINAL` class-level flag in `ImmutablePoint.class`. **Step 2 тАФ Inheritance Attempt** When `ColoredPoint` is compiled with `extends ImmutablePoint`, the compiler reads the class file header and detects the `ACC_FINAL` flag. **Step 3 тАФ Error Generation** ``` error: cannot inherit from final class ImmutablePoint public class ColoredPoint extends ImmutablePoint { ^ ``` **Logical Outcome:** $$ \text{ClassExtensibility} = \begin{cases} \text{Class declared final} & \Rightarrow \text{Inheritance forbidden} \\ \text{Class not declared final} & \Rightarrow \text{Inheritance allowed (subject to access)} \end{cases} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Package Directory Mapping Consider the Java source file `MyApp.java`: ```java package com.ktu.oop.module2; public class MyApp { } ``` **Step-by-step mapping logic:** **Step 1 тАФ Parse Package Declaration** The compiler reads `package com.ktu.oop.module2;` and tokenizes the package name into segments: `com`, `ktu`, `oop`, `module2`. **Step 2 тАФ Directory Structure Creation** The number of segments = depth of directory nesting. $$ \text{Directory Depth} = \vert \text{PackageName} \vert_{\text{dots}} + 1 $$ For `com.ktu.oop.module2`: $$ \text{Directory Depth} = 3 + 1 = 4 $$ **Step 3 тАФ Compilation Command** ``` javac -d . MyApp.java ``` This produces the directory structure: ``` ./ тФФтФАтФА com/ тФФтФАтФА ktu/ тФФтФАтФА oop/ тФФтФАтФА module2/ тФФтФАтФА MyApp.class ``` **Step 4 тАФ Execution Command** ``` java com.ktu.oop.module2.MyApp ``` The JVM splits the fully qualified name `com.ktu.oop.module2.MyApp` and uses the part before the last dot as the package path. $$ \text{FullyQualifiedName} = \text{PackagePath} \cup \text{ClassName} $$ $$ \text{com.ktu.oop.module2.MyApp} = \text{com.ktu.oop.module2} \cup \text{MyApp} $$ ## 3.5 Analytical Walkthrough: Compiling and Running a Package **Scenario**: Create a package `mypack` with class `Demo`, then use it in another program. **Step 1 тАФ Create Source File** Save the following as `Demo.java`: ```java package mypack; public class Demo { public void show() { System.out.println("Hello from mypack.Demo"); } } ``` **Step 2 тАФ Compile with `-d` flag** The `-d` flag tells the compiler where to place generated `.class` files. ``` javac -d . Demo.java ``` This automatically creates a directory named `mypack` and places `Demo.class` inside it. **Step 3 тАФ Create User File in Default Package** Save the following as `Test.java`: ```java import mypack.Demo; public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { Demo d = new Demo(); d.show(); } } ``` **Step 4 тАФ Compile and Run** ``` javac Test.java java Test ``` **Output:** ``` Hello from mypack.Demo ``` **Logical Flow Chart:** $$ \text{Source File} \xrightarrow{\text{javac -d}} \text{Package Directory} \xrightarrow{\text{import}} \text{Consumer Code} \xrightarrow{\text{java}} \text{Output} $$ ## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Final vs Other Modifiers | Feature | `final` | `abstract` | `static` | `private` | |---------|---------|------------|----------|-----------| | **Class Level** | Prevents inheritance | Forces inheritance | Cannot be at class level | Cannot be at class level (top-level) | | **Method Level** | Prevents overriding | Forces overriding | Belongs to class, not instance | Prevents access from other classes | | **Variable Level** | Value cannot change | Not applicable at variable level | Single copy shared | Hidden from outside class | | **Combinable?** | Yes, with `static` | No, with `final` | Yes, with `final` | Yes, with `static` (becomes file-level) | | **KTU Frequency** | High | High | High | Medium | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Combination Rules (KTU 2024)**: > - `final` + `abstract` is **illegal** (abstract needs overriding; final prevents it). > - `final` + `static` is **allowed** (used for global constants like `Math.PI`). > - `final` + `private` is **allowed** (private methods are not overridden anyway, but explicit `final` adds clarity). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Final Keyword Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Java Entity] --> B{Entity Type?} B -->|Class| C[Final Class?] B -->|Method| D[Final Method?] B -->|Variable| E[Final Variable?] C -->|Yes| F1[Cannot be inherited
All methods become final] C -->|No| F2[Can be inherited normally] D -->|Yes| G1[Cannot be overridden
Inlining enabled by JVM] D -->|No| G2[Can be overridden in subclass] E -->|Yes| H1[Constant - single assignment
Must be initialized] E -->|No| H2[Regular variable
Can be reassigned] F1 --> I[Lock the design] F2 --> I G1 --> I G2 --> I H1 --> I H2 --> I ``` ## 4.2 Package Architecture and Directory Mapping ```mermaid graph TB Root[Project Root Directory] --> Src[src/] Src --> ComDir[com/] ComDir --> KtuDir[ktu/] KtuDir --> OopDir[oop/] OopDir --> ModDir[module2/] ModDir --> A1[MyApp.java
Source File] ModDir --> A2[Helper.java
Source File] ModDir --> A3[Calculator.java
Source File] ModDir --> B1[MyApp.class
Compiled] ModDir --> B2[Helper.class
Compiled] ModDir --> B3[Calculator.class
Compiled] ModDir --> Pkg[Package: com.ktu.oop.module2] A1 -.belongs to.-> Pkg A2 -.belongs to.-> Pkg A3 -.belongs to.-> Pkg ``` ## 4.3 Import Statement Workflow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Dev as Developer participant Src as Source File participant Compiler as javac Compiler participant ClassPath as CLASSPATH participant JVM as Java Runtime Dev->>Src: Writes 'import java.util.ArrayList' Dev->>Compiler: Runs 'javac Program.java' Compiler->>ClassPath: Search for java/util/ArrayList.class ClassPath-->>Compiler: Found - returns location Compiler->>JVM: Generate bytecode with full reference JVM->>ClassPath: Load class at runtime ClassPath-->>JVM: Returns ArrayList.class bytes JVM-->>Dev: Program executes successfully ``` ## 4.4 Class Loading with Final and Package Constraints ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source Code] --> B{Has package decl?} B -->|Yes| C[Place in matching dir] B -->|No| D[Default package] C --> E[Compile with -d] D --> E E --> F[Bytecode Verification] F --> G{Check ACC_FINAL flag} G -->|Class final| H[Reject inheritance] G -->|Method final| I[Reject override] G -->|Variable final| J[Reject reassignment] H --> K[Class Loaded] I --> K J --> K K --> L[Execute Program] ``` ## 4.5 Package Access Control Matrix Visualization ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Pkg1[Package: com.ktu.alpha] A1[ClassA - public] A2[ClassB - default] A3[Helper - default] end subgraph Pkg2[Package: com.ktu.beta] B1[ClassC - public] B2[ClassD - default] B3[MainApp - public] end B3 -->|public access OK| A1 B3 -->|CANNOT access default| A2 B3 -->|CANNOT access default| A3 B2 -->|public access OK| A1 B1 -->|public access OK| A1 ``` ## 4.6 Compilation and Execution Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Developer writes code]) --> Step1[Step 1: Write source file with package declaration] Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Open terminal in source root] Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Run javac -d . MyClass.java] Step3 --> Step4{Compilation successful?} Step4 -->|No| Error1[Fix errors and recompile] Error1 --> Step3 Step4 -->|Yes| Step5[Step 4: Package directory auto-created] Step5 --> Step6[Step 5: Set CLASSPATH if needed] Step6 --> Step7[Step 6: Run java fully.qualified.ClassName] Step7 --> Step8{Execution successful?} Step8 -->|No| Error2[Check classpath and package] Error2 --> Step6 Step8 -->|Yes| Output([Program output displayed]) ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023] **Q: Differentiate between a `final` class and a `final` method in Java. Give one example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Final Class | Final Method | |--------|-------------|--------------| | **Declaration** | `final class ClassName { }` | `final returnType methodName() { }` | | **Effect** | The class cannot be inherited (no subclass allowed) | The method cannot be overridden in any subclass | | **Scope of restriction** | Applies to the entire class as a whole | Applies to a single method only | | **Example** | `final class String` (from `java.lang`) | `final int hashCode()` (in `Object` class) | | **Implication** | All members of a final class become effectively final | Other methods in the same class can still be overridden | **Why this distinction matters**: A final class prevents any form of inheritance, providing total immutability (e.g., `String`). A final method prevents selective override while still allowing inheritance for other behaviors тАФ a finer-grained restriction. *(1 mark for definition of final class + 1 mark for final method + 1 mark for examples)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024] **Q: What is a package in Java? List any two advantages of using packages.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** A package in Java is a namespace that organizes a set of related classes and interfaces. It is declared using the `package` keyword as the first statement in a Java source file and corresponds to a directory structure in the file system. Conceptually, packages act as containers that group functionally or logically similar types, preventing name collisions and providing controlled access. **Advantages (1.5 marks тАФ 0.75 each):** 1. **Namespace Management**: Two classes with the same name can coexist in different packages (e.g., `java.util.Date` and `java.sql.Date`), eliminating ambiguity. 2. **Access Control**: Packages enable `default` and `protected` access levels. Classes/members with default access are accessible only within the same package, providing encapsulation at the package level. 3. *(Bonus тАФ optional)*: Improved reusability, easier maintenance, and logical organization of large codebases. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) тАФ Internal Choice ### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam тАУ Model Paper 2024] **Q: (a)** Explain the use of the `final` keyword in Java with respect to variables, methods, and classes. Write a Java program to demonstrate that a final variable cannot be reinitialized and a final method cannot be overridden. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Define a package in Java. Explain the steps to create and use a user-defined package with a suitable example program. **[7 marks]** --- #### Part (a) тАФ Model Solution [7 marks] **Theoretical Explanation (3 marks):** The `final` keyword in Java is a non-access modifier used to apply restrictions on class, method, or variable: - **Final Variable (1 mark)**: Once assigned, its value cannot be changed. It essentially becomes a constant. For instance variables, it must be initialized at declaration or in every constructor. - **Final Method (1 mark)**: It cannot be overridden by subclasses. The compiler enforces this, ensuring the method's implementation remains intact across the inheritance hierarchy. - **Final Class (1 mark)**: The class cannot be extended. All methods inside become effectively final, ensuring the class's design contract is not altered by inheritance. **Java Program (4 marks):** ```java class Base { final int LIMIT = 100; // Final variable (constant) final void display() { // Final method System.out.println("Base display: LIMIT = " + LIMIT); } } class Derived extends Base { // void display() { } // COMPILE ERROR - cannot override final method void tryChange() { // LIMIT = 200; // COMPILE ERROR - cannot reassign final variable } } public class FinalDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Derived d = new Derived(); d.display(); System.out.println("Final variable LIMIT = " + d.LIMIT); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Base display: LIMIT = 100 Final variable LIMIT = 100 ``` **Explanation of compilation errors (1 mark):** - If `Derived` tries to declare `void display() { }`, the compiler produces: *"cannot override display() in Base; overridden method is final"*. - If `Derived.tryChange()` tries to reassign `LIMIT = 200`, the compiler produces: *"cannot assign a value to final variable LIMIT"*. --- #### Part (b) тАФ Model Solution [7 marks] **Definition of Package (2 marks):** A package in Java is a mechanism to encapsulate a group of classes, interfaces, and sub-packages into a single namespace. It serves three primary purposes: 1. Prevents naming conflicts. 2. Provides access control. 3. Makes searching/locating classes easier. **Steps to Create and Use a User-Defined Package (3 marks):** **Step 1:** Create a directory matching the package name (e.g., `mypackage`). **Step 2:** Write the source file with the package declaration as the first line. **Step 3:** Compile using `javac -d . FileName.java`.
