The `-d` flag automatically creates the package directory. **Step 4:** In the consuming file, use the `import` statement to access the packaged class. **Step 5:** Compile and run the consumer class. Ensure the CLASSPATH includes the parent directory of the package. **Example Program (2 marks):** **File 1: `MyClass.java`** (in `mypackage/` directory): ```java package mypackage; public class MyClass { public void greet() { System.out.println("Hello from mypackage.MyClass!"); } } ``` **Compilation:** `javac -d . MyClass.java` **File 2: `MainApp.java`** (in root directory): ```java import mypackage.MyClass; public class MainApp { public static void main(String[] args) { MyClass obj = new MyClass(); obj.greet(); } } ``` **Compilation and Execution:** ``` javac MainApp.java java MainApp ``` **Output:** ``` Hello from mypackage.MyClass! ``` --- ### Question B [14 Marks тАФ Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam тАУ Model Paper 2024] **Q: (a)** Discuss why the `String` class in Java is declared as `final`. What are the implications if `String` were not final? Write a Java program to demonstrate that the `String` class cannot be extended. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Explain the following with examples: **(i)** Static import **(ii)** Access protection using packages **(iii)** Naming convention for packages. **[7 marks]** --- #### Part (a) тАФ Model Solution [7 marks] **Why `String` is declared `final` (4 marks):** 1. **Security (1 mark)**: Strings are used to store sensitive data like usernames, passwords, file paths, and URLs. If `String` were extensible, a malicious subclass could override methods like `equals()` to bypass authentication. 2. **String Pool Optimization (1 mark)**: Java maintains a string literal pool in the heap. The JVM relies on the fact that `String` objects are immutable (guaranteed by `final`). If subclasses could alter string content, the pool's integrity would break. 3. **Thread Safety (1 mark)**: Immutable strings are inherently thread-safe, allowing safe sharing across threads without synchronization. 4. **Caching Hashcode (1 mark)**: `String` caches its hashcode on first call. This optimization is safe only if the string content cannot change тАФ guaranteed by `final`. **Implications if `String` were not final**: Programmers could create mutable strings via inheritance, breaking hash-based collections (`HashMap`, `HashSet`), causing security holes, and forcing the JVM to disable the string pool optimization. **Java Program (3 marks):** ```java // The following code WILL NOT COMPILE because String is final /* class MyString extends String { // COMPILE ERROR public MyString(String s) { super(s); } } */ // Demonstration: Use String directly and show its immutability public class StringFinalDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { String s1 = "Hello"; String s2 = s1.concat(" World"); System.out.println("Original: " + s1); // Still "Hello" System.out.println("Modified: " + s2); // "Hello World" // Reflection-based attempt (advanced - shows how final can be bypassed) // Note: This is for academic discussion only // Proof that String is final Class cls = s1.getClass(); System.out.println("Is String final? " + java.lang.reflect.Modifier.isFinal(cls.getModifiers())); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Original: Hello Modified: Hello World Is String final? true ``` --- #### Part (b) тАФ Model Solution [7 marks] **(i) Static Import (2.5 marks):** Static import allows members of a class (static variables and methods) to be used directly without the class name qualification. Introduced in Java 5. ```java import static java.lang.Math.*; import static java.lang.System.out; public class StaticImportDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { double result = sqrt(25); // No need to write Math.sqrt(25) out.println("Square root: " + result); // No need to write System.out } } ``` **Output:** ``` Square root: 5.0 ``` **(ii) Access Protection Using Packages (2.5 marks):** Packages enable `default` and `protected` access modifiers. A class with default access is accessible only within its own package. Consider two files in different packages: **File 1: `pkg1/Helper.java`** ```java package pkg1; class Helper { // default access - package-private void message() { // default access method System.out.println("Helper says hello"); } } ``` **File 2: `pkg2/MainApp.java`** ```java import pkg1.Helper; // COMPILE ERROR - Helper is not public public class MainApp { public static void main(String[] args) { // Helper h = new Helper(); // CANNOT access - Helper is package-private to pkg1 } } ``` The compiler rejects this because `Helper` has default (package-private) access, restricting it to `pkg1` only. **(iii) Naming Convention for Packages (2 marks):** Java packages follow these conventions: 1. **All lowercase letters** тАФ e.g., `java.util`, not `Java.Util`. 2. **Reverse domain name prefix** тАФ e.g., for `ktu.edu.in`, packages begin with `in.edu.ktu`. 3. **Hierarchical with dots** тАФ e.g., `com.ktu.oop.module2` represents nested directories. 4. **No Java keywords** тАФ package names like `int`, `class`, `for` are illegal. 5. **Avoid underscores and special characters** тАФ though digits are allowed (not as the first character). **Example:** ``` in.edu.ktu.cse.fourthsem.oop.assignments ``` This corresponds to the directory: `in/edu/ktu/cse/fourthsem/oop/assignments/` --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams (Module 2)**: > > **Mistake 1: Confusing `final` with `finally` and `finalize` (Loss: 1-2 marks)** > - `final` тАФ a keyword (modifier) for variables, methods, classes. > - `finally` тАФ a block used with try-catch for cleanup code. > - `finalize()` тАФ a method in `Object` class (deprecated in Java 9+), called by GC before object destruction. > > **Mistake 2: Writing `package` declaration NOT as the first statement (Loss: 2-3 marks)** > - The `package` declaration must appear **before any import statements and class definitions**. A single comment line is allowed before it, but no executable code. > > **Mistake 3: Forgetting the `-d` flag during compilation (Loss: 1-2 marks)** > - Without `javac -d . FileName.java`, the `.class` files are placed in the current directory, NOT in the package directory. This leads to `ClassNotFoundException` at runtime. > > **Mistake 4: Mismatch between package name and directory structure (Loss: 2 marks)** > - If you declare `package com.ktu.oop;`, the file MUST be located in `com/ktu/oop/` directory. The JVM uses the package path to locate classes. > > **Mistake 5: Trying to override a `final` method and not knowing the error message (Loss: 1 mark)** > - The exact error: *"cannot override the final method from ParentClass"*. KTU examiners love to ask what happens when you attempt this. > > **Mistake 6: Confusing `default` access with `public` (Loss: 1-2 marks)** > - A class without any access modifier has **default** (package-private) access, NOT public. It is accessible only within the same package. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ `final`, Packages & Interfaces (Module 2)** > > **`final` Keyword:** > - [ ] `final` variable тЖТ constant; assign once, never change > - [ ] `final` method тЖТ cannot be overridden in subclass > - [ ] `final` class тЖТ cannot be inherited (e.g., `String`, `Math`) > - [ ] `final` is **incompatible** with `abstract` > - [ ] `final` is **compatible** with `static` (e.g., `static final int MAX = 100;`) > - [ ] Blank final variables must be initialized in **every constructor** > - [ ] Examples: `String`, `Integer`, `Math`, `System` are all final classes > > **Packages:** > - [ ] Package = namespace + directory mapping > - [ ] Declaration: `package` keyword as **first statement** > - [ ] Compilation: `javac -d . FileName.java` (creates package directory) > - [ ] Execution: `java fully.qualified.ClassName` > - [ ] Import: `import package.name.ClassName;` or `import package.name.*;` > - [ ] `java.lang` is auto-imported (no explicit import needed) > - [ ] Naming convention: all lowercase, reverse domain (e.g., `in.edu.ktu.cse`) > - [ ] Two types: built-in (JDK packages) and user-defined > > **Access Control via Packages:** > - [ ] `private` тАФ same class only > - [ ] `default` (no modifier) тАФ same package only > - [ ] `protected` тАФ same package + subclasses (any package) > - [ ] `public` тАФ everywhere > > **Common Built-in Packages to Remember:** > - [ ] `java.lang` тАФ `String`, `Math`, `Object`, `System` (auto-imported) > - [ ] `java.util` тАФ `Scanner`, `ArrayList`, `HashMap`, `Date` > - [ ] `java.io` тАФ `File`, `FileReader`, `BufferedReader`, `PrintWriter` > - [ ] `java.sql` тАФ `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet` > - [ ] `javax.swing` тАФ `JFrame`, `JButton`, `JTextField` > > **KTU 2024 Board Exam Hot Topics from This Module:** > - [ ] Final class with real-world example (String immutability) > - [ ] Step-by-step package creation and usage > - [ ] Differences between `final`, `finally`, `finalize` > - [ ] Access control matrix > - [ ] `import` vs `import static` > - [ ] CLASSPATH and its role in package resolution

topics.title: CLASSPATH
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** CLASSPATH ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **CLASSPATH** is an **environment variable** and/or **command-line argument** that supplies the Java compiler (`javac`), the Java Virtual Machine (`JVM`), and the Java Runtime Environment (`JRE`) with the exact list of **directories, JAR (Java ARchive) files, and ZIP files** where the user-defined (third-party) classes and packages are located. The classpath acts as the **search path** the JVM consults to resolve symbolic class references into actual binary `.class` file locations during dynamic class loading. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEERT416 Module 2):** > Under the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework, the Classpath concept is a **fundamental prerequisite** for understanding the Java runtime architecture, package resolution, and modular deployment. Mastery of this topic is essential for the lab-based Continuous Evaluation Test (CET) and End Semester Evaluation (ESE). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Library Catalog System** > > Imagine you walk into a massive public library to read a book titled *"Effective Java"*. You don't know which shelf it is on, but the **librarian** does. The librarian follows a strict lookup procedure: > 1. First checks the **library's own reference section** (built-in shelves). > 2. Then checks the **partner network branches** (associated libraries). > 3. Finally checks the **external book repositories** you personally pointed to when you registered (your custom list). > > In Java: > - The **Librarian** = the **ClassLoader subsystem** of the JVM. > - The **Book** = the **class file** (`.class`) you want to load. > - The **"external repositories you pointed to"** = the **CLASSPATH** you configured. > - The **Librarian's lookup order** = the **delegation model** (Parent-First Delegation). ### 1.3 The Three Mandatory Components of Classpath Resolution The classpath string is a sequence of **three types of entries**, separated by the **OS-specific path separator**: | Component Type | Symbol/Format | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Directory Entry** | `./classes` or `C:\myproject\bin` | Tells the JVM to scan a folder for top-level `.class` files and sub-folders that mirror package structures. | | **JAR File Entry** | `libs/mysql.jar` | Points to a single compressed Java ARchive file containing many `.class` files. | | **Wildcard Directory** | `libs/*` | Shorthand to include **every `.jar` and `.zip` file** in the specified directory. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Path Separator Convention:** > - **Linux/macOS:** `:` (colon) тАФ e.g., `.:/home/user/lib/*` > - **Windows:** `;` (semicolon) тАФ e.g., `.;C:\libs\*` ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Why CLASSPATH Exists тАФ The Core Engineering "Why" Java's design philosophy is **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)**. To support this, the language **does not embed absolute file paths** into compiled bytecode (unlike C/C++ which links to `.dll`/`.so` files at specific OS locations). Instead, every compiled class contains only **symbolic names** (e.g., `com.ktu.utils.Calculator`). At runtime, the JVM must **dynamically resolve** these names to actual files. The **CLASSPATH** is the configuration mechanism that enables this dynamic resolution. ### 2.2 The Three Methods to Set CLASSPATH The classpath can be configured in **three distinct ways**, each with different precedence and persistence: #### Method 1: Default (Implicit) Classpath - If no classpath is set anywhere, the JVM assumes the **current working directory (`.`)** as the classpath. - This is the **default behavior** of `java` and `javac` tools. #### Method 2: Command-Line Argument (`-cp` or `-classpath`) - **Highest precedence** among runtime configurations. - Overrides any environment variable setting for that specific invocation. - **Example:** ```bash java -cp ".;C:\libs\*" com.ktu.Main ``` #### Method 3: Environment Variable (`CLASSPATH`) - Set via OS shell. - **Persists across sessions** (until the terminal is closed or unset). - **Lower precedence** than the `-cp` flag. - **Linux/macOS:** `export CLASSPATH=.:/home/user/libs/*` - **Windows:** `set CLASSPATH=.;C:\libs\*` > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Many students set the environment variable globally and then wonder why their command-line `-cp` argument "doesn't work". The rule is: **`-cp` always wins** over the environment variable for that specific process. ### 2.3 Precedence Hierarchy (Order of Resolution) The JVM follows a **strict three-tier hierarchy** when loading any class: | Priority | Source | ClassLoader Responsible | Contents | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 (Highest) | **Bootstrap Classpath** | Bootstrap ClassLoader (native C/C++ code) | Core Java SE classes (`rt.jar`, `java.lang`, `java.util`) | | 2 | **Extension Classpath** | Platform ClassLoader (formerly Extension Loader) | Classes in `jre/lib/ext` and `java.ext.dirs` | | 3 (Lowest) | **User/Application Classpath** | Application ClassLoader (System ClassLoader) | The directories, JARs, and ZIPs defined by `-cp` / `CLASSPATH` env var | ### 2.4 The Wildcard (`*`) Expansion Rule When the classpath contains an entry like `libs/*`, the JVM behaves as follows: 1. It expands the `*` into a list of all files in the `libs/` directory that match the pattern `*.jar` and `*.zip`. 2. It does **NOT** recurse into sub-directories (no recursive wildcard). 3. It does **NOT** include class files (`.class`) in the folder тАФ only compressed archives. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Value / Rule | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Default Classpath** | `.` (current directory) | Used when no `-cp` or env var is set | | **Path Separator (Linux/macOS)** | `:` (colon) | Between multiple entries | | **Path Separator (Windows)** | `;` (semicolon) | Between multiple entries | | **Wildcard Character** | `*` (asterisk) | Matches all `.jar` and `.zip` files in one directory | | **Wildcard Scope** | Non-recursive (single level only) | Sub-directories are **not** scanned | | **Precedence Order** | `-cp` flag > `CLASSPATH` env var > Default `.` | Command-line always wins | | **Bootstrap Classpath** | `JAVA_HOME/jre/lib/rt.jar` | Contains core Java SE APIs | | **ClassLoader Order** | Bootstrap $\rightarrow$ Platform $\rightarrow$ Application | Parent-First Delegation Model | | **Search Trigger** | First reference to a class | Lazy loading (not preloaded) | | **Error on Miss** | `java.lang.ClassNotFoundException` | Thrown if class absent from all paths | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Enterprise Application Servers** (Tomcat, JBoss, WebLogic) use extended classpath mechanisms to isolate web applications. - **Build Tools** (Maven, Gradle) automatically construct classpaths from `pom.xml` / `build.gradle` dependency declarations. - **Microservices** deployed as fat JARs (Spring Boot) embed all dependencies, eliminating the need for manual classpath configuration in production. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 The Class Loading Algorithm тАФ Step-by-Step Resolution When a Java program executes a statement that **first references** a class (e.g., `new MyClass()` or `MyClass.staticMethod()`), the JVM triggers the following **deterministic resolution sequence**: **Step 1: Bootstrap ClassLoader Search** The JVM consults the bootstrap classpath (hardcoded location of the core Java SE runtime). If the class is found here (e.g., `java.lang.String`), it is loaded immediately and the search terminates. **Step 2: Platform ClassLoader Search** If not found in Step 1, the request is forwarded to the Platform (Extension) ClassLoader, which scans the extension directories (`jre/lib/ext`). **Step 3: Application ClassLoader Search** If still not found, the Application (System) ClassLoader consults the **user-defined classpath** (the one set via `-cp` or `CLASSPATH`). It iterates through each entry in the order they appear in the classpath string: $$ \text{Search Sequence} = E_1 \rightarrow E_2 \rightarrow E_3 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow E_n $$ where each $E_i$ is a directory or JAR file entry. **Step 4: Directory Entry Resolution** For a directory entry $E_i = \text{"./classes"}$, the JVM constructs the path: $$ \text{Target Path} = E_i \cup \text{Package Path} \cup \text{ClassName.class} $$ For example, to load `com.ktu.utils.Calculator`, the JVM checks: $$ \text{Target Path} = \text{"./classes"} / \text{"com/ktu/utils"} / \text{"Calculator.class"} $$ $$ \text{Target Path} = \text{"./classes/com/ktu/utils/Calculator.class"} $$ **Step 5: JAR Entry Resolution** For a JAR entry $E_i = \text{"libs/mysql.jar"}$, the JVM opens the archive and looks for the entry: $$ \text{JAR Internal Path} = \text{"com/mysql/jdbc/Driver.class"} $$ **Step 6: Wildcard Expansion** If the entry is of the form `libs/*`, the JVM first computes the set: $$ W = \{ f \in \text{libs}/ \mid f \text{ endsWith ".jar" or ".zip"} \} $$ Then for each $w \in W$, it applies Step 5. **Step 7: Failure Handling** If the class is not found in any entry, the JVM throws: $$ \text{java.lang.ClassNotFoundException} $$ ### 3.2 Worked Example тАФ Complete Classpath Resolution **Scenario:** You want to run the class `com.ktu.Main` with classpath `.:./libs/*` on Linux. **Step 1:** The JVM receives the request to load `com.ktu.Main`. **Step 2:** Bootstrap and Platform loaders are consulted тАФ the class is not a JDK class, so the request passes down. **Step 3:** The Application ClassLoader begins iterating through the entries: - Entry 1: `.` (current directory) - Entry 2: `./libs/*` (all JARs in `./libs/`) **Step 4:** For Entry 1, the JVM checks if the file `./com/ktu/Main.class` exists. **Step 5:** If found, it is loaded and the search terminates. If not, the JVM proceeds to Entry 2. **Step 6:** For Entry 2, suppose `./libs/utility.jar` exists. The JVM opens it and checks for the internal entry `com/ktu/Main.class`. **Step 7:** If found, it is loaded. If not, `ClassNotFoundException` is thrown. ### 3.3 The ClassLoader Delegation Model (Parent-First) The delegation is **mathematically expressible** as a recursive function: $$ \text{loadClass}(C, L) = \begin{cases} \text{cacheLookup}(C) & \text{if } C \in L.\text{cache} \\ \text{parent.loadClass}(C) & \text{if } L.\text{parent} \neq \text{null} \\ \text{findClass}(C) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ where $L$ is the current ClassLoader, $C$ is the class name, and `parent.loadClass(C)` recursively delegates to the parent before `findClass` is invoked on the current loader. This guarantees that **no class loader ever overrides a JDK class** with a user-supplied malicious class (a critical security feature known as the **Sandbox Model**). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 ClassLoader Hierarchy (Parent-First Delegation) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Bootstrap ClassLoader
Native C/C++ Code
Loads: rt.jar, java.lang, java.util"] B["Platform ClassLoader
Loads: jre/lib/ext classes"] C["Application ClassLoader
Loads: User CLASSPATH directories and JARs"] D["Custom ClassLoader
User-defined loaders for plugins"] A --> B B --> C C --> D A2["Step 1: Request for class X"] -.-> A A3["Step 2: Check Bootstrap path"] -.-> A A4["Step 3: Delegate to Platform"] -.-> B A5["Step 4: Delegate to Application"] -.-> C A6["Step 5: User CLASSPATH search"] -.-> D ``` ### 4.2 CLASSPATH Resolution Flow тАФ Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["Program Execution
java -cp .;libs/ com.ktu.Main"] P2["Trigger: First reference to com.ktu.Main"] P3{"Bootstrap
ClassLoader
Search"} P4{"Found in
rt.jar?"} P5["Load from Bootstrap Path
Terminate Search"] P6{"Platform
ClassLoader
Search"} P7{"Found in
jre/lib/ext?"} P8["Load from Extension Path
Terminate Search"] P9["Application ClassLoader Activated"] P10["Iterate through CLASSPATH entries"] P11{"Entry is
Directory?"} P12["Build path: dir + package + classname.class"] P13{"File
Exists?"} P14["Load .class File
Terminate Search"] P15{"Entry is
Wildcard *?"} P16["Expand to all .jar and .zip files"] P17["Search inside each JAR archive"] P18{"Class found
in JAR?"} P19["Load from JAR
Terminate Search"] P20["Move to next entry in CLASSPATH"] P21["All entries exhausted"] P22["Throw java.lang.ClassNotFoundException"] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 -- Yes --> P5 P4 -- No --> P6 P6 --> P7 P7 -- Yes --> P8 P7 -- No --> P9 P9 --> P10 P10 --> P11 P11 -- Yes --> P12 P12 --> P13 P13 -- Yes --> P14 P13 -- No --> P20 P11 -- No --> P15 P15 -- Yes --> P16 P16 --> P17 P17 --> P18 P18 -- Yes --> P19 P18 -- No --> P20 P15 -- No --> P17 P20 --> P10 P10 --> P21 P21 --> P22 ``` ### 4.3 CLASSPATH Configuration Methods тАФ Comparison View ```mermaid flowchart LR M1["Method 1: Default
Current Directory ."] M2["Method 2: -cp flag
java -cp path App"] M3["Method 3: CLASSPATH env var
export CLASSPATH=path"] R["JVM Resolves Classpath
Highest Precedence Wins"] OUT["Final Effective Classpath"] M1 --> R M2 --> R M3 --> R R --> OUT M1x["Precedence: 3 (Lowest)"] M2x["Precedence: 1 (Highest)"] M3x["Precedence: 2 (Medium)"] M1 --- M1x M2 --- M2x M3 --- M3x ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Define CLASSPATH in Java. Why is it necessary for executing Java programs?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** CLASSPATH is an environment variable and/or command-line argument that specifies the locations (directories, JAR files, ZIP files) where the Java compiler and JVM should search for user-defined classes and packages. **Necessity (2 Marks):** Java compiled bytecode (`.class` files) does not contain absolute file paths. It only stores symbolic class names. When a program is executed, the JVM must dynamically resolve these symbolic names into actual file locations. The CLASSPATH provides the search path that enables this dynamic resolution, making Java's "Write Once, Run Anywhere" portability possible. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between the `-cp` command-line option and the `CLASSPATH` environment variable for setting the classpath in Java.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | `-cp` / `-classpath` Flag | `CLASSPATH` Environment Variable | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Scope (1 Mark)** | Applies only to the current JVM invocation. | Persists across multiple sessions and commands. | | **Precedence (1 Mark)** | Higher precedence тАФ overrides the environment variable. | Lower precedence тАФ overridden by `-cp` if both are set. | | **Setup (1 Mark)** | Passed as an argument to `java` or `javac` per execution. | Set once via OS shell (`export` / `set` command). | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 тАФ Modified]** **(a) Explain the different ways to set the CLASSPATH in Java with suitable examples. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The CLASSPATH can be configured in **three distinct ways**: **Way 1: Default Classpath (1 Mark)** When no classpath is explicitly set, the JVM automatically uses the **current working directory (`.`)** as the classpath. For example, if you execute `java MyApp` from the directory containing `MyApp.class`, the JVM finds it without any additional configuration. **Way 2: Command-Line `-cp` Option (3 Marks)** The `-cp` (or `-classpath`) flag is passed directly to the `java` or `javac` command. It has the **highest precedence** and applies only to the current invocation. ```bash java -cp ".;C:\libs\*" com.ktu.Main ``` In the above command: - `.` includes the current directory - `C:\libs\*` uses the wildcard to include all JAR files in `C:\libs\` - Multiple entries are separated by `;` (on Windows) or `:` (on Linux/macOS) **Way 3: CLASSPATH Environment Variable (3 Marks)** The environment variable is set at the OS level and persists across sessions: - **Linux/macOS:** `export CLASSPATH=.:/home/user/libs/*` - **Windows:** `set CLASSPATH=.;C:\libs\*` **Precedence Rule:** If both `-cp` and `CLASSPATH` are set, **the `-cp` flag wins** for that specific execution. --- **(b) Describe the ClassLoader hierarchy in Java with a neat diagram. Explain the Parent-First Delegation Model. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The JVM uses a **hierarchical chain of ClassLoaders** to dynamically load classes. The hierarchy consists of three primary loaders: **1. Bootstrap ClassLoader (2 Marks)** - Implemented in **native C/C++ code** (not a Java class). - Loads the core Java SE APIs from `JAVA_HOME/jre/lib/rt.jar`. - Responsible for classes like `java.lang.Object`, `java.lang.String`, `java.util.*`. - Has **no parent** тАФ it is the root of the hierarchy. **2. Platform (Extension) ClassLoader (2 Marks)** - A child of the Bootstrap ClassLoader. - Loads classes from the **Java Extension directories** (`JAVA_HOME/jre/lib/ext`) or any directory specified by the `java.ext.dirs` system property. - Used for optional JDK extensions. **3. Application (System) ClassLoader (1 Mark)** - A child of the Platform ClassLoader. - Loads classes from the **user-defined classpath** (the `-cp` value or the `CLASSPATH` environment variable). - This is the classloader used to load the applicationтАЩs main class. **Parent-First Delegation Model (2 Marks):** When a classloader receives a request to load a class, it does **not** attempt to load it immediately. Instead, it **delegates the request to its parent** first. The parent then delegates to its parent, and so on, until the request reaches the Bootstrap ClassLoader. Only if **no parent finds the class** does the current classloader attempt to load it itself. This model ensures: - **Security:** User code cannot replace core JDK classes (e.g., a malicious `java.lang.String` would never be loaded). - **Uniqueness:** A class is loaded only once in the JVM, avoiding inconsistent behavior. **Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Bootstrap ClassLoader
(Native C/C++ Code)"] B["Platform ClassLoader"] C["Application ClassLoader"] D["Custom ClassLoader"] A --> B B --> C C --> D ``` --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 тАФ Modified]** **(a) What is the default CLASSPATH in Java? Explain the search order followed by the JVM to locate classes with a step-by-step procedure. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Default CLASSPATH (1 Mark):** When the CLASSPATH is not explicitly set via the `-cp` flag or the `CLASSPATH` environment variable, the JVM uses the **current working directory (represented as `.`)** as the default classpath. **Search Order тАФ Step-by-Step Procedure (6 Marks):** When the JVM encounters the **first reference** to a class (e.g., during execution), the following sequence is followed: **Step 1: Bootstrap ClassLoader Check** The JVM checks if the class belongs to the core Java SE runtime (`rt.jar`). If yes, it is loaded and the search terminates. **Step 2: Platform ClassLoader Check** If not found, the request is forwarded to the Platform ClassLoader, which searches the `jre/lib/ext` directory. **Step 3: Application ClassLoader Check** If still not found, the Application ClassLoader iterates through **each entry in the user classpath** in the order they appear: - **Step 3a:** If the entry is a **directory** (e.g., `./classes`), the JVM constructs the path: $$\text{Path} = \text{Directory} / \text{Package Path} / \text{ClassName.class}$$ For example, to load `com.ktu.Calculator`, it checks `./classes/com/ktu/Calculator.class`. - **Step 3b:** If the entry is a **JAR file** (e.g., `libs/utility.jar`), the JVM opens the archive and searches for the internal entry `com/ktu/Calculator.class`. - **Step 3c:** If the entry is a **wildcard** (e.g., `libs/*`), the JVM expands it to all `.jar` and `.zip` files in that directory and searches each one. **Step 4: Failure** If no entry contains the class, the JVM throws `java.lang.ClassNotFoundException`. --- **(b) Explain the use of the wildcard (`*`) in CLASSPATH with suitable examples. List its advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Wildcard Syntax (2 Marks):** The wildcard character `*` is used as a shortcut to include **all JAR and ZIP files** in a specified directory. It is appended to a directory path separated by the path separator. **Example:** ```bash java -cp ".;C:\myproject\libs\*" com.ktu.Main ``` In this command, the `*` causes the JVM to automatically include every `.jar` and `.zip` file located in `C:\myproject\libs\`, without requiring the developer to list them one by one. **Advantages (3 Marks):** 1. **Convenience:** Developers do not need to manually update the classpath every time a new library JAR is added to the directory. 2. **Reduced errors:** Eliminates the risk of forgetting to add a new JAR to the classpath string. 3. **Simplified build scripts:** Build tools and shell scripts become cleaner and easier to maintain. **Limitations (2 Marks):** 1. **Non-recursive:** The wildcard scans **only the immediate directory** тАФ it does **not** descend into sub-directories. For nested folders, each level must be specified explicitly. 2. **Archives only:** The wildcard includes only `.jar` and `.zip` files. It does **not** include loose `.class` files in the directory. To include loose class files, the directory itself (without `*`) must be added as a separate classpath entry. 3. **Order unpredictability:** The order in which JAR files are loaded when using `*` is **not guaranteed** to be alphabetical. This can cause subtle bugs when two JARs contain classes with the same fully qualified name. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Wrong path separator:** Using `;` on Linux/macOS or `:` on Windows results in `ClassNotFoundException`. Always state the **OS-specific separator explicitly** in answers. > 2. **Confusing CLASSPATH with PATH:** `PATH` is for locating **executable programs** (like `java.exe`), while `CLASSPATH` is for locating **Java class files**. Many students interchange these terms. > 3. **Forgetting the dot (`.`):** Omitting the current directory from the classpath is a frequent error. Always include `.` if your application depends on classes in the working directory. > 4. **Wildcard misuse:** Writing `libs/*.jar` instead of `libs/*` is incorrect. The correct form is `libs/*` тАФ the JVM implicitly appends `.jar`/`.zip`. > 5. **ClassLoader order confusion:** Remember the delegation is **parent-first**, not child-first. Writing the reverse order will cost marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ CLASSPATH** - тнР **CLASSPATH** = the search path used by `javac` and `JVM` to locate user classes, JARs, and ZIPs. - тнР **Default value** = current working directory (`.`). - тнР **Three setting methods:** Default (`.`), `-cp` flag, `CLASSPATH` environment variable. - тнР **Precedence order:** `-cp` flag $\rightarrow$ `CLASSPATH` env var $\rightarrow$ Default `.`. - тнР **Path separator:** `:` on Linux/macOS, `;` on Windows. - тнР **Wildcard `*`** = include all `.jar` and `.zip` files in a single directory (non-recursive). - тнР **Three ClassLoaders:** Bootstrap $\rightarrow$ Platform $\rightarrow$ Application. - тнР **Delegation Model:** Parent-First (a classloader always asks its parent first). - тнР **Error on miss:** `java.lang.ClassNotFoundException`. - тнР **Lazy loading:** Classes are loaded only on first reference, not at program start. - тнР **CLASSPATH vs PATH:** CLASSPATH = for `.class` files; PATH = for OS executables. - тнР **JAR wildcards do not include loose `.class` files** тАФ only archives. - тнР **Bootstrap ClassLoader is native (C/C++)** тАФ not a Java object. - тнР **Parent-First Delegation is a security feature** тАФ prevents user code from spoofing JDK classes.

topics.title: Access Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Access Protection # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Access Protection** in Java is a language-level encapsulation mechanism implemented through **Access Modifiers** (also called **Visibility Modifiers**). These are reserved keywords placed before class, method, or variable declarations to control the *visibility* and *accessibility* of program entities across packages, classes, and inheritance hierarchies. Java provides **four** levels of access control, which align with the KTU 2024 Scheme's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) focus on encapsulation, modularity, and secure code design. The four access modifiers are: 1. **`private`** тАУ Most restrictive; accessible **only within the same class**. 2. **`default`** (no keyword / package-private) тАУ Accessible within the **same package only**. 3. **`protected`** тАУ Accessible within the **same package** AND by **subclasses in other packages**. 4. **`public`** тАУ Least restrictive; accessible from **everywhere** in the application. > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme classifies this topic under **CO2: Apply Object Oriented Principles** (Module 2 тАУ Classes, Objects & Inheritance). Mastery of access modifiers is a **mandatory pre-requisite** for understanding inheritance, polymorphism, and packages in subsequent modules. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАУ The "Smart Home" Model Imagine your house is a **Java class**, and the rooms inside represent **members (variables/methods)** of that class. | House Concept | Java Equivalent | Real-Life Meaning | |---------------|----------------|-------------------| | Your **private bedroom** | `private` member | Only *you* (the same class) can enter. No one else, not even family. | | **Living room** (family-only) | `default` member | Family members (same package) can freely access it. Outsiders cannot. | | **Guest room** (extended family) | `protected` member | Family + close relatives (subclasses in other packages) can access it. | | **Front garden & gate** | `public` member | Any visitor, postman, or stranger from outside can access it. | This "room-locking" idea is exactly what Java's access modifiers do тАФ they let a programmer **lock** data behind controlled doors, and hand out **selective keys** to trusted code (subclasses/package members). > [!TIP] > If a student remembers only **one thing**: *Default visibility is NOT the same as public.* Forgetting to write a modifier defaults the member to **package-private**, which is a frequent KTU exam pitfall. ## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Bold The access protection system in Java enforces the following **standard, language-defined metrics** (these are not physical constants but immutable language rules mandated by the **Java Language Specification тАУ JLS ┬з6.6**): - **4 distinct access levels** defined by the JLS. - **0 keywords** required to express *default (package-private)* access. - **1 keyword per member** for `public`, `protected`, or `private`. - **Compile-time enforcement**: Access violations are caught at **compilation**, not at runtime. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Access Protection тАУ Operational Logic Java's access system works on a **hierarchical opening** principle. Each modifier *expands* the visibility scope one level outward. ### Pillar 1: `private` тАУ The Locked Vault - **Scope**: Same class ONLY. - **Use case**: Data hiding, encapsulation of internal state. - **Inheritance interaction**: NOT inherited by subclasses. - **Cannot be used on**: Top-level (outer) classes. `private` is allowed only on **inner classes**, **member classes**, **methods**, and **fields**. ### Pillar 2: `default` (Package-Private) тАУ The Neighbourhood Gate - **Scope**: Same class + Same package. - **Use case**: When you want helper classes/methods to collaborate tightly inside one package but remain hidden from the outside world. - **Inheritance interaction**: NOT directly accessible to subclasses in *different* packages (though subclasses inherit it as a private-like member). ### Pillar 3: `protected` тАУ The Extended Family Key - **Scope**: Same class + Same package + Subclasses (anywhere) + Same-package non-subclasses. - **Use case**: Exposing a class's behaviour to its children for extension, while still hiding it from unrelated code. - **Inheritance interaction**: Subclasses in *different packages* can access `protected` members **only through inheritance** (i.e., on themselves or their own subtype instances тАФ not on parent-class references). ### Pillar 4: `public` тАУ The Open Plaza - **Scope**: Universal. - **Use case**: APIs, method contracts, constants meant to be globally available. - **Inheritance interaction**: Publicly inherited unless explicitly overridden with a more restrictive modifier (which is **illegal** in Java тАФ overriding cannot reduce visibility). ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Level Java's designers (Gosling, Joy, Steele et al., 1995) built access protection around **three engineering goals**: 1. **Encapsulation** тАУ Hide implementation details; expose only behaviour. 2. **Loose Coupling** тАУ Allow modules in different packages to interact through narrow public APIs. 3. **Safe Extensibility** тАУ Let subclasses reuse and extend protected internals without exposing them to the world. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet тАУ Access Control Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important table** for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) Module 2 questions. Memorize the **"Yes/No" pattern** for each row/column. | Access Modifier | Same Class | Same Package (non-subclass) | Subclass (Different Package) | Other Classes (Different Package, Non-Subclass) | |------------------|:----------:|:---------------------------:|:----------------------------:|:-------------------------------------------------:| | `public` | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | | `protected` | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No** | | `default` | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No** | **No** | | `private` | **Yes** | **No** | **No** | **No** | > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners often rephrase the same matrix as: *"Can a subclass in a different package access a `default` member of its superclass?"* The correct answer is **No**. ## 2.4 Access Control on Classes vs. Members | Entity | Allowed Modifiers | Disallowed | |---------------------|--------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------| | Top-level class | `public`, `default` | `private`, `protected` | | Inner (nested) class| `public`, `protected`, `default`, `private` | None тАФ all four allowed | | Interface | `public`, `default` (implicitly public) | `private`, `protected` | | Interface members | `public` (implicitly) | Any other modifier (pre-Java 9) | | Enum | `public`, `default` | `private`, `protected` | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Access protection is the **cornerstone of every enterprise Java framework**: - **Spring Framework**: `@Autowired` fields are often `private`, exposed via public getters/setters. - **Java Standard Library**: `ArrayList.add()` is `public`; internal `modCount` is `private`. - **Android SDK**: Activity lifecycle methods are `protected` so the framework can invoke them via inheritance. - **REST APIs (JAX-RS / Spring Boot)**: Public methods form the API surface; everything else is private/protected. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation тАУ The "Access Decision Algorithm" Java's compiler evaluates accessibility of a member using a deterministic 4-step algorithm. We derive it here step-by-step, because KTU questions frequently ask: *"Given this access scenario, determine if compilation succeeds."* Let the following variables be defined: - $C_{decl}$ = Class that **declares** the member. - $C_{ref}$ = Class from which the member is **referenced** (accessed). - $P_{decl}$ = Package of $C_{decl}$. - $P_{ref}$ = Package of $C_{ref}$. - $R$ = Reference (parent class reference vs. subclass reference). **Step 1 тАФ Same Class Check** $$\text{Step 1 Result} = \begin{cases} \text{ACCESS GRANTED} & \text{if } C_{ref} \equiv C_{decl} \\ \text{Proceed to Step 2} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ *Explanation*: If you are inside the class that owns the member, ALL access modifiers тАФ even `private` тАФ allow access. This is the strongest rule. **Step 2 тАФ Member Modifier Identification** $$\text{Modifier} \in \{ \texttt{public}, \texttt{protected}, \texttt{default}, \texttt{private} \}$$ *Explanation*: Read the modifier keyword used in the declaration. If no keyword is present, the modifier is `default` (package-private). **Step 3 тАФ `public` Short-Circuit** $$\text{If Modifier} = \texttt{public} \Rightarrow \text{ACCESS GRANTED}$$ *Explanation*: `public` is universal. No further checks are needed. **Step 4 тАФ Package & Inheritance Cross-Check** We define the boolean conditions: $$\text{SamePackage} = (P_{ref} \equiv P_{decl})$$ $$\text{IsSubclass} = (C_{ref} \text{ extends } C_{decl} \text{ OR } C_{ref} \text{ implements } C_{decl})$$ $$\text{RefIsSuperType} = (R \text{ is a reference of type } C_{decl} \text{ pointing to a } C_{ref} \text{ object})$$ Now the final decision: $$ \text{Access Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{GRANTED} & \text{if Modifier} = \texttt{default} \text{ AND SamePackage} \\ \text{GRANTED} & \text{if Modifier} = \texttt{protected} \text{ AND (SamePackage OR (IsSubclass AND NOT RefIsSuperType))} \\ \text{GRANTED} & \text{if Modifier} = \texttt{private} \text{ AND } C_{ref} \equiv C_{decl} \\ \text{DENIED} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ *Explanation*: The only subtle case is `protected` across packages тАФ it requires both inheritance AND a self-type reference (not a parent-type reference), enforcing the **"package-only OR inheritance-only"** dual nature of `protected`. ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 тАФ Default Access Across Packages ```java // File: package com.ktu.pkg1; package com.ktu.pkg1; class Account { // default class double balance; // default field void deposit() { } // default method } ``` ```java // File: package com.ktu.pkg2; package com.ktu.pkg2; import com.ktu.pkg1.Account; class Bank extends Account { void check() { // balance = 5000; // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR // deposit(); // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR } } ``` **Step-by-step solution (model answer for KTU 7-mark question):** 1. Class `Account` is declared without `public`, so it has **default** access. 2. Class `Bank` is in package `com.ktu.pkg2`, a **different package** from `Account`. 3. `Bank` extends `Account`, so it IS a subclass тАФ but the `balance` field and `deposit()` method have **default** visibility. 4. According to the access matrix in Section 2.3, **default** members are NOT accessible to subclasses in a different package. 5. Result: Compilation fails with *"balance is not public in Account; cannot be accessed from outside its package"*. ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 тАФ The `protected` Reference-Type Trap ```java // package com.ktu.parent; package com.ktu.parent; public class Vehicle { protected void start() { System.out.println("Vehicle started"); } } ``` ```java // package com.ktu.child; package com.ktu.child; import com.ktu.parent.Vehicle; public class Car extends Vehicle { void test() { start(); // тЬЕ Works Vehicle v = new Vehicle(); v.start(); // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR Car c = new Car(); c.start(); // тЬЕ Works } } ``` **Step-by-step model answer:** 1. Method `start()` is declared `protected` in `Vehicle` (package `com.ktu.parent`). 2. Class `Car` is in package `com.ktu.child` and **extends** `Vehicle`. 3. Calling `start()` directly inside `Car` works because Car inherits the method. 4. Calling `v.start()` (where `v` is a `Vehicle` reference) **FAILS** because `protected` cross-package access requires the reference to be of the **accessing class type** (`Car`), not the declaring class type (`Vehicle`). 5. Calling `c.start()` works because `c` is a `Car` reference. 6. This is the **most-asked KTU trick question** on access protection. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix тАФ When to Choose Each Modifier | Engineering Goal | Recommended Modifier | Justification | |-------------------------------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------------------------------------| | Hide internal helper logic | `private` | Maximum encapsulation; outside code cannot depend on it | | Group collaborating classes in same package | `default` | No leak outside the package; clean module boundary | | Allow subclass extension but block others | `protected` | Strict "subclass-only" extension contract | | Expose a public API / library method | `public` | Universal access required for the API surface | | Define constants for global use | `public static final`| Combination often seen with `public` | | Inner helper class used only by outer class | `private static` | Scoped entirely to the enclosing class | ## 3.5 Overriding Rule тАФ The Visibility Cannot Shrink Java enforces an **invariant** when overriding methods: $$ \text{Visibility}_{\text{override}} \geq \text{Visibility}_{\text{parent}} $$ Concretely: - `public` method тЖТ must be overridden as `public` (cannot be `protected`, `default`, or `private`). - `protected` method тЖТ can be overridden as `protected` or `public`. - `default` method тЖТ can be overridden as `default`, `protected`, or `public`. - `private` method тЖТ **cannot be overridden** at all (it is not inherited, so "hiding" is the actual mechanism, not overriding). > [!NOTE] > The compiler enforces this rule. A `public` method overridden as `private` will cause a **compile-time error**: *"attempting to assign weaker access privileges; was public"*. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram тАУ The Access Hierarchy Funnel The diagram below visualizes access protection as **concentric visibility rings**, where each ring is a strictly larger scope than the inner one. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["public
Universal Access
All Classes, All Packages"] B["protected
Extended Access
Same Package + Subclasses Anywhere"] C["default
Package-Private
Same Package Only"] D["private
Class-Private
Same Class Only"] D ==> C C ==> B B ==> A ``` **Interpretation of the flow:** - The **innermost** level is `private` тАФ the most restrictive. - Each `==>` arrow represents an **expansion of scope** by one level. - The **outermost** level is `public` тАФ fully unrestricted. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram тАУ Class-Level Access Architecture This diagram shows how two classes in **different packages** interact through the lens of access modifiers, mapping the *Engineer тЖТ Mechanic* real-world analogy. ```mermaid classDiagram class Engine { +int horsepower -int serialNumber #int torque ~int warrantyYears +startEngine() void -logDiagnostics() void #performSelfTest() void ~registerService() void } class Car { +Car(model String) +startCar() void +accessEngineMembers() void } Car --> Engine : references note for Engine "Symbols: + public, - private, # protected, ~ default" ``` **Symbol Legend (UML):** | Symbol | Java Modifier | Visibility Scope | |--------|---------------|--------------------------| | `+` | `public` | All | | `-` | `private` | Same class only | | `#` | `protected` | Package + subclasses | | `~` | `default` | Package only | ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram тАУ Cross-Package Access Decision Flow A decision-tree representation of the **Access Decision Algorithm** derived in Section 3.1. ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Attempt to access member M] check1{Same Class as
declaration?} check2{Modifier is
public?} check3{Same Package?} check4{Is subclass AND
ref is own type?} grant[ACCESS GRANTED] deny[COMPILE-TIME ERROR] start --> check1 check1 -- Yes --> grant check1 -- No --> check2 check2 -- Yes --> grant check2 -- No --> check3 check3 -- Yes --> grant check3 -- No --> check4 check4 -- Yes --> grant check4 -- No --> deny ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]` **Q: Explain the four access modifiers in Java with their visibility scope.** **Model Answer:** Java provides four levels of access control to enforce encapsulation and modular design. 1. **`private`** тАУ The member is accessible only within the class in which it is declared. It is the most restrictive level and is primarily used for data hiding. 2. **`default`** (package-private) тАУ When no access modifier is specified, the member is accessible to all classes within the same package, but not from classes in other packages. 3. **`protected`** тАУ The member is accessible within the same package and also by subclasses (even if the subclass is defined in a different package), provided the access is through the subclass's own type. 4. **`public`** тАУ The member is accessible from any class in any package, making it the least restrictive modifier. These four levels collectively allow the programmer to expose a precise interface and hide implementation details. *(3 marks тАФ 1 mark for naming the four modifiers, 1 mark for scope, 1 mark for encapsulation context.)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]` **Q: Can a top-level class in Java be declared `private` or `protected`? Justify your answer.** **Model Answer:** No. A top-level (outer) class in Java can be declared **only with `public` or default (package-private)** access. It cannot be declared `private` or `protected`. **Justification:** The Java Language Specification (JLS ┬з7.6) restricts top-level classes to only these two visibility levels because: - A top-level class represents the highest-level unit of compilation and packaging. Restricting it to `private` or `protected` would make it inaccessible to the JVM class loader, which always loads classes by their fully qualified name from a package. - `private` and `protected` are meaningful only in the context of containing/related classes (nested classes, subclasses, same-package classes), which is not applicable to a top-level class. However, **nested (inner) classes** can use all four modifiers because they have an enclosing class context. *(3 marks тАФ 1 for answer, 2 for justification.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam тАУ Model Paper 2024]` **Q: (a)** Explain the access control mechanism in Java with a neatly labeled access matrix table. Discuss how `protected` access differs when accessed from the same package versus from a subclass in a different package. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Explanation of Access Control Mechanism:** Access control in Java is a compile-time mechanism enforced by the Java compiler to restrict the visibility of classes, methods, and variables. It supports four levels of access defined by the Java Language Specification. The mechanism helps achieve: - **Encapsulation** тАУ Internal state is hidden from external interference. - **Modularity** тАУ Packages act as self-contained units. - **Secure extensibility** тАУ Subclasses can extend but not modify the parent's contract. **Access Matrix Table:** | Access Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Different Package) | Other Packages | |-----------------|:----------:|:------------:|:----------------------------:|:--------------:| | `public` | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | `protected` | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | | `default` | Yes | Yes | No | No | | `private` | Yes | No | No | No | **`protected` Access тАФ Same Package vs. Different-Package Subclass:** - **From the same package:** A `protected` member behaves exactly like a `default` member. Any class in the package тАФ subclass or not тАФ can access it freely. - **From a subclass in a different package:** The subclass can access the `protected` member, **but only through references of the subclass's own type** (or a further descendant's type). Accessing it through a reference of the parent class type (e.g., `Parent p = new Parent(); p.member;`) results in a **compile-time error**. This dual behaviour is a deliberate design to prevent "leakage" of `protected` internals to unrelated code while still allowing controlled inheritance. *(7 marks тАФ 3 for explanation, 2 for matrix, 2 for `protected` comparison.)* --- **(b)** Consider the following Java code and explain whether it compiles successfully. If not, identify the line numbers where errors occur and justify. **(7 marks)** ```java // File 1: package com.ktu.demo; package com.ktu.demo; public class Student { private int id = 101; protected String name = "Anu"; double cgpa = 8.5; public void display() { System.out.println(id + " " + name + " " + cgpa); } } ``` ```java // File 2: package com.ktu.test; package com.ktu.test; import com.ktu.demo.Student; public class Result extends Student { public void show() { // Line A System.out.println(id); // Line B System.out.println(name); // Line C System.out.println(cgpa); // Line D display(); } } ``` **Model Answer:** - **Line A** тАФ `id` is declared `private` in `Student`. Even though `Result` extends `Student`, `private` members are **not inherited** and are **not accessible** outside the `Student` class. **COMPILE ERROR: "id has private access in Student".** - **Line B** тАФ `name` is declared `protected`. `Result` is a subclass in a different package and is accessing `name` through inheritance (implicit `this` reference). **COMPILES SUCCESSFULLY.** - **Line C** тАФ `cgpa` is declared with **default** (package-private) access. Since `Result` is in `com.ktu.test` and `Student` is in `com.ktu.demo`, the default access is denied. **COMPILE ERROR: "cgpa is not public in Student; cannot be accessed from outside package".** - **Line D** тАФ `display()` is declared `public`, so it is universally accessible. **COMPILES SUCCESSFULLY.** **Summary Table:** | Line | Member | Modifier | Outcome | Reason | |------|--------|----------|------------|-----------------------------------| | A | `id` | private | тЭМ Error | Not inherited; class-private | | B | `name` | protected| тЬЕ OK | Subclass access via inheritance | | C | `cgpa` | default | тЭМ Error | Package-private, different package| | D | `display()` | public | тЬЕ OK | Universal access | *(7 marks тАФ 1 mark per line identification + justification.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam тАУ Model Paper 2024]` **Q: (a)** Differentiate between `public`, `private`, `protected`, and default access specifiers in Java. Provide a real-world analogy for each. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Modifier | Scope of Access | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Use Case | |------------|--------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------| | `public` | Accessible from any class in any package | A **public park** тАФ anyone in the city can enter | Library APIs, framework method contracts | | `protected`| Same package + subclasses in any package | A **family guest room** тАФ only relatives and your children can stay | Hook methods for subclass customization | | `default` | Accessible only within the same package | An **apartment complex** тАФ only residents of the building can use the gym | Internal helper classes within a module | | `private` | Accessible only within the declaring class | Your **personal diary** тАФ only you can read it | Data fields, internal state of an object | **Additional Explanation:** - `public` offers the **widest visibility** but increases coupling; overusing it can lead to fragile designs where internal changes break external code. - `protected` strikes a **balance** between encapsulation and extensibility тАФ it is the canonical choice for methods that subclasses are *expected* to override. - `default` (package-private) is **underused** by beginners, yet it is a powerful tool to mark classes/members as "implementation details of a package" that should not leak into the public API. - `private` enforces the **strongest encapsulation** and is the default choice for fields; access is mediated through public getter/setter methods. *(7 marks тАФ 2 marks for table, 3 marks for analogy, 2 marks for engineering use cases.)* --- **(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of all four access specifiers. Create a class `Employee` in package `com.ktu.company` with one member of each access type, and a class `HRManager` in a different package that extends `Employee`. From `HRManager`, demonstrate which members are accessible and which are not. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution Code:** ```java // File: com/ktu/company/Employee.java package com.ktu.company; public class Employee { public String empName = "Rahul"; // public protected int empId = 1001; // protected double salary = 50000.0; // default private String password = "secret123"; // private public void showPublic() { System.out.println("Public method called"); } protected void showProtected() { System.out.println("Protected method called"); } void showDefault() { System.out.println("Default method called"); } private void showPrivate() { System.out.println("Private method called"); } } ``` ```java // File: com/ktu/hr/HRManager.java package com.ktu.hr; import com.ktu.company.Employee; public class HRManager extends Employee { public void testAccess() { System.out.println(empName); // тЬЕ public System.out.println(empId); // тЬЕ protected (subclass) // System.out.println(salary); // тЭМ default тАУ different package // System.out.println(password); // тЭМ private тАУ not inherited showPublic(); // тЬЕ public showProtected(); // тЬЕ protected // showDefault(); // тЭМ default // showPrivate(); // тЭМ private } public static void main(String[] args) { HRManager mgr = new HRManager(); mgr.testAccess(); } } ``` **Expected Output:** ``` Rahul 1001 Public method called Protected method called ``` **Justification Table:** | Member | Modifier | Accessible in HRManager? | Reason | |------------------|------------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------------| | `empName` | `public` | тЬЕ Yes | Universal access | | `empId` | `protected`| тЬЕ Yes | Subclass in different package | | `salary` | `default` | тЭМ No | Package-private, different package | | `password` | `private` | тЭМ No | Not inherited outside declaring class | | `showPublic()` | `public` | тЬЕ Yes | Universal | | `showProtected()`| `protected`| тЬЕ Yes | Subclass access | | `showDefault()` | `default` | тЭМ No | Package boundary | | `showPrivate()` | `private` | тЭМ No | Class-private | *(7 marks тАФ 3 for code, 2 for output, 2 for justification table.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusing `default` with `public`:** Many students write code without an access modifier, assuming it is `public`. In Java, it is `default` (package-private). This is the **#1 reason for losing 2-3 marks** in KTU viva and ESE. > 2. **Forgetting the `protected` reference-type rule:** Writing `Parent p = new Parent(); p.protectedMethod();` in a subclass of a different package causes a compile error. Examiners specifically test this. > 3. **Overriding with reduced visibility:** A `public` method overridden as `private` is illegal. Students often lose 2 marks by not stating this rule. > 4. **Writing `private` on a top-level class:** This is a syntax error. Always write `public` or default for top-level classes. > 5. **Not importing the package correctly:** A `default` (package-private) class cannot even be **imported** by another package тАФ students sometimes confuse *import error* with *access error*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Access Protection (Module 2):** - Java has **exactly 4** access levels: `public`, `protected`, `default`, `private`. - **No keyword = `default` (package-private)**, not public. - `private` is the **most restrictive**; `public` is the **least restrictive**. - `private` members are **not inherited** by subclasses. - `default` members are accessible only within the **same package**. - `protected` = same package access + subclass access across packages. - `protected` access from a subclass in a different package requires the **reference to be of the subclass's own type** (not the parent's). - **Top-level classes** can only be `public` or default. `private` and `protected` are illegal. - **Nested/inner classes** can use all four modifiers. - **Overriding cannot reduce visibility** (e.g., public тЖТ private is illegal). - Access checks happen at **compile time**, not at runtime. - The **access matrix** (Section 2.3) is the single most important table for KTU questions. - `private` + getter/setter is the canonical JavaBean / encapsulation pattern. - `protected` is used for **template method / hook methods** in inheritance. - `default` is preferred over `public` for **package-internal helpers** to reduce coupling. - `public static final` is the standard way to declare **global constants** in Java. ---

topics.title: Importing Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Importing Packages # ЁЯУж Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Package (Java Definition):** > A *package* in Java is a **namespace** that organizes a set of related **classes**, **interfaces**, **enumerations**, and **sub-packages**. It acts as a **container** that provides **access protection** and **namespace management**, preventing class name collisions and enabling modular software architecture aligned with OOP encapsulation principles. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Importing Packages Definition:** > *Importing* a package is the mechanism by which a Java source file makes classes from another package **accessible by their simple (unqualified) name** without using their fully qualified class name (FQCN). It is declared using the `import` keyword and is a **compile-time directive** тАФ it does not embed any code, but simply tells the compiler where to look for class definitions. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "Office Building" Model Imagine a large **corporate office building** with many floors: | Real-World Object | Java Equivalent | Function | |---|---|---| | The building itself | Java Library (JDK) | The complete environment | | Floors (Floor 3, Floor 5) | Packages (`java.util`, `java.io`) | Logical grouping of related items | | Individual rooms on a floor | Classes inside the package | Specific reusable units | | Reception desk directory | `import` statement | Tells you *which room* you may visit directly | | Calling room "HR-201" directly (without directory) | **Fully Qualified Name** usage | Verbose but always works | | Asking "let me into ALL rooms on Floor 3" | `import java.util.*;` (wildcard) | Risky but convenient | > [!TIP] > **Why not just put everything in one giant folder?** Just as a real office would become chaotic if all 10,000 employees were in one room, Java programs need **packages** to keep classes organized, prevent **naming conflicts** (two classes both named `List`), and enforce **access control**. --- ## 1.3 The Two Flavors of Packages in Java 1. **Built-in Packages** тАФ Pre-shipped with the JDK (e.g., `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.net`, `java.sql`, `java.awt`). 2. **User-defined Packages** тАФ Created by programmers using the `package` keyword to organize their own project classes. > [!WARNING] > The `java.lang` package is **automatically imported** into every Java program. You never need to write `import java.lang.*;` тАФ that's why `String`, `System`, `Object`, and `Math` work without any import. --- ## 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics in Java - **Class Name Length Limit:** up to **255 characters** - **Package Name Recommended Convention:** all **lowercase** to avoid conflict with class names - **Implicit Imports:** exactly **1 package** (`java.lang.*`) is auto-imported - **Maximum Sub-package Depth:** practically **unlimited**, but **2тАУ4 levels** is industry standard (e.g., `com.ktu.cse.s3.oop`) # тЪЩя╕П Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Syntax Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Ways to Access a Class from Another Package Java provides **three distinct mechanisms** to use a class that resides in a different package. Every KTU examiner expects you to know all three. ### **Method 1 тАФ Specific Class Import (Recommended)** ```java import java.util.ArrayList; // imports ONLY ArrayList import java.util.Scanner; // imports ONLY Scanner ``` - тЬЕ **Best practice** тАФ improves readability - тЬЕ Avoids namespace pollution - тЬЕ Compiler knows exactly which class to resolve - тЭМ Can become verbose if many classes from the same package are needed ### **Method 2 тАФ Wildcard (Asterisk) Import** ```java import java.util.*; // imports ALL public classes of java.util ``` - тЬЕ Concise when many classes from the same package are needed - тЭМ **Does NOT import sub-packages** тАФ a very common KTU exam trap! - тЭМ Can cause **ambiguity** if two packages contain a class with the same name > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Fact:** `import java.util.*;` imports classes in `java.util` directly, but it does **NOT** import classes inside `java.util.jar`, `java.util.regex`, or `java.util.zip`. Sub-packages must always be imported **explicitly**. ### **Method 3 тАФ Fully Qualified Class Name (No Import)** ```java public class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) { java.util.ArrayList list = new java.util.ArrayList<>(); java.util.Scanner sc = new java.util.Scanner(System.in); } } ``` - тЬЕ **No `import` statement needed** at all - тЬЕ Useful when **two imported packages have classes with the same name** (resolves ambiguity) - тЭМ Makes code long and harder to read --- ## 2.2 Static Import (Advanced тАФ frequently asked in KTU Part A) > [!NOTE] > **Static Import** allows you to access **static members** (static variables and static methods) of a class **without class-name qualification**, by using `import static`. ```java import static java.lang.Math.sqrt; // imports only sqrt() import static java.lang.Math.*; // imports all static members of Math ``` ### Example Use-Case: ```java import static java.lang.Math.*; public class CircleArea { public static void main(String[] args) { double r = 5.0; double area = PI * pow(r, 2); // no need to write Math.PI or Math.pow System.out.println("Area = " + area); } } ``` > [!WARNING] > Overuse of static import **harms code readability** and is considered a **bad practice** in industry (Google Java Style Guide discourages it except for constants like `PI`). --- ## 2.3 KTU Syntax / Cheat-Sheet Table | # | Construct | Syntax | Scope | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Specific class import | `import pkg.ClassName;` | Single class | тнРтнРтнРтнРтнР | | 2 | Wildcard import | `import pkg.*;` | All public classes in *that* package only | тнРтнРтнРтнРтнР | | 3 | Sub-package import | `import pkg.subpkg.Class;` | One class in sub-package | тнРтнРтнРтнР | | 4 | Fully qualified usage | `pkg.ClassName obj = new pkg.ClassName();` | Inline, no import needed | тнРтнРтнРтнР | | 5 | Static member import | `import static pkg.Class.member;` | One static member | тнРтнРтнР | | 6 | Static wildcard import | `import static pkg.Class.*;` | All static members | тнРтнР | | 7 | Default auto-import | (none) | `java.lang.*` always available | тнРтнРтнРтнРтнР | | 8 | Package declaration | `package com.ktu.oop;` | First line of file | тнРтнРтнРтнРтнР | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Enterprise Java (Spring Boot, Jakarta EE):** Massive projects use packages like `com.company.project.service`, `com.company.project.repository` to organize **thousands of classes**. - **Android Development:** `android.app`, `android.widget`, `androidx.recyclerview` are packages imported into every Activity. - **Android Studio / IntelliJ IDEA** auto-generates `import` statements when you type a class name (you'll see greyed-out auto-imports). - **API Design:** The **Java Standard Library itself** is organized as packages тАФ this is what you import every day. --- ## 2.5 Naming Convention Rules (KTU Hot Topic) | Rule | Correct | Incorrect | |---|---|---| | Package names in **lowercase** | `com.ktu.cse` | `Com.KTU.Cse` | | Reverse domain notation | `in.ac.ktu.oop` | `oop.ktu.ac.in` | | No Java keywords | `com.example.if` тЭМ | `com.example.control` тЬЕ | | Underscores allowed but discouraged | `com.example.my_project` | (avoid) | # ЁЯзк Section 3: Step-by-Step Code Analysis & Resolution Walkthrough ## 3.1 Resolving a Class Reference тАФ What the Compiler Does Whenever the compiler sees an identifier that is not a local variable, it follows this **3-step resolution algorithm**: $$ \text{Resolution Order} = \begin{cases} \text{Step 1:} & \text{Local scope of the current block} \\ \text{Step 2:} & \text{Class members (fields \& methods)} \\ \text{Step 3:} & \text{Single-type-import declarations (explicit `import`)} \\ \text{Step 4:} & \text{Same-package classes (no import needed)} \\ \text{Step 5:} & \text{Import-on-demand (`import pkg.*`)} \\ \text{Step 6:} & \text{java.lang package (implicit)} \end{cases} $$ > This is the **JLS (Java Language Specification) ┬з6.4.1** order. KTU often asks: *"In what order does the compiler resolve a class name?"* --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 тАФ Ambiguity Resolution Suppose both `java.util` and `java.sql` contain a class named **`Date`**. ### Program 3.2.A тАФ Causes Compile-Time Error ```java import java.util.Date; import java.sql.Date; // тЭМ ERROR: Duplicate class 'Date' is already imported public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { Date d = new Date(); // Compiler is confused } } ``` **Resolution Walkthrough:** | Step | Compiler Action | Result | |---|---|---| | 1 | Sees `Date` in source code | Needs to resolve | | 2 | Checks single-type imports | `java.util.Date` and `java.sql.Date` both match | | 3 | Reports ambiguity error | **Compilation fails** | ### Program 3.2.B тАФ Correct Fix Using Fully Qualified Name ```java import java.util.Date; // okay тАФ only one specific import public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { java.sql.Date sqlDate = new java.sql.Date(0); // FQCN used inline java.util.Date utilDate = new java.util.Date(); // FQCN used inline System.out.println("SQL : " + sqlDate); System.out.println("Util : " + utilDate); } } ``` **Resolution Walkthrough:** | Step | Compiler Action | Result | |---|---|---| | 1 | Sees `java.sql.Date` | Exact FQCN тАФ no ambiguity | | 2 | Sees `java.util.Date` | Exact FQCN тАФ no ambiguity | | 3 | Program compiles | тЬЕ Output printed | --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 тАФ Sub-Package Trap ```java import java.util.*; // imports ArrayList, Scanner, HashMap, ... public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { java.util.regex.Pattern p = java.util.regex.Pattern.compile("a"); // тЬЕ works // Pattern p2 = Pattern.compile("a"); // тЭМ ERROR } } ``` **Why does the commented line fail?** The compiler performs the following trace: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Compiler trace for } \texttt{Pattern} & : \\ 1.\ \text{Check } \texttt{java.lang} & \rightarrow \text{no match} \\ 2.\ \text{Check } \texttt{java.util} \text{ (import-on-demand)} & \rightarrow \text{no class named } \texttt{Pattern} \\ 3.\ \text{Check } \texttt{java.util.regex} & \rightarrow \text{NOT imported} \\ 4.\ \text{Resolution fails} & \rightarrow \textbf{compile error} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Correct fix:** add an explicit import `import java.util.regex.Pattern;` or use the FQCN `java.util.regex.Pattern` inline. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 тАФ Setting Up a User-Defined Package **File 1: `MyMath.java` (saved in folder `com/ktu/util/`)** ```java package com.ktu.util; // тЖР first non-comment line public class MyMath { public static int square(int n) { return n * n; } public static int cube(int n) { return n * n * n; } } ``` **File 2: `Main.java` (saved in project root)** ```java import com.ktu.util.MyMath; // тЖР imports our own package public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Square of 5 = " + MyMath.square(5)); System.out.println("Cube of 3 = " + MyMath.cube(3)); } } ``` **Compilation sequence (from terminal):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1 (compile MyMath):} \quad & \texttt{javac -d . MyMath.java} \\ & \rightarrow \text{creates } \texttt{com/ktu/util/MyMath.class} \\[4pt] \text{Step 2 (compile Main):} \quad & \texttt{javac -d . Main.java} \\ & \rightarrow \text{creates } \texttt{Main.class} \\[4pt] \text{Step 3 (run):} \quad & \texttt{java Main} \\ & \rightarrow \text{prints } \texttt{Square of 5 = 25, Cube of 3 = 27} \end{aligned} $$ > The `-d .` flag tells the compiler where to **place generated `.class` files**, mirroring the package directory structure. --- ## 3.5 Worked Example 4 тАФ Static Import in Action ```java import static java.lang.System.out; // imports 'out' specifically import static java.lang.Math.PI; import static java.lang.Math.sqrt; public class StaticDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { double r = 9.0; out.println("Radius = " + r); // no System. prefix out.println("PI value = " + PI); // no Math. prefix out.println("sqrt(81) = " + sqrt(81)); // no Math. prefix } } ``` **Resolution trace for `out`:** | Step | Lookup | Match? | |---|---|---| | 1 | Local variable named `out`? | тЭМ | | 2 | Member of class `StaticDemo`? | тЭМ | | 3 | Single static import `java.lang.System.out`? | тЬЕ **FOUND** | This makes the output statements much shorter тАФ but as noted, readability suffers in large programs. # ЁЯЧ║я╕П Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Java Package Hierarchy тАФ Mermaid Tree ```mermaid graph TD A0["JDK - Java Development Kit"] A0 --> A1["java"] A0 --> A2["javax"] A0 --> A3["org"] A0 --> A4["com - user packages"] A1 --> B1["java.lang - auto imported"] A1 --> B2["java.util"] A1 --> B3["java.io"] A1 --> B4["java.net"] A1 --> B5["java.sql"] A1 --> B6["java.awt"] B1 --> C1["String"] B1 --> C2["System"] B1 --> C3["Object"] B1 --> C4["Math"] B2 --> D1["ArrayList"] B2 --> D2["Scanner"] B2 --> D3["HashMap"] B2 --> D4["LinkedList"] B2 --> E1["java.util.regex - sub package"] B2 --> E2["java.util.zip - sub package"] B2 --> E3["java.util.jar - sub package"] E1 --> F1["Pattern"] E1 --> F2["Matcher"] A2 --> G1["javax.swing"] A2 --> G2["javax.sql"] A3 --> H1["org.w3c.dom"] A4 --> I1["com.ktu.util - user defined"] I1 --> J1["MyMath class"] I1 --> J2["MyString class"] style A0 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style A1 fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style A4 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style B1 fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000 style B2 fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff style E1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff ``` > [!TIP] > The **red nodes** highlight the most commonly-tested KTU concept: **sub-packages are separate namespaces** and are *not* covered by `import java.util.*;`. --- ## 4.2 Decision Flow тАФ Choosing the Right Import Style ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["You need a class from another package"]) --> Q1{"Do you need only ONE
class from that package?"} Q1 -- YES --> S1["Use specific import:
import pkg.ClassName;"] Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"Do you need MANY classes
from the SAME package?"} Q2 -- YES --> Q2a{"Is the class name unique
or does it clash
with another package?"} Q2a -- UNIQUE --> S2["Use wildcard import:
import pkg.asterisk;"] Q2a -- CLASH --> S3["Use Fully Qualified Name inline:
pkg.ClassName obj = new pkg.ClassName();"] Q2 -- NO --> S3 S1 --> End(["Write your program"]) S2 --> End S3 --> End style Start fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style End fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style S1 fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style S2 fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff style S3 fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Compile-Time Class Resolution Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Compiler sees a class name in source"] --> B["1. Check current block scope"] B --> C{"Found?"} C -- YES --> Z["Use it - DONE"] C -- NO --> D["2. Check current class members"] D --> E{"Found?"} E -- YES --> Z E -- NO --> F["3. Check single type imports
import pkg.ClassName"] F --> G{"Exactly ONE match?"} G -- YES --> Z G -- MULTIPLE --> ERR1["COMPILE ERROR - ambiguous import"] G -- NONE --> H["4. Check same package classes
no import needed"] H --> I{"Found?"} I -- YES --> Z I -- NO --> J["5. Check import on demand
import pkg.asterisk"] J --> K{"Found?"} K -- YES --> Z K -- NO --> L["6. Check java.lang - auto imported"] L --> M{"Found?"} M -- YES --> Z M -- NO --> ERR2["COMPILE ERROR - cannot find symbol"] style A fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style Z fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style ERR1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style ERR2 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.4 Static vs Regular Import тАФ Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Regular["REGULAR IMPORT"] R1["import java.util.ArrayList;"] R2["ArrayList is a CLASS"] R3["Lets you use class name
WITHOUT package prefix"] R1 --> R2 --> R3 end subgraph Static["STATIC IMPORT"] S1["import static java.lang.Math.PI;"] S2["PI is a STATIC FIELD"] S3["Lets you use field name
WITHOUT class name prefix"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 end style Regular fill:#dbeafe,color:#000000 style Static fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000 ``` # ЁЯУЭ Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]** *Explain the different ways to import a package in Java. Give one example for each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Java provides **three ways** to access classes from another package: 1. **Specific class import** тАФ imports a single class by name. ```java import java.util.Scanner; ``` 2. **Wildcard import** тАФ imports all public classes of a package. ```java import java.util.*; ``` 3. **Fully qualified class name** тАФ uses the class by its complete path, no import needed. ```java java.util.Scanner sc = new java.util.Scanner(System.in); ``` > *(1 mark for naming the three ways + 1 mark for syntax + 1 mark for explanation/usage scenario)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]** *What is a static import in Java?
