State its advantage and one disadvantage.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **static import** is a feature introduced in Java 5 that allows members (fields/methods) declared as `static` inside a class to be referenced **without qualifying them with the class name**. It is declared as: ```java import static java.lang.Math.PI; import static java.lang.Math.sqrt; ``` - **Advantage:** Code becomes more concise тАФ `sqrt(16)` instead of `Math.sqrt(16)`. (1 mark) - **Disadvantage:** Reduces readability and can cause **name conflicts** when two static-imported classes have members of the same name. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B тАФ Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### тнР **Question A тАФ [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023 Model]** **Q. (a)** *Explain the concept of packages in Java. What are the advantages of using packages?* **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Definition (2 marks):** A package in Java is a mechanism for **grouping related classes, interfaces, and sub-packages** into a single namespace. It is created using the `package` keyword as the first statement in a source file. ```java package com.ktu.cse.s3; ``` **Types of packages (2 marks):** | Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | Built-in | Pre-defined in JDK | `java.util`, `java.io` | | User-defined | Created by the programmer | `com.ktu.util` | **Advantages (3 marks):** 1. **Namespace management** тАФ prevents name clashes (two classes can be named `List` in different packages). 2. **Access protection** тАФ default and protected access modifiers can restrict visibility across packages. 3. **Reusability & modularity** тАФ classes can be reused across projects; logical grouping improves maintainability. 4. **Easier distribution** тАФ packaged JAR/WAR files bundle related classes for deployment. --- **Q. (b)** *With suitable examples, explain the different ways of importing packages in Java. Also discuss what happens when two imported packages contain a class with the same name.* **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Three ways of importing (4 marks):** 1. **Specific import** ```java import java.util.ArrayList; ``` 2. **Wildcard import** ```java import java.util.*; ``` 3. **Fully qualified name (no import)** ```java java.util.ArrayList list = new java.util.ArrayList<>(); ``` **Handling name clashes (3 marks):** When two packages contain a class with the same name (e.g., `java.util.Date` and `java.sql.Date`), the compiler throws an **ambiguity error** if both are wildcard-imported or even if both are specific-imported. **Resolution strategy:** Use a **fully qualified name** for the ambiguous class: ```java import java.util.Date; // okay, single import public class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) { java.sql.Date sqlD = new java.sql.Date(0); // FQCN disambiguates System.out.println(sqlD); } } ``` --- ### тнР **Question B тАФ [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024 Model тАФ Alternative Choice]** **Q. (a)** *What is a Java package? Explain built-in and user-defined packages with examples. How are packages created and accessed?* **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Package definition (1 mark):** A package is a folder-like container that organizes a set of related Java types. **Built-in packages (3 marks):** | Package | Key Classes | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `java.lang` | `String`, `Math`, `Object` | Core language (auto-imported) | | `java.util` | `ArrayList`, `HashMap`, `Scanner` | Data structures & utilities | | `java.io` | `File`, `FileReader` | Input/Output streams | | `java.net` | `Socket`, `URL` | Networking | **User-defined packages (3 marks):** Created by writing the `package` keyword at the top of a file: ```java package com.ktu.cse.util; public class Calculator { public int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } } ``` The file is saved in a folder path matching the package name (`com/ktu/cse/util/Calculator.java`) and compiled with `javac -d . Calculator.java`. --- **Q. (b)** *Explain static import in Java with an example. Mention two situations where static import is useful and one situation where it should be avoided.* **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Definition (2 marks):** Static import (Java 5+) allows access to **static members** of a class without writing the class name. Syntax: ```java import static java.lang.Math.PI; import static java.lang.Math.sqrt; ``` **Example (2 marks):** ```java import static java.lang.Math.*; public class CircleCalc { public static void main(String[] args) { double r = 7.0; double area = PI * r * r; // no Math. prefix System.out.println("sqrt(49) = " + sqrt(49)); } } ``` **Useful situations (2 marks):** 1. Frequent use of mathematical constants (`PI`, `E`) and functions (`pow`, `sqrt`). 2. Writing test code that calls many `assertEquals`, `assertTrue` from JUnit. **When to avoid (1 mark):** Avoid when the unqualified name can be confused with a local variable or a method in the same class тАФ it **harms readability** in large production code. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places KTU students LOSE marks:** > 1. **Stating that `import java.util.*;` imports sub-packages** тАФ this is **FALSE** and costs 1тАУ2 marks almost every exam. Sub-packages must be imported explicitly (`import java.util.regex.Pattern;`). > 2. **Forgetting that `java.lang` is auto-imported** тАФ when asked *"which package does String belong to?"*, students sometimes write `import java.lang.String;` and lose a mark for redundant import. > 3. **Writing `import` after `package` declaration** тАФ the legal order is: `package` тЖТ `import` тЖТ `class`. Reverse order causes compile error. > 4. **Confusing static import with regular import** тАФ static import applies to **members**, regular import applies to **classes**. > 5. **Not mentioning CLASSPATH** in user-defined package questions тАФ 1 mark reserved for "How does the JVM locate your package?" тЖТ answer involves `CLASSPATH` environment variable or `-cp` flag. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ЁЯЪА - тЬЕ A **package** = namespace + access-control boundary + modular grouping. - тЬЕ **Three import styles:** specific class, wildcard (`*`), fully qualified name. - тЬЕ `import` is a **compile-time directive** тАФ no runtime cost, no code is copied. - тЬЕ `java.lang.*` is **auto-imported** in every Java file. - тЬЕ **Wildcard import does NOT import sub-packages** тАФ biggest KTU trap. - тЬЕ **Static import** = for static members only; use sparingly. - тЬЕ **Naming convention:** lowercase, reverse-domain (e.g., `in.ac.ktu.oop`). - тЬЕ **Compilation command:** `javac -d . FileName.java` for user-defined packages. - тЬЕ **Legal order in source file:** `package` тЖТ `import` тЖТ `class`/`interface`. - тЬЕ To resolve **name clashes**, prefer **fully qualified name** inline rather than removing one import entirely. - тЬЕ **CLASSPATH** (or `-cp` flag) tells JVM where to find user-defined `.class` files. - тЬЕ KTU loves asking: *"Differentiate between `import pkg.Class;` and `import pkg.*;`"* тАФ memorize the trade-off table from ┬з2.3.

topics.title: Interfaces
Exception Handling - Checked Exceptions
Unchecked Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unchecked Exceptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unchecked Exceptions** in Java are exceptions that are **not verified by the Java compiler at compile-time**. They are subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException` (directly or indirectly), and therefore the compiler does not enforce the programmer to either catch them using a `try-catch` block or declare them in the method signature using the `throws` clause.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this topic falls under **Module 3 тАУ Exception Handling**, where students are expected to understand the **Exception Class Hierarchy**, the distinction between **Checked vs Unchecked** exceptions, and the role of the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** in runtime exception propagation.

The Java Language Specification formally classifies any exception that is *not* a checked exception as an unchecked exception. This includes:

1. All subclasses of `RuntimeException`.
2. All errors (subclasses of `java.lang.Error`, e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically requires students to identify, demonstrate, and handle common unchecked exceptions such as `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `ClassCastException`, `NumberFormatException`, and `IllegalArgumentException`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. The traffic police (the **compiler**) check certain mandatory things *before* you start the journey тАФ a valid driving license, registration certificate, insurance (these are **Checked Exceptions** тАФ the police *force* you to have them before letting you start).

However, there are some issues that the police **cannot foresee** at the starting point тАФ a sudden tyre puncture, the car running out of fuel mid-way, a bird suddenly hitting your windshield. These issues are detected **only while the car is moving** (during **runtime**). You may or may not have a spare tyre, but the police did not check for it.

These mid-journey surprises are **Unchecked Exceptions** тАФ the **JVM** detects them while your program is executing, and they typically indicate **programming logic errors** rather than predictable external conditions.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** Unchecked exceptions usually represent **bugs in the program logic** (e.g., dividing by zero, accessing a null reference, accessing an invalid array index). The compiler trusts the programmer to write logically correct code; when logic fails, the JVM steps in to flag the error.

## 1.3 Position in the Java Exception Hierarchy

$$
\text{java.lang.Throwable} \;\Big/ \begin{cases} \text{java.lang.Error} \quad \text{(Unchecked тАУ JVM-level fatal issues)} \\ \text{java.lang.Exception} \;\Big/ \begin{cases} \text{IOException, SQLException, ...} \quad \text{(Checked)} \\ \text{RuntimeException} \quad \text{(Unchecked тАУ base class)} \end{cases} \end{cases}
$$

Every unchecked exception (except `Error` subclasses) is ultimately a descendant of `RuntimeException`.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Categories

| Property | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception |
|---|---|---|
| **Base Class** | `java.lang.Exception` (but not `RuntimeException`) | `java.lang.RuntimeException` |
| **Compile-Time Check** | **Enforced** by compiler | **Not enforced** by compiler |
| **Detection Time** | Compile-time | Run-time (by JVM) |
| **Typical Cause** | External resources (file, network, DB) | Program logic errors |
| **Handling Mandate** | Mandatory `try-catch` or `throws` | Optional (best practice to handle) |
| **Recovery Possibility** | Usually recoverable | Usually indicates a bug |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Unchecked Exceptions

The Java designers introduced the concept of unchecked exceptions with three primary motivations, all of which are **high-yield** for KTU theory questions:

1. **Reducing Boilerplate Code:** If every possible runtime condition (like division by zero) had to be declared, the language would become excessively verbose. Unchecked exceptions free the programmer from declaring predictable logic errors.

2. **Representing Programming Bugs:** Unchecked exceptions conventionally indicate that the program is doing something *fundamentally wrong* (e.g., dereferencing `null`, indexing out of bounds). These are conditions that *should not occur* in a correctly written program.

3. **The "Liskov Substitution Principle" Argument:** Many API methods cannot meaningfully declare every exceptional condition. For example, `Object.clone()` cannot reasonably be required to declare every possible `RuntimeException` a subclass might throw.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Mechanism of an Unchecked Exception

When an unchecked exception is thrown, the following sequence occurs in the JVM:

- **Step 1 тАФ Detection:** The JVM's bytecode interpreter/execution engine detects an invalid operation (e.g., integer division where the divisor is 0).
- **Step 2 тАФ Instantiation:** The JVM creates a new instance of the corresponding exception class (e.g., `new ArithmeticException("/ by zero")`).
- **Step 3 тАФ Stack Trace Capture:** The JVM walks back the call stack, capturing each stack frame.
- **Step 4 тАФ Unwinding the Stack:** The JVM begins unwinding the call stack, frame by frame, until it finds a matching `catch` block.
- **Step 5 тАФ Propagation:** If no matching handler is found, the exception propagates to the `main` method, and then to the default uncaught exception handler, terminating the thread.
- **Step 6 тАФ Program Termination:** The default handler prints the stack trace to `System.err` and terminates the program (non-zero exit code).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Even though unchecked exceptions are *not enforced* at compile-time, the JVM still performs full stack-trace generation and unwinding. The compile-time check only relates to whether the *programmer* must declare or catch the exception тАФ the runtime behavior is identical to checked exceptions.

## 2.3 Common Unchecked Exceptions тАФ KTU Cheat Sheet

| Exception Class | Trigger Condition | Typical Cause in Code |
|---|---|---|
| `ArithmeticException` | Division by zero (integers) | `int x = 10 / 0;` |
| `NullPointerException` | Dereferencing a `null` object reference | `String s = null; s.length();` |
| `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Array index $\lt 0$ or $\geq$ length | `arr[10]` when `arr.length == 5` |
| `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Invalid index on a `String` | `"hello".charAt(10)` |
| `IndexOutOfBoundsException` | Invalid index on Lists, etc. | `list.get(100)` on a 5-element list |
| `ClassCastException` | Invalid type cast | `Object o = "Hi"; Integer i = (Integer) o;` |
| `NumberFormatException` | Invalid string-to-number parsing | `Integer.parseInt("abc");` |
| `IllegalArgumentException` | Method received an illegal argument | `Thread.sleep(-1);` |
| `IllegalStateException` | Method invoked at illegal time/state | `Iterator.remove()` before `next()` |
| `UnsupportedOperationException` | Operation not supported | Modifying an unmodifiable collection |
| `NegativeArraySizeException` | Array created with negative size | `new int[-5];` |
| `SecurityException` | Security manager denies operation | `System.exit()` in an applet |
| `NoSuchElementException` | Element requested but not present | `Iterator.next()` with no more elements |
| `ConcurrentModificationException` | Collection modified during iteration | Modifying a list inside a `for-each` loop |
| `StackOverflowError` (Error) | Recursion too deep | Infinite recursive calls |

> [!NOTE]
> The subscript markers in formulas must never use the **vertical pipe `|`** character. For set-builder or conditional notation, use `\vert` (e.g., $i \lt 0 \;\vert\; i \geq n$).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Throwable Hierarchy)

$$
\text{Throwable} \;\Big/ \begin{aligned}[t] &\text{Error} \;\Big/ \; \begin{aligned}[t] &\text{VirtualMachineError, OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError} \end{aligned} \\ &\text{Exception} \;\Big/ \; \begin{aligned}[t] &\text{IOException (Checked)} \\ &\text{SQLException (Checked)} \\ &\text{ClassNotFoundException (Checked)} \\ &\text{RuntimeException (Unchecked)} \;\Big/ \; \begin{aligned}[t] &\text{ArithmeticException} \\ &\text{NullPointerException} \\ &\text{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException} \\ &\text{ClassCastException} \\ &\text{NumberFormatException} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}
$$

## 2.5 Boundary Conditions for Common Triggers

Let $a, b \in \mathbb{Z}$ be integers and $n \in \mathbb{N}$ be a natural number (array length):

$$
\text{ArithmeticException triggered} \iff a \neq 0 \;\land\; b = 0 \quad \text{when computing} \quad a \div b
$$

$$
\text{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException triggered} \iff i \lt 0 \;\vert\; i \geq n \quad \text{when accessing} \quad \text{arr}[i]
$$

$$
\text{NullPointerException triggered} \iff \text{obj} == \texttt{null} \quad \text{when invoking} \quad \text{obj.method()}
$$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real-world engineering, unchecked exceptions are used in:

- **Web Frameworks (Spring, Jakarta EE):** Controllers throw `IllegalArgumentException` for invalid REST payloads; the framework converts them to **HTTP 400 Bad Request** responses.
- **Financial Systems:** `NumberFormatException` is caught during CSV parsing to skip malformed rows without halting the entire batch.
- **Embedded Systems:** `StackOverflowError` detection in recursive hardware-software co-design helps prevent device lockups.
- **Game Development:** `IndexOutOfBoundsException` is a frequent cause of crashes; defensive programming with bounds checks is a standard practice.
- **API Design:** Library designers mark conditions that are "client errors" as `IllegalArgumentException` / `IllegalStateException` rather than checked exceptions, to keep client code clean.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: How the JVM Detects an Unchecked Exception

We will trace, step-by-step, what happens when the JVM encounters an `ArithmeticException` in a Java program. This analytical trace is a **frequently asked KTU theory question**.

Consider the following Java code:

```java
public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int result = compute(10, 0);
        System.out.println(result);
    }
    public static int compute(int a, int b) {
        return a / b;
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1 тАФ Bytecode Execution Initiation**
The JVM invokes the `main` method, which in turn calls `compute(10, 0)`. The integer division bytecode (`idiv`) is reached.

**Step 2 тАФ Pre-Division Check**
The JVM checks the divisor operand before performing the `idiv` operation:

$$
\text{if} \quad b = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{trigger} \quad \text{ArithmeticException} \;\; (\text{"/ by zero"})
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Exception Object Instantiation**
The JVM allocates a new `ArithmeticException` object on the heap:

$$
\text{new ArithmeticException}(" \; / \; \text{by zero} \;")
$$

**Step 4 тАФ Stack Trace Construction**
The JVM walks the call stack from the current frame upward:

$$
\text{Stack: } \bigl[\texttt{compute(10, 0)} \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{main(String[])} \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{<JVM startup>} \bigr]
$$

**Step 5 тАФ Search for Handler**
The JVM searches each frame from innermost to outermost for a `catch` clause matching `ArithmeticException` or any of its supertypes. Since none exists, propagation continues to the `main` method.

**Step 6 тАФ Uncaught Exception Handler**
The default uncaught exception handler executes:

$$
\text{System.err.println(} \; \text{e.printStackTrace()} \; \text{)}
$$

**Step 7 тАФ Thread Termination**
The `main` thread terminates abnormally with a non-zero exit code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note on `double` and `float`:** Division by zero with floating-point types does **not** throw `ArithmeticException`. It produces `Infinity` (for `1.0/0.0`) or `NaN` (for `0.0/0.0`), per the **IEEE 754** floating-point standard. This is a classic KTU trick question.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Exception Type vs. Trigger Condition

Let us define the *trigger domain* $\mathcal{D}_{exc}$ for each exception:

$$
\mathcal{D}_{\text{Arith}} = \bigl\{ (a, b) \in \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z} \;\big\vert\; b = 0 \bigr\}
$$

$$
\mathcal{D}_{\text{NPE}} = \bigl\{ \text{obj} \in \text{JavaRef} \;\big\vert\; \text{obj} == \texttt{null} \bigr\}
$$

$$
\mathcal{D}_{\text{AIOOBE}} = \bigl\{ (i, n) \in \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{N} \;\big\vert\; i \lt 0 \;\vert\; i \geq n \bigr\}
$$

$$
\mathcal{D}_{\text{CCE}} = \bigl\{ (o, T) \in \text{Object} \times \text{Class} \;\big\vert\; \neg (o \; \text{isinstanceof} \; T) \bigr\}
$$

$$
\mathcal{D}_{\text{NFE}} = \bigl\{ s \in \text{String} \;\big\vert\; s \not\in \text{Regex}( \text{IntegerLiteral} ) \bigr\}
$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Demonstration: The Five Mandatory Unchecked Exceptions

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists **five mandatory unchecked exceptions** for Module 3. Below is the exhaustive demonstration matrix.

### 3.3.1 `ArithmeticException`

```java
public class ArithDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int result = 10 / 0;        // Line A
            System.out.println(result);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:** `Caught: / by zero`

**Trace:** The `idiv` bytecode at Line A triggers the JVM check. Since $b = 0$, condition $\mathcal{D}_{\text{Arith}}$ is satisfied, and an `ArithmeticException` is thrown.

### 3.3.2 `NullPointerException`

```java
public class NullDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String s = null;
        try {
            int len = s.length();        // Line B
            System.out.println(len);
        } catch (NullPointerException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught NPE: object is null");
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:** `Caught NPE: object is null`

**Trace:** At Line B, the JVM attempts to read the field `length` of the reference `s`. Since $s == \texttt{null}$, condition $\mathcal{D}_{\text{NPE}}$ is satisfied.

### 3.3.3 `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`

```java
public class ArrayDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int[] arr = new int[5];
        try {
            arr[10] = 99;                // Line C
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught AIOOBE: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:** `Caught AIOOBE: Index 10 out of bounds for length 5`

**Trace:** Here $i = 10$ and $n = 5$. The condition $i \geq n$ is satisfied, triggering $\mathcal{D}_{\text{AIOOBE}}$.

### 3.3.4 `ClassCastException`

```java
public class CastDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Object o = "Hello";
        try {
            Integer i = (Integer) o;     // Line D
        } catch (ClassCastException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught CCE: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:** `Caught CCE: Cannot cast String to Integer`

**Trace:** At Line D, the runtime type check fails. `o` is `String`, but the cast demands `Integer`. Condition $\mathcal{D}_{\text{CCE}}$ is satisfied.

### 3.3.5 `NumberFormatException`

```java
public class NFDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int n = Integer.parseInt("abc");   // Line E
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught NFE: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:** `Caught NFE: For input string: "abc"`

**Trace:** The string `"abc"` does not match the regex pattern for integer literals. Condition $\mathcal{D}_{\text{NFE}}$ is satisfied.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Table

| Step | Phase | Activity | Output / Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Trigger | Invalid bytecode operation | Exception object created |
| 2 | Snapshot | Stack trace captured | Frames stored |
| 3 | Search | Frame-by-frame handler search | Matching `catch` found or not |
| 4 | Unwind | Stack frames popped | Control transferred |
| 5 | Handle | `catch` block executes | Recovery attempt |
| 6 | Cleanup | `finally` block executes (if any) | Resources released |
| 7 | Propagate | If unhandled, thread dies | `printStackTrace()` printed |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Java Exception Class Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[java.lang.Throwable] --> B[java.lang.Error]
    A --> C[java.lang.Exception]
    B --> B1[VirtualMachineError]
    B --> B2[OutOfMemoryError]
    B --> B3[StackOverflowError]
    C --> C1[IOException CHECKED]
    C --> C2[SQLException CHECKED]
    C --> C3[ClassNotFoundException CHECKED]
    C --> D[java.lang.RuntimeException UNCHECKED]
    D --> D1[ArithmeticException]
    D --> D2[NullPointerException]
    D --> D3[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    D --> D4[ClassCastException]
    D --> D5[NumberFormatException]
    D --> D6[IllegalArgumentException]
    D --> D7[IllegalStateException]
    D --> D8[UnsupportedOperationException]
    D --> D9[IndexOutOfBoundsException]
    D9 --> D3
    D9 --> D10[StringIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    D --> D11[ConcurrentModificationException]
    D --> D12[NoSuchElementException]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: JVM Unchecked Exception Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Program Execution]) --> EXEC[Execute Bytecode Instruction]
    EXEC --> CHECK{Invalid Operation Detected}
    CHECK -- No --> NEXT[Continue Execution]
    CHECK -- Yes --> INST[Instantiate RuntimeException Object]
    INST --> TRACE[Capture Stack Trace]
    TRACE --> SEARCH[Search Call Stack for Matching Catch]
    SEARCH --> FOUND{Matching Handler Found}
    FOUND -- Yes --> CATCH[Transfer Control to Catch Block]
    FOUND -- No --> UNCAUGHT[Default Uncaught Exception Handler]
    CATCH --> FINALLY{Finally Block Present}
    FINALLY -- Yes --> CLEAN[Execute Cleanup Code]
    FINALLY -- No --> RESUME[Resume Program After Catch]
    CLEAN --> RESUME
    UNCAUGHT --> PRINT[Print Stack Trace to stderr]
    PRINT --> DIE[Terminate Thread Non-Zero Exit]
    RESUME --> NEXT
    NEXT --> STOP([Program Ends Normally])
    DIE --> STOP2([Program Ends Abnormally])
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix Diagram: Checked vs Unchecked at Compile-Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Method Throws Exception] --> Q{Is it a subclass of RuntimeException?}
    Q -- Yes --> UN[Unchecked: No compile-time mandate]
    Q -- No --> CHK[Checked: Compiler enforces try-catch or throws]
    UN --> OPT{Programmer Choice}
    OPT -- Handles --> HC[Catch Block]
    OPT -- Ignores --> HJ[Uncaught Propagates to JVM]
    CHK --> MAN[Mandatory Handling Required]
    MAN --> CATCH2[try-catch Block]
    MAN --> THROWS2[throws Clause in Signature]
```

## 4.4 Trigger Condition Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Unchecked Exception Triggers))
    Arithmetic
      Integer division by zero
      Modulo by zero
    Null
      Invoking method on null
      Accessing field on null
      Array length on null array
    Index
      Array negative index
      Array index >= length
      String charAt out of range
    Type
      Cast object to incompatible type
    Format
      parseInt on non numeric
      valueOf on invalid string
    Argument
      Thread sleep with negative
      setPriority with invalid value
    State
      Iterator remove before next
      Enumeration beyond bounds
```

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]`
**Define unchecked exceptions in Java. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Unchecked exceptions are exceptions that are not checked at compile-time by the Java compiler. They are subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException`. The compiler does not force the programmer to catch them or declare them using the `throws` clause. They are typically caused by programming logic errors and occur at runtime.

**Examples:**
1. `ArithmeticException` тАФ thrown when an integer is divided by zero (e.g., `10/0`).
2. `NullPointerException` тАФ thrown when an application attempts to use a `null` object reference (e.g., invoking a method on a `null` object).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]`
**Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception |
|---|---|---|
| Base class | `java.lang.Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | `java.lang.RuntimeException` |
| Compile-time check | Enforced by the compiler | Not enforced |
| Detection | Mostly at compile-time | At run-time by the JVM |
| Cause | External resources (I/O, DB) | Program logic errors |
| Example | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NPE` |
| Handling | Mandatory `try-catch` or `throws` | Optional but recommended |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of unchecked exceptions in Java. List and demonstrate with suitable Java code snippets **any three** common unchecked exceptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that reads an array of `n` integers from the user and performs the following operations: divide the element at index `i` by the element at index `i+1`, handling all possible unchecked exceptions using a single `try-catch` block with multiple `catch` clauses. Demonstrate the output for at least three test cases. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

Unchecked exceptions are a category of exceptions in Java that descend from `java.lang.RuntimeException`. Unlike checked exceptions, they are not validated at compile-time, meaning the compiler does not mandate the programmer to handle them in `try-catch` blocks or declare them with the `throws` keyword. They typically represent programming errors that occur at runtime, such as logic mistakes, invalid type casts, or arithmetic overflows.

**Three common unchecked exceptions with code demonstrations:**

**1. `ArithmeticException`** тАФ Triggered on integer division by zero:

```java
public class Arith {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = 10, b = 0;
        try {
            System.out.println(a / b);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Cannot divide by zero: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**2. `NullPointerException`** тАФ Triggered on null reference dereference:

```java
public class NullEx {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String s = null;
        try {
            System.out.println(s.length());
        } catch (NullPointerException e) {
            System.out.println("Object is null: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**3. `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`** тАФ Triggered on invalid array indexing:

```java
public class ArrEx {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int[] arr = {1, 2, 3};
        try {
            System.out.println(arr[5]);
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Invalid index: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

#### Model Solution for (b)

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class DivideArray {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        try {
            System.out.print("Enter size n: ");
            int n = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine());

            int[] arr = new int[n];
            System.out.println("Enter " + n + " integers:");
            for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
                arr[i] = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine());
            }

            for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
                int result = arr[i] / arr[i + 1];
                System.out.println(arr[i] + " / " + arr[i + 1] + " = " + result);
            }
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Arithmetic error: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Index error: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Format error: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (NegativeArraySizeException e) {
            System.out.println("Size error: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            sc.close();
            System.out.println("Program terminated cleanly.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Test Case Outputs:**

| Test Case | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `n=3`, values `10 0 5` | `Arithmetic error: / by zero` |
| 2 | `n=2`, values `5 abc` | `Format error: For input string: "abc"` |
| 3 | `n=-2` | `Size error: Negative array size` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When writing multi-catch blocks, students often place the **parent class exception before the child class exception**, causing a *compile-time error: "exception has already been caught"*. Always order the `catch` clauses from **most specific to most general** (e.g., `ArithmeticException` before `RuntimeException` before `Exception`). Marks are commonly deducted for incorrect ordering.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the Java exception class hierarchy. Clearly mark the position of unchecked exceptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that accepts a string from the user and tries to convert it to an integer. If the conversion fails, the program should catch the `NumberFormatException` and prompt the user to re-enter. The program should also catch `NullPointerException` if the user enters an empty string. Demonstrate with sample output. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

The Java exception hierarchy originates from the root class `java.lang.Throwable`, which has two direct subclasses: `java.lang.Error` and `java.lang.Exception`. The class `Error` represents catastrophic failures that are typically not recoverable by the application (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`). The class `Exception` represents conditions that an application might want to catch. It is further divided into two major categories: **Checked Exceptions** (e.g., `IOException`, `SQLException`) and **Unchecked Exceptions** (subclasses of `RuntimeException`).

```
                       java.lang.Throwable
                       /               \
              java.lang.Error      java.lang.Exception
              /        \            /              \
       VirtualMachineError  OutOfMemoryError   IOException (Checked)
                                          \         SQLException (Checked)
                                           \                |
                                  java.lang.RuntimeException (Unchecked)
                                  /        |        |         \
                  ArithmeticException  NullPointer  ClassCast  NumberFormat
                                     Exception   Exception   Exception
```

Unchecked exceptions sit under `java.lang.RuntimeException` and include all the runtime logic errors. The JVM throws them at runtime when it detects a violation such as division by zero, null dereference, or invalid type casting. The compiler does not require them to be caught or declared.

#### Model Solution for (b)

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class ParseInteger {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        String input = null;
        int number = 0;
        boolean valid = false;

        while (!valid) {
            try {
                System.out.print("Enter a number: ");
                input = sc.nextLine();

                if (input == null || input.trim().isEmpty()) {
                    input = null;
                    throw new NullPointerException("Input string is empty");
                }

                number = Integer.parseInt(input.trim());
                valid = true;
                System.out.println("You entered: " + number);
            } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
                System.out.println("Invalid number format. Please try again.");
            } catch (NullPointerException e) {
                System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage() + ". Please try again.");
            }
        }
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Output:**

| Iteration | User Input | System Response |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `hello` | `Invalid number format. Please try again.` |
| 2 | (empty) | `Error: Input string is empty. Please try again.` |
| 3 | `42` | `You entered: 42` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):** In part (b), students often forget that `sc.nextLine()` can return an **empty string but never `null`** in standard console input. Explicitly demonstrating that an empty string triggers the `NullPointerException` (via manual nullification) is a common KTU board expectation. Failing to justify *why* `NullPointerException` can occur in this context leads to loss of 1тАУ2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Unchecked Exceptions**

- **Definition:** Unchecked exceptions are subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException` (and `Error`); they are **not** checked at compile-time.
- **Compile-Time Rule:** The Java compiler does **not** mandate `try-catch` or `throws` for unchecked exceptions.
- **Common Unchecked Exceptions (5 mandatory for KTU):**
  1. `ArithmeticException` тАФ integer divide by zero.
  2. `NullPointerException` тАФ null object dereference.
  3. `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` тАФ invalid array index.
  4. `ClassCastException` тАФ invalid type cast.
  5. `NumberFormatException` тАФ invalid numeric string parsing.
- **Floating-Point Exception:** Division by zero with `float`/`double` does **NOT** throw `ArithmeticException`; it produces `Infinity` or `NaN` (IEEE 754 standard).
- **JVM Behavior:** Unchecked exceptions are detected and thrown by the **JVM** at runtime, not by user code (although user code may also explicitly `throw` them).
- **Catch Order:** Always place **more specific** exception classes **before** more general ones in multi-catch blocks.
- **`finally` Block:** Always executes (unless `System.exit()` is called), regardless of whether an exception was thrown or caught.
- **Hierarchy Memory Trick:** *Errors and RuntimeExceptions are unchecked; everything else under Exception is checked.*
- **Best Practice:** Even though unchecked exceptions are optional to handle, robust code **should** catch them at appropriate boundaries to prevent program crashes.
- **Constructor & `super()`:** All exception classes inherit constructors that accept a `String message`; pass a meaningful message via `super(message)`.
- **Subclass Rule:** A subclass overriding a method can declare the same or fewer checked exceptions, but **can declare any number of unchecked exceptions** since they are not part of the checked-exception contract.
- **Stack Trace:** Every `Throwable` object carries a stack trace accessible via `printStackTrace()`, `getStackTrace()`, or `getMessage()`.
- **`throw` vs `throws`:** `throw` is used to explicitly throw an exception object; `throws` is a method-signature clause declaring potential checked exceptions.
- **Common Error vs Exception Confusion:** `Error` is unchecked but is **not** typically caught in application code; it indicates JVM-level fatal conditions.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: try Block and catch Clause
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** try Block and catch Clause ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **Java Programming Language**, a **`try` block** is a compound statement that encapsulates a region of code in which an exception (a runtime anomaly disrupting the normal flow of instructions) might occur. It serves as the **guarded region** monitored by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) and the compiler for abnormal conditions. A **`catch` clause** (also termed an *exception handler*) is the formal parameter declaration that immediately follows a `try` block, designed to **intercept**, **receive**, and **process** a thrown exception object of a specific type (or its subtypes) when the corresponding failure manifests within the protected region. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 mandates the study of *Exception Handling Fundamentals*, with explicit emphasis on the syntactic structure of `try-catch`, the **Exception Class Hierarchy** rooted at `java.lang.Throwable`, the distinction between *checked* and *unchecked* exceptions, and the flow of control when an exception is raised. Mastery of `try-catch` is a **CO3 (Apply)** mapped outcome. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **chef working in a busy kitchen** preparing a multi-step recipe. - The **kitchen counter where you actually cook** = the `try` block. This is where the *risky* operations (cutting, frying, pouring hot oil) take place. - The **fire extinguisher and first-aid box mounted on the wall right next to the counter** = the `catch` clause. It does *nothing* while everything is fine. The moment something goes wrong (oil splashes, knife slips, gas leaks), the extinguisher is **activated and handles the emergency**. - The **type label on the extinguisher** (e.g., "Class K тАФ Kitchen Fires") = the **exception type** declared in the `catch` parameter. It only responds to the *matching* emergency. - The **recipe continues** after the emergency is contained тАФ just as program execution resumes after a `catch` block completes. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** The `try` block says *"I am about to do something that might fail."* The `catch` clause says *"If it fails in *this specific way*, here is what I will do about it."* The two together convert a *crash* into a *controlled response*. ### 1.3 Standard Java Terminology (KTU Board-Expected Terms) The following terms are **mandatory vocabulary** for KTU 2024 Scheme answers and must be memorised verbatim: | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Guarded Region** | The body of the `try` block, monitored for exceptions | | **Exception Handler** | The `catch` block that processes a caught exception | | **Thrown Object** | An instance of `Throwable` (or subclass) passed up the call stack | | **Matching** | JVM checks if the thrown object's class is `instanceof` the catch parameter type | | **Polymorphic Catch** | A `catch (Exception e)` catches the parameter type *and all its subclasses* | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric тАФ Java Exception Hierarchy Depth:** The root is `java.lang.Object` тЖТ `java.lang.Throwable` тЖТ (branches into `Exception` and `Error`). The `Error` branch (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`) is **not recoverable** and should **not** be caught in normal application code. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of the `try` Block A `try` block is demarcated by the keyword `try` followed by a parenthesized *optional* set of resource declarations (try-with-resources, Java 7+) and a body enclosed in curly braces `{ ... }`. The body may contain **any valid Java statements**: variable declarations, method invocations, object instantiations, loops, conditionals, and even nested `try-catch` constructs. ```java try { // Statement 1 // Statement 2 (potential exception source) // Statement 3 } ``` **Operational rules of a `try` block:** - It **cannot stand alone**. It must be immediately followed by at least one `catch` clause **or** a `finally` block (or both). - The scope of any local variable declared *inside* the `try` block is **strictly limited** to that block. The variable is invisible outside. - A `try` block can be **nested** within another `try` block. Inner exceptions are first offered to inner handlers; if unhandled, they propagate to outer handlers. - The compiler enforces that certain *checked* exceptions (subclasses of `Exception` other than `RuntimeException`) declared by methods called inside the `try` must be either caught or declared in a `throws` clause. ### 2.2 Anatomy of the `catch` Clause A `catch` clause begins with the keyword `catch`, followed by a parenthesized declaration of **exactly one parameter** (the exception variable), and a body block `{ ... }`. Multiple `catch` clauses may follow a single `try` block, each handling a *distinct* exception type. ```java try { // risky code } catch (ArithmeticException ae) { // handler for division by zero } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException aie) { // handler for bad array index } catch (Exception e) { // generic fallback handler } ``` **Operational rules of a `catch` clause:** - The parameter type determines **which exceptions** this handler can intercept. The JVM uses `instanceof` semantics: if the thrown object's class is `==` to or a subclass of the parameter type, the catch *matches*. - The parameter variable (e.g., `ae`, `e`) is a *local final effectively* variable inside the catch body. It is initialized by the JVM with the reference to the thrown object, granting access to methods like `getMessage()`, `printStackTrace()`, and `toString()`. - **Multi-catch** (Java 7+): A single `catch` can handle multiple unrelated exception types using the pipe operator: `catch (IOException | SQLException ex) { ... }`. - The exception types in successive `catch` clauses must be ordered from **most specific to most general**. A more general exception declared first causes a *compile-time error* (unreachable code) because it would shadow the more specific handlers. - Once a `catch` block completes, **execution continues at the statement immediately after the entire try-catch construct** тАФ *not* back into the `try` block. ### 2.3 Flow of Control: Step-by-Step Logic The `try-catch` mechanism follows a deterministic state machine: 1. **Entry:** JVM enters the `try` block, pushing a *protected frame* marker onto the runtime stack. 2. **Normal Execution:** Statements execute sequentially. If no exception occurs, all `catch` clauses are **skipped entirely**, and control flows to the first statement after the entire try-catch sequence. 3. **Exception Detection:** When an exception object is thrown (either explicitly via `throw new ...` or implicitly by JVM for runtime errors), the JVM searches the current frame for a `catch` whose parameter type matches. 4. **Stack Unwinding:** If no matching catch exists in the current try-catch, the current method **aborts**, the frame is popped, and the JVM searches the *caller's* frame. This process repeats until a handler is found or the thread terminates. 5. **Handler Execution:** When a matching catch is found, the thrown object is bound to the catch parameter, and the catch body executes. Control then transfers to the first statement *after* the entire try-catch. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Memorisation Table тАФ Compiled Per KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation Key** | Construct | Syntax Skeleton | Key Constraint | Common Mistake | |---|---|---|---| | Basic `try-catch` | `try { ... } catch (X e) { ... }` | `try` alone is illegal | Forgetting a `catch` or `finally` | | Multiple `catch` | `try { ... } catch (A a) {...} catch (B b) {...}` | Order: specific тЖТ general | Placing `Exception` before its subclass | | Multi-catch (Java 7+) | `catch (A \| B ex) { ... }` | Types must be siblings (no inheritance relation) | `catch (IOException \| Exception e)` тЖТ compile error | | Nested `try` | `try { try {...} catch(X e){...} } catch(Y e){...}` | Inner catch handles first | Assuming outer always sees the exception | | Polymorphic catch | `catch (Exception e)` | Catches *all* checked and unchecked subclasses | Using it to "swallow" all errors | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility In professional software engineering, `try-catch` is the **bedrock of robust software design**. It is used in: - **Input Validation Systems** (parsing user input without crashing on `NumberFormatException`). - **Database Connectivity Layers** (handling `SQLException` for failed queries). - **Network Programming** (managing `IOException` from socket timeouts). - **Financial Transaction Systems** (rolling back state on transaction failure). - **Embedded & IoT Systems** (preventing sensor read errors from halting the entire controller). > [!NOTE] > **Anti-Pattern Warning:** Catching `Exception` or `Throwable` broadly and ignoring it (an *empty catch block*) is a well-known code smell. KTU examiners specifically test whether students can *justify* the granularity of their catch clauses. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Code Walkthroughs ### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Basic try-catch with ArithmeticException Below is a complete, line-by-line analytical trace of a canonical KTU textbook program. ```java public class TryCatchDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { int numerator = 10; int denominator = 0; int result; try { System.out.println("Step 1: Entering try block"); result = numerator / denominator; // <-- exception thrown here System.out.println("Step 2: Result = " + result); // <-- SKIPPED } catch (ArithmeticException ae) { System.out.println("Step 3: Caught exception: " + ae.getMessage()); result = 0; // recovery action } System.out.println("Step 4: Program continues. Final result = " + result); } } ``` **Line-by-line execution trace:** 1. **Line 3:** `main` method begins; local variables `numerator=10`, `denominator=0`, `result` (uninitialized) are pushed onto the stack. 2. **Line 7:** `"Step 1: Entering try block"` is printed. The JVM is now inside the guarded region. 3. **Line 8:** The expression `numerator / denominator` evaluates. The JVM detects integer division by zero and **implicitly throws** a new `ArithmeticException` object with the message `"/ by zero"`. Execution in the `try` block **aborts immediately**. 4. **Line 9:** **Skipped.** Control does not return to the `try` block. 5. **Catch Matching:** The JVM inspects the available catch clauses. `ArithmeticException` is `instanceof` the parameter `ArithmeticException ae` тАФ **match found**. 6. **Line 10:** The thrown object reference is bound to `ae`. The body executes, printing `"Step 3: Caught exception: / by zero"`. 7. **Line 11:** Recovery: `result` is assigned `0`. 8. **Line 14:** After the catch block completes, execution continues *outside* the try-catch. `"Step 4: Program continues. Final result = 0"` is printed. 9. The `main` method returns normally; the program terminates cleanly without a crash. **Expected Console Output:** ``` Step 1: Entering try block Step 3: Caught exception: / by zero Step 4: Program continues. Final result = 0 ``` ### 3.2 Analytical Trace: Multiple catch clauses with ordering ```java try { int[] arr = new int[5]; arr[10] = 100; // throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException int x = 10 / 0; // unreachable } catch (ArithmeticException ae) { System.out.println("Arithmetic"); } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException aie) { System.out.println("Array"); } catch (Exception e) { System.out.println("Generic"); } ``` **Execution Trace:** - Line 2: Array of length 5 created. - Line 3: Index 10 is out of bounds. JVM throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. - Line 4: **Skipped** (unreachable in this run). - JVM scans catch clauses in source order: - `catch (ArithmeticException ae)` тАФ `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` is **NOT** an instance of `ArithmeticException` тЖТ **no match**. - `catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException aie)` тАФ **exact match** тЖТ handler runs. - Output: `"Array"`. The third catch clause is **not evaluated**. ### 3.3 Invalid Ordering тАФ Compile-Time Error Demonstration ```java try { // risky code } catch (Exception e) { // line A тАФ too general // ... } catch (ArithmeticException ae) { // line B тАФ unreachable // ... } ``` **Why this fails to compile:** The compiler enforces the rule *unreachable-catch-clause*. On line A, `catch (Exception e)` matches **every** exception (including `ArithmeticException`). If the JVM ever reached line B, it would already have been caught by line A. Therefore, the compiler rejects the program: ``` error: exception java.lang.ArithmeticException has already been caught ``` **Correction:** Reverse the order тАФ declare specific handlers *first*, generic handlers *last*. ### 3.4 Multi-Catch (Java 7+) тАФ Worked Example ```java import java.io.*; public class MultiCatchDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { try { int data = System.in.read(); // throws IOException int n = Integer.parseInt("abc"); // throws NumberFormatException } catch (IOException | NumberFormatException ex) { System.out.println("Handled: " + ex.getMessage()); } } } ``` **Rule Applied:** The `|` operator in a multi-catch declares that the handler accepts **any** of the listed types. Crucially, the types in a multi-catch must be **disjoint** тАФ no type may be a subtype of another. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why disjoint types?** If `IOException` and `Exception` were multi-caught, the more specific `IOException` would be redundant. The compiler enforces this as a **compile-time error**. ### 3.5 Nested try-catch тАФ Analytical Matrix ```java try { System.out.println("Outer try entered"); try { int x = 10 / 0; // ArithmeticException } catch (NullPointerException npe) { System.out.println("Inner catch тАФ NPE"); } System.out.println("Outer try continues"); // <-- DOES this run? } catch (ArithmeticException ae) { System.out.println("Outer catch тАФ Arithmetic"); } ``` **Execution Trace:** - Outer try entered; inner try entered. - `10 / 0` throws `ArithmeticException`. - Inner `catch (NullPointerException npe)` тАФ **no match**. - Inner try-catch terminates *abnormally*; the exception **propagates outward**. - Outer `catch (ArithmeticException ae)` тАФ **match**; handler runs. - Output: `"Outer try entered"` followed by `"Outer catch тАФ Arithmetic"`. - The line `"Outer try continues"` is **never reached** because the inner block threw an uncaught exception that aborted the remainder of the outer try block. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** When an inner `try-catch` fails to handle an exception, the exception propagates to the **outer** try-catch. The remainder of the outer try block (after the nested construct) is **not executed**. Only code in the matching outer catch (or finally) runs. ### 3.6 Summary Analytical Matrix тАФ Catch Matching Logic | Thrown Exception Type | `catch (ArithmeticException)` | `catch (RuntimeException)` | `catch (Exception)` | `catch (Throwable)` | |---|---|---|---|---| | `ArithmeticException` | тЬЕ Match | тЬЕ Match (subclass) | тЬЕ Match (subclass) | тЬЕ Match (subclass) | | `NullPointerException` | тЭМ No match | тЬЕ Match (subclass) | тЬЕ Match (subclass) | тЬЕ Match (subclass) | | `IOException` | тЭМ No match | тЭМ No match (not RuntimeException) | тЬЕ Match (subclass) | тЬЕ Match (subclass) | | `Error` (e.g., `OOMError`) | тЭМ No match | тЭМ No match | тЭМ No match (not Exception) | тЬЕ Match (subclass) | | `Throwable` (custom) | тЭМ No match | тЭМ No match | тЭМ No match | тЬЕ Match (exact) | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Java Exception Class Hierarchy (Architecture Flow) The following block-level functional architecture diagram maps the class inheritance relationships that govern `try-catch` matching in Java. ```mermaid graph TD obj["java.lang.Object"] thr["java.lang.Throwable"] err["java.lang.Error"] exc["java.lang.Exception"] rtex["java.lang.RuntimeException"] ioe["java.io.IOException"] sql["java.sql.SQLException"] aex["ArithmeticException"] npe["NullPointerException"] aiobe["ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException"] nfe["NumberFormatException"] oom["OutOfMemoryError"] soe["StackOverflowError"] obj --> thr thr --> err thr --> exc exc --> rtex exc --> ioe exc --> sql rtex --> aex rtex --> npe rtex --> aiobe rtex --> nfe err --> oom err --> soe ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows denote `extends` (inheritance). A `catch` parameter declared at any node catches exceptions thrown at that node *or any descendant*. Hence `catch (Throwable t)` intercepts *everything*, while `catch (ArithmeticException ae)` intercepts only that exact type. ### 4.2 try-catch Control Flow State Machine ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Program execution enters try block"] --> B{"Statement throws an exception?"} B -- "No" --> C["All catch clauses skipped"] C --> D["Execution continues after try-catch"] B -- "Yes" --> E["JVM builds Throwable object"] E --> F{"Scan catch clauses in order
catch parameter?"} F -- "Match found" --> G["Bind thrown object to catch parameter"] G --> H["Execute catch body"] H --> I["Execution continues after try-catch"] F -- "No match in any catch" --> J["Pop current method frame
Propagate to caller"] J --> K{"Caller has matching handler?"} K -- "Yes" --> G K -- "No" --> L["Stack unwinds to main
Thread terminates abnormally"] ``` ### 4.3 Multi-catch Conceptual Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR T["try block
C1["catch IOException"] T --> C2["catch SQLException"] T --> C3["catch NumberFormatException"] C1 --> M["Multi-catch handler
catch IOException or SQLException or NumberFormatException"] C2 --> M C3 --> M M --> R["Unified recovery logic"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** Multi-catch is *syntactic sugar* that allows disparate exception types to share a single recovery routine *without* inheriting from a common superclass. It reduces code duplication and improves maintainability. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]** *What is the purpose of a `try` block in Java? Can a `try` block exist without a `catch` clause? Justify your answer with an example.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A `try` block in Java is a **guarded region** of code that is monitored by the JVM for the occurrence of exceptions. It encapsulates statements that might throw an exception, allowing the program to handle the exception gracefully rather than terminating abruptly. A `try` block **cannot exist alone**. According to the Java Language Specification, a `try` statement must be followed by either: - At least one `catch` clause, **or** - A `finally` block, **or** - Both. ```java try { int data = 100 / 0; // risky operation } finally { System.out.println("Finally executes regardless"); } ``` In the example above, the `try` block is paired only with a `finally` block (no catch). The `finally` block executes irrespective of whether an exception was thrown, but the exception itself remains unhandled and will propagate further. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024]** *Explain the role of a `catch` clause. What happens if no matching `catch` clause is found for a thrown exception?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A `catch` clause is an **exception handler** that intercepts a thrown exception object matching its declared parameter type. It receives the exception reference, processes it (via methods like `getMessage()` or `printStackTrace()`), and enables the program to continue normal execution after the handler completes. If no matching `catch` clause is found in the current method, the JVM performs **stack unwinding**: the current method's frame is popped, the exception propagates to the *caller*, and the search for a matching handler resumes in the caller's scope. If no handler is found up the entire call stack, the thread terminates abnormally, and the JVM prints a stack trace to `System.err`. --- ### 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks тАФ Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **(a)** Explain the syntax and execution flow of a `try-catch` block in Java. Describe what happens when an exception is generated inside the `try` block. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Write a Java program that reads two integers from the user and performs integer division. Use a `try-catch` mechanism to handle both `ArithmeticException` (division by zero) and `InputMismatchException` (non-integer input). Display appropriate messages for each case. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution (a) тАФ 7 Marks:** **Syntax:** ```java try { // statements that may throw exceptions } catch (ExceptionType1 e1) { // handler for ExceptionType1 } catch (ExceptionType2 e2) { // handler for ExceptionType2 } ``` **Execution Flow:** 1. The JVM enters the `try` block and begins executing statements sequentially. 2. If no exception is raised, all `catch` clauses are bypassed, and execution continues with the first statement after the try-catch construct. 3. If an exception is raised (implicitly by JVM or explicitly via `throw`), the remaining statements of the `try` block are **skipped**. 4. The JVM scans the catch clauses in source order. For each clause, it performs an `instanceof` check: does the thrown object match the catch parameter type? 5. The **first matching catch** is selected; the thrown object is assigned to the catch parameter, and the catch body executes. 6. After the catch body finishes, control transfers to the first statement after the entire try-catch sequence. The program does not return to the `try` block. **Model Solution (b) тАФ 7 Marks:** ```java import java.util.Scanner; import java.util.InputMismatchException; public class DivisionHandler { public static void main(String[] args) { Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); int a = 0, b = 0, result = 0; boolean valid = false; while (!valid) { try { System.out.print("Enter numerator: "); a = sc.nextInt(); System.out.print("Enter denominator: "); b = sc.nextInt(); result = a / b; valid = true; } catch (ArithmeticException ae) { System.out.println("Error: Division by zero is undefined. Try again."); sc.nextLine(); // clear buffer } catch (InputMismatchException ime) { System.out.println("Error: Please enter valid integers only. Try again."); sc.nextLine(); // clear buffer } } System.out.println("Result: " + a + " / " + b + " = " + result); sc.close(); } } ``` **Explanation of key lines:** - `a = sc.nextInt();` throws `InputMismatchException` if the user types a non-integer. - `result = a / b;` throws `ArithmeticException` if `b == 0`. - `sc.nextLine();` inside both catch blocks discards the invalid input from the buffer, preventing an infinite loop. - The `while (!valid)` loop re-prompts the user until a valid division is completed. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024, Model Paper 1]** **(a)** Differentiate between *checked* and *unchecked* exceptions in Java. Provide two examples for each category. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Explain *multi-catch* (introduced in Java 7) with a suitable code example. State the compile-time rule that must be satisfied when using multi-catch. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution (a) тАФ 7 Marks:** | Feature | Checked Exceptions | Unchecked Exceptions | |---|---|---| | **Compiler Verification** | Checked at *compile time* | Not checked at compile time | | **Parent Class** | `java.lang.Exception` (excluding `RuntimeException`) | `java.lang.RuntimeException` | | **Handling Mandate** | Must be either caught or declared in `throws` | Handling is optional | | **Typical Cause** | External resources (I/O, DB, network) | Programming logic errors | | **Examples** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | | **Recoverability** | Usually recoverable (retry, fallback) | Often indicates a bug in the code | **Examples of Checked Exceptions:** 1. `IOException` тАФ thrown when an I/O operation fails (e.g., file not found). 2. `SQLException` тАФ thrown when a database access error occurs. **Examples of Unchecked Exceptions:** 1. `ArithmeticException` тАФ thrown for arithmetic errors like integer division by zero. 2. `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` тАФ thrown when accessing an array with an illegal index. **Model Solution (b) тАФ 7 Marks:** **Definition:** Multi-catch is a Java 7 feature that allows a single `catch` block to handle **multiple unrelated exception types** in a unified handler, reducing code duplication. **Syntax:** ```java try { // code that may throw multiple exception types } catch (IOException | SQLException ex) { // common recovery logic } ``` **Complete Code Example:** ```java import java.io.*; import java.sql.*; public class MultiCatchExample { public static void main(String[] args) { try { FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt"); // may throw FileNotFoundException int ch = fr.read(); // may throw IOException Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection("jdbc:mysql://localhost/test"); // may throw SQLException } catch (IOException | SQLException ex) { System.out.println("Operation failed: " + ex.getMessage()); ex.printStackTrace(); } } } ``` **Compile-Time Rule:** The exception types listed in a multi-catch must be **disjoint** тАФ no type may be a subtype of another. The following code will **not compile**: ```java } catch (IOException | Exception ex) { // ERROR: Exception is a supertype of IOException ``` The compiler rejects this because listing `IOException` is redundant when `Exception` is already present. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Wrong Catch Order:** Declaring `catch (Exception e)` *before* `catch (ArithmeticException ae)`. The compiler rejects this, costing all marks for the output/run. > 2. **Confusing `Throwable` with `Exception`:** `catch (Throwable t)` is *legal* but considered bad practice. Examiners expect students to articulate *why* catching `Error` subclasses is discouraged (they signal unrecoverable conditions). > 3. **Forgetting the `finally`/`catch` requirement:** Writing a bare `try { ... }` block without a catch or finally. This is a **compile-time error**. > 4. **Variable Scope Errors:** Attempting to use a variable declared inside the `try` block *outside* the try-catch construct. Such variables have block scope and are invisible externally. > 5. **Ignoring Multi-catch Disjointness Rule:** Combining related exception types like `IOException` and `FileNotFoundException` in a multi-catch тАФ this fails to compile. > 6. **Empty Catch Blocks:** Catching an exception and doing nothing inside the catch body is a code smell. Examiners award marks only when the catch body contains meaningful recovery logic (logging, default value, re-prompt, etc.). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist тАФ KTU 2024 Scheme** - тЬЕ A `try` block **must** be followed by at least one `catch` or a `finally` block. - тЬЕ A `catch` parameter accepts the thrown exception object and any of its subclasses (polymorphic matching). - тЬЕ Multiple `catch` clauses must be ordered **specific тЖТ general**; otherwise, compile error. - тЬЕ The variable declared in the catch parameter is **block-scoped** to the catch body only. - тЬЕ Once a `catch` executes, control transfers to the statement *after* the entire try-catch; it does **not** return to the `try` block. - тЬЕ **Multi-catch** (Java 7+) uses `|` to handle multiple unrelated types in one handler; types must be **disjoint**. - тЬЕ **Nested try-catch:** inner exception not caught by inner handler propagates to outer handler; remaining outer-try code is skipped. - тЬЕ Checked exceptions are subclasses of `Exception` (excluding `RuntimeException`); unchecked are subclasses of `RuntimeException`. - тЬЕ `Error` and its subclasses (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) generally should **not** be caught. - тЬЕ An empty catch block is a **code smell** тАФ always include meaningful recovery logic. - тЬЕ `Throwable` is the root of all exceptions and errors; `Exception` is a direct subclass used for *recoverable* conditions. - тЬЕ The JVM uses `instanceof` semantics for catch matching, supporting **polymorphic dispatch** of handlers.

topics.title: Multiple catch Clauses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multiple catch Clauses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java programming language, **Multiple Catch Clauses** refer to a robust exception-handling construct where a single `try` block is followed by **two or more `catch` blocks**, each designed to trap and process a distinct category of exception. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Object Oriented Programming using Java, this mechanism allows a programmer to respond differently based on the *type* of failure encountered during program execution, rather than treating every error identically.

The general academic syntax is:

```java
try {
    // Protected code that may throw exceptions
    statement_1;
    statement_2;
    ...
} catch (ExceptionType1 e1) {
    // Handler for ExceptionType1
} catch (ExceptionType2 e2) {
    // Handler for ExceptionType2
} catch (ExceptionTypeN eN) {
    // Handler for ExceptionTypeN
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 тАУ Exception Handling):**
> The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) evaluates `catch` blocks **sequentially, from top to bottom**. The *first* `catch` block whose exception type is **assignment-compatible** (matches or is a superclass) with the thrown object is executed. All subsequent `catch` blocks are bypassed for that particular exception event.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a hospital emergency-room triage nurse. Patients arrive with different ailments тАФ a broken arm, a heart attack, or the common flu. You do **not** send every patient to the same doctor. Instead, you *classify* the symptoms and route each patient to the correct specialist.

In Java, the `try` block is the hospital entrance. The patients are the exceptions. The multiple `catch` clauses are the various specialist doctors standing by. Each specialist (catch) knows how to handle exactly one *category* of problem (exception type). If you sent a cardiac patient to an orthopaedic specialist, treatment would fail тАФ which is exactly why **catch ordering matters** (specialists for severe/rare conditions must be listed *before* general physicians).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Recap:**
> A `try` block = "Try to do this risky work."
> A `catch` clause = "If something specific goes wrong, do *this* recovery."
> Multiple catch = "If several *different* specific things go wrong, I have a separate recovery plan for each."

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Value/Standard | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `Exception` direct subclasses used commonly | ~**15 built-in** | Common checked/unchecked errors |
| Java Multi-catch feature introduced | **Java SE 7** (2011) | Allows `catch (A \| B e)` syntax |
| Try-with-resources introduced | **Java SE 7** | Related exception-handling improvement |
| Minimum catch blocks allowed after a try | **0 or more** (but `finally` optional) | A try with no catch is valid if `finally` is present |
| Catch block scope | **Block-level** | Variable `e` is local to its catch |

> [!TIP]
> The "Java Multi-catch" syntax (`catch (IOException | SQLException e)`) is a **favourite KTU Part-A 3-mark question**. Always mention the **Java 7** release when discussing it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic тАФ Step by Step

When a `try` block is executed, the JVM follows this deterministic decision tree:

1. **Step 1 тАФ Entry:** Control enters the `try` block. Statement execution begins from the first line.
2. **Step 2 тАФ Normal Flow:** Each statement executes in sequence. If no exception is thrown, all `catch` blocks are skipped, and execution continues after the last `catch`.
3. **Step 3 тАФ Exception Detection:** The moment an exception is thrown (explicitly via `throw` or implicitly by the JVM), the remaining lines of the `try` block are **abandoned**.
4. **Step 4 тАФ Catch Matching (Top-Down Scan):** The JVM inspects the `catch` blocks **in the order they appear in the source code**. It checks whether the thrown exception object is `instanceof` the parameter type of the catch.
5. **Step 5 тАФ First Match Wins:** The *first* matching `catch` block is selected. Its body executes.
6. **Step 6 тАФ Resumption:** Control transfers to the statement immediately following the entire `try-catch` construct.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Catch Ordering

Java's exception class hierarchy is a tree rooted at `java.lang.Throwable`:

```
Throwable
тФЬтФАтФА Error                    (unchecked, JVM-level тАФ usually not caught)
тФФтФАтФА Exception
    тФЬтФАтФА IOException          (checked)
    тФЬтФАтФА SQLException         (checked)
    тФЬтФАтФА ClassNotFoundException (checked)
    тФЬтФАтФА RuntimeException     (unchecked)
    тФВ   тФЬтФАтФА ArithmeticException
    тФВ   тФЬтФАтФА NullPointerException
    тФВ   тФЬтФАтФА ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
    тФВ   тФФтФАтФА NumberFormatException
    тФФтФАтФА ... (other checked exceptions)
```

If a `catch (Exception e)` block were placed **above** `catch (ArithmeticException e)`, the second block would become **unreachable code** тАФ a compile-time error. This is because every `ArithmeticException` is *also* an `Exception`, so the first block would always match first.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cardinal Rule of Catch Ordering:**
> Place more **specific (subclass)** exception catches *before* more **general (superclass)** ones. Otherwise, the code fails to compile.

### 2.3 Multi-Catch Syntax (Java 7+)

Java 7 introduced a single `catch` block that can handle **multiple unrelated exception types** using the pipe (`|`) symbol. The variable declared is *implicitly final*, so it cannot be reassigned within the block.

```java
try {
    // risky code
} catch (IOException | SQLException e) {
    logger.error("Database or I/O failure: " + e.getMessage());
}
```

**Constraint (Java 7/8):** No type in a multi-catch may be a subclass of another type in the same multi-catch тАФ for example, `catch (IOException | FileNotFoundException e)` is **illegal** because `FileNotFoundException` extends `IOException`.

> [!NOTE]
> Since **Java 10**, you can use `var` in a multi-catch:
> `catch (var e) { ... }` тАФ but this is rarely tested in KTU.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield "Formula Sheet" тАФ Conceptual Mapping

| Concept | Rule | Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|
| Try block minimum | Must be followed by at least one `catch` OR `finally` | A `try` alone is a compile error |
| Catch parameter type | Must be `Throwable` or any subclass | Including custom exceptions |
| Ordering | Specific (subclass) тЖТ General (superclass) | Reverse order = compile error |
| Variable scope | `e` is local to its own `catch` block | Each catch has its own identifier scope |
| Multi-catch (Java 7+) | `catch (A \| B e)` handles both A and B | Variable `e` is implicitly `final` |
| Multi-catch rule | No two types in `\|` may be related by inheritance | Sub + Super = compile error |
| Unreachable catch | General catch above specific catch | "Unreachable code" error |
| Re-throwing | `throw e;` inside catch propagates the exception | Must declare if checked |
| Java 7 improvement | `catch (Exception \| ... e)` in single block | Pipe-separated types |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multiple catch clauses are essential in:

- **Enterprise Web Applications:** Servlets distinguish between `IOException` (network failure), `SQLException` (database failure), and `NumberFormatException` (bad user input), logging each appropriately.
- **Banking Software:** Separates `InsufficientFundsException` (custom) from generic `RuntimeException` for differentiated recovery (e.g., reverse a transaction vs. log and exit).
- **Network Protocol Parsers:** Differentiate between `MalformedURLException`, `UnknownHostException`, and `SocketTimeoutException` to give the user precise diagnostic messages.
- **IoT / Embedded Java:** Distinguishes hardware I/O faults from parsing faults for selective device reset.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a CS/Java topic, "derivations" here take the form of **code-trace analysis** and **rule-proving exercises** that KTU examiners frequently set.

### 3.1 Analytical Exercise 1 тАФ Proving the "Specific Before General" Rule

**Problem Statement:** Examine the following code and determine whether it compiles. If not, explain why and rewrite it correctly.

```java
try {
    int result = 10 / 0;            // throws ArithmeticException
} catch (Exception e) {
    System.out.println("Generic exception");
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {   // тЖР Line A
    System.out.println("Divide by zero");
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Deduction:**

1. The expression `10 / 0` triggers JVM-level throw of `java.lang.ArithmeticException`.
2. `ArithmeticException` extends `RuntimeException` extends `Exception` extends `Throwable`.
3. Therefore, an `ArithmeticException` object **is-an** `Exception` (assignment-compatible).
4. At runtime, the JVM's first-match scan would find `catch (Exception e)` to match and execute it.
5. Consequently, the `catch (ArithmeticException e)` at **Line A** would **never** be reached.

**Compiler Verdict:** тЭМ **Compilation Error** тАФ *"Unreachable catch block for ArithmeticException. It is already handled by the catch block for Exception."*

**Correctly Ordered Version:**

```java
try {
    int result = 10 / 0;
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {   // тЖР Specific FIRST
    System.out.println("Divide by zero: " + e.getMessage());
} catch (Exception e) {             // тЖР General LAST
    System.out.println("Generic exception");
}
```

### 3.2 Analytical Exercise 2 тАФ Multi-Catch Inheritance Rule

**Problem Statement:** Will the following code compile?

```java
try {
    FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
} catch (IOException | FileNotFoundException e) {
    System.out.println("File issue");
}
```

**Logical Deduction:**

1. `FileNotFoundException` is a **direct subclass** of `IOException`.
2. The Java Language Specification (JLS ┬з14.20.1) explicitly forbids two alternatives in a multi-catch clause from being in a subtype relationship.
3. Reason: a single exception object can only be matched *once*, and listing both would create ambiguity.

**Compiler Verdict:** тЭМ **Compilation Error** тАФ *"Alternatives in a multi-catch clause cannot be related by subclassing."*

**Correct Form:**

```java
try {
    FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
} catch (FileNotFoundException e) {   // subclass first
    System.out.println("File missing");
} catch (IOException e) {              // superclass second
    System.out.println("I/O issue");
}
```

### 3.3 Analytical Exercise 3 тАФ Full Multi-Catch Demonstration (Working Program)

```java
public class MultiCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String[] data = {"100", "abc", null, "50"};

        for (String s : data) {
            try {
                int n = Integer.parseInt(s);   // may throw NumberFormatException / NPE
                int result = 1000 / n;         // may throw ArithmeticException
                System.out.println("Result = " + result);
            } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
                System.out.println("[Handler 1] Bad number format: " + s);
            } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
                System.out.println("[Handler 2] Math error: divide by zero");
            } catch (NullPointerException e) {
                System.out.println("[Handler 3] Null input received");
            } catch (RuntimeException e) {
                System.out.println("[Handler 4] Other runtime issue: " + e);
            }
        }
    }
}
```
