**Trace Table (Iteration-by-Iteration):**

| Iteration | Input `s` | Thrown Exception | Matching Catch (Top-Down) | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `"100"` | None | (skipped) | `Result = 10` |
| 2 | `"abc"` | `NumberFormatException` | Handler 1 | `[Handler 1] Bad number format: abc` |
| 3 | `null` | `NullPointerException` | Handler 3 | `[Handler 3] Null input received` |
| 4 | `"50"` | None | (skipped) | `Result = 20` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice in iteration 3, although `NullPointerException` *is-a* `RuntimeException`, **Handler 3** (more specific) is matched, not Handler 4. This empirically proves the **first-match-wins** rule.

### 3.4 Analytical Exercise 4 тАФ Multi-Catch with `final` Variable Rule

```java
try {
    // risky code
} catch (IOException | SQLException e) {
    e = new IOException();        // тЖР Line X (legal?)
    System.out.println(e);
}
```

**Logical Deduction:**

1. The parameter `e` in a multi-catch is **implicitly final** (JLS ┬з14.20.1).
2. Reassigning to a final variable is a compile-time error.
3. Therefore, Line X causes a **compile error**: *"The parameter e of a multi-catch clause cannot be reassigned."*

**Examiner Tip:** This is a high-value KTU question because it tests understanding of the *implicit-final* behaviour of multi-catch parameters, which is unique to Java 7+.

### 3.5 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix

| Source Code Pattern | Compiles? | Executes? | KTU Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific catch before general | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | Correct ordering |
| General catch before specific | тЭМ | N/A | Unreachable code error |
| Multi-catch with unrelated types | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | Java 7+ feature |
| Multi-catch with related types (sub-super) | тЭМ | N/A | JLS ┬з14.20.1 violation |
| Reassign multi-catch variable | тЭМ | N/A | Implicitly final |
| Try with no catch but with finally | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | Valid construct |
| Try with no catch and no finally | тЭМ | N/A | Compile error |
| Two catch blocks for the *same* exception type | тЭМ | N/A | Duplicate handler error |
| Catching `Error` (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) | тЬЕ (with warning) | Risky | Generally bad practice |
| Custom exception in catch list | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | Allowed if extends Throwable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart тАФ Runtime Exception Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Enter try block] --> B{Statement throws exception?}
    B -- No --> C[Execute remaining try statements]
    C --> D[Skip all catch blocks]
    D --> E[Continue after try-catch]
    B -- Yes --> F[Abandon remaining try code]
    F --> G{Catch 1 type matches?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Execute Catch 1 body]
    H --> E
    G -- No --> I{Catch 2 type matches?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Execute Catch 2 body]
    J --> E
    I -- No --> K{Catch 3 type matches?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Execute Catch 3 body]
    L --> E
    K -- No --> M[Exception propagates up the call stack]
    M --> N[Caller must handle or program terminates]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram тАФ Java Exception Class Hierarchy (Relevant Subset)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Thr[Throwable] --> Err[Error]
    Thr --> Exc[Exception]
    Err --> OOM[OutOfMemoryError]
    Err --> Stack[StackOverflowError]
    Exc --> IOE[IOException]
    Exc --> SQLE[SQLException]
    Exc --> CNFE[ClassNotFoundException]
    Exc --> RT[RuntimeException]
    IOE --> FNF[FileNotFoundException]
    IOE --> EOF[EOFException]
    RT --> Arith[ArithmeticException]
    RT --> NPE[NullPointerException]
    RT --> AIOOBE[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    RT --> NFE[NumberFormatException]
    RT --> CCE[ClassCastException]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram тАФ Try-Catch-Finally Architectural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProtectedZone[Protected Code Region]
        T[try block] --> S1[Statement 1]
        S1 --> S2[Statement 2]
        S2 --> S3[Statement 3]
    end
    S3 -- Normal completion --> F[finally block executes]
    S3 -- Exception thrown --> C1{catch block 1 matches?}
    C1 -- Yes --> H1[Handler 1 executes]
    C1 -- No --> C2{catch block 2 matches?}
    C2 -- Yes --> H2[Handler 2 executes]
    C2 -- No --> C3{catch block 3 matches?}
    C3 -- Yes --> H3[Handler 3 executes]
    C3 -- No --> UP[Uncaught: propagates to caller]
    H1 --> F
    H2 --> F
    H3 --> F
    UP -.-> CallerMethod
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree тАФ Catch-Block Compilation Rules

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Writing catch clauses] --> Q1{Are exception types in correct order? Subclass first, superclass last}
    Q1 -- No --> Fail1[COMPILE ERROR: Unreachable catch block]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Using multi-catch with pipe?}
    Q2 -- No --> OK[Code compiles successfully]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Any two types in pipe related by inheritance?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Fail2[COMPILE ERROR: Subclass alternatives not allowed]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Reassigning the catch parameter inside block?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Fail3[COMPILE ERROR: Implicitly final parameter]
    Q4 -- No --> OK
```

> [!TIP]
> These diagrams mirror the kind of *labelled architecture* the KTU 2024 syllabus asks you to draw in 14-mark questions. Mermaid source is not required in your answer тАФ but knowing the *logical flow* of catch matching earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Model]**

*What is the purpose of having multiple `catch` blocks in Java? Why is the order of catch blocks important?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Multiple `catch` blocks in Java allow a single `try` block to handle **different types of exceptions** with **different recovery logic** for each. Each `catch` block is dedicated to a specific exception category, providing precise and context-appropriate error handling.

The **order** is critical because the JVM matches catch blocks **top-down**, executing the *first* one whose parameter type is assignment-compatible with the thrown exception. If a *general* superclass catch appears *before* a *specific* subclass catch, the subclass catch becomes **unreachable code**, causing a **compilation error**. Therefore, more specific exceptions must be listed first, followed by more general ones, following the order of the exception class hierarchy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1) + Order explanation (1) + Example/Rule (1) = **3 marks**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024, Model]**

*Explain the multi-catch feature introduced in Java 7. What restriction applies to the types in a multi-catch clause?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **multi-catch** feature, introduced in **Java SE 7**, allows a single `catch` block to handle **multiple unrelated exception types** by listing them separated by the pipe `|` symbol. For example:

```java
catch (IOException | SQLException e) { ... }
```

This reduces code duplication when the same recovery logic applies to multiple exception categories.

**Restriction:** No two alternative types in a multi-catch may be related by inheritance (i.e., one cannot be a subclass of another). For instance, `catch (IOException | FileNotFoundException e)` is **illegal** because `FileNotFoundException` extends `IOException`. Additionally, the catch parameter `e` is **implicitly final** and cannot be reassigned within the block.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of multiple catch clauses in Java with a suitable example. Discuss why the order of catch blocks matters with respect to the exception class hierarchy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that reads an array of strings from the user, attempts to convert each to an integer and divide 100 by it, and demonstrates the use of **three distinct catch blocks** for `NumberFormatException`, `ArithmeticException`, and a **general** `Exception`. Show sample output. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) тАФ 7 marks:**

Multiple catch clauses allow one `try` block to be paired with several `catch` blocks, each tailored to a different exception type. The JVM examines catch blocks **sequentially** and executes the *first* one that matches.

**Why order matters:**

1. Java exceptions form a class hierarchy rooted at `Throwable`. A subclass exception object is *also* an instance of every one of its superclasses (due to inheritance).
2. If we write `catch (Exception e)` *before* `catch (ArithmeticException e)`, then the second block can never be reached тАФ the first block will always match first.
3. The Java compiler detects this and reports an **"unreachable catch block"** error at compile time.
4. Therefore, the rule is: **subclass exceptions first, superclass exceptions last**.

**Example:**

```java
try {
    int x = 10 / 0;
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {        // specific тАУ first
    System.out.println("Math error: " + e.getMessage());
} catch (RuntimeException e) {           // intermediate
    System.out.println("Runtime issue: " + e);
} catch (Exception e) {                  // general тАУ last
    System.out.println("Generic error: " + e);
}
```

This ordering compiles correctly. Reversing any two adjacent blocks would cause a compilation error. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (b) тАФ 7 marks:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class MultiCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        String[] inputs = new String[3];

        System.out.println("Enter 3 strings:");
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
            inputs[i] = sc.nextLine();
        }

        for (String s : inputs) {
            try {
                int n = Integer.parseInt(s);     // may throw NumberFormatException
                int result = 100 / n;            // may throw ArithmeticException
                System.out.println("100 / " + n + " = " + result);
            } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
                System.out.println("[Handler 1] '" + s + "' is not a valid integer.");
            } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
                System.out.println("[Handler 2] Cannot divide 100 by zero.");
            } catch (Exception e) {
                System.out.println("[Handler 3] Unexpected error: " + e.getMessage());
            }
        }
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Run:**

```
Enter 3 strings:
25
hello
0
100 / 25 = 4
[Handler 1] 'hello' is not a valid integer.
[Handler 2] Cannot divide 100 by zero.
```

**Explanation of behaviour:**
- Input `"25"` тЖТ parses successfully, `100/25 = 4`.
- Input `"hello"` тЖТ `Integer.parseInt` throws `NumberFormatException`, caught by Handler 1.
- Input `"0"` тЖТ `100/0` throws `ArithmeticException`, caught by Handler 2.
- Handler 3 (`Exception`) is the safety net for any unforeseen error.

**(7 marks тАФ Code 4 + Sample output 1 + Explanation 2)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Differentiate between a single `catch` block and multiple `catch` blocks. What is the **multi-catch** syntax in Java 7+? Provide a code example illustrating the multi-catch feature with **three unrelated exception types**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following Java code. Identify **all** compilation errors, if any. Justify each error and rewrite the code in its **corrected** form with detailed comments. **(7 marks)**

```java
class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int[] arr = new int[5];
            arr[10] = 50;
            String s = null;
            System.out.println(s.length());
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Generic");
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Array issue");
        } catch (NullPointerException | ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Null or Array");
        }
    }
}
```

---

**Model Solution for (a) тАФ 7 marks:**

**Single vs Multiple Catch:**

| Aspect | Single Catch | Multiple Catch |
|---|---|---|
| Number of handlers | One | Two or more |
| Granularity | Coarse тАФ treats all exceptions identically | Fine-grained тАФ distinct logic per type |
| Code clarity | Lower for diverse error scenarios | Higher; each error has its own response |
| Compile rule | No ordering issue | Specific-before-general required |

**Multi-catch syntax (Java 7+):**

```java
try {
    // risky code
} catch (IOException | SQLException | ClassNotFoundException e) {
    // common recovery logic
    logger.error("Recoverable failure: " + e.getMessage());
}
```

The variable `e` is **implicitly final** and represents whichever exception was actually thrown. The handling code is shared across all listed types.

**Example illustrating three unrelated types:**

```java
import java.io.*;
import java.sql.*;

public class MultiCatchExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            if (args.length == 0) throw new IOException("No args");
            Class.forName("com.mysql.jdbc.Driver");   // throws ClassNotFoundException
            // Connection c = DriverManager.getConnection(...); // throws SQLException
        } catch (IOException | SQLException | ClassNotFoundException e) {
            System.out.println("Resource issue: " + e.getClass().getSimpleName());
        }
    }
}
```

**(7 marks тАФ Comparison table 2 + Syntax 2 + Code 3)**

---

**Model Solution for (b) тАФ 7 marks:**

**Error Identification:**

| Line | Error | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e)` (2nd catch) | **Unreachable catch** | The earlier `catch (Exception e)` already handles it. Order violates the specific-after-general rule. |
| `catch (NullPointerException \| ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e)` (3rd catch) | **Duplicate / related-type error** | `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` is unrelated to `NullPointerException` (so the first issue is fine) тАФ but combined with the previous line, this catch is *unreachable* because `Exception` already covers both. Also, the entire 3rd catch is redundant since the 1st catch is the general one. |
| Also note: `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` was *already* listed in the multi-catch; this is legal by itself, but the *multi-catch* and the *earlier general catch* make every later catch unreachable. |

**Corrected Code:**

```java
class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int[] arr = new int[5];
            arr[10] = 50;                          // throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            String s = null;
            System.out.println(s.length());        // throws NullPointerException
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {  // specific subclass FIRST
            System.out.println("Array issue");
        } catch (NullPointerException e) {             // specific subclass NEXT
            System.out.println("Null issue");
        } catch (Exception e) {                       // general superclass LAST
            System.out.println("Generic: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Justification of fix:** Catches are now arranged from most specific to most general, satisfying the inheritance-order rule. At runtime, the first matching catch handles the exception, and the rest are skipped. **(7 marks тАФ 2 errors identified, 3 marks correction, 2 marks justification)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Multiple-Catch Questions:**
>
> 1. **Reversed catch order** тАФ Students frequently place `catch (Exception e)` first "to be safe." This *always* causes a compile error. The KTU examiner awards **0 marks** for the entire code snippet if it does not compile. Always order **subclass тЖТ superclass**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the `final` rule in multi-catch** тАФ Reassigning `e` inside `catch (A | B e)` is illegal. Marks lost: **1тАУ2**.
>
> 3. **Mixing checked and unchecked exceptions incorrectly** тАФ A method that does not declare a checked exception in its `throws` clause cannot *throw* that checked exception, so the catch is meaningless. KTU examiners often set this as a trick.
>
> 4. **Confusing "unreachable code" with "dead code"** тАФ `catch (Exception e)` after a `catch (ArithmeticException e)` is *unreachable*, not *dead*. Terminology matters in 7-mark answers.
>
> 5. **Missing `e.getMessage()`** тАФ The variable `e` carries diagnostic information. KTU evaluators expect at least a call to `e.getMessage()` or `e.toString()` for full credit on the "explanation" sub-part.
>
> 6. **Using `Throwable` instead of `Exception` in catch** тАФ Although legal, catching `Error` (a subclass of `Throwable`) is bad practice. Examiners often deduct 1 mark for catching `Throwable` in a 14-mark question.
>
> 7. **Not closing resources** тАФ If a `try` opens a `FileReader`, `Scanner`, etc., the code should mention `finally` or try-with-resources (Java 7+). Omitting this loses **1тАУ2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- тЬЕ **Definition:** Multiple catch clauses = several `catch` blocks for one `try`, each handling a different exception type.
- тЬЕ **First-match-wins rule:** JVM checks catches top-down and executes the first match.
- тЬЕ **Order = Subclass first, Superclass last** тАФ otherwise: *unreachable code* compile error.
- тЬЕ **Multi-catch (Java 7+):** `catch (A | B e)` handles multiple types in one block; `e` is implicitly `final`.
- тЬЕ **Multi-catch restriction:** No two alternatives may be related by inheritance.
- тЬЕ **Unreachable catch** = compile error, **not** runtime error.
- тЬЕ **`finally` block** executes regardless of whether an exception occurred (use for resource cleanup).
- тЬЕ **Try alone (no catch and no finally)** = compile error.
- тЬЕ **Each catch has its own scope** for the parameter `e`.
- тЬЕ **Re-throw** using `throw e;` inside catch тАФ must declare checked exceptions.
- тЬЕ **`Throwable` is the root** of the Java exception hierarchy.
- тЬЕ **Common checked exceptions** to know: `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`.
- тЬЕ **Common unchecked (RuntimeException) exceptions** to know: `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NumberFormatException`, `ClassCastException`.
- тЬЕ **Best practice:** Catch the most specific type possible; avoid swallowing exceptions silently.
- тЬЕ **KTU 2024 Pattern:** 14-mark questions always test (1) ordering rule with a compile-time error scenario and (2) a small program demonstrating 3+ catch blocks.
- тЬЕ **Java 7+ is assumed** in KTU 2024 Scheme тАФ always mention the multi-catch feature in "differentiate" type questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"**S**ubclass **F**irst, **G**eneral **L**ast"* тАФ **S.F.G.L.** тАФ the order of catch clauses from top to bottom in any Java try-catch construct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nested try Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nested try Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Nested try Statement**: A *nested try* block in Java is an exception-handling construct in which a `try` (and its associated `catch`/`finally`) block is placed **inside the body of another `try` block**, another `catch` block, or even inside a `finally` block. When an exception is thrown inside the inner `try`, the JVM first searches for a matching `catch` in the inner scope; if no match is found, the exception **propagates upward** to the enclosing (outer) `try` block for resolution.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEERT416 тАУ Object Oriented Programming using Java, Module 3), this concept belongs to the *Exception Handling* cluster and is essential for understanding **exception propagation, scope-based resolution, and the layered recovery** model of Java.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy тАФ The Building Fire Alarm System**:
> Imagine a multi-storey office building. Each floor has its **own fire extinguisher (inner try-catch)**. If a small fire breaks out in a cubicle, the floor-level extinguisher handles it. But if the fire is too large for the floor extinguisher, the alarm is raised and the **building-level sprinkler system (outer try-catch)** activates. This is *exactly* how nested try works тАФ **localized recovery first, broader recovery as a fallback**.

## 1.3 Why Nested try? тАФ Motivation

- **Granular Recovery**: Different methods/blocks may need different recovery strategies.
- **Localized Resource Management**: Inner `finally` can close inner-loop resources before the outer handler runs.
- **Hierarchical Exception Categorization**: Inner blocks catch *specific* exceptions; outer blocks catch *generic* ones as a safety net.
- **Method-Level Encapsulation**: A method that calls a risky sub-operation can wrap it inside its own try while remaining inside another.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Nested try-catch is a *scope-based* mechanism. The **nearest applicable `catch`** wins тАФ Java does **not** search for the "best" match across scopes; it exhausts the current scope first.

## 1.4 Standard Java Syntax Skeleton

```java
try {                                  // OUTER try
    // outer protected code
    try {                              // INNER try
        // inner protected code
    } catch (InnerExceptionType e) {   // INNER catch
        // inner recovery
    } finally {                        // INNER finally (optional)
        // inner cleanup
    }
    // more outer code
} catch (OuterExceptionType e) {       // OUTER catch
    // outer recovery
}
```

The **outer `try`** is a *try statement*; the **inner `try`** is a *try statement that appears within the block of the outer try statement* тАФ and that is the *literal* definition of nesting in the Java Language Specification (JLS ┬з14.20).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Table

## 2.1 Operational Rules тАФ The "How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Cardinal Rules of Nested try in Java** (high-yield for KTU exams):

1. **Inner-first Resolution Rule**
   When an exception is thrown inside the inner `try`, the **inner `catch` clauses are searched first** in the order they appear.

2. **Propagation Rule (Fallthrough)**
   If the inner `try` (and all its `catch` blocks) does **not** handle the exception, the exception **propagates to the outer `try`'s `catch` blocks** as if it were thrown there.

3. **Scope Independence Rule**
   The outer `catch` **cannot** directly see variables declared inside the inner `try` block. Java's block-level scoping applies to exception variables too.

4. **Catch-once Rule**
   Once an exception is caught at any level, it **does not** continue to propagate to higher levels (unless re-thrown explicitly with `throw e;`).

5. **`finally` Execution Rule**
   The `finally` block of the **inner** scope runs **before** control transfers to the outer `catch` (or to the calling method). Outer `finally` runs after the outer `catch` completes.

## 2.2 Exception Flow тАФ Stepwise Logic

| Step | Event | JVM Action |
|:----:|:------|:-----------|
| 1 | Code enters outer `try` | Push outer scope on stack |
| 2 | Code enters inner `try` | Push inner scope on stack |
| 3 | Exception thrown in inner `try` | Begin inner catch search |
| 4a | Match found in inner `catch` | Execute inner catch тЖТ run inner `finally` тЖТ continue outer block |
| 4b | No match in inner `catch` | Run inner `finally` (if any) тЖТ propagate to outer scope |
| 5 | Begin outer catch search | Same matching logic at outer level |
| 6a | Match found in outer `catch` | Execute outer catch тЖТ run outer `finally` тЖТ continue |
| 6b | No match anywhere | Run all pending `finally` blocks тЖТ propagate to caller |
| 7 | Unhandled at method level | JVM default handler prints stack trace and terminates thread |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table тАФ Nested try Exception Resolution**

| Construct | Search Order | Fallback | Cleans Up |
|:----------|:------------:|:--------:|:---------:|
| `try { try { } }` (basic nesting) | Inner catch тЖТ Outer catch | JVM default handler | Inner finally тЖТ Outer finally |
| `try { catch (A e) { try {} } }` (try inside catch) | New inner try's catches only | Propagates **out of the catch** to caller's try | Inner finally in catch scope |
| `try { try { } finally { } }` (try inside finally) | Outer catch of original try | Propagates as per original try | Inner finally runs **before** outer catch |
| `try { try { } catch (A e) { catch (B e2) {} } }` (catch inside catch) | Inner catch's catches only | Re-propagated from inner catch | Standard |

| Java Exception Type | Checked? | Common Cause in Nested Contexts |
|:-------------------|:--------:|:-------------------------------|
| `ArithmeticException` | Unchecked | Division by zero inside inner try |
| `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Unchecked | Loop index error inside inner try |
| `NullPointerException` | Unchecked | Inner object deref without null check |
| `NumberFormatException` | Unchecked | `Integer.parseInt()` inside inner try |
| `IOException` | Checked | File/stream operation inside inner try |
| `FileNotFoundException` | Checked | `new FileReader(...)` inside inner try |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Nested try is used heavily in **JDBC programming** (try-with-resources nested inside transaction try), **socket programming** (per-packet try inside session try), and **recursive parsers** (per-token try inside document try). Mastering it is essential for **lab viva** and **industry-grade Java**.

## 2.4 The "Why" тАФ Why Java Designed It This Way

Java treats exceptions as **objects with lexical scope**. Block-level scoping naturally produces *hierarchical recovery*: the closer you are to the error, the more contextually aware your recovery can be. A nested `try` is, conceptually, an *early-warning subsystem* layered on top of a *general recovery subsystem* тАФ a clean realization of the **Chain-of-Responsibility design pattern** in the language itself.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Execution Analysis & Code Walkthrough

## 3.1 Reference Program тАФ Classic KTU Pattern

```java
public class NestedTryDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {                                          // [OUTER-1] begin
            int[] arr = {10, 20, 30};
            int divisor = 0;

            try {                                      // [INNER-1] begin
                int result = arr[1] / divisor;         // line A тЖТ ArithmeticException
                System.out.println("Inner result = " + result);
            } catch (ArithmeticException e) {          // [INNER-1] catch-1
                System.out.println("INNER-1 caught ArithmeticException");
                int bad = arr[5];                      // line B тЖТ ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) { // [INNER-1] catch-2 (not reached)
                System.out.println("INNER-1 caught ArrayIndex");
            } finally {                                // [INNER-1] finally
                System.out.println("INNER-1 finally executed");
            }

            System.out.println("Outer continues after inner block");

        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {   // [OUTER-1] catch-1
            System.out.println("OUTER-1 caught ArrayIndex");
        } catch (Exception e) {                        // [OUTER-1] catch-2
            System.out.println("OUTER-1 caught generic Exception");
        } finally {                                    // [OUTER-1] finally
            System.out.println("OUTER-1 finally executed");
        }
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Exhaustive Execution Trace тАФ Every Step

Let $\mathbf{T}$ = the JVM exception-resolution state at any moment. The state transitions are:

$$
\mathbf{T} = \big( \text{scope}, \text{thrown}, \text{searching}, \text{handling} \big)
$$

| Step | Code Location | Action | Resulting State |
|:----:|:--------------|:-------|:----------------|
| 1 | `main` enters | Outer scope opened | $\mathbf{T} = (\text{outer}, \text{none}, \text{false}, \text{false})$ |
| 2 | `arr` initialized | `{10,20,30}` stored | unchanged |
| 3 | `divisor = 0` | Stored | unchanged |
| 4 | Inner scope entered | Push inner frame | $\mathbf{T} = (\text{inner}, \text{none}, \text{false}, \text{false})$ |
| 5 | Line A evaluated | `30 / 0` тЖТ JVM throws `ArithmeticException` | $\mathbf{T} = (\text{inner}, \text{AE}, \text{true}, \text{false})$ |
| 6 | Inner catch-1 search | Type match `ArithmeticException` тЬУ | $\mathbf{T} = (\text{inner}, \text{AE}, \text{false}, \text{true})$ |
| 7 | Print "INNER-1 caught..." | Side effect | тАФ |
| 8 | Line B evaluated | `arr[5]` тЖТ throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | $\mathbf{T} = (\text{inner}, \text{AIOBE}, \text{true}, \text{false})$ |
| 9 | Inner catch search resumes (NOT outer!) | Type match `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` тЬУ at inner catch-2 | $\mathbf{T} = (\text{inner}, \text{AIOBE}, \text{false}, \text{true})$ |
| 10 | Print "INNER-1 caught ArrayIndex" | Side effect | тАФ |
| 11 | Inner finally | Print "INNER-1 finally executed" | Inner scope closes |
| 12 | Control returns to outer block | Print "Outer continues after inner block" | тАФ |
| 13 | Outer scope completes normally | No exception, outer catch NOT entered | тАФ |
| 14 | Outer finally | Print "OUTER-1 finally executed" | Outer scope closes |

## 3.3 Expected Output (Verifiable)

```
INNER-1 caught ArithmeticException
INNER-1 caught ArrayIndex
INNER-1 finally executed
Outer continues after inner block
OUTER-1 finally executed
```

## 3.4 Variant тАФ When Exception Escapes Inner Scope

Now consider what happens if the inner catch does **not** match a re-thrown exception:

```java
try {                                          // OUTER
    try {                                      // INNER
        String s = null;
        System.out.println(s.length());        // throws NullPointerException
    } catch (ArithmeticException e) {          // INNER catch тАФ NO match
        System.out.println("Inner AE");
    }                                          // no inner finally
    System.out.println("This line is SKIPPED");
} catch (NullPointerException e) {             // OUTER catch тАФ MATCHES
    System.out.println("OUTER caught NullPointer");
}
```

Trace:

$$
\text{Inner scope} \;\xrightarrow{\text{no match}}\; \text{Exit inner try} \;\xrightarrow{\text{propagate}}\; \text{Outer scope search}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{OUTER catch (NullPointerException) executes}
$$

Output:
```
OUTER caught NullPointer
```

## 3.5 The Re-throw Pattern (High-Yield for KTU 14-mark questions)

```java
try {                                          // OUTER
    try {                                      // INNER
        int x = 10 / 0;
    } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
        System.out.println("Inner: logging");
        throw e;                               // RE-THROW to outer scope
    }
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {
    System.out.println("Outer: re-caught the same exception");
}
```

The `throw e;` statement **re-enters the exception search** starting from the *next enclosing scope* тАФ the outer `try` in this case. This is a powerful technique used in **layered architectures** (e.g., presentation тЖТ service тЖТ DAO layers).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Execution Flow

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart тАФ Exception Resolution in Nested try

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Method] --> B[Enter OUTER try]
    B --> C[Execute outer code]
    C --> D[Enter INNER try]
    D --> E[Execute inner code]
    E --> F{Exception thrown?}
    F -- No --> G[Execute inner finally]
    G --> H[Continue outer code]
    H --> I[Execute outer finally]
    I --> J[End method normally]
    F -- Yes --> K{Inner catch matches?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Execute inner catch block]
    L --> M{New exception in catch?}
    M -- No --> G
    M -- Yes --> N{Inner catch again matches?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Execute new inner catch]
    O --> G
    N -- No --> P[Run inner finally if any]
    P --> Q[Propagate to OUTER scope]
    Q --> R{Outer catch matches?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Execute outer catch]
    S --> I
    R -- No --> T[Propagate to caller]
    T --> U[JVM default handler]
    U --> V[Stack trace printed / thread terminates]
    K -- No --> P
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OScope[OUTER SCOPE]
        OC1[OUTER try block]
        OC2[OUTER catch handler A]
        OC3[OUTER catch handler B]
        OC4[OUTER finally block]
    end
    subgraph IScope[INNER SCOPE]
        IC1[INNER try block]
        IC2[INNER catch handler 1]
        IC3[INNER catch handler 2]
        IC4[INNER finally block]
    end
    OC1 --> IC1
    IC1 --> IC2
    IC1 --> IC3
    IC1 --> IC4
    IC2 -. re-throw .-> OC2
    IC3 -. propagate .-> OC2
    IC4 --> OC1
    OC2 --> OC4
    OC3 --> OC4
    OC4 --> END[Continue execution]
```

## 4.3 Scope Search Order тАФ Visual Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Level 1: Innermost try] -->|no match| L2[Level 2: Enclosing try]
    L2 -->|no match| L3[Level 3: Method-level throws]
    L3 -->|no match| L4[Level 4: Caller's try]
    L4 -->|no match| L5[Level 5: JVM default handler]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation**: The exception "climbs the ladder" one rung at a time. At each rung, it checks *every* `catch` clause from top to bottom **before** moving up. **`finally` is NOT a rung** тАФ it executes at the level where the throw is *resolved*, just before leaving that scope.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define nested try statement in Java. When is it useful?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A nested try statement is a try-catch block placed within another try block, catch block, or finally block. *(1 mark)* When an exception occurs in the inner try, the inner catch blocks are searched first; if unhandled, the exception propagates to the outer try. *(1 mark)* It is useful when different sections of code require **different exception recovery strategies** тАФ e.g., a specific arithmetic error caught locally, but a broader I/O error handled at the outer level. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**What happens if an exception is thrown inside the inner try block and is NOT caught by any of its catch handlers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The exception propagates to the **enclosing (outer) try** block. *(1 mark)* The inner `finally` block (if present) executes *before* the propagation. *(1 mark)* The outer `try`'s catch blocks are then searched in order. If matched, the outer catch handles it and execution continues after the outer try; otherwise, it propagates further to the calling method. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: For nested try questions, the most common **mark-deduction reasons** are:
> 1. **Skipping the inner `finally` execution** in the trace (loses ~2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing "propagation" with "re-throw"** тАФ they are different! Propagation is implicit; re-throw is explicit via `throw e;`.
> 3. **Forgetting that inner catch variables shadow outer ones** тАФ a `catch (Exception e)` inside cannot be re-caught by an identical outer `catch (Exception e)` for the *same* exception object.
> 4. **Wrong output sequence** when multiple finally blocks exist тАФ always list execution in **innermost-first** order.
> 5. **Not mentioning the `throw` keyword explicitly** when the question says "re-throw".

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** тАФ *[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2022, Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the concept of **nested try statements** in Java with a suitable example. Discuss how exception propagation works when the inner catch does not handle the exception. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates a try block inside another try block, where the inner block throws an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` that is caught by the **outer** catch block (not the inner one). Also show the use of `finally` at both levels. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) тАФ 7 marks**

- **Definition** (2 marks): A nested try is a try statement placed inside the body of another try, catch, or finally block. The inner forms a *localized recovery zone*; the outer forms a *fallback zone*.
- **Propagation mechanism** (3 marks): When an exception is thrown in the inner try and no inner `catch` matches, control exits the inner try тЖТ inner `finally` runs (if any) тЖТ exception is propagated to the outer try тЖТ outer catch blocks are searched in declaration order.
- **Illustration**: Use a code skeleton showing both matched and unmatched scenarios (2 marks).

**(b) тАФ 7 marks** тАФ Reference program:

```java
public class OuterCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {                                              // OUTER
            int[] numbers = {1, 2, 3};
            try {                                          // INNER
                System.out.println("Inner: accessing index 10");
                int x = numbers[10];   // ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            } catch (ArithmeticException e) {              // INNER catch тАФ no match
                System.out.println("Inner caught AE");
            } finally {
                System.out.println("Inner finally executed");
            }
            System.out.println("This line will NOT be printed");
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {       // OUTER catch тАФ MATCHES
            System.out.println("OUTER caught ArrayIndexOutOfBounds");
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Outer finally executed");
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Inner: accessing index 10
Inner finally executed
OUTER caught ArrayIndexOutOfBounds
Outer finally executed
```

**Marking breakdown:** Correct nesting (2), exception thrown from inner (1), propagation to outer (1), outer catch matches (1), both finally blocks present and correctly ordered (2).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** тАФ *[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** With a neat diagram and example, explain **how the finally block behaves** when try blocks are nested. What is the order of execution of multiple `finally` blocks? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between **nested try statements** and **multiple catch blocks** in a single try. When would you prefer one over the other? Provide a Java code example illustrating both approaches handling the same scenario. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) тАФ 7 marks**

- **Concept** (2 marks): A `finally` block executes regardless of whether an exception is thrown or caught. When try blocks are nested, **the innermost `finally` runs first**, then control moves outward.
- **Order of execution** (3 marks): If exception occurs in inner try тЖТ inner finally тЖТ (propagate or not) тЖТ outer catch (if any) тЖТ outer finally. If no exception тЖТ inner finally (if inner try had finally) тЖТ outer finally.
- **Diagram** (2 marks): A block diagram with arrows showing the order INNER finally тЖТ OUTER finally.

**(b) тАФ 7 marks**

| Feature | Nested try | Multiple catch (single try) |
|:--------|:----------:|:---------------------------:|
| Granularity | Per-block recovery | Per-exception-type recovery |
| Code locality | Spread across scopes | Compact, flat structure |
| Use case | Different recovery for different code regions | Different recovery for different exception *types* in same region |
| Scope isolation | Each level has its own variables | All catches share the try's variable scope |

**Code example** (2 marks): Show same scenario тАФ division + array access тАФ handled first by multiple catch on a single try, then by nested try with re-throw. Conclude that **multiple catch** is preferred when exceptions come from the *same code region*; **nested try** is preferred when exceptions come from *logically distinct sub-operations* (e.g., outer = I/O, inner = parsing).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist тАФ Nested try Statements**

- тЬЕ **Definition**: A `try` block declared inside another `try`, `catch`, or `finally` block is called *nested try*.
- тЬЕ **Resolution order**: Inner catch blocks are searched **first**; only if none match does the exception propagate to the outer try.
- тЬЕ **`finally` always runs** at the scope where the throw is *resolved* тАФ innermost first, outermost last.
- тЬЕ **Re-throw** (`throw e;`) explicitly sends the exception to the **next enclosing scope**, bypassing any remaining catches in the current scope.
- тЬЕ **Scope rule**: Variables declared in an inner try (including catch parameters) are **not visible** in the outer try or outer catch.
- тЬЕ **Catch-once rule**: An exception object is caught **at most once** per scope chain unless explicitly re-thrown.
- тЬЕ **Checked vs unchecked**: Nested try does not change the checked-exception rules тАФ a checked exception must be caught or declared at *every* scope it can propagate through, OR a `throws` clause must exist on the enclosing method.
- тЬЕ **Common errors in exams**: forgetting inner finally; confusing propagation with re-throw; misordering catch clauses (subclass before superclass).
- тЬЕ **Engineering use cases**: JDBC transactions, recursive parsing, layered architectures (DAO тЖТ Service тЖТ Presentation), socket-per-message handling.
- тЬЕ **Golden rule**: Keep nesting **shallow** (тЙд 2 levels) for readability; deeper nesting usually signals a refactoring opportunity into separate methods.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

String Length
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** String Length

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 тАФ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Java programming language, the **String Length** refers to the total count of `char` values (UTF-16 code units) that constitute a `java.lang.String` object. It is retrieved by invoking the instance method `length()` declared in the `java.lang.String` class. The method is a non-static, parameter-less member function that returns an `int` value representing the sequential sequence of characters contained in the string.

$$ \text{Length}(s) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 1 \text{ for every } s[i] \in \text{UTF-16 Code Units} $$

**Method Signature (as per JDK 17+ Documentation):**

```java
public int length()
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Java, the `String` class is a **final**, **immutable** class. The `length()` method is declared as `public int length()` тАФ note the parentheses, which distinguish it from array's `length` *field*. This is one of the most frequently tested conceptual traps in KTU university examinations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The Beaded Necklace

Imagine you have a **beaded necklace**. Each bead represents one character of a string. If you ask, *"How long is this necklace?"*, you naturally start counting: bead #1, bead #2, bead #3, and so on, until you reach the last bead.

Now, here's the catch тАФ what if one of the "beads" is actually **two smaller beads glued together** (a *surrogate pair*)? In Java's UTF-16 encoding, a single emoji or rare script character may occupy two slots. The `length()` method counts **slots**, not the *visible* characters. This subtle distinction between **code units** and **code points** is critical for advanced KTU questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy Summary Table**

| Real Object | Java Equivalent | Counted By |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Beads on a necklace | `char` (UTF-16 code unit) | `String.length()` |
| Number of physical beads | Logical "characters" | `String.codePointCount(0, len)` |
| Box of chocolates | Array of items | `array.length` (field, no parens) |

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundary Values

- **Return Type:** `int` (a **32-bit signed integer**, allowing strings up to **2,147,483,647 characters**).
- **Empty String:** A string with `length() == 0` is valid and represented as `""`.
- **Maximum Practical Length:** Limited by the JVM's maximum array size (typically `Integer.MAX_VALUE - 8` for byte arrays).
- **Time Complexity:** **O(1)** тАФ Constant time, because the length is stored as a cached field inside the `String` object.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** The expression `str.length` (without parentheses) is a **compilation error** for a `String`, but `arr.length` (without parentheses) is **valid** for an array. Mixing these up costs easy marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 тАФ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Memory Representation

In Java's JDK implementation, a `String` object internally holds:

1. A `byte[]` (or `char[]` in older JVMs) holding the character data.
2. A cached `int hash` field (initialized to 0).
3. **Crucially, the length is NOT stored separately** тАФ it is computed by reading the backing array's `.length` attribute.

```java
// Simplified JDK source (Java 8 and earlier):
public final class String {
    private final char value[];   // backing character array
    // length() simply returns the array size
    public int length() {
        return value.length;
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Java 9+ Compact Strings Optimization:** Since JDK 9, strings use a `byte[]` with a `coder` flag (LATIN1 or UTF16). For ASCII content, the string is stored in **1 byte per character**, but `length()` still returns the **character count**, not byte count.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 тАФ Invocation:**
The compiler resolves `str.length()` to a method invocation on the `String` object referenced by `str`.

**Step 2 тАФ Backing Array Access:**
Internally, the method accesses the private `value` field, which is the actual character array (or byte array in compact strings).

**Step 3 тАФ Array Length Retrieval:**
It returns `value.length`, which is the count of code units (UTF-16 chars).

**Step 4 тАФ Boxing Avoidance:**
Because the return type is a primitive `int`, no autoboxing occurs, making this extremely fast.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Syntax | Return Type | Complexity |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **String Length** | `str.length()` | `int` | O(1) |
| **Array Length** | `arr.length` | `int` | O(1) |
| **Code Point Count** | `str.codePointCount(0, str.length())` | `int` | O(n) |
| **Empty Check** | `str.isEmpty()` тЖТ `str.length() == 0` | `boolean` | O(1) |
| **Blank Check (Java 11+)** | `str.isBlank()` тЖТ uses `Character.isWhitespace()` | `boolean` | O(n) |
| **Concatenation Length** | $\vert s_1 \vert + \vert s_2 \vert = \vert s_1 + s_2 \vert$ | `int` | O(1) |
| **Substring Length** | $\vert s[i..j] \vert = j - i$ | `int` | O(1) |

> **Convention used:** $\vert s \vert$ denotes the length of string $s$.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

In real-world engineering systems, string length operations are used for:

- **Input Validation:** Ensuring passwords meet minimum length (e.g., `if (pwd.length() < 8) reject`).
- **Database Column Constraints:** Mapping to `VARCHAR(n)` column sizes in SQL.
- **Network Protocols:** Validating packet payload sizes (e.g., HTTP headers).
- **Cryptographic Hashing:** Length-checking pre-image candidates before SHA-256 computation.
- **GUI Rendering:** Determining pixel widths for text layout in JavaFX/Swing applications.

> [!TIP]
> **Why O(1) matters:** A naive `length()` implementation that iterates through the array would be O(n). By caching or directly accessing the array's length field, Java achieves constant-time retrieval тАФ critical for hot-path operations in production systems processing millions of strings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 тАФ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Length of a Substring

Given a string $s$ with indices from $0$ to $n-1$, the length of a substring from index $a$ to index $b$ (inclusive) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{sub}} &= s[a..b] \text{ length} \\[6pt]
&= (b - a + 1) \\[6pt]
&= (b + 1) - a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of transition:**
- **Line 1 тЖТ Line 2:** If we start at index $a$ and count up to and including $b$, the total count is the difference plus one (the `+1` accounts for inclusive counting).
- **Line 2 тЖТ Line 3:** Algebraic rearrangement; useful when implementing `substring(beginIndex, endIndex)` where `endIndex` is **exclusive**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Concatenated String Length

For two strings $s_1$ and $s_2$, the length of their concatenation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert s_1 + s_2 \vert &= \vert s_1 \vert + \vert s_2 \vert \\[6pt]
\text{where } s_1 &= s_1[0]s_1[1]\ldots s_1[m-1] \text{ and } m = \vert s_1 \vert \\[6pt]
\text{and } s_2 &= s_2[0]s_2[1]\ldots s_2[n-1] \text{ and } n = \vert s_2 \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The `+` operator on `String` creates a **new `String` object** containing $m + n$ characters in sequence. The resulting length is simply the sum of the operand lengths.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Code Unit vs Code Point Length

For a string containing supplementary characters (e.g., emoji ЁЯША represented as surrogate pair `\uD83D\uDE00`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{length}() \text{ returns:} \quad L_{\text{codeunits}} &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \text{count}(s[i]) \\[6pt]
\text{where } \text{count}(c) &= \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } c \text{ is a BMP character} \\ 2, & \text{if } c \text{ is a high/low surrogate} \end{cases} \\[6pt]
\text{codePointCount}(0, n) &= L_{\text{actual chars}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** For a string `"ЁЯШАHi"`:
> - `length()` returns **3** (ЁЯША counts as 2 surrogate code units + 1 for 'H' + 1 for 'i').
> - `codePointCount(0, length())` returns **3** logical characters.
> - But if you asked "how many *visible* glyphs?", the answer is also **3** (ЁЯША, H, i).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Determine the length of the Java string literal `"Hello, KTU!"`.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \text{"Hello, KTU!"} \\[6pt]
\text{Characters at indices:} \quad & s[0]=\text{'H'}, \; s[1]=\text{'e'}, \; s[2]=\text{'l'}, \; s[3]=\text{'l'}, \; s[4]=\text{'o'} \\[6pt]
& s[5]=\text{','}, \; s[6]=\text{' '}, \; s[7]=\text{'K'}, \; s[8]=\text{'T'}, \; s[9]=\text{'U'}, \; s[10]=\text{'!'} \\[6pt]
\text{Total indices:} \quad & 0 \text{ to } 10 \text{ (inclusive)} = 11 \text{ positions} \\[6pt]
\therefore \quad s.\text{length}() &= 11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using substring length formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{full string}} &= s[0..10] \text{ length} \\[6pt]
&= (10 - 0 + 1) \\[6pt]
&= 11 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 тАФ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Internal Architecture of a Java String

```mermaid

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Flow of `length()` Method Invocation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000

    classDef endNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    P2["Invoke instance method length()"]:::processNode

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Length Retrieval Mechanism

```mermaid

    classDef apiBox fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000

    L1["Application Layer: User Code"]:::layerBox
    L2["Java API Layer: String.length()"]:::apiBox
    L3["JVM Layer: Internal value[] array access"]:::implBox
    L4["Hardware: Memory address of array header"]:::layerBox

    L1 -->|"s.length()"| L2
    L2 -->|"return value.length"| L3

    L2 -->|"int: 11"| L1

    style L1 font-size:13px
    style L2 font-size:13px
    style L3 font-size:13px
    style L4 font-size:13px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 тАФ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the `length()` method of the `String` class in Java. Why does it use parentheses while the array's `length` field does not?**

**Model Answer:**

The `length()` method is a public, non-static member of `java.lang.String` that returns the count of UTF-16 code units (i.e., `char` values) in the string.

**Syntax:**
```java
public int length()
```

**Why parentheses for String but not for arrays:**
- `String` is a **class**, and `length()` is a **method** (member function). All methods in Java require parentheses, even if they take no arguments.
- An **array** in Java is not a class тАФ it is a built-in language construct. Its `length` is a **public final field**, not a method, hence no parentheses.

**Example:**
```java
String s = "KTU";
int a = s.length();   // Method call -> 3
char[] c = {'K','T','U'};
int b = c.length;     // Field access -> 3
```

> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Syntax (1) + Justification with example (1) = **3 Marks**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the time complexity of the `String.length()` method? Justify your answer with respect to the internal implementation.**

**Model Answer:**

The time complexity of `String.length()` is **O(1) тАФ constant time**.

**Justification:**
- The `String` class internally maintains a `final char[] value` array (or `byte[]` in JDK 9+ compact strings).
- The `length()` method simply returns `value.length`, which is the array's built-in size property stored in the JVM's object header.
- No iteration through the character array is performed; the JVM retrieves the pre-computed size in a single memory access.

**Comparison:**
- A naive implementation that iterates char-by-char would be O(n).
- Java's optimized implementation avoids this overhead, making `length()` an O(1) operation.

> **Marks Distribution:** Complexity statement (1) + Internal implementation reasoning (1) + Comparison (1) = **3 Marks**

---

## Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, ESE Module 3]`

#### Part (a) тАФ 7 Marks

**Q: Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of `String.length()`. Explain what happens when you call `length()` on:**
**(i) An empty string**  
**(ii) A `null` string reference**  
**(iii) A string containing special characters and emojis.**

**Model Solution:**

```java
public class StringLengthDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // (i) Empty string
        String empty = "";
        System.out.println("Empty string length: " + empty.length());  // 0

        // (ii) Null string
        String nullStr = null;
        try {
            System.out.println("Null string length: " + nullStr.length());
        } catch (NullPointerException e) {
            System.out.println("Exception: Cannot invoke length() on null");
        }

        // (iii) Special characters & emoji
        String special = "Hi ЁЯША!";
        System.out.println("String: '" + special + "'");
        System.out.println("length(): " + special.length());          // 6
        System.out.println("codePointCount: " + 
            special.codePointCount(0, special.length()));             // 5
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Empty string length: 0
Exception: Cannot invoke length() on null
String: 'Hi ЁЯША!'
length(): 6
codePointCount: 5
```

**Explanation:**

**(i) Empty String:** Returns `0` without error. A `String` object exists in memory but contains no characters.

**(ii) Null Reference:** Calling `length()` on a `null` reference throws a **`NullPointerException`** at runtime because the JVM cannot dereference a `null` object to access its method.

**(iii) Emoji Handling:** The emoji ЁЯША (U+1F600) is encoded as a **surrogate pair** `\uD83D\uDE00`, occupying **2 UTF-16 code units**. So `"Hi ЁЯША!"` has 6 code units but only 5 logical code points.

> **Marks Distribution:** Program code (3) + Correct output (2) + Conceptual explanation (2) = **7 Marks**

---

#### Part (b) тАФ 7 Marks

**Q: Differentiate between `String.length()`, `Array.length`, and `String.codePointCount()`. Provide a comparative table and explain a real-world scenario where confusing them could cause a bug.**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparative Table:**

| Feature | `String.length()` | `Array.length` | `String.codePointCount()` |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Belongs to** | `java.lang.String` class | Java language (array) | `java.lang.String` class |
| **Type** | Method (requires `()`) | Field (no `()`) | Method (requires `()`) |
| **Returns** | Number of UTF-16 code units | Number of array elements | Number of Unicode code points |
| **Time Complexity** | O(1) | O(1) | O(n) |
| **Counts surrogate pairs as** | 2 | N/A (arrays are flat) | 1 |
| **Throws on null** | `NullPointerException` | `NullPointerException` | `NullPointerException` |

**Real-World Bug Scenario:**

Consider a chat application that stores user messages in a database with a `VARCHAR(10)` column, assuming 10 characters fit. However, if a user sends the message `"HelloЁЯШАWorld"` (10 visible characters), the actual `length()` returns **12** (because ЁЯША is 2 code units), causing a **database insertion error** or silent truncation.

**Correct Approach:**
```java
if (msg.codePointCount(0, msg.length()) <= 10) {
    database.save(msg);
} else {
    System.out.println("Message too long!");
}
```

> **Marks Distribution:** Table (3) + Real-world scenario (2) + Correct solution code (2) = **7 Marks**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, ESE Module 3]`

#### Part (a) тАФ 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the internal storage of strings in Java with respect to `length()`. How does the JDK 9+ Compact Strings optimization affect the `length()` return value?**

**Model Solution:**

**Pre-JDK 9 Storage (Legacy):**
- Each `String` contained a `final char[] value` array.
- Each character was **2 bytes** (UTF-16).
- `length()` returned `value.length`.

```java
// Legacy String class
public final class String {
    private final char value[];
    private int hash;
    public int length() { return value.length; }
}
```

**JDK 9+ Compact Strings Optimization:**
- Strings now use `byte[] value` and a `byte coder` flag.
- **LATIN1 (single-byte):** Used for ASCII-only strings. Each char is 1 byte.
- **UTF16:** Used for strings with non-ASCII characters. Each char is 2 bytes.

```java
// Modern String class (JDK 9+)
public final class String {
    private final byte[] value;
    private final byte coder;   // LATIN1 or UTF16
    public int length() { 
        return coder == LATIN1 ? value.length : value.length >> 1; 
    }
}
```

**Effect on `length()` Return Value:**
- For `"Hello"`, `value.length` is **5 bytes**, `coder` is LATIN1 тЖТ returns **5**.
- For `"H├йllo"`, `value.length` is **6 bytes** (UTF16), `coder` is UTF16 тЖТ returns `6 >> 1 = 3`... 

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** The actual implementation uses a **separate `count` field** in modern JDKs to avoid the bit-shift complexity. The conceptual point remains: `length()` always returns the **character count**, not the byte count.

**Memory Savings:**
- ASCII-heavy applications (typical web/server workloads) save **~50% heap memory** for string storage.

> **Marks Distribution:** Legacy explanation (2) + JDK 9+ details (3) + Effect on length() (2) = **7 Marks**

---

#### Part (b) тАФ 7 Marks

**Q: Write a Java program that:**
1. Takes a string input from the user.
2. Counts and displays:
   - Total length using `length()`.
   - Number of vowels (using `charAt(i)` and `length()`).
   - Number of words (using `split()` and `length()`).
3. Handles edge cases: empty string and string with only spaces.

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class StringAnalysis {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter a string: ");
        String input = sc.nextLine();

        // (i) Total length
        int totalLength = input.length();
        System.out.println("Total length: " + totalLength);

        // (ii) Vowel count
        int vowelCount = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < input.length(); i++) {
            char ch = Character.toLowerCase(input.charAt(i));
            if (ch == 'a' || ch == 'e' || ch == 'i' || 
                ch == 'o' || ch == 'u') {
                vowelCount++;
            }
        }
        System.out.println("Number of vowels: " + vowelCount);

        // (iii) Word count
        if (input.trim().isEmpty()) {
            System.out.println("Number of words: 0");
        } else {
            String[] words = input.trim().split("\\s+");
            System.out.println("Number of words: " + words.length);
        }

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Run 1:**
```
Enter a string: Hello World KTU
Total length: 15
Number of vowels: 3
Number of words: 3
```

**Sample Run 2 (Edge Case):**
```
Enter a string:    
Total length: 3
Number of vowels: 0
Number of words: 0
```

**Explanation of Edge Case Handling:**
- `input.trim().isEmpty()` checks whether the string contains **only whitespace**. If true, we report 0 words instead of `split()` returning `[""]` (which would give a word count of 1 incorrectly).
- The regex `\\s+` splits on **one or more whitespace characters**, handling multiple consecutive spaces gracefully.

> **Marks Distribution:** Input handling (1) + Length & vowel logic (2) + Word count logic (2) + Edge case handling (2) = **7 Marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting parentheses:** Writing `str.length` instead of `str.length()` тАФ **2 marks deducted** (compilation error).
> 2. **NullPointerException oversight:** Not handling null inputs тАФ **1-2 marks deducted** for incomplete programs.
> 3. **Surrogate pair confusion:** Claiming `"ЁЯША"` has `length() == 1` тАФ **2 marks deducted**.
> 4. **Off-by-one in loops:** Using `<=` instead of `<` in `for (int i = 0; i < str.length(); i++)` тАФ causes `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException`.
> 5. **Empty string vs null confusion:** An empty string `""` is a **valid object** (length 0), while `null` is **no object at all** (causes NPE).

---

## ЁЯУМ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- тЬЕ `String.length()` is a **method** (uses parentheses) that returns an `int` тАФ count of UTF-16 code units.
- тЬЕ `Array.length` is a **field** (no parentheses) тАФ do not confuse them.
- тЬЕ Time complexity is **O(1)** тАФ constant time, due to cached array size.
- тЬЕ **Empty string** `""` has length `0` and is a valid, non-null object.
- тЬЕ **`null` reference** causes `NullPointerException` when `length()` is invoked.
- тЬЕ **Emojis and surrogate pairs** count as **2 code units** in `length()` but **1 code point** in `codePointCount()`.
- тЬЕ **JDK 9+ Compact Strings** uses `byte[]` internally for ASCII optimization, but `length()` still returns character count.
- тЬЕ `String` is **immutable** тАФ concatenating does not modify the original string.
- тЬЕ Use `isEmpty()` for an empty check; use `isBlank()` (Java 11+) for whitespace-only check.
- тЬЕ Maximum theoretical string length is `Integer.MAX_VALUE` (~2.1 billion characters), limited by JVM heap.
- тЬЕ In KTU exams, always mention **UTF-16 encoding** when explaining `length()` to fetch full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special String Operations - Character Extraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Special String Operations - Character Extraction # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Character Extraction in Java `String` Class** > In Java, **character extraction** refers to the set of predefined instance methods of the immutable `java.lang.String` class that allow a programmer to retrieve, copy, or convert the underlying sequence of Unicode `char` values that constitute a `String` object. Because every `String` internally maintains a `final char[] value[]` array (since Java 9 it is stored as a `byte[]` with a `coder` field, but the logical interface remains `char`-based), these methods act as the **gateway between textual abstractions and primitive `char`/`byte` data types**. The Java Standard Edition (Java SE) API provides **four canonical character extraction methods**, each with a distinct return semantics: | # | Method Signature | Return Type | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `public char charAt(int index)` | `char` | Returns **one** character at a given index. | | 2 | `public void getChars(int srcBegin, int srcEnd, char[] dst, int dstBegin)` | `void` | Copies a **range** of characters into an external `char[]`. | | 3 | `public byte[] getBytes()` | `byte[]` | Encodes the `String` into a platform-default byte array. | | 4 | `public char[] toCharArray()` | `char[]` | Returns a **deep copy** of all characters as a `char[]`. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Relevance**: As per the *PEERT416* syllabus (Module 3 тАУ Special String Operations), these methods are assessable under **CO3**: *тАЬApply object-oriented programming concepts using strings, vectors, and inheritance to develop Java applications.тАЭ* Mastery of character extraction is a prerequisite for later modules covering I/O streams, regular expressions, and network programming. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **The Library Bookshelf Analogy ЁЯУЪ** > Imagine a `String` as a **locked, glass-fronted bookshelf** containing books labelled with characters from index `0` to `length() - 1`. You cannot insert or remove books (strings are immutable), but the library offers you four *windows* through which you may examine the contents: > > 1. **`charAt(i)`** тАФ a **magnifying glass** that lets you read the spine label of **one specific book** at position `i`. > 2. **`getChars(begin, end, dst, dstBegin)`** тАФ a **photocopying machine** that copies a *range* of books onto a tray (`char[]`) of your choosing, starting at slot `dstBegin`. > 3. **`getBytes()`** тАФ a **translation service** that converts every book label into its numeric shelf-code (UTF-8/ASCII byte), useful when shipping the bookshelf across borders (network/file I/O). > 4. **`toCharArray()`** тАФ a **full clone** of the entire bookshelf delivered to your personal desk, so you may rearrange, sort, or analyse the books freely. This intuition immediately clarifies *why* these methods exist: text data must frequently cross boundaries between **high-level `String` objects** and **low-level `char[]` / `byte[]` structures** used by compilers, I/O subsystems, and cryptographic routines. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Constants in Character Extraction** > - **Index base**: Java uses **0-based indexing** тАФ the first character lives at index `0`, the last at `index = length() - 1`. > - **Valid index range**: $0 \le i < \text{length}()$ > - **`char` size in JVM**: **16 bits (2 bytes)** тАФ JavaтАЩs `char` is based on the original **UTF-16** encoding, so it can represent any Unicode code point in the Basic Multilingual Plane (BMP), $U+0000$ to $U+FFFF$. > - **Default platform charset for `getBytes()`** (no-arg form): the JVMтАЩs **default charset**, typically `UTF-8` on Linux/macOS and `windows-1252` (Cp1252) on legacy Western Windows installations. --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Method-by-Method Logical Breakdown ### 2.1.1 `charAt(int index)` The simplest and most frequently used character extraction routine. It is an **O(1)** constant-time operation because the JVM performs a direct array access on the backing `char[]`. $$ \text{charAt}(i) \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{value}[i] & \text{if } 0 \le i < \text{length}() \\ \text{throws } \texttt{StringIndexOutOfBoundsException} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ - **Parameter**: `index` тАФ the position of the desired character. - **Returns**: the `char` value at that position. - **Throws**: `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` (a `RuntimeException`) when `index < 0` or `index \ge length()`. ### 2.1.2 `getChars(int srcBegin, int srcEnd, char[] dst, int dstBegin)` A **bulk-copy** primitive that transfers characters from a source range `[srcBegin, srcEnd)` (half-open interval) into a destination array. It does *not* allocate a new array тАФ you supply the target buffer. $$ \text{getChars}(b, e, \text{dst}, d) \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{dst}[d + k] \;=\; \text{value}[b + k], \quad \forall k \in [0,\; e-b) $$ - **Parameters**: - `srcBegin` тАФ index of the first character to copy (inclusive). - `srcEnd` тАФ index **one past** the last character to copy (exclusive). - `dst` тАФ the destination `char[]` buffer. - `dstBegin` тАФ the offset into `dst` where copying begins. - **Throws**: `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` if any of the indices are negative or exceed bounds of either the source or the destination. > [!NOTE] > The half-open interval `[srcBegin, srcEnd)` is the *same convention* used by `substring()`, `subSequence()`, and most ranges in the Java Collections Framework тАФ a deliberate API consistency. ### 2.1.3 `getBytes()` Encodes the `String` into a sequence of bytes using a **charset**. The no-argument form uses the **JVM default charset**, which is **strongly discouraged** for portable applications. The recommended forms accept a `Charset` or charset name. $$ \text{getBytes}(\text{charset}) \;:\; \texttt{String} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{byte[]} $$ - **Returns**: a freshly allocated `byte[]` (length may differ from `char[]` length, e.g. UTF-8 expansion of non-ASCII characters). - **Modern overloads (Java 1.6+)**: - `getBytes(Charset charset)` - `getBytes(String charsetName)` - **Throws**: `UnsupportedEncodingException` (for the `String` overload only) тАФ a checked exception. ### 2.1.4 `toCharArray()` Returns a **brand-new `char[]`** containing *every* character of the string. This is the canonical bridge from the immutable `String` world into a mutable `char[]` world тАФ essential when you must sort, reverse, or apply in-place transformations before reconstructing the result. $$ \text{toCharArray}() \;:\; \texttt{String} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{char}[], \quad \vert\,\text{result}\,\vert \;=\; \text{length}() $$ - **Time complexity**: $\mathcal{O}(n)$ where $n = \text{length}()$. - **Memory**: allocates a new array of exactly $n$ `char` slots тАФ typically $2n$ bytes plus the object header overhead. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Table тАФ Character Extraction Methods** | Method | Signature (Pseudo-formal) | Time | Space | Boundary Condition | Exception | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | `charAt` | $\text{charAt} : \mathbb{Z} \to \texttt{char}$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $0 \le i < \text{len}$ | `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` | | `getChars` | $\text{getChars} : (b,e,\text{dst},d) \to \texttt{void}$ | $\mathcal{O}(e-b)$ | $\mathcal{O}(e-b)$ | $0 \le b < e \le \text{len}$, $d \ge 0$, $d+(e-b) \le \vert\,\text{dst}\,\vert$ | `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` | | `getBytes` | $\text{getBytes} : \texttt{Charset} \to \texttt{byte[]}$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | Always succeeds for valid Charset | `UnsupportedEncodingException` (String overload) | | `toCharArray` | $\text{toCharArray} : \emptyset \to \texttt{char[]}$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | Always succeeds | None | > **Mnemonic** тАФ *тАЬC-G-G-TтАЭ* = **C**harAt, **G**etChars, **G**etBytes, **T**oCharArray. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Domain | Why Character Extraction Matters | |---|---| | **Compiler Design** | Lexers tokenize source code character-by-character using `charAt()` walking the source `String`. | | **Network Protocols (HTTP, SMTP)** | Headers and payloads are transmitted as `byte[]`; servers call `getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8)` to marshal outgoing text. | | **Cryptography** | AES/DES operate on `byte[]`; `getBytes()` converts plaintext into cipher input. | | **Database Drivers (JDBC)** | Parameter values must be encoded as bytes for wire transfer; `getBytes()` is invoked under the hood. | | **Text Editors / IDEs** | Buffers typically store text in `char[]` (or `byte[]` post-Java 9) for O(1) indexing; `toCharArray()` bridges SwingтАЩs `JTextComponent` with the `Document` model. | | **Image Steganography** | Bit-level extraction of hidden messages requires `getBytes()` + `charAt()` for LSB analysis. | --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example A тАФ `charAt()` Walk-through **Given `String s = "Hello";`** with `s.length() == 5` and valid indices $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$. | Call | Index Validity | Result | Exception? | |---|---|---|---| | `s.charAt(0)` | $0 \in [0, 5)$ | `'H'` | No | | `s.charAt(4)` | $4 \in [0, 5)$ | `'o'` | No | | `s.charAt(5)` | $5 \notin [0, 5)$ | тАФ | `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` | | `s.charAt(-1)` | $-1 \notin [0, 5)$ | тАФ | `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` | ### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation For an arbitrary index $i$, the JVM executes the following verification: $$ \text{check}(i) \;=\; (i < 0) \,\vee\, (i \ge \text{length}()) $$ $$ \text{access}(i) \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{throw StringIndexOutOfBoundsException} & \text{if } \text{check}(i) = \text{true} \\ \text{value}[i] & \text{if } \text{check}(i) = \text{false} \end{cases} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{For } i = 0: & \quad (0 < 0) \vee (0 \ge 5) = \text{false} \vee \text{false} = \text{false} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \text{access}(0) = \text{value}[0] = \text{`H'} \\[4pt] \text{For } i = 5: & \quad (5 < 0) \vee (5 \ge 5) = \text{false} \vee \text{true} = \text{true} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \text{access}(5) = \text{throw StringIndexOutOfBoundsException} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example B тАФ `getChars()` Range Copy **Problem**: Given `String s = "Hello World";` copy the substring `"World"` into a destination `char[]` buffer of length 10 starting at offset 2. **Step 1 тАФ Identify source range**: `"World"` occupies indices `6, 7, 8, 9, 10`. Thus `srcBegin = 6`, `srcEnd = 11` (exclusive). **Step 2 тАФ Verify destination capacity**: destination length must satisfy `dstBegin + (srcEnd - srcBegin) тЙд dst.length`, i.e. $2 + 5 = 7 \le 10$ тЬУ. **Step 3 тАФ Apply the copy formula**: $$ \text{dst}[2 + k] \;=\; \text{s.value}[6 + k], \quad \forall k \in [0, 5) $$ **Step 4 тАФ Trace each $k$ explicitly**: | $k$ | $\text{src index} = 6 + k$ | $\text{dst index} = 2 + k$ | Character copied | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 6 | 2 | `'W'` | | 1 | 7 | 3 | `'o'` | | 2 | 8 | 4 | `'r'` | | 3 | 9 | 5 | `'l'` | | 4 | 10 | 6 | `'d'` | **Step 5 тАФ Final buffer state**: $$ \text{dst} \;=\; [\,\_,\; \_,\; \texttt{'W'},\; \texttt{'o'},\; \texttt{'r'},\; \texttt{'l'},\; \texttt{'d'},\; \_,\; \_,\; \_ \,] $$ where `\_` denotes uninitialised slots (or `'\u0000'` after default initialisation). ## 3.3 Worked Example C тАФ `toCharArray()` vs Direct Access **Problem**: Reverse the string `"Java"` in place using character extraction. ### Na├пve Attempt (IMPOSSIBLE) ```java String s = "Java"; s.toCharArray()[0] = 'a'; // does NOT modify s System.out.println(s); // still prints "Java" ``` **Why?** Because `String` objects are **immutable**; the internal `value[]` is `final`. `toCharArray()` returns a *new* `char[]` that is independent of the original. ### Correct Approach Using `toCharArray()` ```java String s = "Java"; char[] arr = s.toCharArray(); // allocate fresh array int n = arr.length; // n = 4 // two-pointer swap for (int i = 0, j = n - 1; i < j; ++i, --j) { char tmp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = tmp; } String reversed = new String(arr); // wrap back into String ``` ### Mathematical Formulation of the Reversal Let the original string $S = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots s_{n-1}$ where $s_i = \text{charAt}(i)$. The reversed character array $\text{arr}'$ is defined by: $$ \text{arr}'[i] \;=\; s_{n-1-i}, \quad \forall i \in [0, n) $$ **Trace for `"Java"`** ($n = 4$): | $i$ | $\text{arr}[i]$ before | $\text{arr}[i]$ after iteration 1 | After iteration 2 | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | `'J'` | `'a'` | `'a'` | | 1 | `'a'` | `'v'` | `'v'` | | 2 | `'v'` | `'a'` | `'a'` | | 3 | `'a'` | `'J'` | `'J'` | Final reversed array: `['a', 'v', 'a', 'J']` тЖТ `"avaJ"` тЬУ. ## 3.4 Worked Example D тАФ `getBytes()` Charset Sensitivity For the same `String s = "Caf├й";` (note the accented `'├й'`, Unicode `U+00E9`): | Charset | `getBytes()` Output (numeric) | Length | Decoded back | |---|---|---|---| | **UTF-8** (default on most modern systems) | `[67, 97, 102, -61, -87]` | **5** | `"Caf├й"` | | **ISO-8859-1** (Latin-1) | `[67, 97, 102, -23]` | **4** | `"Caf├й"` | | **UTF-16BE** | `[0, 67, 0, 97, 0, 102, 0, -23]` | **8** | `"Caf├й"` | > [!TIP] > **Engineering takeaway**: `'├й'` (a single BMP code point) expands to **2 bytes in UTF-8** because its code-point value (`0xE9 = 233`) lies in the range `0x80тАУ0x7FF`. Always specify the charset explicitly in production code: > > ```java > byte[] payload = message.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8); > ``` ### Mathematical Derivation of UTF-8 Byte Length For a Unicode code point $U$: $$ \text{bytes}(U) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } 0 \le U \le 0x7F \\ 2 & \text{if } 0x80 \le U \le 0x7FF \\ 3 & \text{if } 0x800 \le U \le 0xFFFF \\ 4 & \text{if } 0x10000 \le U \le 0x10FFFF \end{cases} $$ For `'├й' = U+00E9` we have $0x80 \le 0xE9 \le 0x7FF$, hence $\text{bytes}(\text{├й}) = 2$. Combined with the 3 ASCII characters `'C'`, `'a'`, `'f'` (each 1 byte), the total UTF-8 byte length is $3 + 2 = 5$ тЬУ. --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Internal Memory Layout of a `String` (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph STR_OBJ["String Object (Heap)"] STR_FINAL["final char[] value
hash int
int hash"] end subgraph CHAR_ARRAY["char[] value"] IDX0["value[0] = 'H'"] IDX1["value[1] = 'e'"] IDX2["value[2] = 'l'"] IDX3["value[3] = 'l'"] IDX4["value[4] = 'o'"] end STR_FINAL --> CHAR_ARRAY IDX0 --- IDX1 --- IDX2 --- IDX3 --- IDX4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram**: The `String` object on the heap holds a *reference* to a backing `char[]`.
