## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q1. What is the return value of `String.indexOf()` when the target substring is not found? Justify why this specific value is used.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

When the target substring is not found, the `indexOf()` method returns **-1** (minus one).

*Justification (2 marks):* The value $-1$ is used as the universal "not found" sentinel in Java because it falls **outside the valid index range** of any string. Since valid indices are in the range $[0, \text{length}-1]$, the value $-1$ is mathematically guaranteed to never collide with a legitimate position. This makes it an **unambiguous marker** for failure, allowing programmers to check for matches with a simple conditional like `if (str.indexOf(sub) != -1)`. Using any non-negative value (like 0) would be ambiguous because 0 is a valid index for the first character of the string.

---

> **[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Q2. Differentiate between `indexOf()` and `lastIndexOf()` methods of the `String` class in Java. Provide one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | `indexOf()` | `lastIndexOf()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Search Direction** | Left-to-right (forward) | Right-to-left (backward) |
| **Return Value** | Index of the **first** occurrence | Index of the **last** occurrence |
| **Failure Value** | $-1$ | $-1$ |

**Example (1 mark):**
For `String s = "banana";`
- `s.indexOf('a')` returns `1` (the first `'a'`)
- `s.lastIndexOf('a')` returns `5` (the last `'a'`)

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam тАУ Model Paper based on 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the various `indexOf()` and `lastIndexOf()` method overloads available in Java's `String` class. Provide the method signatures, return types, and a brief description of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java program that takes a sentence as input from the user, counts the number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u) using the `indexOf()` and `charAt()` methods, and displays the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation (7 Marks):**

The `String` class in Java provides **four overloads of `indexOf()`** and **four overloads of `lastIndexOf()`**, distinguished by the type of argument (`int` for characters, `String` for substrings) and the inclusion of a starting index.

**`indexOf()` overloads:**

1. **`int indexOf(int ch)`** тАФ Searches the entire string for the first occurrence of character `ch` (specified as a Unicode value). Returns the index of the match, or $-1$ if not found.

2. **`int indexOf(int ch, int fromIndex)`** тАФ Searches for character `ch` starting at index `fromIndex` and proceeding to the end. The character at `fromIndex` is included in the search.

3. **`int indexOf(String str)`** тАФ Searches the entire string for the first occurrence of the substring `str`. Returns the starting index of the match, or $-1$ if not found.

4. **`int indexOf(String str, int fromIndex)`** тАФ Searches for substring `str` starting at index `fromIndex`. The character at `fromIndex` is included.

**`lastIndexOf()` overloads:**

1. **`int lastIndexOf(int ch)`** тАФ Searches the entire string backwards for the last occurrence of character `ch`. Returns the index of the last match, or $-1$ if not found.

2. **`int lastIndexOf(int ch, int fromIndex)`** тАФ Searches backwards starting at `fromIndex`. The character at `fromIndex` is included.

3. **`int lastIndexOf(String str)`** тАФ Searches backwards for the last occurrence of substring `str`.

4. **`int lastIndexOf(String str, int fromIndex)`** тАФ Searches backwards from `fromIndex` for the last occurrence of `str`.

All eight methods are defined in the `java.lang.String` class and are available to every `String` object in Java. The parameter `int ch` accepts a Unicode code point, allowing the search for any character from any language.

**(b) Java Program (7 Marks):**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class VowelCounter {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter a sentence: ");
        String sentence = sc.nextLine().toLowerCase();
        
        int vowelCount = 0;
        String vowels = "aeiou";
        
        for (int i = 0; i < sentence.length(); i++) {
            char ch = sentence.charAt(i);
            if (vowels.indexOf(ch) != -1) {
                vowelCount++;
            }
        }
        
        System.out.println("Number of vowels: " + vowelCount);
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Output Trace:**
For input `"Hello World"`:
1. `sentence.toLowerCase()` тЖТ `"hello world"`
2. Iteration:
   - `i=0`: `'h'` тЖТ `vowels.indexOf('h')` returns $-1$ тЖТ skip
   - `i=1`: `'e'` тЖТ `vowels.indexOf('e')` returns $1$ тЖТ count $= 1$
   - `i=4`: `'o'` тЖТ `vowels.indexOf('o')` returns $4$ тЖТ count $= 2$
   - `i=7`: `'o'` тЖТ `vowels.indexOf('o')` returns $4$ тЖТ count $= 3$
3. **Final Output:** `Number of vowels: 3`

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Explain the `startsWith()`, `endsWith()`, and `contains()` methods of the `String` class with suitable examples. What is the return type and behavior of each method? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to validate an email address entered by the user using string searching methods. The email must contain exactly one `'@'` symbol, end with `".com"`, and must not start with `'@'`. Display appropriate validation messages. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation (7 Marks):**

**1. `startsWith(String prefix)` тАФ (2 Marks)**
- **Signature:** `public boolean startsWith(String prefix)`
- **Return Type:** `boolean`
- **Behavior:** Returns `true` if the string begins with the specified prefix, `false` otherwise.
- **Overload:** `startsWith(String prefix, int offset)` checks if the substring starting at `offset` begins with `prefix`.
- **Example:** `"https://ktu.edu".startsWith("https")` returns `true`.

**2. `endsWith(String suffix)` тАФ (2 Marks)**
- **Signature:** `public boolean endsWith(String suffix)`
- **Return Type:** `boolean`
- **Behavior:** Returns `true` if the string ends with the specified suffix, `false` otherwise.
- **Example:** `"report.pdf".endsWith(".pdf")` returns `true`.

**3. `contains(CharSequence s)` тАФ (3 Marks)**
- **Signature:** `public boolean contains(CharSequence s)`
- **Return Type:** `boolean`
- **Behavior:** Returns `true` if the string contains the specified sequence of character values. This method was introduced in **Java 5** and is implemented internally using `indexOf()`. The argument can be any `CharSequence` (e.g., `String`, `StringBuilder`).
- **Example:** `"Hello World".contains("World")` returns `true`. `"Hello".contains("xyz")` returns `false`.

**(b) Java Program (7 Marks):**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class EmailValidator {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter email: ");
        String email = sc.nextLine();
        
        int atIndex = email.indexOf('@');
        int lastAtIndex = email.lastIndexOf('@');
        int atCount = lastAtIndex - atIndex >= 0 ? 
            (lastAtIndex == atIndex ? 1 : -1) : 0;
        
        if (atIndex == 0) {
            System.out.println("Invalid: Email cannot start with '@'");
        } else if (atIndex == -1) {
            System.out.println("Invalid: Missing '@' symbol");
        } else if (lastAtIndex != atIndex) {
            System.out.println("Invalid: Multiple '@' symbols");
        } else if (!email.endsWith(".com")) {
            System.out.println("Invalid: Must end with .com");
        } else {
            System.out.println("Valid Email Address");
        }
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Output Trace Examples:**
| Input | Validation Logic | Output |
|---|---|---|
| `admin@ktu.com` | `atIndex=5`, `lastAtIndex=5`, ends with `.com` | `Valid Email Address` |
| `@ktu.com` | `atIndex=0` | `Invalid: Email cannot start with '@'` |
| `adminktu.com` | `atIndex=-1` | `Invalid: Missing '@' symbol` |
| `a@b@c.com` | `atIndex=1`, `lastAtIndex=3` | `Invalid: Multiple '@' symbols` |
| `admin@ktu.in` | does not end with `.com` | `Invalid: Must end with .com` |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting 0-based indexing:** Students often write `charAt(1)` expecting the first character. Always remember: **first character = index 0**.
> 
> 2. **Confusing `.length` with `.length()`:** In Java, arrays use the field `.length` (no parentheses), while Strings use the **method** `.length()`. Writing `str.length` (without parentheses) is a compilation error.
> 
> 3. **Using `==` instead of `.equals()`:** When comparing the result of `indexOf()` with $-1$, use `if (str.indexOf(s) == -1)`. Do not confuse this with String content comparison, which requires `.equals()`.
> 
> 4. **Assuming `contains()` works on older Java:** The `contains()` method was introduced in **Java 5 (JDK 1.5)**. For pre-1.5 compatibility, students should use `str.indexOf(s) >= 0` instead.
> 
> 5. **Not handling `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException`:** Calling `charAt(i)` with an invalid index throws a runtime exception. In KTU programs, always validate the index range before calling `charAt()`.
> 
> 6. **Treating `indexOf(char, fromIndex)` as exclusive:** The `fromIndex` parameter is **inclusive**. The character at position `fromIndex` is part of the search.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for String Searching:**

- тЬЕ Java uses **0-based indexing** for all string character positions.
- тЬЕ **`indexOf()`** performs a **forward** search and returns the index of the **first** occurrence.
- тЬЕ **`lastIndexOf()`** performs a **backward** search and returns the index of the **last** occurrence.
- тЬЕ Both methods return **-1** on failure. This is the universal "not found" sentinel.
- тЬЕ **`charAt(int index)`** retrieves a single character; throws `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` for invalid indices.
- тЬЕ **`startsWith(prefix)`** and **`endsWith(suffix)`** return `boolean` and check string boundaries.
- тЬЕ **`contains(CharSequence s)`** was introduced in **Java 5**; equivalent to `indexOf(s.toString()) >= 0`.
- тЬЕ **`regionMatches(ignoreCase, toffset, other, ooffset, len)`** performs partial substring comparison with optional case-insensitivity.
- тЬЕ String searching methods are defined in the **`java.lang.String`** class and are available to all `String` objects without explicit import.
- тЬЕ The `String` class is **immutable** тАФ none of the search methods modify the original string.
- тЬЕ For **complex pattern matching** (e.g., email validation, phone number formats), use the `java.util.regex.Pattern` and `java.util.regex.Matcher` classes.
- тЬЕ The `StringTokenizer` class (from `java.util`) is a **legacy** tool for splitting strings by delimiters; modern code prefers `String.split()` with regular expressions.
- тЬЕ Time complexity of `indexOf()` is $O(n \cdot m)$ in the worst case, where $n$ is the length of the string and $m$ is the length of the search pattern.
- тЬЕ `String.length()` is a **method call** (with parentheses), not a property тАФ unlike C/C++ arrays.
- тЬЕ The `indexOf(int ch, int fromIndex)` parameter is **inclusive** тАФ the search begins at `fromIndex`, not after it.
- тЬЕ All `indexOf` and `lastIndexOf` methods accept `int` for character arguments because Java represents characters as **Unicode code points** internally.
- тЬЕ Java's `String` class implements the **`CharSequence`** interface, enabling polymorphic search across `String`, `StringBuilder`, and `StringBuffer`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modifying Strings, Multithreaded Programming - The Java Thread Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modifying Strings, Multithreaded Programming - The Java Thread Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Modifying Strings in Java тАФ Formal Definition

In Java, **String** objects are *immutable* тАФ once a `String` object is created, its contents cannot be changed. Any operation that appears to "modify" a string actually returns a **new String object**, leaving the original untouched in the **String Constant Pool**.

To overcome this performance overhead, Java provides two mutable string-handling classes defined in `java.lang`:

| Class | Thread-Safety | Performance | Introduced In |
|---|---|---|---|
| `StringBuffer` | **Thread-safe (synchronized)** | Slower | JDK 1.0 |
| `StringBuilder` | **Not thread-safe (unsynchronized)** | Faster | JDK 1.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A `String` is immutable because the designers of Java wanted security, caching (String Pool), and safe use as keys in `HashMap`. When you need *frequent in-place modifications*, you must use `StringBuffer` (older, synchronized) or `StringBuilder` (newer, faster, non-synchronized).

---

### 1.2 The Java Thread Model тАФ Formal Definition

A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution within a process. The **Java Thread Model** is built directly on the underlying OS thread model via native code support, providing:

1. A single, consistent API for multithreading across all platforms.
2. Built-in support for `Thread` class and `Runnable` interface (`java.lang`).
3. **Pre-emptive scheduling** (on most JVMs) тАФ the OS decides when to context-switch.
4. Each Java thread has its own **stack**, **program counter (PC)**, and **local variables**, but shares **heap memory** with other threads in the same process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Multithreading):** Imagine a single cook (single-threaded program) preparing a 3-course meal. He chops vegetables, then boils rice, then fries paneer тАФ *one task at a time*. Now, hire **3 cooks** (multi-threaded program) тАФ one chops, one boils, one fries тАФ *simultaneously*! Each cook has their own cutting board and knife (**private stack**), but they all share the same kitchen pantry and gas stove (**shared heap memory**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Mutable Strings):** Think of a `String` as a **photograph** тАФ once printed, you cannot erase a part of it. If you want to change it, you must reprint a new photograph (`String s2 = s1.replace(...)`). A `StringBuilder` is like a **whiteboard** тАФ you can erase, append, and rewrite as many times as you want, on the *same* board.

---

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

- **Course Outcome (CO) mapping:** This topic directly supports **CO3** тАФ *Apply object-oriented concepts such as inheritance, polymorphism, and multithreading to develop Java programs.*
- **Bloom's Level:** Apply / Analyze.
- **Weightage:** Usually 14-mark question in **Module 3** ESE, often combined with inheritance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The String Class тАФ Why It Is Immutable

The `String` class is declared as `public final class String` in `java.lang`. The internal storage is a `private final char[] value` (in Java 8 and below) or `byte[]` (compact strings in Java 9+).

**Three pillars of String immutability:**

1. **Security** тАФ Strings are used in classloaders, file paths, network URLs, database connections. A mutable string could be hijacked mid-execution.
2. **String Constant Pool** тАФ JVM caches string literals. If strings were mutable, the same literal `"hello"` shared by 1000 references could be corrupted by any one of them.
3. **Hashcode caching** тАФ `String.hashCode()` is computed once and cached, enabling O(1) lookup in `HashMap`. This is safe only because the value never changes.

---

### 2.2 StringBuffer vs StringBuilder тАФ The Comparison

| Feature | `StringBuffer` | `StringBuilder` |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.lang` | `java.lang` |
| Mutability | Mutable | Mutable |
| Thread-safety | **Synchronized** (all methods are `synchronized`) | **Not synchronized** |
| Performance | Slower (lock overhead) | Faster (no locking) |
| Initial Capacity | **16 characters** | **16 characters** |
| Auto-growth | `2 * old + 2` | `2 * old + 2` |
| Since | JDK 1.0 | JDK 1.5 |
| Recommended Use | Multi-threaded environments | Single-threaded environments (default choice) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Differentiate between String, StringBuffer, and StringBuilder."* тАФ Memorize the table above verbatim.

---

### 2.3 Key Methods of StringBuffer / StringBuilder

| Method | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `append(x)` | Adds `x` at the end | `sb.append("Java")` |
| `insert(int offset, x)` | Inserts `x` at `offset` | `sb.insert(0, "Hi ")` |
| `replace(int start, int end, str)` | Replaces characters in range | `sb.replace(0, 3, "Bye")` |
| `delete(int start, int end)` | Removes chars in range `[start, end)` | `sb.delete(0, 5)` |
| `deleteCharAt(int index)` | Removes char at `index` | `sb.deleteCharAt(0)` |
| `reverse()` | Reverses the character sequence | `sb.reverse()` |
| `capacity()` | Returns current allocated capacity | `sb.capacity()` |
| `length()` | Returns number of stored characters | `sb.length()` |
| `setCharAt(int index, char ch)` | Replaces char at index | `sb.setCharAt(0, 'A')` |
| `substring(int start, int end)` | Returns a new String | `sb.substring(0, 4)` |
| `toString()` | Converts to immutable `String` | `String s = sb.toString()` |

---

### 2.4 Java Thread Model тАФ Conceptual Architecture

The **Java Thread Model** (from *The Java Tutorials*, Oracle) has the following key components:

- **A `Thread` class** тАФ encapsulates a thread of execution.
- **A `Runnable` interface** тАФ defines the task to be executed.
- **A thread scheduler** тАФ managed by the JVM (which delegates to the OS).
- **Thread priorities** тАФ `MIN_PRIORITY (1)`, `NORM_PRIORITY (5)`, `MAX_PRIORITY (10)`.
- **Daemon threads** тАФ low-priority background threads (e.g., Garbage Collector).
- **Synchronization** тАФ to prevent race conditions on shared resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Important Fact:** The main thread is created automatically when the JVM starts. You can obtain it via `Thread.currentThread()`.

---

### 2.5 Two Ways to Create a Thread

**Method 1: Extending `Thread` class**

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        // task code
    }
}
// Execution:
MyThread t = new MyThread();
t.start();   // calls run() in a NEW thread
```

**Method 2: Implementing `Runnable` interface** *(Recommended by KTU)*

```java
class MyTask implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        // task code
    }
}
// Execution:
Thread t = new Thread(new MyTask());
t.start();
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `start()` and not `run()`?** Calling `run()` directly executes the code in the **current (caller) thread** тАФ no new thread is spawned. Calling `start()` registers the thread with the scheduler and invokes `run()` in a **fresh call stack** inside a new thread.

---

### 2.6 Thread Lifecycle (KTU High-Yield Diagram Description)

A thread exists in one of these states (defined in `Thread.State` enum):

1. **New** тАФ `Thread t = new Thread(...)` тАФ not yet started.
2. **Runnable** тАФ `t.start()` called, ready to run or running.
3. **Blocked / Waiting / Timed-Waiting** тАФ temporarily inactive (`sleep()`, `wait()`, I/O).
4. **Terminated (Dead)** тАФ `run()` returned or exception escaped `run()`.

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Default thread priority | `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY = 5` | Integer $\in [1, 10]$ |
| Minimum priority | `Thread.MIN_PRIORITY = 1` | Lowest |
| Maximum priority | `Thread.MAX_PRIORITY = 10` | Highest |
| Default `StringBuffer` capacity | $C_0 = 16$ | characters |
| New capacity after overflow | $C_{new} = 2 \cdot C_{old} + 2$ | characters |
| Effective capacity formula | $C_{eff} = C_{old} \cdot 2 + 2$ | when length $\geq$ capacity |
| Stack size of main thread | **OS-dependent** (typically 512 KB) | bytes |
| `String` immutability | `final char[] value` (pre-Java 9) | bytes in UTF-16 code units |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT** confuse `length()` (number of stored characters) with `capacity()` (allocated buffer size) of `StringBuffer`. KTU often tests: *"If `StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Kerala")`, what is `sb.length()` and `sb.capacity()`?"* тЖТ length = 6, capacity = 16 + 6 = 22 (since constructor with String adds `default capacity + str.length()`).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why `new StringBuffer("Kerala").capacity() == 22`?

Let us exhaustively derive the capacity rule.

**Given:**

$$ sb = \texttt{new StringBuffer("Kerala")} $$

**Step 1 тАФ Identify the constructor used.**

The constructor `StringBuffer(String str)` is invoked.

**Step 2 тАФ Internal capacity initialization rule.**

For `StringBuffer(String str)`, the JVM sets:

$$ C_{initial} = C_{default} + \texttt{str.length()} $$

where $C_{default} = 16$ (the default empty-buffer capacity).

**Step 3 тАФ Plug in the values.**

$$ \texttt{str.length()} = \texttt{"Kerala".length()} = 6 $$

$$ C_{initial} = 16 + 6 = 22 $$

**Step 4 тАФ Conclusion.**

$$ \texttt{sb.capacity()} = 22 \quad \blacksquare $$

> Similarly, `sb.length()` = number of characters actually stored = 6.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Capacity Growth After Append Overflow

Suppose we have `StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer();` (default capacity 16). We append a 30-character string.

**Step 1 тАФ Detect overflow.**

After append, internal length $L = 30$, but $C_{old} = 16$. Since $L > C_{old}$, overflow occurs.

**Step 2 тАФ Apply growth formula.**

$$ C_{new} = 2 \cdot C_{old} + 2 $$

**Step 3 тАФ Substitute.**

$$ C_{new} = 2 \cdot 16 + 2 = 34 $$

**Step 4 тАФ Verify.**

Since $L = 30 \leq 34$, the new buffer is sufficient. Final capacity = 34. $\blacksquare$

> **General Formula:** After the $n$-th overflow, $C_n = 2 \cdot C_{n-1} + 2$, which gives the sequence: 16 тЖТ 34 тЖТ 70 тЖТ 142 тЖТ ...

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix тАФ StringBuilder vs StringBuffer Method-by-Method

| Operation | `String` (immutable) | `StringBuilder` | `StringBuffer` |
|---|---|---|---|
| `s + "World"` | Creates **new** String | N/A | N/A |
| `sb.append("World")` | N/A | Modifies in-place, **fast** | Modifies in-place, **synchronized** |
| Memory per modification | $O(n)$ new object | $O(1)$ amortized | $O(1)$ amortized |
| Safe in multi-threading? | Yes (immutable тЗТ safe) | **No** | **Yes** |
| Use case | Constant text, keys | Single-threaded loops, log building | Multi-threaded shared buffers |

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: How `Thread.start()` Works Internally

**Step 1 тАФ `new Thread(task)`** creates a Thread object in **New** state. The OS-level thread is *not* yet created.

**Step 2 тАФ `t.start()`** invokes a native method `start0()` (in C). This calls the OS to:
- Allocate a new stack (default ~512 KB).
- Register a new thread of execution with the scheduler.

**Step 3 тАФ Inside the new thread**, the JVM calls `t.run()` (the user-defined task).

**Step 4 тАФ When `run()` returns**, the thread transitions to **Terminated** state, and its stack is released.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake:** Calling `t.run()` directly does **not** create a new thread; it simply runs the code in the caller's thread, defeating the purpose of multithreading.

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Demonstrating Main Thread

```java
public class MainThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();
        System.out.println("Current thread: " + t.getName());
        t.setName("MyMain");
        System.out.println("After rename: " + t.getName());
    }
}
```

**Dry Run Trace:**

| Line | Output | State |
|---|---|---|
| `t.getName()` (before rename) | `main` | Default name |
| `t.setName("MyMain")` | (no output, mutates thread name) | Renamed |
| `t.getName()` (after rename) | `MyMain` | Now displayed |

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix тАФ Thread Creation Approaches

| Aspect | Extending `Thread` | Implementing `Runnable` |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance | Uses up the only inheritance slot (Java no multiple inheritance) | Frees the class to extend another class |
| Object sharing | Each task requires a new subclass | Multiple threads can share **one** `Runnable` instance |
| Reusability | Poor | Excellent |
| KTU Recommendation | Allowed | **Preferred** |

> **Why Runnable is preferred:** You can have `class MyTask extends Something implements Runnable`, but you cannot do `class MyTask extends Thread extends Something` in Java.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Thread Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> newState : new Thread()
    newState --> runnableState : start()
    runnableState --> blockedState : sleep()\nwait()\nI/O
    blockedState --> runnableState : notify()\ntimeout\nI/O done
    runnableState --> terminatedState : run() returns\nuncaught exception
    terminatedState --> [*]
```

> **Read this as:** A thread begins in **New** тЖТ `start()` makes it **Runnable** тЖТ it may move to **Blocked/Waiting** тЖТ eventually reaches **Terminated**, after which it cannot be restarted.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Memory Layout of a Java Process with Two Threads

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROC["JVM Process"]
        HEAP["Shared HEAP Memory\nObjects, Instance Vars, Static Vars"]
        subgraph T1["Thread 1 Stack"]
            T1PC["PC Register"]
            T1LV["Local Variables"]
            T1FR["Frame: MyThread.run()"]
        end
        subgraph T2["Thread 2 Stack"]
            T2PC["PC Register"]
            T2LV["Local Variables"]
            T2FR["Frame: MyTask.run()"]
        end
        TC["Thread Scheduler\n(OS-level)"]
    end
    T1 -.scheduled by.-> TC
    T2 -.scheduled by.-> TC
    T1 -.can access.-> HEAP
    T2 -.can access.-> HEAP
    T1 -.NOT visible to.-> T2
    T2 -.NOT visible to.-> T1
```

> **Key Insight:** Each thread has its own **private stack** (local variables, method frames), but all threads share the **same heap**. Local variables in `run()` are thread-safe; instance/static variables are *not*.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: StringBuilder Internal Buffer Growth

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Empty Buffer\ncapacity=16\nlength=0"] -->|append 20 chars| B["Overflow!\nGrow to 34\nlength=20"]
    B -->|append 20 more chars| C["Overflow!\nGrow to 70\nlength=40"]
    C -->|append 35 more chars| D["Overflow!\nGrow to 142\nlength=75"]
    D -->|final state| E["Buffer has\ncapacity=142\nlength=75"]
```

> **Engineering insight:** Amortized time per `append()` is **O(1)** because the buffer doubles geometrically. Total work over $n$ appends is $O(n)$, similar to how `ArrayList` grows.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Two Ways to Create a Thread

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START(["Need a Thread"]) --> Q1{"Can the class\ninherit from\nThread?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes, simple case| A1["class MyThread\nextends Thread\n{\n  public void run(){...}\n}"]
    Q1 -->|No, already extending\nanother class| A2["class MyTask\nextends SomeBase\nimplements Runnable\n{\n  public void run(){...}\n}"]
    A1 --> B1["MyThread t = new MyThread()\nt.start()"]
    A2 --> B2["Thread t = new Thread(new MyTask())\nt.start()"]
    B1 --> END(["New thread begins\nt.run() in fresh stack"])
    B2 --> END
```

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Flow: How `start()` vs `run()` Differs

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Thread
    participant NewT as New Thread
    Main->>Main: t = new MyThread()
    alt If you call t.start()
        Main->>NewT: start() registers with scheduler
        NewT->>NewT: New stack allocated
        NewT->>NewT: t.run() executes HERE
        Note over Main,NewT: Parallel execution
    else If you mistakenly call t.run()
        Main->>Main: t.run() executes in MAIN thread
        Note over Main,NewT: NO new thread created
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Explain why String objects are immutable in Java. List any two consequences of this immutability.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
String objects are immutable because the `String` class is declared as `final` and its internal `char[] value` array is `private final`. Once created, the contents cannot be modified. (1 mark)

**Consequences:**
1. **String Constant Pool optimization** тАФ JVM reuses string literals, saving memory. (1 mark)
2. **Thread safety** тАФ immutable objects can be freely shared between threads without synchronization. (1 mark)

*(Also acceptable: hashcode caching for `HashMap` performance, security in classloaders and network paths.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between `String` and `StringBuffer` in Java.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `String` | `StringBuffer` |
|---|---|---|
| Mutability | Immutable (1) | Mutable (1) |
| Memory | New object on every modification | Modifies the same object in-place (0.5) |
| Thread-safety | Inherently safe | Synchronized methods (0.5) |

---

### 5.2 Part B тАФ Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern)

---

#### **Question A тАФ 14 Marks** *(Choose one)*

**[KTU University Exam тАУ July 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of `StringBuffer` class. Your program should:
- Create a `StringBuffer` initialized with `"Object Oriented"`.
- Append `" Programming"` to it.
- Insert `"Java "` at position 0.
- Reverse the contents.
- Display the final string, its length, and its capacity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Java Thread Model. List the different states in the thread life cycle with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution тАФ Part (a) (7 marks):**

```java
import java.lang.StringBuffer;
public class SBufDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Object Oriented");
        sb.append(" Programming");
        sb.insert(0, "Java ");
        sb.reverse();
        System.out.println("Final String: " + sb);
        System.out.println("Length: " + sb.length());
        System.out.println("Capacity: " + sb.capacity());
    }
}
```

**Dry-run trace:**

| Step | Operation | Resulting String | Length | Capacity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `new StringBuffer("Object Oriented")` | `Object Oriented` | 15 | 16+15 = 31 |
| 2 | `append(" Programming")` | `Object Oriented Programming` | 28 | 31 (no overflow) |
| 3 | `insert(0, "Java ")` | `Java Object Oriented Programming` | 33 | grow: 2├Ч31+2 = 64 |
| 4 | `reverse()` | `gnimmargorP detneirO tcejbO avaJ` | 33 | 64 |

**Final Output:**
```
Final String: gnimmargorP detneirO tcejbO avaJ
Length: 33
Capacity: 64
```

**Mark distribution:** Correct class usage (2) + correct method invocations (3) + correct output reasoning (2).

---

**Model Solution тАФ Part (b) (7 marks):**

The **Java Thread Model** is a unified, OS-independent framework that allows a Java program to execute multiple tasks concurrently within a single process. (1 mark)

**Key features:**
- A `Thread` class encapsulates a thread of execution. (1 mark)
- A `Runnable` interface defines the task to be executed. (1 mark)
- The JVM uses **pre-emptive, priority-based scheduling** (delegated to the OS). (1 mark)
- All threads share the **heap memory** but have **private stacks**. (1 mark)

**Thread Life Cycle States (2 marks):**

1. **New** тАФ `Thread` object created, not yet started.
2. **Runnable** тАФ `start()` called, ready or running.
3. **Blocked / Waiting / Timed-Waiting** тАФ temporarily inactive (`sleep()`, `wait()`, I/O).
4. **Terminated** тАФ `run()` finished or uncaught exception.

> See **Section 4.1** for the state diagram.

---

#### **Question B тАФ 14 Marks** *(Alternative choice)*

**[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare `String`, `StringBuffer`, and `StringBuilder` in Java. Write a program that uses `StringBuilder` to construct the string `"Hello-World-Java"` by chaining `append()` calls, and then demonstrate `reverse()` and `delete()`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to create two threads using the `Runnable` interface. Both threads should print their names 5 times with a small delay. Explain why implementing `Runnable` is preferred over extending `Thread`. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution тАФ Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | `String` | `StringBuffer` | `StringBuilder` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mutability | Immutable | Mutable | Mutable |
| Thread-safe | Yes (by design) | Yes (synchronized) | No |
| Speed | Slow (modification creates new object) | Moderate | Fast |
| Storage | String Pool | Heap | Heap |
| Since | JDK 1.0 | JDK 1.0 | JDK 1.5 |

**Program (3 marks):**

```java
public class SBDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        sb.append("Hello").append("-").append("World").append("-").append("Java");
        System.out.println("Original: " + sb);
        sb.reverse();
        System.out.println("Reversed: " + sb);
        sb.delete(0, sb.length());  // clear
        sb.append("Cleared!");
        System.out.println("After delete & append: " + sb);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Original: Hello-World-Java
Reversed: avaJ-dlroW-olleH
After delete & append: Cleared!
```

---

**Model Solution тАФ Part (b) (7 marks):**

```java
class MyTask implements Runnable {
    private String threadName;
    MyTask(String name) { this.threadName = name; }
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(threadName + " - count " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(100); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println(e); }
        }
    }
}

public class TwoThreads {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t1 = new Thread(new MyTask("Worker-A"));
        Thread t2 = new Thread(new MyTask("Worker-B"));
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Why `Runnable` is preferred (3 marks):**
1. Java does not support multiple inheritance. By implementing `Runnable`, our class is free to extend another class. (1)
2. A single `Runnable` instance can be passed to multiple `Thread` objects, allowing the *same task* to be executed by *different threads* with shared state. (1)
3. It follows the **"favor composition over inheritance"** design principle. (1)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Calling `run()` instead of `start()`** тАФ Examiners check this carefully. If you write `t.run()` in your program, you get **0 marks** for the multithreading part because no new thread is created.
> 2. **Confusing `length()` and `capacity()`** тАФ `length()` is the number of characters stored; `capacity()` is the size of the internal buffer. For `new StringBuffer("Kerala")`, length = 6 but capacity = 22.
> 3. **Saying "StringBuffer is faster than StringBuilder"** тАФ This is **wrong** and will cost you 1 mark. `StringBuilder` is faster because it lacks synchronization.
> 4. **Forgetting to handle `InterruptedException`** in `Thread.sleep()` тАФ required in any program using `sleep()`.
> 5. **Spelling "immutable" as "inmutable" or "unmutable"** тАФ surprisingly common; examiners deduct marks for technical spelling.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **`String` is immutable**; `StringBuffer` and `StringBuilder` are mutable.
- [x] **`StringBuilder` is the default choice** for single-threaded string manipulation; use `StringBuffer` only in multithreaded contexts.
- [x] **Default capacity of empty `StringBuffer`/`StringBuilder`** = **16 characters**.
- [x] **Capacity growth formula:** $C_{new} = 2 \cdot C_{old} + 2$.
- [x] **`StringBuffer(str)` initial capacity** = 16 + `str.length()`.
- [x] **A thread** is the smallest unit of execution inside a process.
- [x] **Java Thread Model** is built on top of the native OS thread model; scheduling is **pre-emptive** and **priority-based**.
- [x] **Two ways to create a thread:** (1) extend `Thread`, (2) implement `Runnable` тАФ KTU prefers **Runnable**.
- [x] **Always call `start()`**, never `run()`, to spawn a new thread.
- [x] **Thread priorities:** `MIN=1`, `NORM=5`, `MAX=10` (constants in `Thread` class).
- [x] **Thread lifecycle states:** New тЖТ Runnable тЖТ Blocked/Waiting тЖТ Terminated.
- [x] **Main thread** can be obtained via `Thread.currentThread()`.
- [x] **Shared resources** (instance/static variables) require `synchronized` blocks or methods to prevent race conditions.
- [x] **`Thread.sleep(ms)`** throws `InterruptedException` тАФ must be caught or declared.
- [x] **Once a thread is terminated, it cannot be restarted** with another `start()` call.
- [x] **Local variables** in `run()` are thread-safe (each thread has its own stack); **instance/static fields** are not.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Main Thread
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Main Thread

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)**
> The **Main Thread** in Java is the *first* and *primary* user thread that is automatically created and started by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) when a Java application begins execution. Every standalone Java program has at least one thread тАФ the thread that executes the `main()` method тАФ and this thread is referred to as the **Main Thread**. It serves as the *parent* or *root* thread from which all additional "child" threads (worker threads) are typically spawned.

In strict JVM terminology:
- When a Java program is launched, the JVM creates one initial thread with the conventional name **`main`**.
- The priority of the main thread is the **default priority of NORM_PRIORITY (5)** unless explicitly modified.
- The `main()` method is invoked **from** this thread, not the other way around.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Stage Manager of a Theatre**
> Imagine a theatre play. Before any actor (worker thread) steps onto the stage, there is a **Stage Manager** standing at the wings, holding the script. The Stage Manager:
> 1. **Opens the play** (starts the program).
> 2. **Calls each actor onto the stage** in order (spawns child threads via `Thread t = new Thread(...)`).
> 3. **Coordinates the timing** тАФ actors wait for cues, speak lines in sequence (synchronization).
> 4. **Closes the play** only when the final actor has exited (main thread must be the last to finish).
>
> In Java, the **Main Thread is the Stage Manager**. The actors are your `Thread` objects. If the Stage Manager leaves too early (program ends before child threads complete), the play collapses тАФ leading to abrupt thread termination.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchors

| Term | Meaning in Main Thread Context |
|---|---|
| **Main Thread** | The default thread running `public static void main(String[] args)` |
| **currentThread()** | Static method of `Thread` class that returns a reference to the *currently executing* thread object |
| **Child Thread** | Any thread spawned programmatically from within the main thread |
| **NORM_PRIORITY** | Default thread priority value **5** (range: `MIN_PRIORITY = 1` to `MAX_PRIORITY = 10`) |
| **Daemon Thread** | Background service thread (e.g., `Garbage Collector`); JVM exits when only daemons remain |

## 1.4 Why the Main Thread Matters (KTU High-Yield Point)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Main Thread is the last thread to finish.** When the `main()` method completes, the JVM checks if any user threads (non-daemon) are still alive. If yes, the JVM **waits**. If no, the program terminates. Therefore, a solid understanding of the main thread is critical for designing long-running concurrent applications (servers, schedulers, GUI apps).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Lifecycle of the Main Thread

The Main Thread progresses through the following structured states:

1. **Thread Creation (JVM Boot)**
   - JVM invokes the **Main Thread** as part of class-loading and initialization.
   - Stack frame for `main(String[] args)` is allocated.
   - Thread state тЖТ `NEW` (transient) тЖТ `RUNNABLE`.

2. **Execution of `main()` Method**
   - Top-level statements of the program execute sequentially on the main thread.
   - Other user threads may be created via `Thread` subclasses or `Runnable` implementations.

3. **Thread Spawning Phase**
   - Calling `t.start()` on a new `Thread` object creates a **new call stack** parallel to the main thread's.
   - The main thread continues executing in parallel with the child thread.

4. **Termination Phase**
   - The `main()` method returns.
   - JVM checks: **Are any non-daemon threads still alive?**
   - If **Yes** тЖТ JVM waits until they finish.
   - If **No** тЖТ JVM invokes `System.exit(0)` and shuts down.

## 2.2 The `currentThread()` Mechanism тАФ How Java "Knows" Which Thread

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept**
> Inside any method that is executing on a thread, calling `Thread.currentThread()` returns a **reference to the `Thread` object representing the thread that is currently executing that line of code**. This is how the main thread gets its own reference for self-control.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. The JVM internally maintains a thread-local variable pointing to the executing thread.
2. When `Thread.currentThread()` is called from `main()`, it returns a `Thread` object whose `getName()` returns `"main"`.
3. This returned reference can be used to:
   - Query the thread's name, ID, priority, state
   - Modify name, priority
   - Invoke `sleep()`, `join()`, `yield()` on the main thread itself

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table тАФ it is heavily tested in KTU Module 3 questions.**

| Method | Signature | Return Type | Purpose (when called on Main Thread) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `currentThread()` | `static Thread currentThread()` | `Thread` | Returns reference to currently executing thread |
| `getName()` | `String getName()` | `String` | Returns thread's name (default for main: `"main"`) |
| `setName(String name)` | `void setName(String name)` | `void` | Renames the thread |
| `getId()` | `long getId()` | `long` | Returns unique thread ID assigned by JVM |
| `getPriority()` | `int getPriority()` | `int` | Returns priority (default **5** for main) |
| `setPriority(int p)` | `void setPriority(int p)` | `void` | Sets priority (valid range **1тАУ10**) |
| `isAlive()` | `boolean isAlive()` | `boolean` | Tests if thread has not yet terminated |
| `sleep(long ms)` | `static void sleep(long ms)` | `void` | Suspends current thread for `ms` milliseconds |
| `join()` | `void join()` | `void` | Waits for this thread to die |
| `yield()` | `static void yield()` | `void` | Hints scheduler to give other threads a chance |
| `getState()` | `Thread.State getState()` | `Thread.State` | Returns thread's current state (enum) |

### 2.4 Priority Constants (Must Memorize)

$$
\text{MIN\_PRIORITY} = 1 \quad \quad \text{NORM\_PRIORITY} = 5 \quad \quad \text{MAX\_PRIORITY} = 10
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Setting priority does **NOT guarantee** execution order. It is a *hint* to the OS thread scheduler. The JVM spec does not enforce strict priority-based scheduling тАФ a frequent source of confusion in viva voce.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Main Thread concept underpins:

- **GUI Frameworks (Swing, JavaFX):** The Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) is conceptually derived from the main thread; UI updates must be thread-safe.
- **Web Servers (Tomcat, Jetty):** The main thread accepts connections and spawns worker thread pools.
- **Android Development:** The main thread (UI thread) is strictly separated from background threads via `AsyncTask`/`Handler`.
- **Microservices:** The `main()` method initializes the Spring context and embarks the main thread into an event loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In production systems, a developer who lets the main thread block on I/O effectively stalls the entire application's responsiveness. Hence, **threading off** heavy work from the main thread is a foundational architectural pattern.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Example тАФ Obtaining and Controlling the Main Thread

Below is the **standard KTU-board exemplar program** demonstrating the main thread. Each line is annotated for KTU-level clarity.

```java
// File: MainThreadDemo.java
class MainThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        // STEP 1: Obtain a reference to the currently executing thread.
        // Since we are inside main(), this reference is the Main Thread.
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();

        // STEP 2: Display default properties of the main thread.
        System.out.println("Current thread: " + t);
        System.out.println("Name           : " + t.getName());
        System.out.println("Priority       : " + t.getPriority());
        System.out.println("ID             : " + t.getId());
    }
}
```

### 3.1.1 Expected Output Trace

```
Current thread: Thread[main,5,main]
Name           : main
Priority       : 5
ID             : 1
```

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

| Line | Code | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Thread t = Thread.currentThread();` | `currentThread()` is a `static` method. The JVM looks up the thread-local pointer of whichever thread invoked it. Here, it's `main`. A `Thread` object representing the main thread is returned and stored in `t`. |
| 2 | `t` (implicit `toString()`) | Default `Thread.toString()` format: `Thread[name, priority, groupName]`. Group name is also `"main"` for the main thread. |
| 3 | `t.getName()` | Returns the thread's name. Default is `"main"`. |
| 4 | `t.getPriority()` | Returns integer priority. Default is `5` (`NORM_PRIORITY`). |
| 5 | `t.getId()` | Returns a unique positive `long` ID. The main thread usually gets ID `1`. |

## 3.2 Renaming the Main Thread тАФ Worked Example

```java
class RenameMainThread {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();
        System.out.println("Before: " + t.getName());
        t.setName("KTU-MainWorker");
        System.out.println("After : " + t.getName());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Before: main
After : KTU-MainWorker
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** `setName()` mutates the internal `name` field of the `Thread` object. Since `t` and the actual executing thread point to the **same** object, the rename takes effect immediately for the live thread.

## 3.3 Sleeping the Main Thread тАФ Worked Example

```java
class SleepMainThread {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println("Main Thread tick: " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(1000);  // Suspends main thread for 1 second
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println("Main thread interrupted!");
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### 3.3.1 Derivation of Timing

$$
\text{Total Runtime} \approx N \times \Delta t
$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of loop iterations
- $\Delta t$ = sleep duration per iteration in **milliseconds**

For this example:
$$
\text{Total Runtime} = 5 \times 1000 \text{ ms} = 5000 \text{ ms} = 5 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on precision:** `Thread.sleep()` guarantees a *minimum* sleep duration, not an *exact* one. OS scheduling granularity may add 1тАУ10 ms overhead. KTU questions typically assume the **ideal** $N \times \Delta t$ model unless stated otherwise.

## 3.4 Complete Analytical Matrix тАФ Main Thread vs Child Thread

| Property | Main Thread | Child Thread |
|---|---|---|
| **Created by** | JVM automatically | Programmer via `new Thread(...)` + `start()` |
| **Default name** | `"main"` | `"Thread-0"`, `"Thread-1"`, ... |
| **Default priority** | `5` (NORM_PRIORITY) | Inherits parent's priority (usually `5`) |
| **Reference method** | `Thread.currentThread()` inside `main()` | Stored in programmer-declared variable |
| **Termination** | Must be last non-daemon thread | Independent lifecycle |
| **Daemon status** | Always **non-daemon** (user thread) | Can be set via `setDaemon(true)` before `start()` |
| **Exception in `main()`** | Causes program termination | Caught by uncaught handler or propagates |

## 3.5 KTU-Favorite Code Snippet тАФ Main Thread Spawning a Child

```java
class ChildThread extends Thread {
    ChildThread() {
        super("Demo-Child");
        System.out.println("Child thread created: " + this);
        start();  // Spawns the child thread
    }
    public void run() {
        try {
            for (int i = 5; i > 0; i--) {
                System.out.println("Child Thread: " + i);
                Thread.sleep(500);
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("Child interrupted.");
        }
        System.out.println("Exiting child thread.");
    }
}

class MainAndChild {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Main thread starting: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
        new ChildThread();

        try {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
                System.out.println("Main Thread: " + i);
                Thread.sleep(1000);
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("Main interrupted.");
        }
        System.out.println("Exiting main thread.");
    }
}
```

### 3.5.1 Interleaved Output (Representative)

```
Main thread starting: main
Child thread created: Thread[Demo-Child,5,main]
Child Thread: 5
Main Thread: 1
Child Thread: 4
Main Thread: 2
Child Thread: 3
Main Thread: 3
Child Thread: 2
Main Thread: 4
Child Thread: 1
Exiting child thread.
Main Thread: 5
Exiting main thread.
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The output is **non-deterministic** because the OS scheduler arbitrarily interleaves the two threads. KTU may ask you to predict *a possible* output, not *the exact* output. Always include scheduling disclaimers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Main Thread Lifecycle тАФ Mermaid State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : JVM Boot
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start() called by JVM
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : Waiting for monitor lock
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : sleep() / join() / wait()
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep(ms) / join(ms)
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : main() returns
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : Lock acquired
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify() / timeout
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : Timeout elapsed
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The Main Thread's state can be queried at runtime via `Thread.currentThread().getState()`. This returns an enum from `Thread.State` тАФ a frequently asked KTU viva question.

## 4.2 Main Thread + Child Thread Architecture тАФ Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[JVM Startup] --> B[Create Main Thread]
    B --> C[Invoke main String args]
    C --> D{Programmer spawns child?}
    D -- No --> E[Execute main sequentially]
    D -- Yes --> F[new Thread runnable]
    F --> G[child.start]
    G --> H[New Call Stack spawned]
    H --> I[Child Thread RUNNABLE]
    E --> J[main returns]
    I --> K[Child run completes]
    J --> L{Any non daemon alive?}
    K --> L
    L -- Yes --> M[JVM waits]
    L -- No --> N[Program terminates]
    M --> L
```

## 4.3 Main Thread Reference Acquisition тАФ Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant JVM
    participant MainThread as Main Thread
    participant Code as main method body
    participant API as Thread API

    JVM->>MainThread: Create & start
    MainThread->>Code: Invoke main(String[] args)
    Code->>API: Thread.currentThread()
    API-->>Code: Thread reference (name=main, priority=5)
    Code->>API: t.getName()
    API-->>Code: "main"
    Code->>API: t.getPriority()
    API-->>Code: 5
    Code->>API: t.setName(KTU-Main)
    API-->>Code: void
    Code->>API: t.sleep(1000)
    API-->>MainThread: Suspend for 1000 ms
```

## 4.4 Priority Hierarchy тАФ Visual Cheat Sheet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P10[10 MAX PRIORITY]:::hi
    P7[7 High]:::mid
    P5[5 NORM PRIORITY - Main Default]:::def
    P3[3 Low]:::mid
    P1[1 MIN PRIORITY]:::lo

    classDef hi fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef mid fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#000

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Anchor:** The Main Thread is the **green node** at priority 5. Recall: **M**ain = **M**edium (5).
