# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: What is the Main Thread in Java? Explain how you can obtain a reference to it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Main Thread** is the first user thread automatically created by the JVM when a Java program begins execution. It is responsible for executing the `public static void main(String[] args)` method and serves as the parent thread from which other threads are typically spawned.

**To obtain a reference** to the main thread, we use the static method `Thread.currentThread()`:

```java
Thread t = Thread.currentThread();   // t now refers to the main thread
```

This reference allows us to query or modify properties like name, priority, and to invoke control methods such as `sleep()` on the main thread.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: List any three methods that can be invoked on the Main Thread to control its behavior. State the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **`setName(String name)`** тАФ Renames the thread. For example, `Thread.currentThread().setName("Worker-1")` changes the main thread's name to `"Worker-1"`.

2. **`setPriority(int p)`** тАФ Sets the thread's priority (valid range: **1 to 10**). E.g., `setPriority(Thread.MAX_PRIORITY)` sets priority to 10.

3. **`sleep(long millis)`** тАФ Suspends the current thread's execution for the specified number of milliseconds, allowing other threads to execute.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks тАФ Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of the Main Thread in Java. Write a program to demonstrate how to obtain a reference to the main thread, display its default properties, and rename it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable code example, illustrate the use of `Thread.sleep()` and `setPriority()` on the main thread. Explain the significance of these methods. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Concept (3 marks):**
The Main Thread is the JVM-spawned thread that runs the `main()` method. Every Java application has exactly one main thread by default. The main thread is always a non-daemon (user) thread and must be the last thread to complete for the program to terminate gracefully.

**Code (4 marks):**

```java
class MainThreadProperties {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Obtain reference to main thread
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();

        // Step 2: Display default properties
        System.out.println("=== Before Renaming ===");
        System.out.println("Thread Object : " + t);
        System.out.println("Name          : " + t.getName());
        System.out.println("ID            : " + t.getId());
        System.out.println("Priority      : " + t.getPriority());
        System.out.println("Is Alive      : " + t.isAlive());

        // Step 3: Rename the main thread
        t.setName("KTU-PrimaryThread");

        // Step 4: Verify rename
        System.out.println("\n=== After Renaming ===");
        System.out.println("New Name      : " + t.getName());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
=== Before Renaming ===
Thread Object : Thread[main,5,main]
Name          : main
ID            : 1
Priority      : 5
Is Alive      : true

=== After Renaming ===
New Name      : KTU-PrimaryThread
```

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Code (5 marks):**

```java
class MainThreadControl {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();
        
        // Change priority to MAX (10)
        t.setPriority(Thread.MAX_PRIORITY);
        System.out.println("Main thread priority set to: " + t.getPriority());

        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println("Iteration " + i + " | Priority: " + t.getPriority());
            try {
                Thread.sleep(800);  // Pause for 800 ms
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println("Interrupted: " + e);
            }
        }
        System.out.println("Main thread exiting.");
    }
}
```

**Output (approximate):**
```
Main thread priority set to: 10
Iteration 1 | Priority: 10
Iteration 2 | Priority: 10
Iteration 3 | Priority: 10
Main thread exiting.
```

**Significance (2 marks):**

- **`setPriority()`**: Suggests to the scheduler the relative importance of the thread. Higher priority threads *may* get more CPU time, though this is OS-dependent.
- **`Thread.sleep()`**: Forces the thread to pause, allowing other threads (or the OS scheduler) to perform work. Essential for avoiding CPU monopolization and for pacing operations in time-sensitive applications.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] тАФ *Alternative Choice*

**Q: (a)** Differentiate between the Main Thread and a user-defined child thread in Java. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program where the Main Thread spawns a child thread. Show how the main thread waits for the child to complete using `join()`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

| Basis | Main Thread | Child Thread |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Created by JVM at program startup | Created by programmer using `new Thread()` |
| **Name (default)** | `"main"` | `"Thread-0"`, `"Thread-1"`, ... |
| **Priority (default)** | `5` (NORM_PRIORITY) | Inherits from parent (usually `5`) |
| **Daemon status** | Always non-daemon | Can be set to daemon via `setDaemon(true)` |
| **Entry point** | `main(String[] args)` | `run()` method |
| **Lifecycle end** | Must be the last non-daemon thread | Independent тАФ can finish before or after main |
| **Reference** | `Thread.currentThread()` inside `main()` | Stored in a programmer-declared variable |

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

```java
class WorkerThread extends Thread {
    WorkerThread(String name) {
        super(name);
    }
    public void run() {
        System.out.println(getName() + " started.");
        try {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 4; i++) {
                System.out.println(getName() + " tick: " + i);
                Thread.sleep(400);
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted.");
        }
        System.out.println(getName() + " finished.");
    }
}

class MainWithJoin {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        System.out.println("Main thread started.");
        WorkerThread worker = new WorkerThread("Worker-1");
        worker.start();

        // Main thread waits for worker to complete
        worker.join();
        
        System.out.println("Main thread: Worker has finished. Now exiting.");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Main thread started.
Worker-1 started.
Worker-1 tick: 1
Worker-1 tick: 2
Worker-1 tick: 3
Worker-1 tick: 4
Worker-1 finished.
Main thread: Worker has finished. Now exiting.
```

**Explanation:**
- `worker.start()` launches the child thread in parallel with the main thread.
- `worker.join()` blocks the **main thread** until `worker` terminates.
- Without `join()`, the main thread could complete first, potentially terminating the program before the child finishes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Calling `run()` instead of `start()`** тАФ If a student writes `worker.run()` instead of `worker.start()`, the child's `run()` executes *sequentially on the main thread*, not as a separate thread. **Marks deducted: 2тАУ3**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting `throws InterruptedException`** тАФ `sleep()` and `join()` throw checked `InterruptedException`. Omitting the `try-catch` or `throws` clause causes compilation failure. **Marks deducted: 1тАУ2**.
>
> 3. **Setting priority outside 1тАУ10** тАФ Java throws `IllegalArgumentException` if priority is `< 1` or `> 10`. **Marks deducted: 1**.
>
> 4. **Confusing `currentThread()` with `this`** тАФ Inside a `Thread` subclass, `this` refers to the *child* thread object. To get the *executing* thread, use `Thread.currentThread()`. In a static context, `this` is invalid тАФ `currentThread()` is mandatory. **Marks deducted: 2**.
>
> 5. **Assuming `setPriority()` guarantees execution order** тАФ This is a conceptual error. Priority is a *hint*, not a contract. **Marks deducted: 1тАУ2**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ The Main Thread**
>
> - The **Main Thread** is auto-created by the JVM and runs `public static void main(String[] args)`.
> - Default **name**: `"main"`. Default **priority**: `5` (`NORM_PRIORITY`).
> - Get a reference using **`Thread.currentThread()`** тАФ this is the *only* way to self-reference the main thread.
> - Key methods: `getName()`, `setName()`, `getId()`, `getPriority()`, `setPriority()`, `isAlive()`, `getState()`.
> - Static control methods: `sleep(long ms)`, `yield()`, `currentThread()`.
> - Main thread is **always non-daemon**; JVM waits for all non-daemon threads to finish before exiting.
> - `sleep()` requires handling `InterruptedException` (checked exception).
> - Priority range: **1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX)**; setting outside this throws `IllegalArgumentException`.
> - Priority is a *hint* to the scheduler, **not a guarantee** of execution order.
> - `join()` on a child thread causes the main thread to **wait** for that child to die.
> - In `Thread.toString()` output `Thread[name, priority, groupName]`, the group name for the main thread is also `"main"`.
> - Calling `run()` directly executes on the **caller's thread**, not as a new thread тАФ always use `start()`.
> - The Main Thread concept is foundational for GUI (EDT), server (worker pools), and Android (UI thread) architectures.

---
**End of Notes тАФ The Main Thread | Module 3 | PEERT416**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Thread
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Creating Thread**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thread (Java Definition):** A **thread** is the smallest independent unit of execution scheduled by the operating system within a single process. In Java, a thread is represented as an object instance of the class `java.lang.Thread` (or any subclass thereof), or as an object whose class implements the functional interface `java.lang.Runnable`. Every Java program has at least one user thread тАФ the **main thread** тАФ which is spawned automatically by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) when `main()` begins execution.

In the context of **Module 3 тАУ Multithreaded Programming**, the topic **"Creating Thread"** refers to the two canonical mechanisms sanctioned by the Java Language Specification (JLS) for instantiating a new thread of control:

1. **Subclassing the `java.lang.Thread` class** and overriding its `public void run()` method.
2. **Implementing the `java.lang.Runnable` interface** (a Single Abstract Method / SAM interface) and passing that implementation to a `Thread` constructor.

Both approaches ultimately delegate the actual thread-of-control creation to the JVM and the underlying OS, but they differ in inheritance flexibility, object-oriented design purity, and suitability in class hierarchies that already extend another class (since Java does not support multiple class inheritance).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy тАФ The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen. The **restaurant** is your Java program (a *process*). Inside that restaurant, the **head chef** is the **main thread** тАФ they start cooking the first dish (executing `main()`) on their own. Now suppose an order comes in for a long, slow-cooked stew that will take 2 hours. The head chef has two choices:
> - **Option A (Extending Thread):** Hire a *specialized* "Stew-Cook" who **is a type of chef** (subclass of `Thread`). This new chef has one job: the stew.
> - **Option B (Implementing Runnable):** Hire any general **cook** and hand them a **recipe card** (`Runnable` object) that says "cook the stew." The cook then follows the recipe.
>
> Both cooks work **simultaneously** in the same kitchen (same process / same memory space), but each follows their own instruction set. The kitchen manager (the **JVM/Thread Scheduler**) decides who stirs the pot at any given instant.

This analogy explains the crucial idea: **threads share the same memory (the kitchen) but execute independent sequences of code (their own recipes).**

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Process** | An independent executing program with its own memory space. |
| **Thread** | A lightweight sub-process; the basic CPU execution unit. |
| **Main Thread** | The thread automatically started by JVM to execute `main()`. |
| **`Thread` class** | The concrete class in `java.lang` that represents and controls a thread. |
| **`Runnable` interface** | A SAM interface (`void run()`) that defines the *task* to be executed. |
| **Time Slicing** | The scheduler's technique of giving each thread a small CPU quantum. |
| **Context Switching** | Saving the state of one thread and loading another. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `java.lang.Thread` Class тАФ Anatomy

The `Thread` class is declared as:

```java
public class Thread implements Runnable
```

This is the **most important** fact about `Thread` тАФ *it itself implements `Runnable`*. Therefore, every `Thread` object is, in effect, a `Runnable`. The class encapsulates:

- A private **target** `Runnable` field (the task to run, if provided externally).
- The thread's **name**, **priority**, **daemon flag**, and **state**.
- Native (JNI) handles to the underlying OS thread.

### 2.2 The `java.lang.Runnable` Interface тАФ Anatomy

```java
@FunctionalInterface
public interface Runnable {
    public abstract void run();
}
```

It is intentionally **minimal** тАФ it contains only the contract for the work to be done. It carries *no* thread-management responsibilities (no `start()`, no `sleep()`, no `join()`). This separation of *task* (Runnable) from *worker* (Thread) is a textbook example of the **Strategy Design Pattern**.

### 2.3 The Two Standard Ways of Creating a Thread

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module-3 syllabus explicitly tests both mechanisms and the rationale for choosing one over the other.

#### **Method 1 тАФ Extending the `Thread` class**

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Declare a class that **extends `java.lang.Thread`**.
2. **Override** the `public void run()` method to define the task.
3. In the calling code, instantiate your subclass: `MyThread t = new MyThread();`
4. Invoke `t.start();` тАФ *never* call `t.run()` directly if you want a new thread.

**Why override `run()`?** Because `Thread` itself implements `Runnable` and provides a *default* empty `run()`. The JVM's thread machinery internally calls `run()` once `start()` initializes the new OS thread.

#### **Method 2 тАФ Implementing the `Runnable` interface**

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Declare a class that **implements `java.lang.Runnable`**.
2. Implement the `public void run()` method.
3. Create an instance: `MyRunnable r = new MyRunnable();`
4. Pass it to a `Thread` constructor: `Thread t = new Thread(r);`
5. Invoke `t.start();`

> The `Thread` constructor used here is `Thread(Runnable target)`. The new `Thread` object stores `r` in its private `target` field; its own `run()` method internally calls `target.run()`.

### 2.4 High-Yield Comparison Table

| Criterion | Extending `Thread` | Implementing `Runnable` |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance used | Consumes the only class-inheritance slot | Leaves the class free to extend another class |
| Object-Oriented purity | Lower (inherits thread management unnecessarily) | Higher (separates *task* from *worker*) |
| Reusability of the same task object | Each thread needs its own subclass instance | One `Runnable` can be passed to **many** `Thread` objects (resource sharing) |
| Lambda compatibility (Java 8+) | тЭМ Not possible | тЬЕ Possible: `new Thread(() -> { ... }).start();` |
| **Recommended by industry** | **No** | **Yes** (Effective Java, Item 33) |
| KTU board answer preference | Acceptable, often used in textbook examples | **Strongly preferred** in 14-mark questions |

### 2.5 Critical API Methods (Formula Sheet Style)

> [!NOTE]
> These methods are *guaranteed* to be asked in either Part A (definition) or Part B (programmatic) questions.

| Method | Signature | Purpose | KTU Board Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| `start()` | `void start()` | Creates a new OS thread and invokes `run()` on it | тнРтнРтнРтнРтнР |
| `run()` | `void run()` | The entry point of the new thread's task | тнРтнРтнРтнРтнР |
| `currentThread()` | `static Thread currentThread()` | Returns a reference to the currently executing thread | тнРтнРтнРтнР |
| `getName()` / `setName()` | `String` / `void` | Read / change thread's name (default: `Thread-N`) | тнРтнРтнР |
| `getId()` | `long getId()` | Returns the unique positive ID assigned by JVM | тнРтнР |
| `getPriority()` / `setPriority()` | `int` / `void` | 1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX); default = 5 (NORM) | тнРтнРтнР |
| `isAlive()` | `boolean` | True if the thread has started and not yet died | тнРтнРтнР |
| `sleep(ms)` | `static void sleep(long)` | Pauses the *current* thread for `ms` milliseconds | тнРтнРтнРтнРтнР |
| `join()` | `void join()` | Calling thread waits until this thread dies | тнРтнРтнРтнР |
| `yield()` | `static void yield()` | Hints the scheduler to give other threads a chance | тнРтнР |
| `setDaemon(boolean)` | `void` | Marks thread as daemon (JVM exits when only daemons remain) | тнРтнР |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GUI frameworks (Swing/JavaFX):** The *Event Dispatch Thread* renders UI; background workers must be **separate threads** to prevent the UI from freezing.
- **Web servers (Tomcat, Jetty):** Each incoming HTTP request is handled on its own thread created by a `ThreadPoolExecutor`.
- **Big-data processing (Hadoop/Spark on JVM):** Tasks are parallelized by spawning thousands of threads.
- **Banking systems:** Concurrent transactions use threads, but synchronized via `synchronized` blocks (covered later in the module).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough тАФ Method 1 (Extending `Thread`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is a **fully expanded** program followed by an *exhaustive line-by-line* explanation. Every syntactic and runtime detail is included тАФ no shortcuts.

**Complete Program:**

```java
// File: ExtendingThreadDemo.java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    // Step 1: Constructor (optional) тАФ set a custom name
    public MyThread(String name) {
        super(name);                    // Call Thread(String) constructor
    }

    // Step 2: Override run() тАФ this is the new thread's entry point
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " тАФ value: " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(500);      // Pause 500 ms
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted.");
            }
        }
        System.out.println(getName() + " finished.");
    }
}

public class ExtendingThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Worker-A");
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Worker-B");

        t1.start();   // Spawns a NEW OS thread that calls t1.run()
        t2.start();   // Spawns a NEW OS thread that calls t2.run()

        System.out.println("Main thread ID: " + Thread.currentThread().getId());
    }
}
```

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Explanation:**

| Line / Block | Runtime Behaviour |
|---|---|
| `class MyThread extends Thread` | Establishes an **IS-A** relationship: every `MyThread` *is a* `Thread`. Inherits all 40+ methods of `Thread`. |
| `super(name)` | Invokes `Thread(String name)` constructor тЖТ sets the thread's name field. |
| `@Override public void run()` | Replaces the empty default `Thread.run()` with our task. **Crucially**, the JVM does **not** call this directly. It is called by the *new* thread that `start()` spawns. |
| `Thread.sleep(500)` | A `static` method тАФ pauses the **currently executing** thread (here, `t1` or `t2`), releasing the CPU for ~500 ms. Throws checked `InterruptedException`. |
| `MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Worker-A");` | Allocates a `MyThread` object on the heap. At this moment **no new OS thread exists** тАФ the object is just data. |
| `t1.start();` | JVM performs three actions: (a) registers a brand-new OS-level thread, (b) assigns it a unique ID, (c) invokes `t1.run()` **on that new thread**. |
| `Thread.currentThread().getId()` | Returns the ID of the thread executing `main()` тАФ typically **1**. |

**Sample Output (interleaving is non-deterministic):**

```
Main thread ID: 1
Worker-A тАФ value: 1
Worker-B тАФ value: 1
Worker-A тАФ value: 2
Worker-B тАФ value: 2
Worker-A тАФ value: 3
Worker-B тАФ value: 3
...
```

### 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough тАФ Method 2 (Implementing `Runnable`)

```java
// File: ImplementingRunnableDemo.java
class MyTask implements Runnable {
    private final String taskName;

    public MyTask(String taskName) {
        this.taskName = taskName;
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(taskName + " executing iteration " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(400);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(taskName + " was interrupted.");
            }
        }
        System.out.println(taskName + " has finished execution.");
    }
}

public class ImplementingRunnableDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // The TASK (decoupled from any thread)
        MyTask sharedTask = new MyTask("SharedTask");

        // Two different THREADS executing the SAME task object
        Thread worker1 = new Thread(sharedTask, "Worker-1");
        Thread worker2 = new Thread(sharedTask, "Worker-2");

        worker1.start();
        worker2.start();
    }
}
```

**The Internal Call Stack тАФ Why `start()` тЙа `run()`:**

Let us trace the call hierarchy precisely. The `Thread` class has (conceptually) the following internal logic:

$$
\text{Thread.run()} \;\equiv\; \begin{cases} \text{target.run()} & \text{if target} \neq \text{null} \\ \text{(empty default)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

So when `worker1.start()` is called:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{JVM creates new OS thread } T_1 \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\text{Thread scheduler picks } T_1 \text{ and invokes } \text{worker1.run()} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\text{worker1.run() detects target} \neq \text{null (it is sharedTask)} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\text{sharedTask.run() executes the for-loop on thread } T_1
\end{aligned}
$$

If the developer mistakenly writes `worker1.run()` instead of `worker1.start()`, **no new thread is created**; the for-loop simply runs on the **main thread**, and the program becomes single-threaded. This is a classic KTU exam pitfall.

### 3.3 The Lambda-Shortcut (Java 8+)

> [!NOTE]
> Since `Runnable` is a *functional interface*, we can replace the entire anonymous class with a lambda expression. KTU 2024 frequently tests this in **Part A** for 3 marks.

```java
public class LambdaThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Runnable task = () -> {
            for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
                System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " тЖТ " + i);
            }
        };

        new Thread(task, "LambdaThread-1").start();
        new Thread(task, "LambdaThread-2").start();
    }
}
```

**Conversion logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Runnable } r &= \text{ new Runnable}() \{ \text{ public void run()} \{ \text{ body} \} \} \\
&\Updownarrow \quad \text{(functional interface contraction)} \\
\text{Runnable } r &= () \rightarrow \{ \text{ body} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix тАФ Choosing the Right Method

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Your class must extend another class (e.g., `Applet`, `HttpServlet`) | **Runnable** | Java forbids multiple class inheritance |
| You want to share data between threads using one task object | **Runnable** | One `Runnable` instance can be passed to N `Thread`s |
| You need to override other `Thread` methods (e.g., `destroy`, `suspend` тАФ deprecated) | **Thread** | Direct subclassing exposes inherited methods |
| You want short, idiomatic code with a lambda | **Runnable** | Lambdas only target functional interfaces |
| The class is a "worker" and only exists to be a thread | **Either is acceptable** | Either suffices; syllabus accepts both |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Class-Architecture Diagram (Both Methods)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Runnable {
        <<interface>>
        +run() void
    }
    class Thread {
        -target Runnable
        -name String
        -priority int
        -daemon boolean
        +start() void
        +run() void
        +sleep(long) static void
        +currentThread() static Thread
        +getName() String
        +setName(String) void
        +join() void
        +isAlive() boolean
    }
    class MyThreadSubclass {
        +run() void
    }
    class MyRunnableImpl {
        +run() void
    }
    Runnable <|.. Thread : implements
    Thread <|-- MyThreadSubclass : extends (Method 1)
    Runnable <|.. MyRunnableImpl : implements (Method 2)
    Thread o-- Runnable : holds target
```

### 4.2 Thread-Creation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program begins in main thread] --> B{Choose creation method}
    B -- Method 1: Extends Thread --> C[Declare class MyThread extends Thread]
    C --> D[Override public void run]
    D --> E[new MyThread]
    E --> F[Call myThread.start]
    F --> G[JVM spawns new OS thread]
    G --> H[New thread calls myThread.run]

    B -- Method 2: Implements Runnable --> I[Declare class MyTask implements Runnable]
    I --> J[Implement public void run]
    J --> K[new MyTask]
    K --> L[Thread t = new Thread myTask]
    L --> M[Call t.start]
    M --> G

    H --> N[Task executes concurrently with main]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Thread scheduler allocates CPU time slices]
    O --> P[run returns, thread dies]
```

### 4.3 The start() vs run() Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer writes t dot something] --> B{Which method?}
    B -- t.start() --> C[NEW OS thread created]
    C --> D[t.run invoked ON the new thread]
    D --> E[Concurrent execution achieved]

    B -- t.run() --> F[NO new thread created]
    F --> G[run invoked ON the current call stack]
    G --> H[Single threaded sequential execution]

    style C fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a7f37
    style E fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a7f37
    style F fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b91c1c
    style H fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b91c1c
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The green path is the **correct** KTU-board-expected approach. The red path is the common student mistake (calling `run()` instead of `start()`), which is a guaranteed mark-loser in viva and code-trace questions.

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture тАФ Thread Lifecycle (Creation & Termination)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New : new MyThread or new Thread target
    New --> Runnable : t.start invoked
    Runnable --> Blocked : sleep, wait, I O
    Blocked --> Runnable : timer expires, lock acquired
    Runnable --> Terminated : run method returns or throws
    Terminated --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **New** тАФ Object exists but no OS thread yet.
- **Runnable** тАФ Eligible for CPU time (the OS may or may not run it instantly).
- **Blocked/Waiting/Timed-Waiting** тАФ Cannot run yet (e.g., sleeping, waiting for I/O, waiting for another thread via `join()`).
- **Terminated** тАФ `run()` has finished; the thread cannot be restarted.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]**
*Define a thread in Java. List the two ways of creating a thread.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **thread** is an independent path of execution within a program. In Java, it is represented as an object of the `java.lang.Thread` class or any class that implements the `java.lang.Runnable` interface.
>
> The **two ways** of creating a thread in Java are:
> 1. **By extending the `Thread` class** and overriding its `run()` method.
> 2. **By implementing the `Runnable` interface** and passing the implementing object to a `Thread` constructor.
>
> *(1 mark тАФ definition, 2 marks тАФ listing the two ways with one-line explanation each)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024]**
*Differentiate between calling `start()` and calling `run()` on a Thread object.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `start()` | `run()` |
|---|---|---|
| New thread created? | **Yes**, a new OS-level thread is spawned by the JVM | **No**, executes in the current thread |
| Invocation | Called **once** by user to begin the thread | Called **automatically** by the new thread after `start()`, or **directly** by user (incorrect usage) |
| Effect | Enables **concurrent** execution | Causes **sequential** execution (acts like a normal method call) |

> *(1 mark per row + 1 mark for concluding sentence about concurrent vs sequential behaviour)*

---

### 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam тАФ Model Question Paper 2024]**
*Write a Java program to create a thread by extending the `Thread` class. The thread should print numbers from 1 to 10 with a 1-second delay between each number. Also write the main class that creates and starts two such threads.*

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
// File: NumberPrinterThread.java
class NumberPrinterThread extends Thread {
    public NumberPrinterThread(String name) {
        super(name);
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        try {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
                System.out.println(getName() + " prints: " + i);
                Thread.sleep(1000);  // 1-second delay
            }
            System.out.println(getName() + " has finished printing.");
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " was interrupted. Exiting.");
        }
    }
}

// File: MainDemo.java
public class MainDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        NumberPrinterThread t1 = new NumberPrinterThread("Thread-1");
        NumberPrinterThread t2 = new NumberPrinterThread("Thread-2");

        t1.start();  // spawns new OS thread that invokes t1.run()
        t2.start();  // spawns new OS thread that invokes t2.run()

        System.out.println("Main thread ends here.");
    }
}
```

**Mark-splitting (7 marks):**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct class declaration `extends Thread` | 1 |
| Correct `run()` override + loop logic | 2 |
| Use of `Thread.sleep(1000)` with try-catch | 1 |
| Constructor using `super(name)` | 1 |
| `main()` correctly creates two objects | 1 |
| Uses `start()` (not `run()`) | 1 |

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam тАФ Model Question Paper 2024]**
*Write a Java program to create a thread by implementing the `Runnable` interface. The thread should accept a `String` message in its constructor and print the message five times, with the current thread name displayed each time.*

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
class MessagePrinter implements Runnable {
    private final String message;

    public MessagePrinter(String message) {
        this.message = message;
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " тЖТ " + message + " (" + i + "/5)");
            try {
                Thread.sleep(700);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println("Interrupted: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
            }
        }
    }
}

public class RunnableDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MessagePrinter task1 = new MessagePrinter("Hello from Runnable");
        MessagePrinter task2 = new MessagePrinter("Java threading rocks");

        Thread t1 = new Thread(task1, "Worker-A");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task2, "Worker-B");

        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Mark-splitting (7 marks):**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct `implements Runnable` declaration | 1 |
| Field, constructor, message storage | 1 |
| Override `run()` with loop | 2 |
| Use of `Thread.currentThread().getName()` | 1 |
| `main()` passes `Runnable` to `Thread` constructor | 1 |
| `start()` invoked on both threads | 1 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls (lose 1тАУ3 marks each):**
> 1. **Calling `run()` instead of `start()`** тЖТ No new thread is created; the program becomes sequential. Examiners **always deduct at least 2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting `try-catch` around `Thread.sleep()`** тЖТ Compilation error. Worth 1 mark.
> 3. **Using `implements Thread`** тЖТ Syntax error. The `Thread` class is not an interface.
> 4. **Multiple inheritance attempts** (e.g., `class A extends Thread extends Applet`) тЖТ Compilation error; use `Runnable` instead.
> 5. **Attempting to restart a terminated thread** (calling `start()` twice on the same `Thread` object) тЖТ Throws `IllegalThreadStateException` at runtime.
> 6. **Missing `@Override` annotation on `run()`** тЖТ Does not lose marks, but examiners appreciate the OOP best-practice signal.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the KTU 2024 Module-3 viva & exam:**

- тЬЕ A **thread** is a lightweight sub-process; Java programs have at least the **main thread**.
- тЬЕ The **two ways** to create a thread: **extend `Thread`** or **implement `Runnable`**.
- тЬЕ Always call **`start()`** тАФ never call **`run()`** directly, or the new thread is never spawned.
- тЬЕ **`start()` registers a new OS thread and internally calls `run()`** on that new thread.
- тЬЕ **`Thread` itself implements `Runnable`**, which is why subclassing works.
- тЬЕ **`Runnable` is a functional interface** тЖТ supports **lambda expressions** in Java 8+.
- тЬЕ **Implement `Runnable`** when your class already **extends another class** or when you need **multiple threads to share one task object**.
- тЬЕ Key methods to memorize: `start()`, `run()`, `currentThread()`, `getName()`, `sleep(ms)`, `join()`, `isAlive()`, `setPriority(int)`.
- тЬЕ **`Thread.sleep()` is `static`** тАФ it pauses the *currently executing* thread, not a specific `Thread` object.
- тЬЕ Thread states: **New тЖТ Runnable тЖТ Blocked/Waiting тЖТ Terminated**.
- тЬЕ A terminated thread **cannot be restarted** тАФ `start()` on it throws `IllegalThreadStateException`.
- тЬЕ KTU 2024 boards prefer the **`Runnable` approach** for 14-mark questions тАФ show OOP design awareness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Multiple Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Creating Multiple Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Multithreading** in Java is the concurrent execution of **two or more threads** within a single process, where each thread represents an independent path of execution sharing the same memory space (heap) but maintaining its own **call stack**, **program counter**, and **local variables**.

**Creating Multiple Threads** refers to the programmatic mechanism in Java through which a developer instantiates and starts more than one thread of execution. The Java API exposes this capability primarily through the `java.lang.Thread` class and the `java.lang.Runnable` functional interface, both of which are part of the foundational `java.lang` package.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Bank:** A *Thread* in Java is the smallest unit of dispatchable code. The **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** allows an application to have multiple threads of execution running concurrently. Each thread is associated with a `Thread` object and has a priority that influences scheduling.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "Multi-Chef Kitchen"

Imagine a single restaurant kitchen with **one chef** (single-threaded program). No matter how many orders arrive, every dish must be prepared sequentially. Now consider the same kitchen with **three chefs** (multiple threads):

- **Chef 1 (Thread-A)** тЖТ Prepares pasta
- **Chef 2 (Thread-B)** тЖТ Prepares salad
- **Chef 3 (Main Thread)** тЖТ Takes orders and plates food

All three chefs **share the same kitchen** (heap memory тАФ common objects, variables), but each maintains their **own cutting board and knife** (stack тАФ local variables, method frames). The head chef (the **JVM thread scheduler**) decides whose dish gets plated next based on priority, order arrival time, and CPU availability.

> This is precisely how Java multithreading works: a single process spawns many threads, sharing resources but executing independently.

---

## 1.3 Why Create Multiple Threads? тАФ Engineering Motivation

| **Use Case** | **Why Multithreading Helps** |
|---|---|
| GUI Applications (Swing/JavaFX) | Keeps UI responsive while background tasks execute |
| Server Applications (Tomcat) | Handles thousands of client requests in parallel |
| Animations & Games | Renders frames independently of game logic |
| I/O Bound Tasks (file/network) | CPU does useful work while waiting for I/O |
| Scientific Computing | Parallelizes matrix operations, simulations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight тАФ Concurrency vs Parallelism:** Concurrency is about *dealing with* many things at once (single CPU, time-sliced). Parallelism is about *doing* many things at once (multiple CPUs). Java threads support **both** тАФ concurrency via time-slicing, and parallelism on multi-core hardware.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Standard Methods to Create a Thread (KTU Syllabus Mandate)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes **two canonical techniques** for thread creation. Both ultimately delegate to the same underlying `Thread.start()` mechanism.

### Method 1 тАФ Extending the `java.lang.Thread` Class

The user class inherits from `Thread` and overrides the `public void run()` method. The thread begins execution when `start()` is invoked (which internally calls `run()` on a new call stack).

**Class Hierarchy Representation:**

$$
\text{java.lang.Object} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{java.lang.Thread} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{MyThread}
$$

**Key Rule:** A class can extend only **one** parent class in Java (single inheritance), which is the principal design drawback of this approach тАФ it consumes the inheritance slot.

---

### Method 2 тАФ Implementing the `java.lang.Runnable` Interface

The user class implements `Runnable` (a functional interface with a single `run()` method) and is then passed to a `Thread` constructor. This preserves the inheritance slot because the user class can extend any other class.

**Interface Contract:**

$$
\text{Runnable} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{void run()}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Most Preferred Approach:** Implementing `Runnable` is the **recommended best practice** because it decouples the *task* (what to execute) from the *thread* (how to execute), adheres to the **"favor composition over inheritance"** OOP principle, and works seamlessly with **lambda expressions** introduced in Java 8.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Thread Method Cheat Sheet

| **Method** | **Signature** | **Purpose / Behavior** | **Static?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| `start()` | `void start()` | Creates new call stack and invokes `run()` | No (instance) |
| `run()` | `void run()` | Entry point of thread execution | No (instance) |
| `sleep(long ms)` | `static void sleep(long)` | Pauses current thread for `ms` milliseconds | **Yes** |
| `join()` | `void join()` | Current thread waits for this thread to die | No (instance) |
| `join(long ms)` | `void join(long)` | Waits at most `ms` milliseconds | No (instance) |
| `yield()` | `static void yield()` | Hints scheduler to give other threads a chance | **Yes** |
| `getName()` | `String getName()` | Returns thread's name | No (instance) |
| `setName(String)` | `void setName(String)` | Sets thread's name | No (instance) |
| `getPriority()` | `int getPriority()` | Returns priority (1 to 10) | No (instance) |
| `setPriority(int)` | `void setPriority(int)` | Sets priority (MIN=1, NORM=5, MAX=10) | No (instance) |
| `isAlive()` | `boolean isAlive()` | True if thread has started and not yet died | No (instance) |
| `currentThread()` | `static Thread currentThread()` | Returns reference to currently executing thread | **Yes** |
| `interrupt()` | `void interrupt()` | Interrupts a sleeping/waiting thread | No (instance) |
| `isDaemon()` | `boolean isDaemon()` | True if thread is a daemon (background) thread | No (instance) |

---

## 2.3 The Thread Lifecycle тАФ State Transition Theory

Every Java thread exists in exactly one of **six lifecycle states** defined in `java.lang.Thread.State` (an enum introduced in Java 5). The transitions are governed by JVM calls and `Thread` method invocations.

**The Six States:**

1. **NEW** тАФ Thread object is created but `start()` not yet called.
2. **RUNNABLE** тАФ Thread is ready to run, waiting for CPU allocation. (Includes both "ready" and "running" тАФ JVM does not distinguish externally.)
3. **BLOCKED** тАФ Thread is blocked waiting for a monitor lock (synchronization).
4. **WAITING** тАФ Thread is waiting indefinitely via `wait()`, `join()` (no timeout), or `LockSupport.park()`.
5. **TIMED_WAITING** тАФ Thread is waiting for a specified time via `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)`.
6. **TERMINATED** тАФ Thread has completed execution (exited `run()`) or thrown an uncaught exception.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **BLOCKED** vs **WAITING**. **BLOCKED** is specifically about waiting to *acquire a monitor lock*. **WAITING** is about waiting for another thread to *signal* (e.g., `notify()`) or *complete* (e.g., `join()`).

---

## 2.4 Priority Constants тАФ Standard Metrics

The Java specification defines three priority constants. These are not absolute guarantees but **hints to the OS scheduler**.

| **Constant** | **Value** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| `Thread.MIN_PRIORITY` | **1** | Lowest priority |
| `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY` | **5** | Default priority for new threads |
| `Thread.MAX_PRIORITY` | **10** | Highest priority |

**Default Priority Rule:** A newly created thread inherits the priority of the thread that created it. The main thread has priority **5** by default.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application of Multiple Threads** |
|---|---|
| Web Servers (Apache Tomcat, Jetty) | One thread per HTTP request |
| Database Engines (H2, Derby) | Separate threads for query, indexing, logging |
| Compilers (javac) | Parallel lexical analysis and code generation |
| IDEs (IntelliJ, Eclipse) | Background indexing, syntax highlighting, builds |
| Real-time Trading | Concurrent order matching, market data ingestion |
| Operating Systems (HotSpot JVM) | Garbage collector runs on dedicated threads |

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Method 1 тАФ Extending the `Thread` Class (Complete Walkthrough)

### 3.1.1 The Source Code

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public MyThread(String name) {
        super(name);   // Calls Thread(String name) constructor
    }
    
    @Override
    public void run() {
        // This code executes on a NEW call stack (not the main stack)
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " тАФ Count: " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(500);   // Pause 500 ms
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted.");
            }
        }
        System.out.println(getName() + " exiting.");
    }
}

public class ThreadDemo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Worker-A");
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Worker-B");
        
        t1.start();   // NEW тЖТ RUNNABLE
        t2.start();   // NEW тЖТ RUNNABLE
        
        System.out.println("Main thread done launching.");
    }
}
```

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Execution Trace

| **Step** | **Action** | **Thread State Change** | **Output (sample interleaving)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Worker-A")` | t1 тЖТ **NEW** | (none) |
| 2 | `MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Worker-B")` | t2 тЖТ **NEW** | (none) |
| 3 | `t1.start()` called | t1 тЖТ **RUNNABLE** | (JVM may schedule immediately) |
| 4 | `t2.start()` called | t2 тЖТ **RUNNABLE** | (JVM may schedule immediately) |
| 5 | `t1.run()` iteration 1, sleeps 500ms | t1 тЖТ **TIMED_WAITING** | `Worker-A тАФ Count: 1` |
| 6 | `t2.run()` may interleave | t2 тЖТ **RUNNABLE / TIMED_WAITING** | `Worker-B тАФ Count: 1` |
| 7 | Both finish loop | t1, t2 тЖТ **TERMINATED** | `Worker-A exiting.` / `Worker-B exiting.` |
| 8 | Main thread prints last line | main тЖТ RUNNABLE then TERMINATED | `Main thread done launching.` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the output order is non-deterministic:** The JVM thread scheduler (which delegates to the OS) decides which RUNNABLE thread gets CPU time. Students **must not** assume any specific order of output between threads.

### 3.1.3 The Critical Error тАФ Calling `run()` Instead of `start()`

If a student mistakenly writes `t1.run();` instead of `t1.start();`, then:

- The `run()` method is invoked on the **main thread's call stack** (no new thread is created).
- The program becomes **single-threaded** тАФ `Worker-B` cannot start until `Worker-A` finishes its entire loop.
- The thread name printed will be `"main"`, not `"Worker-A"`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Favorite Trick Question:** "What is the difference between `t.start()` and `t.run()`?" Answer: `start()` creates a **new** call stack and invokes `run()` asynchronously; `run()` executes synchronously on the **caller's** stack тАФ no new thread.

---

## 3.2 Method 2 тАФ Implementing the `Runnable` Interface (Complete Walkthrough)

### 3.2.1 The Source Code

```java
class MyTask implements Runnable {
    private String taskName;
    
    public MyTask(String taskName) {
        this.taskName = taskName;
    }
    
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(taskName + " executing step " + i +
                               " on " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
            try {
                Thread.sleep(400);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
            }
        }
    }
}

public class ThreadDemo2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyTask task1 = new MyTask("Download");
        MyTask task2 = new MyTask("Upload");
        
        Thread t1 = new Thread(task1, "Download-Thread");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task2, "Upload-Thread");
        
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Constructor & Wiring Logic

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MyTask task1} &= \text{new MyTask("Download")} \quad \text{[task object created on heap]} \\
\text{Thread t1} &= \text{new Thread(task1, "Download-Thread")} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Thread's internal field } \text{target} = \text{task1} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Thread name set to "Download-Thread"} \\
\text{t1.start()} &\Rightarrow \text{JVM calls t1.run()} \Rightarrow \text{which internally calls target.run()} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Invokes MyTask.run() on the NEW call stack}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Internal Source of `Thread.run()` (Conceptual)

$$
\text{Thread.run()} \;\approx\; \begin{cases} \text{target.run()} & \text{if target} \neq \text{null} \\ \text{(empty тАФ no-op)} & \text{if target} = \text{null} \end{cases}
$$

This is why the `Runnable` approach works тАФ `Thread` delegates execution to its `target` field, which is a `Runnable` instance.

---

## 3.3 Method 3 тАФ Lambda Expression (Java 8+ Bonus)

Since `Runnable` is a **functional interface** (single abstract method `run()`), Java 8 lambda expressions can replace anonymous inner classes:

```java
public class ThreadDemo3 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Lambda Runnable тАФ concise syntax
        Runnable r1 = () -> {
            for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
                System.out.println("Lambda thread: " + i);
            }
        };
        
        Thread t = new Thread(r1, "Lambda-Worker");
        t.start();
        
        // Even more concise тАФ inline
        new Thread(() -> System.out.println("One-liner thread!")).start();
    }
}
```

**Lambda Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Anonymous class} &\Rightarrow \text{new Runnable()} \{ \text{public void run()} \{ \dots \} \} \\
\text{Lambda (functional interface)} &\Rightarrow () \to \{ \dots \} \\
\text{Compiler infers} &\Rightarrow \text{return type void, no parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Multiple Thread Coordination тАФ The `join()` Method

When the main thread must wait for child threads to complete before proceeding, we use `join()`:

```java
public class JoinDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        Thread worker = new Thread(() -> {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
                System.out.println("Worker step " + i);
                try { Thread.sleep(300); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
            }
        }, "Worker");
        
        worker.start();
        worker.join();   // Main thread BLOCKS here until worker terminates
        
        System.out.println("Main thread resumes after worker finishes.");
    }
}
```

**State Transition Analysis for `worker.join()`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{main thread state before join()} &= \text{RUNNABLE} \\
\text{main calls worker.join()} &\Rightarrow \text{main} \to \text{WAITING} \\
\text{worker terminates} &\Rightarrow \text{main receives signal} \Rightarrow \text{main} \to \text{RUNNABLE} \\
\text{Worker state after run() exits} &= \text{TERMINATED}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 тАФ Common Coding Mistake:** Calling `join()` on a thread that was never started results in the calling thread returning **immediately**, because the thread is already in a non-alive state. `isAlive()` returns `false`.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix тАФ When to Use Which Creation Method

| **Criterion** | **Extending `Thread`** | **Implementing `Runnable`** | **Lambda** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class can extend another class? | тЭМ No (slot consumed) | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes (no class) |
| Suitable for thread pools (Executor)? | тЭМ Awkward | тЬЕ Standard | тЬЕ Standard |
| Code reusability of the task | тЭМ Tied to Thread subclass | тЬЕ Decoupled | тЪа Limited |
| Java 8+ idiomatic | тЪа Verbose | тЬЕ Preferred | тЬЕ Most concise |
| Inheritance hierarchy clarity | тЭМ Pollutes hierarchy | тЬЕ Clean | тЬЕ Cleanest |
| KTU 2024 Recommended | тЪа Mention only | тЬЕ **Primary** | тЬЕ Mention |

---

## 3.6 Memory Model Summary тАФ Heap vs Stack per Thread

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Heap Memory} &= \text{Shared across ALL threads (objects, instance fields, static fields)} \\
\text{Stack Memory (per thread)} &= \text{Private to each thread (local variables, method frames, PC)} \\
\text{PC Register (per thread)} &= \text{Points to current instruction in that thread's run() method} \\
\text{Native Stack (per thread)} &= \text{Used by JVM for native method calls}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Since all threads share the **heap**, race conditions can occur if two threads modify the same object simultaneously. This motivates the need for **synchronization** (covered later in Module 3 or Module 4 depending on syllabus depth).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Thread Lifecycle State Diagram (Six-State Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : new Thread()
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start()
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : failed to acquire monitor lock
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait() / join() / park()
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify() / notifyAll() / target terminates
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep(ms) / wait(ms) / join(ms)
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout expires / notify()
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run() returns normally
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : uncaught exception
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A thread is **born** in NEW, becomes **eligible** for the CPU in RUNNABLE, may be **paused** in BLOCKED, WAITING, or TIMED_WAITING, and **dies** in TERMINATED. Arrows are one-way except the cyclic return to RUNNABLE from blocking states.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram тАФ Two Methods of Thread Creation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task to Execute] --> B{Choose Creation Method}
    B -->|Method 1| C[Extend java.lang.Thread]
    B -->|Method 2| D[Implement java.lang.Runnable]
    C --> E[Override public void run]
    D --> F[Implement public void run]
    E --> G[Create instance: MyThread t = new MyThread]
    F --> H[Create target: MyTask task = new MyTask]
    H --> I[Wrap in Thread: Thread t = new Thread task]
    G --> J[Invoke t.start]
    I --> J
    J --> K[JVM allocates new Call Stack]
    K --> L[Thread State: NEW to RUNNABLE]
    L --> M[Thread Scheduler picks thread]
    M --> N[run executes on new stack]
    N --> O[Thread State: RUNNABLE to TERMINATED]
```

---

## 4.3 Multiple Threads Execution Flow тАФ Main + Workers

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Thread
    participant Sched as JVM Scheduler
    participant T1 as Worker-A Thread
    participant T2 as Worker-B Thread
    
    Main->>Main: state = RUNNABLE
    Main->>T1: t1.start()
    Main->>T2: t2.start()
    Main->>Sched: yield() (implicit)
    
    par Parallel Execution
        Sched->>T1: allocate CPU
        T1->>T1: run() loop iteration 1
        T1->>T1: sleep(500) тАФ TIMED_WAITING
    and
        Sched->>T2: allocate CPU
        T2->>T2: run() loop iteration 1
        T2->>T2: sleep(500) тАФ TIMED_WAITING
    end
    
    Sched->>T1: wake from sleep
    T1->>T1: continue loop...
    T1-->>Main: (implicit join if called)
    T1->>T1: state = TERMINATED
    
    Sched->>T2: wake from sleep
    T2->>T2: continue loop...
    T2->>T2: state = TERMINATED
    
    Main->>Main: state = TERMINATED
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Reading Guide:** The `par` block in the sequence diagram represents **parallel branches** тАФ the JVM interleaves T1 and T2 execution unpredictably. The actual output order on the console depends on the OS scheduler, NOT the order of `start()` calls.

---

## 4.4 Memory Architecture тАФ Heap Sharing vs Stack Isolation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Process[JVM Process - Shared Heap]
        ObjA[Shared Object A]
        ObjB[Shared Object B]
    end
    
    subgraph T1[Thread-1]
        Stack1[Call Stack]
        Locals1[Local Variables]
        PC1[PC Register]
    end
    
    subgraph T2[Thread-2]
        Stack2[Call Stack]
        Locals2[Local Variables]
        PC2[PC Register]
    end
    
    subgraph TMain[Main Thread]
        StackM[Call Stack]
        LocalsM[Local Variables]
        PCM[PC Register]
    end
    
    T1 -.reads/writes.-> ObjA
    T2 -.reads/writes.-> ObjA
    TMain -.reads/writes.-> ObjB
    T1 -.reads/writes.-> ObjB
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** When multiple threads access the same heap object (e.g., `ObjA`), there is **no automatic protection**. Concurrent modification can corrupt data unless the access is guarded by `synchronized` blocks, `Locks`, or atomic variables. This is the foundation for the **race condition** problem.

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam тАФ June 2023]**
**Differentiate between `start()` and `run()` methods of the `Thread` class in Java. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **`start()`** | **`run()`** |
|---|---|---|
| New thread created? | тЬЕ Yes тАФ allocates new call stack | тЭМ No тАФ runs on caller's stack |
| Invocation | Registers thread with thread scheduler | Ordinary method call |
| Behavior | Asynchronous (parallel) | Synchronous (sequential) |
| Can be called multiple times? | тЭМ Throws `IllegalThreadStateException` | тЬЕ Yes (but pointless) |
| Effect on current thread | Returns immediately | Blocks until `run()` returns |

**Conclusion:** `start()` is the **only** correct way to launch a new thread; calling `run()` directly is a common programming error that results in single-threaded execution.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2022]**
**List any three methods of the `Thread` class and explain the purpose of `sleep()` and `join()`. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Three important methods of the `Thread` class are:

1. **`start()`** тАФ Creates a new call stack and invokes `run()` to begin thread execution.
2. **`sleep(long milliseconds)`** тАФ A static method that pauses the **currently executing** thread for the specified duration. During sleep, the thread is in the **TIMED_WAITING** state and does not release any held locks.
3. **`join()`** тАФ An instance method that causes the **calling thread** to wait until the target thread (on which `join()` is invoked) finishes execution (enters TERMINATED state). It is used to enforce **ordering** between threads.

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]

### QUESTION A тАФ 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024]`

#### Part (a) тАФ 7 Marks
**Explain the two ways of creating threads in Java with suitable examples. Compare them.**

**Model Solution:**

**Way 1: Extending the `Thread` Class**

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public MyThread(String name) { super(name); }
    
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " value: " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(300); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}

public class Test1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Alpha");
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Beta");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Way 2: Implementing the `Runnable` Interface**

```java
class MyRunnable implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " value: " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(300); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}

public class Test2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t1 = new Thread(new MyRunnable(), "Gamma");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(new MyRunnable(), "Delta");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Comparison Table:**

| **Feature** | **Extending `Thread`** | **Implementing `Runnable`** |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance | Consumes the single inheritance slot | Preserves inheritance тАФ class can extend another |
| Object orientation | Less flexible | More flexible (decouples task from thread) |
| Sharing same task object | Not natural | Easy тАФ one `Runnable`, many `Thread`s |
| Best for thread pools | No | Yes (Executors accept `Runnable`) |
| KTU recommendation | Mention as alternative | **Preferred** |

**Conclusion:** Implementing `Runnable` is the **recommended** approach because it separates the *task* (what to do) from the *thread mechanism* (how to execute), enabling better reuse and cleaner object-oriented design.

---

#### Part (b) тАФ 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the lifecycle of a thread in Java. List the six states.**

**Model Solution:**

A Java thread passes through **six states** during its lifetime. The transition between states is governed by method calls and JVM events.

**The Six States:**

1. **NEW** тАФ Thread object exists, but `start()` has not been called.
2. **RUNNABLE** тАФ Thread is eligible for CPU time and may be running or ready.
3. **BLOCKED** тАФ Waiting to acquire a monitor lock for a synchronized block.
4. **WAITING** тАФ Waiting indefinitely (e.g., `wait()`, `join()` without timeout).
5. **TIMED_WAITING** тАФ Waiting for a specific time (e.g., `sleep(ms)`, `join(ms)`).
6. **TERMINATED** тАФ `run()` has completed or an uncaught exception terminated it.

**Lifecycle Diagram (drawn in answer script):**

```
        new Thread()
             |
             v
         +-------+
         |  NEW  |
         +-------+
             |  start()
             v
         +-----------+   acquire lock failed    +----------+
         | RUNNABLE  | ------------------->    | BLOCKED  |
         +-----------+                         +----------+
            |  ^  |  ^                                |
   sleep(ms)|  |  |  notify() / lock acquired        | lock acquired
   wait(ms) |  |  |  join() target dies              v
            v  |  |                            (back to RUNNABLE)
   +----------------+   timeout / signal
   | TIMED_WAITING  |---------> RUNNABLE
   +----------------+
            |
            |  wait() / join() (no timeout)
            v
   +----------------+
   |    WAITING     |
   +----------------+
            |  notify() / target dies
            v
        RUNNABLE
            |
            |  run() returns / exception
            v
       +------------+
       | TERMINATED |
       +------------+
```

**State Transition Examples (from code):**

- `Thread t = new Thread(r);` тЖТ t is in **NEW**.
- `t.start();` тЖТ t becomes **RUNNABLE**.
- Inside `run()`, `Thread.sleep(1000);` тЖТ t becomes **TIMED_WAITING** for 1 second.
- `t.join();` called from another thread тЖТ that thread becomes **WAITING**.
- `run()` returns normally тЖТ t becomes **TERMINATED** (cannot be restarted).

---

### QUESTION B тАФ 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) тАФ 7 Marks
**Write a Java program that creates two threads. One thread prints even numbers from 2 to 20, and the other prints odd numbers from 1 to 19. Use the `Runnable` interface. Explain the output.**

**Model Solution:**

```java
class EvenPrinter implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 2; i <= 20; i += 2) {
            System.out.println("Even: " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(150); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}

class OddPrinter implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 19; i += 2) {
            System.out.println("Odd: " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(150); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}

public class NumberThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread evenThread = new Thread(new EvenPrinter(), "Even-Worker");
        Thread oddThread  = new Thread(new OddPrinter(),  "Odd-Worker");
        
        evenThread.start();
        oddThread.start();
        
        System.out.println("Both threads launched by " +
                           Thread.currentThread().getName());
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Output:**

The output is **non-deterministic** тАФ the order in which "Even: 2" and "Odd: 1" appear depends on the JVM scheduler. A possible sample output is:

```
Both threads launched by main
Odd: 1
Even: 2
Odd: 3
Even: 4
Even: 6
Odd: 5
Even: 8
Odd: 7
... (interleaved) ...
```

But the order may vary on every run. To enforce strict alternation, one would need **synchronization** (using `wait()` and `notify()` or `synchronized` blocks), which is beyond the scope of "Creating Multiple Threads" but is the natural follow-up topic.

**Why `Runnable` is used:** It allows the program to extend other classes if needed and cleanly separates each printing task from the thread mechanism, which is the preferred KTU 2024 design.

---

#### Part (b) тАФ 7 Marks
**Explain the following `Thread` class methods with examples: `sleep()`, `join()`, `yield()`, and `setPriority()`.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. `sleep(long milliseconds)` тАФ Static method**

Pauses the currently executing thread for the specified duration. The thread enters the **TIMED_WAITING** state. Does not release any locks.

```java
try {
    Thread.sleep(2000);   // Sleep 2 seconds
} catch (InterruptedException e) {
    System.out.println("Sleep interrupted.");
}
```

**2. `join()` тАФ Instance method**

The calling thread waits until the target thread on which `join()` is invoked terminates.

```java
Thread t = new Thread(() -> {
    for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        System.out.println("Working...");
        try { Thread.sleep(500); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
    }
});
t.start();
t.join();   // Main thread waits here
System.out.println("Worker finished тАФ main proceeds.");
```

**3. `yield()` тАФ Static method**

Provides a *hint* to the thread scheduler that the current thread is willing to yield its current use of a CPU. The scheduler may ignore this hint. It does NOT cause the thread to sleep тАФ it remains in **RUNNABLE**.

```java
for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
    System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " " + i);
    Thread.yield();   // Suggest other threads get a chance
}
```

**4. `setPriority(int priority)` тАФ Instance method**

Sets the priority of a thread to a value between **1 (MIN)** and **10 (MAX)**, with **5 (NORM)** as the default. Higher-priority threads are *more likely* to be scheduled first, but this is platform-dependent.

```java
Thread low  = new Thread(r, "Low");
Thread high = new Thread(r, "High");
low.setPriority(Thread.MIN_PRIORITY);   // 1
high.setPriority(Thread.MAX_PRIORITY);  // 10
low.start();
high.start();
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Priority is a HINT, not a guarantee.** On many operating systems, the JVM cannot override the OS scheduler, so a low-priority thread may still run frequently. KTU expects students to mention this caveat.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to call `start()`** тАФ Writing `t.run()` instead of `t.start()` is the **#1 mistake**. The examiner deducts **2тАУ3 marks** instantly because it defeats the entire purpose of multithreading.
>
> 2. **Confusing `start()` as restartable** тАФ Calling `start()` twice on the same `Thread` object throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. Students who claim "calling start() again restarts the thread" lose marks.
>
> 3. **Ignoring `InterruptedException`** тАФ `sleep()`, `wait()`, and `join()` are checked exceptions. The code **must** be inside a `try-catch` block. Omitting this causes a **compilation error** тАФ guaranteed loss of marks in a coding question.
>
> 4. **Mixing up `Thread.currentThread().getName()` with `this.getName()`** тАФ In a `Runnable` implementation, `this` refers to the `Runnable` object (which has no `getName()`). Students must use `Thread.currentThread().getName()`. This is a classic KTU trap worth **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Assuming deterministic output order** тАФ Writing in the answer "the output will be..." with a fixed sequence loses marks because the scheduler is non-deterministic. Always state: *"The output order is not guaranteed due to thread scheduling."*
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the inheritance limitation of extending `Thread`** тАФ KTU specifically tests whether students can articulate the **design drawback** of using inheritance for thread creation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Multithreading** = multiple threads of execution within a single JVM process sharing the heap.
- [x] A **thread** is the smallest unit of dispatchable code, represented by a `java.lang.Thread` object.
- [x] **Two standard ways to create threads:** (1) Extending `Thread`, (2) Implementing `Runnable`.
- [x] **Preferred method (KTU 2024):** Implementing `Runnable` тАФ preserves inheritance, decouples task from thread, supports thread pools.
- [x] **`start()`** allocates a new call stack and invokes `run()`; **`run()`** is just a regular method and does **not** create a new thread.
- [x] **Six thread states:** NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED (defined in `Thread.State` enum since Java 5).
- [x] **`sleep(ms)`** тЖТ static, moves to TIMED_WAITING, does NOT release locks.
- [x] **`join()`** тЖТ calling thread waits for target thread to terminate (enters WAITING).
- [x] **`yield()`** тЖТ static hint to scheduler; thread remains RUNNABLE, no guarantee of effect.
- [x] **`setPriority(int)`** тЖТ accepts values 1 to 10; constants MIN_PRIORITY=1, NORM_PRIORITY=5, MAX_PRIORITY=10.
- [x] **`currentThread()`** тЖТ static method returning reference to the currently executing thread (essential in `Runnable` implementations).
- [x] **Lambda expressions** (Java 8+) can replace anonymous `Runnable` classes for concise syntax.
- [x] **Output order is non-deterministic** тАФ controlled by the JVM/OS thread scheduler, not by program order.
- [x] **Heap memory is shared** by all threads; **stacks are private** per thread тАФ basis for race conditions.
- [x] Calling `start()` twice on the same thread throws `IllegalThreadStateException` тАФ threads are **one-shot**.
- [x] A thread can be marked as a **daemon** via `setDaemon(true)` тАФ daemon threads do not prevent JVM exit.

---

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes тАФ "Creating Multiple Threads"**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Syllabus | Prepared per OBE/NEP 2020 guidelines*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synchronization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronization** in Java is the capability to control the access of multiple threads to any shared resource. It is a process of handling resource contention by ensuring that **only one thread** can access a shared resource (critical section) at any given point in time, thereby preventing **race conditions** and ensuring **data consistency**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3: Multithreading & String Handling), synchronization is built upon Java's intrinsic **monitor-based concurrency model**, where every Java object possesses an implicit lock (monitor) that threads must acquire before executing synchronized code.

### The Two Pillars of Synchronization

| Type | Purpose | Java Implementation |
|------|---------|---------------------|
| **Process Synchronization** | Ensure multiple processes don't execute critical sections simultaneously | OS-level / Message Passing |
| **Thread Synchronization** | Ensure multiple threads don't access shared resources simultaneously | `synchronized` keyword, `wait()`, `notify()` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The module focuses specifically on **Thread Synchronization** тАФ the mechanisms Java provides to coordinate the execution of multiple threads within a single process.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Restroom & The Key

Imagine a **public restroom with a single toilet** in a busy railway station. Only one person can use it at a time, right? How do we ensure this?

- The restroom has a **lock on its door**.
- When a person enters, they **lock the door from inside** (acquiring the lock).
- Other people wait outside until the door is unlocked.
- When the person finishes, they **unlock the door** (releasing the lock) and the next person enters.

**This is exactly how Java synchronization works:**
- The **restroom** = Shared resource (a method or object).
- The **door lock** = Java's intrinsic monitor lock.
- The **person** = A thread.
- **Locking the door** = Entering a `synchronized` block.
- **Unlocking the door** = Exiting the `synchronized` block.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Think of synchronization as a **"one-at-a-time policy"** тАФ *One resource, one thread, one moment in time.*

---

## 1.3 The Problem: Why Do We Need Synchronization?

Consider two threads accessing the same bank account object:

| Time | Thread-1 (Withdraw тВ╣1000) | Thread-2 (Withdraw тВ╣1000) | Balance |
|------|---------------------------|---------------------------|---------|
| t1 | Reads balance = тВ╣5000 | тАФ | тВ╣5000 |
| t2 | тАФ | Reads balance = тВ╣5000 | тВ╣5000 |
| t3 | Balance -= 1000 тЖТ writes тВ╣4000 | тАФ | тВ╣4000 |
| t4 | тАФ | Balance -= 1000 тЖТ writes тВ╣4000 | **тВ╣4000** тЭМ |

The account lost **only тВ╣1000** instead of **тВ╣2000** because both threads read the same value before either wrote back. This is called a **Race Condition**.

> [!WARNING]
> A **Race Condition** occurs when the output of a program depends on the unpredictable interleaving of threads. It leads to non-deterministic, erroneous behavior.

---

## 1.4 Java's Built-in Synchronization Tools (The Toolkit)

| Tool | Function | Key Type |
|------|----------|----------|
| `synchronized` method | Locks the entire method on `this` object | Intrinsic Lock |
| `synchronized` block | Locks only a specific section of code | Intrinsic Lock |
| `wait()` | Releases lock and pauses thread | Monitor Wait Set |
| `notify()` | Wakes up one waiting thread | Monitor Signal |
| `notifyAll()` | Wakes up all waiting threads | Monitor Signal |
| `volatile` keyword | Guarantees variable visibility across threads | Memory Barrier |
| `java.util.concurrent.locks` | Explicit lock API (advanced) | ReentrantLock |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the `synchronized` keyword, `wait()`/`notify()` mechanism, and the Producer-Consumer problem. Lock framework is supplementary knowledge.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Java's Intrinsic Lock (Monitor) тАФ The Heart of Synchronization

Every Java object has a **monitor** (also called an **intrinsic lock** or **mutex lock**) implicitly associated with it. The monitor is a single-bit lock тАФ at any instant, it is either **owned by a thread** or **free**.

### The Lifecycle of a Monitor Lock

1. **Free State** тАФ No thread owns the lock.
2. **Acquisition** тАФ A thread attempts to enter a `synchronized` block/method. If free, the thread **acquires** the lock atomically.
3. **Held State** тАФ The thread owns the lock. Other threads attempting to enter are **blocked** (placed in the entry set).
4. **Release** тАФ When the thread exits the `synchronized` block/method (normally or via `wait()`), the lock is **released** and a waiting thread is unblocked.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reentrancy Rule:** Java's intrinsic locks are **reentrant** тАФ a thread that already holds a lock on an object can re-acquire it on the same object (e.g., one synchronized method calling another synchronized method on `this`). The JVM maintains an **acquisition count** that is incremented on entry and decremented on exit.

---

## 2.2 Types of `synchronized` Blocks

### A. Synchronized Instance Method

The entire method body is a critical section, and the lock is on the current object (`this`).

```java
class Table {
    synchronized void printTable(int n) {  // Lock on 'this' object
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(n + " x " + i + " = " + (n * i));
            try { Thread.sleep(400); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}
```

### B. Synchronized Static Method

The lock is on the **Class object** (`Table.class`), not on any instance. This means synchronization spans **all instances** of the class.

```java
class Table {
    static synchronized void printTable(int n) {  // Lock on Table.class
        // ...
    }
}
```

### C. Synchronized Block (Fine-Grained Locking)

Only a specific block of code is locked, on a **user-specified object**. This is the **most performant** option because it minimizes the critical section.

```java
void printTable(int n) {
    synchronized (this) {           // Lock on current object
        // critical section
    }
}

void printTable2(int n) {
    synchronized (MyClass.class) {   // Lock on Class object
        // critical section
    }
}

private final Object lock = new Object();
void printTable3(int n) {
    synchronized (lock) {            // Lock on a dedicated object
        // critical section
    }
}
```

---

## 2.3 The `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` Mechanism тАФ Inter-Thread Communication

The `Object` class in Java provides three **final** methods that can only be called inside a `synchronized` context:

| Method | Returns | Effect on Caller | Effect on Lock |
|--------|---------|------------------|----------------|
| `wait()` | void | Pauses the thread and puts it in the **wait set** | **Releases** the lock |
| `notify()` | void | Wakes up **one** arbitrary thread from the wait set | Does NOT release lock |
| `notifyAll()` | void | Wakes up **all** threads from the wait set | Does NOT release lock |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** `wait()` **releases** the lock, but `notify()` does **NOT** release the lock. The notifying thread continues to hold the lock until it exits the synchronized block. The woken thread must then re-acquire the lock before proceeding.

### The Mandatory Rules (Cited in KTU Answer Keys)

1. `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()` **must** be called from within a `synchronized` context. Otherwise, `IllegalMonitorStateException` is thrown.
2. `wait()` must be enclosed in a `try-catch` block for `InterruptedException`.
3. The condition being waited upon should **always** be checked inside a `while` loop (not an `if` statement) to guard against **spurious wakeups**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Quick Reference

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | Key Point |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| **Object Monitor** | Implicit on every `new` object() | Only one thread can hold it at a time |
| **Class Monitor** | `MyClass.class` (Class object) | Used for static synchronization |
| **Synchronized Method** | `synchronized void m() {}` | Lock = `this` |
| **Synchronized Static Method** | `static synchronized void m() {}` | Lock = `ClassName.class` |
| **Synchronized Block** | `synchronized(obj) { ... }` | Lock = `obj` (user-chosen) |
| **Reentrancy** | Same thread can re-enter | Acquisition count maintained by JVM |
| **`wait()`** | Releases lock, blocks thread | Must be in `synchronized` + `try-catch` |
| **`notify()`** | Wakes one waiter, keeps lock | Must be in `synchronized` |
