This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Memorize Before the Exam:** - **Delegation Event Model** (introduced in **JDK 1.1**) replaced the Java 1.0 inheritance-based model. - **Three Core Components:** Event Source, Event Object, Event Listener. - **Root class** of all events: `java.util.EventObject`. The `getSource()` method comes from this root. - **Registration Syntax:** `component.addXXXListener(listenerObject)`. - **Handler Method Signature:** Always returns `void` and takes the event object as the only argument. - **Interfaces with ONE method** (no adapter exists): `ActionListener`, `ItemListener`. - **Interfaces with MULTIPLE methods** (adapter exists): `MouseListener` (5), `KeyListener` (3), `WindowListener` (7), `FocusListener` (2). - **Adapter Class Naming Convention:** `Adapter` (e.g., `MouseListener` тЖТ `MouseAdapter`). - **Three Implementation Approaches:** Interface implementation, Adapter classes, Anonymous inner classes. - **Java 8+:** Lambda expressions can replace anonymous classes for functional interfaces like `ActionListener`. - **Common Event Constants:** `MouseEvent.BUTTON1_MASK`, `KeyEvent.VK_ENTER`, `WindowEvent.WINDOW_CLOSING`. - **Package:** All core AWT event classes and interfaces are in `java.awt.event`; Swing-specific events are in `javax.swing.event`. - **Window Closing:** Always override `windowClosing(WindowEvent e)` and call `System.exit(0)` to terminate the program. - **Polymorphism Advantage:** A single listener can be registered with multiple sources; a single source can have multiple listeners. - **Key Advantage Over Old Model:** Efficient, modular, and avoids the brittle deep inheritance hierarchy of Java 1.0.

topics.title: Delegation Event Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Delegation Event Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ЁЯУШ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** is the modern, standard event-handling architecture adopted by **Java AWT and Swing (post JDK 1.1)**. It is built on the principle of **"publish-subscribe"** where an *Event Source* (the component that triggers an event) *delegates* the responsibility of handling the event to a separate object known as the *Event Listener* (also called the Event Handler).

In KTU terminology, the model is described as:

> *"A mechanism in which an event generated by a component (source) is forwarded (delegated) to a registered listener object, which contains the logic to respond to that event."*

### Core Constituents of the Model

The Delegation Event Model revolves around **three primary actors** and **two supporting entities**:

| # | Entity | Role |
|---|--------|------|
| 1 | **Event** | An object that describes the state change (e.g., click, key press). |
| 2 | **Event Source** | The GUI component that *generates* / *fires* the event. |
| 3 | **Event Listener / Handler** | The object that *receives* and *processes* the event. |
| 4 | **Adapter Classes** | Empty default-implementation classes to simplify listener coding. |
| 5 | **Event Dispatcher (AWT EventQueue)** | Internal mechanism that routes events to listeners. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "Newspaper Subscription" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the Delegation Event Model like a Newspaper Delivery System.**

- **You (the Event Listener)** *subscribe* to a newspaper. You are not the one who *prints* the paper, you just want to *receive* it.
- **The Newspaper Press (the Event Source)** *publishes* the paper. It does not know exactly *who* is reading, only that someone is subscribed.
- **The Subscription Form (the Listener Registration)** is the agreement: *"I, the listener, promise to receive your news."*
- **The Newspaper itself (the Event Object)** carries the actual content of *what happened* (headline, date, page number).

**In Java terms:**
1. The listener object **registers** itself with the source using `source.addListener(listener)`.
2. When the user interacts (clicks, types, moves), the source **creates an event object**.
3. The source **invokes the listener's method**, passing the event object as a parameter.
4. The listener's method **executes the response logic**.

This is **delegation** тАФ the source delegates handling to someone else, instead of handling it itself (which was the old JDK 1.0 inheritance-based model).

---

## 1.3 KTU Syllabus Highlights & Must-Know Keywords

> [!IMPORTANT]
> High-frequency KTU keywords you must recognize in exam questions:
> - **Event Source** тАФ the generator (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`).
> - **Event Listener** тАФ the receiver; must implement an interface from `java.awt.event` or `javax.swing.event`.
> - **Event Object** тАФ subclass of `java.util.EventObject`.
> - **Registration** тАФ `component.addXxxListener(listenerObj)`.
> - **Multicast listener support** тАФ one listener can be registered to many sources; one source can have many listeners.
> - **Event package** тАФ `java.awt.event.*` (AWT) and `javax.swing.event.*` (Swing).

### Hierarchy Snapshot (Top-Level)

```text
java.util.EventObject
        тФФтФАтФА java.awt.AWTEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА ActionEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА MouseEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА KeyEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА WindowEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА ItemEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА TextEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА AdjustmentEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА ComponentEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА FocusEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА InputEvent
                тФФтФАтФА PaintEvent
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ЁЯУШ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown тАФ The Five-Step Lifecycle of an Event

> [!NOTE]
> A complete event-handling round-trip in Java follows this **deterministic 5-step lifecycle**:

1. **Event Occurrence (User Action):**
   The user performs an action тАФ clicking a button, typing a key, or moving the mouse. The underlying OS notifies the **JVM** of this action.

2. **Event Object Construction:**
   The AWT/Swing toolkit **instantiates an `EventObject` subclass** (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`) on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**. This object encapsulates all relevant data (coordinates, modifiers, time stamp).

3. **Source Identification:**
   The toolkit identifies which **Event Source** generated the event (the `JButton` instance, for example).

4. **Listener Invocation (Delegation):**
   For every listener registered with that source, the source **invokes the appropriate callback method** on the listener object, passing the event object as the argument.

5. **Listener Callback Execution:**
   The listener's method body runs, performing whatever response logic the programmer defined. Return value is generally ignored (`void`).

---

## 2.2 Mechanism Details тАФ Interfaces and Methods

Each event category has a corresponding **listener interface**, all of which extend the marker interface `java.util.EventListener`.

### Key Listener Interfaces & Their Methods

| Listener Interface | Key Method(s) | Triggered By |
|--------------------|---------------|--------------|
| `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | Button click, Enter in text field, menu item select |
| `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` | Mouse interactions |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved` | Mouse movement |
| `KeyListener` | `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped` | Keyboard input |
| `WindowListener` | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated` | Window lifecycle |
| `FocusListener` | `focusGained`, `focusLost` | Component focus change |
| `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` | Checkbox, Radio, Choice |
| `TextListener` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent e)` | Text field change |
| `AdjustmentListener` | `adjustmentValueChanged` | Scrollbar movement |

### Registration Method Convention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All AWT/Swing components follow the **strict naming convention**:
> `public void add<EventType>Listener(<EventType>Listener l)`
> `public void remove<EventType>Listener(<EventType>Listener l)`
>
> Example: For a `JButton` (which fires `ActionEvent`):
> - `button.addActionListener(myListener)` тЬЕ
> - `button.addAction(myListener)` тЭМ *(Does not exist)*

---

## 2.3 The Process of Delegation тАФ Internal Architecture

### 2.3.1 Source-Delegator Mechanics

The **Event Source** maintains an internal data structure (typically a list or vector) of all registered listeners. When an event is generated, it iterates through this list and invokes the appropriate method on each.

### 2.3.2 Multicast vs Unicast

> [!NOTE]
> **Java AWT/Swing listeners are MULTICAST** by design:
> - **One source тЖТ Many listeners:** A single button can fire its `ActionEvent` to multiple `ActionListener` objects.
> - **One listener тЖТ Many sources:** The same `ActionListener` object can be registered to multiple buttons.

This contrasts with the older **JDK 1.0 inheritance-based model** where a component could only respond to its own overridden methods тАФ severely limiting flexibility.

---

## 2.4 Adapter Classes тАФ The Listener Code Reducer

Many listener interfaces (e.g., `MouseListener`, `WindowListener`, `KeyListener`) declare **multiple methods**. If you only need one, you still must implement *all* (or supply empty bodies).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adapter classes solve this** by providing **default empty implementations** of all interface methods. You subclass the adapter and override only what you need.

### Adapter Class Map

| Listener Interface | Corresponding Adapter |
|--------------------|----------------------|
| `MouseListener` | `MouseAdapter` |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `MouseMotionAdapter` |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyAdapter` |
| `WindowListener` | `WindowAdapter` |
| `FocusListener` | `FocusAdapter` |
| `ComponentListener` | `ComponentAdapter` |
| `ContainerListener` | `ContainerAdapter` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interfaces with single methods** (like `ActionListener`, `ItemListener`) have **no adapter classes** тАФ there is nothing to reduce.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Syntax Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Notes |
|---------|------------------|-------|
| Listener Registration | `sourceObj.addXxxListener(listenerObj);` | Mandatory before events fire |
| Listener Deregistration | `sourceObj.removeXxxListener(listenerObj);` | Useful for dynamic UIs |
| Event Object Access | `e.getSource()` | Returns the originating component |
| Get Action Command | `e.getActionCommand()` | For `ActionEvent` |
| Get Mouse Coords | `e.getX()`, `e.getY()` | For `MouseEvent` |
| Get Key Char | `e.getKeyChar()` | For `KeyEvent` |
| Get Window Op | `e.getOppositeWindow()` | For `WindowEvent` |
| Inheritance Keyword | `implements XxxListener` | OR `extends XxxAdapter` |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Delegation Event Model is not just academic тАФ it is the **backbone of every Java desktop application, IDE plugin framework (NetBeans Platform), and Android UI system** (Android events are conceptually a *direct descendant* of this model via `View.OnClickListener`).

In industry:
- **GUI Frameworks:** JavaFX, Swing, AWT тАФ all rely on it.
- **Server frameworks:** Servlet listeners (`HttpSessionListener`, `ServletContextListener`) follow the same delegation pattern.
- **Testing frameworks:** JUnit, TestNG use listener-based hooks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ЁЯУШ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix тАФ "Which Listener Strategy to Use?"

> [!NOTE]
> Before writing listener code, consult this decision matrix тАФ it is a **frequently tested KTU concept**.

```
START
  тФВ
  тФЬтФАтФАтФА Does the listener interface have only ONE method?
  тФВ        тФЬтФАтФА YES тФАтФАтЦ║ Use "implements" directly OR Anonymous Inner Class
  тФВ        тФВ           (e.g., ActionListener, ItemListener)
  тФВ        тФВ
  тФВ        тФФтФАтФА NO тФАтФАтЦ║ Does the listener interface have >1 method?
  тФВ                     тФЬтФАтФА YES тФАтФАтЦ║ Use Adapter class (extends)
  тФВ                     тФВ           (e.g., WindowListener тЖТ WindowAdapter)
  тФВ                     тФВ
  тФВ                     тФФтФАтФА Use case demands only one event at one place?
  тФВ                              тФЬтФАтФА YES тФАтФАтЦ║ Anonymous Inner Class
  тФВ                              тФФтФАтФА NO  тФАтФАтЦ║ Separate Listener Class / Lambda
  тФВ
END
```

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation тАФ Building a Complete Event-Handling Program

We will derive a **Button Click Counter** application in **four progressively sophisticated ways** тАФ a classic KTU module-4 question.

### Method 1: Separate Class Implementing the Interface

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step derivation of "Separate Class" approach:**

**Step 1:** Define the listener class implementing `ActionListener`.

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

class MyHandler implements ActionListener {
    private int count = 0;
    
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        count++;
        System.out.println("Button clicked " + count + " times.");
    }
}
```

**Step 2:** Construct the GUI and register the listener.

```java
public class Demo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Frame f = new Frame("Delegation Model - Demo 1");
        Button b = new Button("Click Me");
        
        // 1. Create listener object
        MyHandler handler = new MyHandler();
        
        // 2. Register (delegate) the listener with the source
        b.addActionListener(handler);
        
        f.add(b);
        f.setSize(300, 200);
        f.setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Step 3 тАФ Trace execution:**
- User clicks button тЖТ AWT creates `ActionEvent e`.
- AWT invokes `handler.actionPerformed(e)`.
- Inside the method, `count` increments; message prints.

---

### Method 2: Same Class Implements Listener

> [!NOTE]
> The outer class itself implements `ActionListener` тАФ common for small programs.

```java
public class Demo2 extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Button b;
    int count = 0;
    
    Demo2() {
        b = new Button("Click Me");
        b.addActionListener(this);   // 'this' is the listener
        add(b);
        setSize(300, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        count++;
        b.setLabel("Clicked: " + count);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Demo2();
    }
}
```

---

### Method 3: Anonymous Inner Class (Most Popular тАФ KTU Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Anonymous inner classes **inline-implement** the interface at the point of registration. **Highly tested in KTU 2024 scheme.**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class Demo3 extends Frame {
    int count = 0;
    
    Demo3() {
        Button b = new Button("Click Me");
        
        // Anonymous ActionListener object тАФ defined and instantiated inline
        b.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                count++;
                System.out.println("Anonymous click count = " + count);
            }
        });
        
        add(b);
        setSize(300, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Demo3();
    }
}
```

**Derivation logic for anonymous inner classes:**

```
new InterfaceName() {
    // override every method (or just the one you need)
    public void methodName(args) {
        // your code
    }
};
```

This syntactic structure is equivalent to:
1. Declaring a class `X implements ActionListener` with no name.
2. Overriding `actionPerformed` in that class.
3. Instantiating it via `new X()`.

---

### Method 4: Using an Adapter Class (Window Closing Example)

> [!NOTE]
> `WindowListener` has **7 methods** тАФ using the adapter saves you from writing 6 empty methods.

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class Demo4 extends Frame {
    Demo4() {
        setSize(300, 200);
        setVisible(true);
        
        // Anonymous subclass of WindowAdapter тАФ override ONLY windowClosing
        this.addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Window is closing...");
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Demo4();
    }
}
```

---

## 3.3 Lambda Expression Variant (Java 8+ тАФ KTU 2024 Bonus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since `ActionListener` is a **functional interface** (single abstract method), it can be replaced with a **lambda expression**.

```java
b.addActionListener(e -> {
    count++;
    System.out.println("Lambda click: " + count);
});
```

This is functionally equivalent to Method 3's anonymous class but is **more concise** and is increasingly appearing in KTU module-level questions.

---

## 3.4 Event Source Identification тАФ `getSource()` Derivation

When the **same listener** is registered to **multiple sources**, you must distinguish them using `e.getSource()`.

```java
class MultiHandler implements ActionListener {
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == buttonA) {
            System.out.println("Button A pressed");
        } else if (e.getSource() == buttonB) {
            System.out.println("Button B pressed");
        }
    }
}
```

**Trace derivation:**
1. User clicks `buttonA` тЖТ AWT creates `ActionEvent` with `source = buttonA`.
2. AWT invokes `multiHandler.actionPerformed(e)`.
3. Inside the method, `e.getSource()` returns the actual button reference.
4. Comparison identifies which button was clicked.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Table тАФ Listener Approaches

| Approach | Verbosity | Reusability | Best Use Case |
|----------|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| Separate class implementing interface | Low | High | Multi-source listeners, large projects |
| Same class implements interface | Low | Low | Single component, small demos |
| Anonymous inner class | Medium | None | One-off, single-source listeners |
| Adapter class | Low | Medium | Interfaces with many methods |
| Lambda expression | **Lowest** | None | Functional interfaces, Java 8+ |

---

## 3.6 Event Generation Trigger Mapping (Analytical Matrix)

| User Action | Event Class | Listener Interface | Method Called |
|-------------|-------------|--------------------|---------------|
| Click a button | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed` |
| Type a character | `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped` |
| Press mouse button | `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` | `mousePressed` |
| Move mouse over component | `MouseEvent` | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseMoved` |
| Close a window | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowClosing` |
| Tick / untick checkbox | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged` |
| Focus enters field | `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | `focusGained` |
| Resize a component | `ComponentEvent` | `ComponentListener` | `componentResized` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ЁЯУШ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Delegation Event Model тАФ Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    userA[USER ACTION - Click, Type, Move] --> jvmA[JVM - Detects Native Event]
    jvmA --> srcA[EVENT SOURCE - e.g. JButton]
    
    srcA --> evtObjA[EVENT OBJECT - e.g. ActionEvent]
    srcA --> lstRegA[LISTENER REGISTRY - Internal List]
    
    lstRegA --> lstA1[LISTENER 1 - e.g. ActionListener 1]
    lstRegA --> lstA2[LISTENER 2 - e.g. ActionListener 2]
    
    evtObjA --> edThreadA[EVENT DISPATCH THREAD - EDT]
    edThreadA --> invA[INVOKE CALLBACK METHOD]
    
    invA --> lstA1
    invA --> lstA2
    
    lstA1 --> respA1[RESPONSE LOGIC 1 - e.g. increment counter]
    lstA2 --> respA2[RESPONSE LOGIC 2 - e.g. log action]
```

---

## 4.2 Event Class Inheritance Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    utilEvt[java util EventObject] --> awtEvt[java awt AWTEvent]
    
    awtEvt --> actEvt[ActionEvent]
    awtEvt --> mouEvt[MouseEvent]
    awtEvt --> keyEvt[KeyEvent]
    awtEvt --> winEvt[WindowEvent]
    awtEvt --> itmEvt[ItemEvent]
    awtEvt --> txtEvt[TextEvent]
    awtEvt --> adjEvt[AdjustmentEvent]
    awtEvt --> focEvt[FocusEvent]
    awtEvt --> compEvt[ComponentEvent]
    awtEvt --> inEvt[InputEvent]
    awtEvt --> paintEvt[PaintEvent]
    
    inEvt --> mouEvt
    inEvt --> keyEvt
    
    compEvt --> focEvt
    compEvt --> paintEvt
    compEvt --> winEvt
```

---

## 4.3 Listener Interface тЖФ Event Class Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    actL[ActionListener] --- actE[ActionEvent]
    mouL[MouseListener] --- mouE[MouseEvent]
    mmL[MouseMotionListener] --- mouE
    keyL[KeyListener] --- keyE[KeyEvent]
    winL[WindowListener] --- winE[WindowEvent]
    focL[FocusListener] --- focE[FocusEvent]
    itmL[ItemListener] --- itmE[ItemEvent]
    txtL[TextListener] --- txtE[TextEvent]
    adjL[AdjustmentListener] --- adjE[AdjustmentEvent]
```

---

## 4.4 Step-by-Step Lifecycle Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1[Step 1: User Interaction] --> step2[Step 2: JVM Captures Native Event]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Toolkit Constructs EventObject Subclass]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Source Identifies Registered Listeners]
    step4 --> step5{Step 5: Listeners Present?}
    step5 -->|No| step6[Step 6: Event Discarded - Silent]
    step5 -->|Yes| step7[Step 7: Invoke Each Listener Method]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Listener Method Executes Response]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Return Control to Event Dispatcher]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10: Await Next Event]
```

## 4.5 Listener Registration Pattern (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    compA[Component - Event Source] --> regM[addActionListener Method]
    regM --> lstStore[Internal Listener Vector]
    
    lstStore --> lstA1[Listener A]
    lstStore --> lstA2[Listener B]
    lstStore --> lstA3[Listener C]
    
    actEvt2[ActionEvent Triggered] --> invokeM[Invoke Action]
    invokeM --> lstA1
    invokeM --> lstA2
    invokeM --> lstA3
```

# ЁЯУШ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ЁЯУЭ PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (2 ├Ч 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. Define the Delegation Event Model. List its key components.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Delegation Event Model** is Java's standard event-handling framework (introduced in JDK 1.1) in which an *Event Source* delegates the responsibility of processing an event to one or more registered *Event Listener* objects, rather than handling the event itself.

**Key Components:**
1. **Event Object** тАФ encapsulates the event's data.
2. **Event Source** тАФ the component that generates the event.
3. **Event Listener** тАФ the registered object that responds.
4. **Event Dispatcher** тАФ internal mechanism routing events.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5 marks) + 4 components with one-line description (1.5 marks).

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between an Event Listener Interface and an Adapter Class with an example.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Listener Interface | Adapter Class |
|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| Definition | Abstract contract of event-handling methods | Default empty implementation of an interface |
| Methods | All abstract | All empty concrete (or default) |
| Usage | `implements` | `extends` |
| Methods to override | Must override *all* | Override *only* required ones |
| Example | `WindowListener` (7 methods) | `WindowAdapter` (all 7 empty) |
| Existence | Always present | Only when interface has > 1 method |

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** `WindowListener` requires you to write all 7 methods even if you only need `windowClosing`. Using `WindowAdapter`, you extend it and override only `windowClosing`.

---

---

## ЁЯУЭ PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 1 ├Ч 14 = 14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Delegation Event Model** in Java with a neat block diagram. Discuss the roles of event source, event object, and event listener. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program using AWT to create a frame with two buttons labeled "RED" and "BLUE". When the user clicks RED, the background color of the frame must change to red, and when BLUE is clicked, it changes to blue. Use the **Delegation Event Model** with **anonymous inner classes**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) тАФ Explanation (7 marks)

**Delegation Event Model Definition (2 marks):**
The Delegation Event Model is a modern event-handling framework in Java AWT/Swing (introduced in JDK 1.1) that follows the **publish-subscribe** pattern. The event-generating component (source) does not handle the event itself; instead, it *delegates* the processing to one or more external *listener* objects that have been registered for that event type.

**Three Key Roles (3 marks):**

1. **Event Object:**
   - Subclass of `java.util.EventObject`.
   - Carries information about *what happened* тАФ e.g., source component, coordinates, modifiers, time.
   - Example: `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`.

2. **Event Source:**
   - The GUI component that *detects* the user action and *fires* the event.
   - Maintains an internal list of registered listeners.
   - Example: `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JFrame`.

3. **Event Listener:**
   - An object implementing a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`).
   - Contains the **callback method** that responds to the event.
   - Receives the event object as parameter and executes the response logic.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Event Source] -->|invokes| lstA[Event Listener]
    srcA -->|creates| evtA[Event Object]
    evtA -->|passed to| lstA
    lstA -->|executes| respA[Response Logic]
```

---

#### Part (b) тАФ Java Program (7 marks)

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ColorChanger extends Frame {
    
    ColorChanger() {
        // Set up the frame
        setTitle("Color Changer - Delegation Model Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setVisible(true);
        
        // Create two buttons
        Button redBtn = new Button("RED");
        Button blueBtn = new Button("BLUE");
        
        // Register anonymous inner class listener for RED button
        redBtn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                setBackground(Color.RED);
            }
        });
        
        // Register anonymous inner class listener for BLUE button
        blueBtn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                setBackground(Color.BLUE);
            }
        });
        
        // Add buttons to frame
        add(redBtn);
        add(blueBtn);
        
        // Close-window handling using WindowAdapter
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ColorChanger();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Code Logic (for full marks):**
- `ColorChanger` extends `Frame` тАФ providing the GUI window.
- Two `Button` objects (RED, BLUE) are created and act as **Event Sources**.
- For each button, an **anonymous inner class** implements `ActionListener`, overriding `actionPerformed`.
- Inside the override, `setBackground()` is called with the appropriate color.
- The `WindowAdapter` is used to gracefully close the window тАФ overriding only `windowClosing`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice**

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** What are **Adapter Classes** in Java? Why are they used? Write a program that uses a `MouseAdapter` to detect and display a message when the mouse is clicked inside a `Frame`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between the **JDK 1.0 event model** and the **Delegation Event Model**. List any three advantages of the Delegation Model. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) тАФ Adapter Classes (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Adapter classes are **concrete helper classes** in `java.awt.event` package that provide **default empty implementations** for listener interfaces having **more than one method**. They simplify event-handling code by allowing programmers to override only the methods they need.

**Why used (2 marks):**
- Reduces code verbosity.
- Avoids writing empty method bodies for unused callbacks.
- Improves readability and maintainability.

**Java Program Using MouseAdapter (3 marks):**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class MouseDemo extends Frame {
    
    MouseDemo() {
        setTitle("Mouse Adapter Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setVisible(true);
        
        // Anonymous subclass of MouseAdapter
        addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
                int x = e.getX();
                int y = e.getY();
                System.out.println("Mouse clicked at (" + x + ", " + y + ")");
            }
        });
        
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new MouseDemo();
    }
}
```

---

#### Part (b) тАФ JDK 1.0 vs Delegation Model (7 marks)

| Aspect | JDK 1.0 Event Model | Delegation Event Model |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Mechanism | Component handles its own events via inheritance | Component delegates to separate listener objects |
| Event hierarchy | Components override methods like `action()`, `mouseDown()` | Listener interfaces with semantically grouped methods |
| Method names | Verbose (`mouseEnter`, `mouseExit`) | Java-conventional (`mouseEntered`, `mouseExited`) |
| Flexibility | Limited тАФ each component had predefined events | High тАФ custom events possible |
| Extensibility | Difficult | Easy via custom EventObject + Listener interfaces |
| SourceтАУListener relationship | Tightly coupled via inheritance | Loosely coupled via composition |
| Multicast support | No | Yes (many listeners per source) |

**Three Advantages of Delegation Model (3 marks):**

1. **Loose Coupling** тАФ Source and listener are independent, promoting modular design.
2. **Multicast Support** тАФ Multiple listeners can respond to the same event from a single source.
3. **Extensibility** тАФ Custom event types can be created by extending `EventObject` and `EventListener` interfaces.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### тЪая╕П KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls
>
> **Where students typically LOSE MARKS in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation** тАФ KTU examiners deduct marks for not following Java best practices.
> 2. **Using the wrong method name** тАФ Students often write `mouseClick` instead of `mouseClicked`; `keyPress` instead of `keyPressed`. Memorize the exact API.
> 3. **Confusing `addActionListener` with `addAction`** тАФ The latter **does not exist**. Always use the listener-suffix convention.
> 4. **Not closing the window in demos** тАФ Examiners expect `addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() { ... windowClosing ... })` in every AWT program.
> 5. **Mixing inheritance and delegation in answers** тАФ When asked about DEM, do NOT mention `Component.handleEvent()` (the JDK 1.0 method).
> 6. **Forgetting the semicolon after anonymous inner class** тАФ `b.addActionListener(new ActionListener() { ... });` тАФ the final `;` is mandatory.
> 7. **Confusing `e.getSource()` with `e.getActionCommand()`** тАФ `getSource()` returns the component reference; `getActionCommand()` returns the button's label string.
> 8. **Forgetting to set `setVisible(true)`** тАФ Program will compile but show nothing; examiner deducts for non-functional output.

---

## ЁЯза TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Delegation Event Model**

### тЬЕ Core Concepts
- [x] **DEM = Publish-Subscribe Pattern** тАФ source publishes, listener subscribes.
- [x] **Three actors:** Event, Event Source, Event Listener.
- [x] **Multicast** by default тАФ one source, many listeners; one listener, many sources.
- [x] **All event classes** extend `java.util.EventObject`.
- [x] **All listener interfaces** extend `java.util.EventListener` (marker interface).

### тЬЕ Critical APIs to Memorize
- [x] `addXxxListener(Listener)` тАФ registration.
- [x] `removeXxxListener(Listener)` тАФ deregistration.
- [x] `e.getSource()` тАФ identify originating component.
- [x] `e.getActionCommand()` тАФ get button label from `ActionEvent`.
- [x] `e.getX()` / `e.getY()` тАФ mouse coordinates.

### тЬЕ Four Listener Implementation Strategies
- [x] **Separate class implementing interface** тАФ most reusable.
- [x] **Same class implements interface** тАФ compact for small demos.
- [x] **Anonymous inner class** тАФ KTU favorite, inline.
- [x] **Adapter class** тАФ for interfaces with > 1 method.
- [x] **Lambda expression** тАФ for functional interfaces (Java 8+).

### тЬЕ Key Adapter Classes
- [x] `MouseAdapter` тЖР `MouseListener` (5 methods)
- [x] `MouseMotionAdapter` тЖР `MouseMotionListener` (2 methods)
- [x] `KeyAdapter` тЖР `KeyListener` (3 methods)
- [x] `WindowAdapter` тЖР `WindowListener` (7 methods)
- [x] `FocusAdapter` тЖР `FocusListener` (2 methods)

### тЬЕ Programming Rule of Thumb
- [x] Single-method interface тЖТ `implements` directly OR lambda.
- [x] Multi-method interface тЖТ use `Adapter` to avoid empty bodies.
- [x] Always close AWT windows using `WindowAdapter`.

### тЬЕ Exam Trivia
- [x] Delegation Model introduced in **JDK 1.1**.
- [x] It **replaced** the JDK 1.0 inheritance-based model.
- [x] Event dispatching happens on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**.
- [x] `Component.handleEvent()` is a **legacy** method from JDK 1.0 тАФ avoid in answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Event Classes** in Java are specialized classes that encapsulate information about an occurrence (event) within a graphical user interface. They are part of the **Delegation Event Model** and reside primarily in the `java.awt.event` and `javax.swing.event` packages. Every event in Java's AWT/Swing framework is represented by an instance of an event class that extends `java.awt.AWTEvent`, which in turn extends `java.util.EventObject`.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEERT416 (Object Oriented Programming using Java)**, Module 4 covers GUI Programming, and **Event Classes** form the foundation of the **Event Handling Mechanism**. When a user interacts with a GUI component (e.g., clicks a button, types on the keyboard, moves the mouse), the AWT/Swing framework creates an event object belonging to the appropriate event class. This object is then dispatched to a registered **Listener** object whose callback method handles the event.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Point:** An *Event Class* defines **what** happened. A *Listener Interface* defines **how** to respond. The two are tightly coupled тАФ each listener interface corresponds to one (or more) specific event class.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a busy **hospital emergency department**:

| Hospital Analogy | Java Event Class Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A patient arrives with chest pain | An **event occurs** (e.g., button click) |
| The triage nurse fills out a **medical form** describing the situation | An **Event object** is instantiated with details (source, type, coordinates, etc.) |
| The form goes to the **cardiology department** | The event is dispatched to the registered **Listener** |
| The cardiologist treats based on the form's content | The listener's **callback method** is invoked |

Just as the hospital has **specialized forms** for different emergencies (cardiac form, trauma form, allergy form), Java has **specialized Event Classes** for different user actions (ActionEvent, MouseEvent, KeyEvent, etc.). Each form contains the precise information needed to handle that specific type of emergency.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Critical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Constants and Designations in Java Event Handling:**
> - **Event Class Root:** `java.util.EventObject` тАФ the ultimate superclass
> - **AWT Event Root:** `java.awt.AWTEvent` тАФ superclass for all AWT events
> - **Total Standard AWT Event Types:** **11 major event classes** (ActionEvent, AdjustmentEvent, ComponentEvent, ContainerEvent, FocusEvent, ItemEvent, KeyEvent, MouseEvent, MouseWheelEvent, TextEvent, WindowEvent)
> - **Event ID Type:** `int` (returned by `getID()`)
> - **Default Package:** `java.awt.event`
> - **Event Source:** Always the **object on which the event originally occurred**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Event Class Hierarchy (Theoretical Foundation)

Java's event classes are organized in a strict **inheritance hierarchy**. Understanding this hierarchy is critical for KTU exams because it appears frequently in both short-answer and 14-mark questions.

```
java.util.EventObject               тЖР Root of all Java events
        тФВ
        тФФтФАтФА java.awt.AWTEvent       тЖР Root of all AWT events
                тФВ
                тФЬтФАтФА ActionEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА AdjustmentEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА ItemEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА TextEvent
                тФЬтФАтФА ComponentEvent
                тФВ       тФЬтФАтФА ContainerEvent
                тФВ       тФЬтФАтФА FocusEvent
                тФВ       тФЬтФАтФА PaintEvent
                тФВ       тФФтФАтФА WindowEvent
                тФФтФАтФА InputEvent
                        тФЬтФАтФА KeyEvent
                        тФФтФАтФА MouseEvent
                                тФФтФАтФА MouseWheelEvent
```

### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Each Event Class

#### **2.2.1 ActionEvent** (`java.awt.event.ActionEvent`)

| Property | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Triggered By** | Button click, menu item selection, list item double-click, pressing **Enter** in a `TextField` |
| **Corresponding Listener** | `ActionListener` |
| **Callback Method** | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| **Key Methods** | `getActionCommand()`, `getModifiers()` |
| **Associated Constant** | `ACTION_PERFORMED` (id = 1001) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ActionEvent is the most commonly tested event class** in KTU exams. A button click is the classic example.

#### **2.2.2 MouseEvent** (`java.awt.event.MouseEvent`)

| Property | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Triggered By** | Mouse click, press, release, enter, exit, move, drag |
| **Corresponding Listeners** | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener`, `MouseWheelListener` |
| **Callback Methods** | `mouseClicked()`, `mousePressed()`, `mouseReleased()`, `mouseEntered()`, `mouseExited()`, `mouseDragged()`, `mouseMoved()`, `mouseWheelMoved()` |
| **Key Methods** | `getX()`, `getY()`, `getPoint()`, `getClickCount()`, `getButton()`, `isPopupTrigger()` |
| **Inherited From InputEvent** | `isAltDown()`, `isControlDown()`, `isShiftDown()`, `isMetaDown()` |

> [!NOTE]
> **MouseListener handles 5 events** (click, press, release, enter, exit), while **MouseMotionListener handles 2** (drag, move). This distinction is a common KTU exam pitfall.

#### **2.2.3 KeyEvent** (`java.awt.event.KeyEvent`)

| Property | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Triggered By** | Any keyboard key press, release, or typing |
| **Corresponding Listener** | `KeyListener` |
| **Callback Methods** | `keyPressed(KeyEvent e)`, `keyReleased(KeyEvent e)`, `keyTyped(KeyEvent e)` |
| **Key Methods** | `getKeyChar()`, `getKeyCode()`, `getKeyText(int keyCode)` |
| **Virtual Key Constants** | `VK_A` through `VK_Z`, `VK_0` through `VK_9`, `VK_ENTER`, `VK_ESCAPE`, `VK_F1` through `VK_F12`, `VK_SHIFT`, `VK_CONTROL`, `VK_ALT` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction:** `keyPressed()` and `keyReleased()` work with **virtual key codes** (non-character keys like arrow keys, function keys). `keyTyped()` works only with **character-producing keys** and uses `getKeyChar()`.

#### **2.2.4 WindowEvent** (`java.awt.event.WindowEvent`)

| Property | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Triggered By** | Any state change of a Window (open, close, iconify, activate, etc.) |
| **Corresponding Listener** | `WindowListener` |
| **Total Event Types** | **7 distinct window events** |
| **Callback Methods** | `windowOpened()`, `windowClosing()`, `windowClosed()`, `windowIconified()`, `windowDeiconified()`, `windowActivated()`, `windowDeactivated()` |
| **Key Methods** | `getWindow()`, `getOppositeWindow()` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The 7 window events and the difference between `windowClosing()` (user clicked close) and `windowClosed()` (window actually disposed) is a **guaranteed exam question**.

#### **2.2.5 FocusEvent** (`java.awt.event.FocusEvent`)

| Property | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Triggered By** | A component gains or loses keyboard focus |
| **Corresponding Listener** | `FocusListener` |
| **Callback Methods** | `focusGained(FocusEvent e)`, `focusLost(FocusEvent e)` |
| **Key Methods** | `isTemporary()`, `getOppositeComponent()` |

#### **2.2.6 ItemEvent** (`java.awt.event.ItemEvent`)

| Property | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Triggered By** | State change in Checkbox, CheckboxMenuItem, Choice, or List |
| **Corresponding Listener** | `ItemListener` |
| **Callback Method** | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |
| **Key Methods** | `getItem()`, `getItemSelectable()`, `getStateChange()` |
| **State Constants** | `SELECTED`, `DESELECTED` |

#### **2.2.7 TextEvent** (`java.awt.event.TextEvent`)

| Property | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Triggered By** | Text content changes in TextComponent subclasses (TextField, TextArea) |
| **Corresponding Listener** | `TextListener` |
| **Callback Method** | `textValueChanged(TextEvent e)` |
| **Key Methods** | Inherits only `getSource()` тАФ **no specialized methods** |

#### **2.2.8 AdjustmentEvent** (`java.awt.event.AdjustmentEvent`)

| Property | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Triggered By** | Scrollbar manipulation in `Scrollbar` class |
| **Corresponding Listener** | `AdjustmentListener` |
| **Callback Method** | `adjustmentValueChanged(AdjustmentEvent e)` |
| **Key Methods** | `getAdjustable()`, `getAdjustmentType()`, `getValue()` |
| **Adjustment Type Constants** | `UNIT_INCREMENT`, `UNIT_DECREMENT`, `BLOCK_INCREMENT`, `BLOCK_DECREMENT`, `TRACK` |

#### **2.2.9 ComponentEvent** (`java.awt.event.ComponentEvent`)

| Property | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Triggered By** | Component moved, resized, shown, or hidden |
| **Corresponding Listener** | `ComponentListener` |
| **Key Method** | `getComponent()` |

#### **2.2.10 ContainerEvent** (`java.awt.event.ContainerEvent`)

| Property | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Triggered By** | A component added to or removed from a container |
| **Corresponding Listener** | `ContainerListener` |
| **Key Methods** | `getContainer()`, `getChild()` |
| **Event Types** | `COMPONENT_ADDED`, `COMPONENT_REMOVED` |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Event Class | Listener(s) | Number of Methods | Primary Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ActionEvent** | `ActionListener` | 1 | Button click, menu select |
| 2 | **MouseEvent** | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener` | 5 + 2 | Mouse interaction |
| 3 | **KeyEvent** | `KeyListener` | 3 | Keyboard input |
| 4 | **WindowEvent** | `WindowListener` | 7 | Window state change |
| 5 | **FocusEvent** | `FocusListener` | 2 | Focus shift |
| 6 | **ItemEvent** | `ItemListener` | 1 | Checkbox/List selection |
| 7 | **TextEvent** | `TextListener` | 1 | Text change |
| 8 | **AdjustmentEvent** | `AdjustmentListener` | 1 | Scrollbar change |
| 9 | **ComponentEvent** | `ComponentListener` | 4 | Component visibility/move |
| 10 | **ContainerEvent** | `ContainerListener` | 2 | Component add/remove |
| 11 | **MouseWheelEvent** | `MouseWheelListener` | 1 | Mouse wheel rotation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Methods (Inherited by All Event Classes):**
> - `Object getSource()` тАФ returns the object on which the event initially occurred
> - `int getID()` тАФ returns the event type identifier
> These two methods are defined in `EventObject` and are available in **every** event class тАФ this is a classic KTU 2-mark question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Event-driven programming using event classes is the foundation of:
- **GUI Application Development** (Desktop apps in Java Swing/JavaFX)
- **Android App Development** (Touch events, gesture events)
- **Web Frontend Development** (DOM events in JavaScript, mirroring Java's model)
- **Real-Time Embedded Systems** (Sensor-triggered interrupts)
- **Network Programming** (Connection events, data arrival events)

In the **Internet of Things (IoT)** and **Industrial Automation** domains, the Java event-delegation model directly maps to **publish-subscribe** architectures used in MQTT brokers and message queues.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Complete Event Handling Flow (Step-by-Step)

Since Event Classes are a conceptual/programmatic topic rather than a pure mathematics topic, the "derivation" here is the **logical flow** of how an event class instance is created, dispatched, and consumed. This systematic breakdown is exactly what KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers.

### Step 1: User Interaction Triggers the Event Source

A physical user action occurs (mouse click, key press, window resize, etc.). The GUI component that detects this action is called the **Event Source**.

> **Example:** The user clicks a `Button` named `b1`. Here, `b1` is the event source.

### Step 2: AWT/Swing Creates an Event Class Instance

The AWT/Swing framework instantiates the **appropriate Event Class** object corresponding to the type of user action.

> **Example:** When `b1` is clicked, Java creates a new `ActionEvent` object internally.

### Step 3: Event Object Encapsulates State

The newly created event object is **populated with relevant data**:
- The source component (obtained via `getSource()`)
- The event ID (obtained via `getID()`)
- Event-specific data (e.g., coordinates for MouseEvent, key code for KeyEvent)

### Step 4: Event Dispatch to Registered Listener

The AWT framework checks the **listener registration list** for that source component and invokes the **appropriate callback method** on the listener, passing the event object as an argument.

> **Example:** If an `ActionListener` is registered on `b1`, its `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` method is called.

### Step 5: Listener Method Executes User Logic

The programmer-defined code inside the callback method runs, performing the desired response (e.g., opening a dialog, updating a label, closing the application).

### Step 6: Control Returns to the Event Dispatch Thread

After the listener method completes, the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** continues processing the next pending event from the queue.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Event Class Selection Guide

The following matrix helps determine **which event class to use** for a given user scenario. This type of analytical table is exactly what KTU expects in exam answers.

| User Scenario | Event Class to Handle | Listener to Implement | Key Callback Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| User clicks a **Submit** button | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed()` |
| User hovers mouse over a panel | `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` | `mouseEntered()` |
| User drags an element across screen | `MouseEvent` | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseDragged()` |
| User presses **F1** for help | `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `keyPressed()` |
| User types their name in a form | `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped()` |
| User closes the application window | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowClosing()` |
| User clicks minimize button | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowIconified()` |
| User tabs from one field to another | `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | `focusLost()` |
| User ticks a checkbox | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged()` |
| User selects item from dropdown | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged()` |
| User scrolls through a list | `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | `adjustmentValueChanged()` |
| User types in a search box | `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | `textValueChanged()` |
| User resizes the window | `ComponentEvent` | `ComponentListener` | `componentResized()` |
| User scrolls with mouse wheel | `MouseWheelEvent` | `MouseWheelListener` | `mouseWheelMoved()` |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Java Program Demonstrating Event Classes

This is a **complete, runnable Java program** that uses **four** major event classes. KTU 14-mark questions often require such programs.

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class EventDemo extends Frame 
    implements ActionListener, MouseListener, KeyListener, WindowListener {
    
    TextField tf;
    Label lbl;
    Button btn;
    int clickCount = 0;
    
    public EventDemo() {
        // Component setup
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setTitle("KTU Event Classes Demonstration");
        setSize(400, 300);
        
        lbl = new Label("Click the button or interact:");
        tf  = new TextField(20);
        btn = new Button("Click Me");
        
        add(lbl);
        add(tf);
        add(btn);
        
        // Listener registration тАФ each line links a source to a listener
        btn.addActionListener(this);    // ActionEvent source
        tf.addKeyListener(this);        // KeyEvent source
        this.addMouseListener(this);    // MouseEvent source
        this.addWindowListener(this);   // WindowEvent source
    }
    
    // тФАтФА ActionEvent Handler тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        clickCount++;
        lbl.setText("Button clicked " + clickCount + " times");
    }
    
    // тФАтФА KeyEvent Handlers (3 methods required) тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
    public void keyPressed(KeyEvent e) {
        int code = e.getKeyCode();
        System.out.println("Key Pressed: " + KeyEvent.getKeyText(code));
    }
    public void keyReleased(KeyEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Key Released: " + e.getKeyChar());
    }
    public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) {
        tf.setText("");  // Clear on any typed character
    }
    
    // тФАтФА MouseEvent Handlers (5 methods required) тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
    public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Mouse clicked at: (" + e.getX() + ", " + e.getY() + ")");
    }
    public void mousePressed(MouseEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Mouse pressed тАФ button: " + e.getButton());
    }
    public void mouseReleased(MouseEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Mouse released");
    }
    public void mouseEntered(MouseEvent e) {
        setBackground(Color.CYAN);
    }
    public void mouseExited(MouseEvent e) {
        setBackground(Color.LIGHT_GRAY);
    }
    
    // тФАтФА WindowEvent Handlers (7 methods required) тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
    public void windowOpened(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window opened");
    }
    public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window closing...");
        dispose();
    }
    public void windowClosed(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window disposed");
        System.exit(0);
    }
    public void windowIconified(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window minimized");
    }
    public void windowDeiconified(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window restored");
    }
    public void windowActivated(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window activated");
    }
    public void windowDeactivated(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window deactivated");
    }
    
    // тФАтФА Main Method тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        EventDemo demo = new EventDemo();
        demo.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Walkthrough of the Program

| Line / Block | Explanation |
|---|---|
| `implements ActionListener, MouseListener, KeyListener, WindowListener` | Single class implements **4 different listener interfaces** to handle **4 different event types** |
| `btn.addActionListener(this);` | Registers the Frame object as the listener for `ActionEvent` on the button |
| `tf.addKeyListener(this);` | Registers the Frame object as the listener for `KeyEvent` on the text field |
| `e.getSource()` is implicit in all callbacks | Every event method receives an event object whose `getSource()` returns the originating component |
| `KeyEvent.getKeyText(code)` | **Static method** that converts a virtual key code (e.g., `VK_F1`) to a human-readable string (e.g., "F1") |
| `dispose()` in `windowClosing()` | Releases OS resources but does not terminate the JVM |
| `System.exit(0)` in `windowClosed()` | Actually terminates the program after the window is fully closed |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Implementing `WindowListener` requires you to define **all 7 methods** even if you only use 1-2 of them. The alternative is to use `WindowAdapter` (an adapter class) which provides empty implementations. This distinction is frequently tested.

## 3.4 The Delegation Event Model Formula

The complete event-handling equation can be conceptualized as:

$$
\text{Event Handling} = \text{Event Source} \times \text{Event Object} \times \text{Event Listener}
$$

Where:
- **Event Source** = The GUI component that generates the event
- **Event Object** = An instance of an Event Class (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`)
- **Event Listener** = An object implementing the appropriate listener interface

The three components must satisfy the **compatibility constraint**:

$$
\text{Listener Interface} \longleftrightarrow \text{Event Class}
$$

This means **ActionListener can only handle ActionEvent**, **MouseListener can only handle MouseEvent**, and so on. The pairing is **strict and one-to-one (or one-to-many in the case of MouseEvent)**.
