# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What are Event Classes in Java? List any four event classes from `java.awt.event` package.**

**Model Answer:**

Event Classes in Java are specialized classes that encapsulate information about user interactions with GUI components. They form part of the **Delegation Event Model** and are used to represent various types of events such as button clicks, mouse movements, and keyboard inputs. Every event class in AWT extends `java.awt.AWTEvent`, which in turn extends `java.util.EventObject`.

Four major event classes are:

| # | Event Class | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ActionEvent** | Generated when a button is clicked, a menu item is selected, or Enter is pressed in a text field |
| 2 | **MouseEvent** | Generated when the mouse interacts with a component (click, move, drag, enter, exit) |
| 3 | **KeyEvent** | Generated when a key is pressed, released, or typed on the keyboard |
| 4 | **WindowEvent** | Generated when a window's state changes (opened, closed, iconified, activated) |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `ActionEvent` and `ItemEvent` in Java AWT with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | ActionEvent | ItemEvent |
|---|---|---|
| **Package** | `java.awt.event` | `java.awt.event` |
| **Listener** | `ActionListener` | `ItemListener` |
| **Callback Method** | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |
| **Generated By** | Button click, menu item selection, Enter in TextField, list double-click | Checkbox toggle, Choice selection, List item selection |
| **Key Methods** | `getActionCommand()`, `getModifiers()` | `getItem()`, `getStateChange()`, `getItemSelectable()` |
| **State Constants** | `ACTION_PERFORMED` | `SELECTED`, `DESELECTED` |
| **Example Scenario** | Clicking a "Login" button to submit a form | Ticking a "Remember Me" checkbox |

**Example Code for ActionEvent:**
```java
Button b = new Button("Submit");
b.addActionListener(e -> System.out.println("Submitted"));
```

**Example Code for ItemEvent:**
```java
Checkbox cb = new Checkbox("Enable");
cb.addItemListener(e -> System.out.println("State: " + (e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED ? "ON" : "OFF")));
```

---

## 5.2 PART B тАФ 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Explain the major Event Classes in Java AWT with their hierarchy, methods, and associated Listener interfaces. Write a Java program to demonstrate `ActionEvent` and `WindowEvent` handling.**

#### **Part (a) тАФ 7 Marks: Event Class Hierarchy and Methods**

**Event Class Hierarchy:**

The root of all event classes is `java.util.EventObject`. The AWT event classes extend `java.awt.AWTEvent`. The complete hierarchy is as follows:

- **ActionEvent** тАФ Triggered by button clicks and menu selections. Key methods: `getActionCommand()`, `getModifiers()`. Listener: `ActionListener`.
- **MouseEvent** тАФ Triggered by mouse actions. Key methods: `getX()`, `getY()`, `getClickCount()`, `getButton()`. Listeners: `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener`.
- **KeyEvent** тАФ Triggered by keyboard. Key methods: `getKeyChar()`, `getKeyCode()`. Listener: `KeyListener`.
- **WindowEvent** тАФ Triggered by window state changes. Key methods: `getWindow()`. Listener: `WindowListener`.
- **FocusEvent** тАФ Triggered by focus changes. Key methods: `isTemporary()`, `getOppositeComponent()`. Listener: `FocusListener`.
- **ItemEvent** тАФ Triggered by item selection. Key methods: `getItem()`, `getStateChange()`. Listener: `ItemListener`.
- **TextEvent** тАФ Triggered by text changes. Listener: `TextListener`.
- **AdjustmentEvent** тАФ Triggered by scrollbar. Key methods: `getValue()`, `getAdjustmentType()`. Listener: `AdjustmentListener`.
- **ComponentEvent** тАФ Triggered by component movement/resize. Key method: `getComponent()`. Listener: `ComponentListener`.
- **ContainerEvent** тАФ Triggered by adding/removing components. Key methods: `getContainer()`, `getChild()`. Listener: `ContainerListener`.

**Universal Methods (from EventObject):**
- `Object getSource()` тАФ Returns the object that generated the event
- `int getID()` тАФ Returns the integer event type identifier

#### **Part (b) тАФ 7 Marks: Java Program Demonstrating ActionEvent and WindowEvent**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ActionWindowDemo extends Frame 
    implements ActionListener, WindowListener {
    
    Label statusLabel;
    int actionCount = 0;
    
    public ActionWindowDemo() {
        setTitle("ActionEvent and WindowEvent Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        
        statusLabel = new Label("No action performed yet");
        Button actionBtn = new Button("Perform Action");
        
        add(statusLabel);
        add(actionBtn);
        
        // Register listeners
        actionBtn.addActionListener(this);    // For ActionEvent
        this.addWindowListener(this);        // For WindowEvent
    }
    
    // ActionEvent handler
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        actionCount++;
        statusLabel.setText("Action #" + actionCount + 
                            " | Command: " + e.getActionCommand());
    }
    
    // WindowEvent handlers (all 7 required)
    public void windowOpened(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window Opened");
    }
    public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window Closing");
        dispose();
    }
    public void windowClosed(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window Closed");
        System.exit(0);
    }
    public void windowIconified(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window Minimized");
    }
    public void windowDeiconified(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window Restored");
    }
    public void windowActivated(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window Activated");
    }
    public void windowDeactivated(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window Deactivated");
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ActionWindowDemo app = new ActionWindowDemo();
        app.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Output Description:** When the button is clicked, the label updates showing the action count and the action command. Console messages trace all window state changes.

---

### **QUESTION B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Explain the Delegation Event Model in Java. With a suitable Java program, demonstrate the handling of `MouseEvent` and `KeyEvent` along with their associated listener interfaces.**

#### **Part (a) тАФ 7 Marks: The Delegation Event Model**

**Definition:** The **Delegation Event Model** is Java's modern approach to event handling, introduced in JDK 1.1. In this model, an **Event Source** delegates the responsibility of handling events to a separate **Event Listener** object. The source maintains a list of registered listeners and notifies them when an event occurs.

**Three Key Components:**

1. **Event Source** тАФ A GUI component (e.g., Button, TextField) that generates events. It provides methods like `addXxxListener()` to register listeners.

2. **Event Object** тАФ An instance of an Event Class (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`) that encapsulates event details such as the source and event type.

3. **Event Listener** тАФ An object that implements a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`) and provides callback methods that respond to events.

**Process Flow:**
- The user interacts with a component (the **source**)
- The source creates an **event object** and passes it to all registered listeners
- Each listener's appropriate **callback method** is invoked
- After execution, control returns to the **Event Dispatch Thread**

**Advantages over the older Inheritance-Based Model (JDK 1.0):**
- Promotes **separation of concerns** тАФ UI design is separate from event handling
- Enables **reusability** тАФ the same listener can handle events from multiple sources
- Supports **multiple listeners** per event type
- Aligns with **OOP principles** (delegation, loose coupling)

#### **Part (b) тАФ 7 Marks: Java Program for MouseEvent and KeyEvent Handling**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class MouseKeyDemo extends Frame 
    implements MouseListener, MouseMotionListener, KeyListener {
    
    TextArea displayArea;
    
    public MouseKeyDemo() {
        setTitle("MouseEvent and KeyEvent Demo");
        setSize(500, 400);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());
        
        displayArea = new TextArea(15, 50);
        displayArea.setEditable(false);
        add(displayArea, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        
        // Register all listeners
        addMouseListener(this);           // For mouse clicks, enter, exit
        addMouseMotionListener(this);     // For mouse drag and move
        addKeyListener(this);             // For keyboard events
    }
    
    // тФАтФА MouseListener methods (5 required) тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
    public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
        displayArea.append("Mouse Clicked at (" + e.getX() + ", " + 
                           e.getY() + ") | Clicks: " + e.getClickCount() + "\n");
    }
    public void mousePressed(MouseEvent e) {
        displayArea.append("Mouse Pressed - Button: " + e.getButton() + "\n");
    }
    public void mouseReleased(MouseEvent e) {
        displayArea.append("Mouse Released\n");
    }
    public void mouseEntered(MouseEvent e) {
        displayArea.append("Mouse Entered the Frame\n");
    }
    public void mouseExited(MouseEvent e) {
        displayArea.append("Mouse Exited the Frame\n");
    }
    
    // тФАтФА MouseMotionListener methods (2 required) тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
    public void mouseDragged(MouseEvent e) {
        displayArea.append("Mouse Dragged to (" + e.getX() + ", " + 
                           e.getY() + ")\n");
    }
    public void mouseMoved(MouseEvent e) {
        setTitle("Mouse at: " + e.getX() + ", " + e.getY());
    }
    
    // тФАтФА KeyListener methods (3 required) тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
    public void keyPressed(KeyEvent e) {
        displayArea.append("Key Pressed: " + 
                           KeyEvent.getKeyText(e.getKeyCode()) + "\n");
    }
    public void keyReleased(KeyEvent e) {
        displayArea.append("Key Released: " + e.getKeyChar() + "\n");
    }
    public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) {
        displayArea.append("Key Typed: " + e.getKeyChar() + "\n");
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MouseKeyDemo app = new MouseKeyDemo();
        app.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** This program uses a `TextArea` to display real-time event information. The class implements three listener interfaces simultaneously: `MouseListener` (5 methods), `MouseMotionListener` (2 methods), and `KeyListener` (3 methods) тАФ totaling 10 callback methods. The window title dynamically updates with mouse coordinates during movement, and all keyboard and mouse events are logged in the text area.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to implement ALL methods of a listener interface** тАФ If your class implements `WindowListener`, you MUST define all 7 methods, even if you only use 1. Many students lose 2-3 marks here. **Solution:** Use `WindowAdapter` class instead, which provides empty default implementations.
>
> 2. **Confusing `windowClosing()` with `windowClosed()`** тАФ `windowClosing()` fires when the user clicks the close button (window is still visible). `windowClosed()` fires only AFTER `dispose()` is called. To actually close the application, you must call `dispose()` in `windowClosing()` and `System.exit(0)` in `windowClosed()`.
>
> 3. **Mixing up `keyPressed()` and `keyTyped()`** тАФ `keyPressed()` works with virtual key codes (works for arrow keys, function keys, shift). `keyTyped()` only works for character-producing keys and uses `getKeyChar()`. Using `getKeyCode()` in `keyTyped()` will return `VK_UNDEFINED`.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to register listeners** тАФ Writing `implements ActionListener` in the class declaration does NOT register the listener. You MUST call `component.addActionListener(this)`. This is the **#1 most common mistake** in 14-mark practical questions.
>
> 5. **Confusing `MouseListener` with `MouseMotionListener`** тАФ MouseListener handles stationary events (clicks, entering, exiting). MouseMotionListener handles movement events (dragging, moving). The `mouseMoved()` method is in MouseMotionListener, NOT MouseListener.
>
> 6. **Not extending `Frame` or `Applet`** тАФ For standalone AWT programs, the class MUST extend `Frame` (for window-based) or `Applet` (for browser-based). Forgetting this is a guaranteed zero in the compilation output.
>
> 7. **Missing `import` statements** тАФ `java.awt.*` and `java.awt.event.*` must be explicitly imported. Modern IDEs auto-import, but in KTU written exams, missing imports show lack of understanding.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Event Classes in Java**

- тШР **Root Class:** All event classes ultimately inherit from `java.util.EventObject`
- тШР **AWT Root:** All AWT event classes extend `java.awt.AWTEvent`
- тШР **Package Location:** All 11 standard event classes reside in `java.awt.event`
- тШР **Universal Methods:** `getSource()` and `getID()` are available on EVERY event object
- тШР **11 Major Event Classes to Memorize:** ActionEvent, MouseEvent, KeyEvent, WindowEvent, FocusEvent, ItemEvent, TextEvent, AdjustmentEvent, ComponentEvent, ContainerEvent, MouseWheelEvent
- тШР **MouseEvent Exception:** It is the ONLY event class handled by 3 different listeners (MouseListener, MouseMotionListener, MouseWheelListener)
- тШР **WindowEvent has 7 subtypes:** OPENED, CLOSING, CLOSED, ICONIFIED, DEICONIFIED, ACTIVATED, DEACTIVATED
- тШР **Listener-Event Pairing is Strict:** Each listener interface corresponds to a specific event class тАФ you cannot mix them
- тШР **Adapter Classes:** Java provides adapter classes (e.g., `WindowAdapter`, `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`) to avoid implementing all methods of a listener interface
- тШР **KeyEvent Distinction:** `keyTyped()` works only with character keys; `keyPressed()`/`keyReleased()` work with all keys
- тШР **Delegation Model Principle:** The event SOURCE delegates event handling to a separate LISTENER object тАФ promoting loose coupling
- тШР **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT):** All GUI events in Java are processed by a single thread called EDT
- тШР **ActionEvent is the Most Common:** Triggered by button clicks, menu selections, list double-clicks, and Enter in text fields
- тШР **ComponentEvent is the Parent:** FocusEvent, WindowEvent, ContainerEvent, and PaintEvent all extend ComponentEvent
- тШР **InputEvent is the Parent:** KeyEvent and MouseEvent both extend InputEvent (which provides modifier key methods like `isShiftDown()`)
- тШР **getActionCommand():** Returns a String identifier for the action тАФ defaults to button label, can be customized with `setActionCommand()`
- тШР **Event Registration Methods:** All follow the pattern `component.addXxxListener(listenerObject)` (e.g., `addActionListener`, `addMouseListener`)
- тШР **Multiple Listeners Allowed:** A single event source can have multiple listeners registered for the same event type
- тШР **Single Listener Reusability:** A single listener object can be registered with multiple event sources

---

**End of Module 4 тАФ Event Classes Notes**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) for PEERT416 тАФ Object Oriented Programming Using Java*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Events
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sources of Events

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) and Swing frameworks, an **Event Source** is defined as any graphical user interface (GUI) component that has the capability to **generate (or "fire") an event** as a result of a user interaction (such as a mouse click, key press, or window resize) or a system-triggered state change. The event source is the *originator* or *producer* of the event object that is dispatched to registered listeners.

Formally, as per the **Event Delegation Model** mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4 тАУ Event Handling), the source is the object that:

1. **Maintains a registry** of listeners interested in its events (using the `addXXXListener()` mechanism).
2. **Instantiates the event object** when the user/system interaction occurs.
3. **Invokes the appropriate listener callback method** by passing the event object to all registered listeners.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The Event Delegation Model has three principal actors тАФ (1) **Event Source**, (2) **Event Object**, and (3) **Event Listener**. This module explicitly focuses on identifying the *sources* of each event class.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **fire alarm system in a large office building**:

- The **smoke detector** (the GUI component, e.g., a `JButton`) is the *event source*. It "detects" something happening.
- The **fire alarm bell** (the *event object*) is created and sent out the moment smoke is detected (user clicks the button).
- The **firefighters** (the *event listeners*) are pre-registered with the building manager (the compiler/JVM) and rush to the location when the bell rings, performing their designated action (calling the handler method).

The smoke detector **does not know** which firefighter will respond тАФ it just fires the alarm. This decoupling is the essence of the **Event Delegation Model**. The component (source) delegates the responsibility of handling the event to a separate listener object.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In Java, a component becomes an event source only when a listener is registered to it via its corresponding `addXXXListener()` method. Without listeners, the source is essentially silent тАФ events are generated but discarded.

## 1.3 Categories of Event Sources in Java AWT/Swing

Java categorizes event sources broadly into the following component families:

| # | Source Category | Examples | Typical Events Generated |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Buttons & Choice Controls** | `JButton`, `JToggleButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton` | `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent` |
| 2 | **Text Components** | `JTextField`, `JTextArea`, `JPasswordField` | `ActionEvent`, `TextEvent`, `KeyEvent` |
| 3 | **Selection Components** | `JList`, `JComboBox`, `JMenuItem` | `ItemEvent`, `ActionEvent` |
| 4 | **Container / Window** | `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JPanel`, `JApplet` | `WindowEvent`, `ContainerEvent` |
| 5 | **Mouse-Driven Components** | Any component inheriting from `Component` | `MouseEvent` |
| 6 | **Keyboard-Driven Components** | Any focusable component | `KeyEvent` |
| 7 | **Scrollable Components** | `JScrollBar`, `JScrollPane` | `AdjustmentEvent` |
| 8 | **Focus-Related Components** | Any focus-traversable component | `FocusEvent` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The JDK defines **12 semantic event types** (and correspondingly 12 listener interfaces) within the `java.awt.event` package, plus Swing-specific events in `javax.swing.event`. Each event type has a designated source class.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Event Delegation Model тАФ Operational Logic

The Java Event Delegation Model works on a **publish-subscribe** pattern. Let us break it down into logical steps:

1. **Component Instantiation:** A GUI component (e.g., `JButton b = new JButton("Click");`) is created. At this point, it is a potential event source but has no listeners.
2. **Listener Registration:** An object implementing the appropriate `XXXListener` interface is registered using the source's `addXXXListener()` method.  
   Example: `b.addActionListener(this);` тАФ now `b` is an active **event source** for `ActionEvent`.
3. **Event Triggering:** When the user interacts (clicks, types, etc.), the AWT/Swing event-dispatch thread invokes a method on the component (e.g., `processActionEvent()`). This method creates a new `ActionEvent` object encapsulating details such as source, ID, command, modifiers, and timestamp.
4. **Event Dispatch:** The event object is passed to **every registered listener** by invoking their respective callback method (e.g., `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`).
5. **Listener Processing:** The listener's callback executes, performing the user-defined action.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" Question (KTU Frequently Asked):** *Why does Java not use inheritance-based event handling (as in older JDK 1.0 model)?*  
> **Answer:** The delegation model provides better **separation of concerns**, **reusability**, and **scalability**. UI logic and business logic are decoupled тАФ the same event can trigger multiple listeners (e.g., logging + business action + UI update).

## 2.2 Detailed Mapping: Event Class тЖТ Source Component тЖТ Listener Interface

This is the **highest-yield table** for KTU Module 4 examinations. Memorize this matrix thoroughly.

| Event Class | Package | Source Components | Listener Interface | Handler Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ActionEvent` | `java.awt.event` | `JButton`, `JList`, `JTextField`, `JMenuItem` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` |
| `ItemEvent` | `java.awt.event` | `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox`, `JCheckBoxMenuItem` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` |
| `MouseEvent` | `java.awt.event` | All `Component` subclasses | `MouseListener` + `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited`, `mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved` |
| `KeyEvent` | `java.awt.event` | All focusable `Component` subclasses | `KeyListener` | `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped` |
| `FocusEvent` | `java.awt.event` | All focusable `Component` subclasses | `FocusListener` | `focusGained`, `focusLost` |
| `TextEvent` | `java.awt.event` | `TextComponent` subclasses | `TextListener` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent)` |
| `AdjustmentEvent` | `java.awt.event` | `Scrollbar`, `JScrollBar` | `AdjustmentListener` | `adjustmentValueChanged` |
| `WindowEvent` | `java.awt.event` | `Window`, `JWindow`, `JFrame`, `JDialog` | `WindowListener` | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated` |
| `ContainerEvent` | `java.awt.event` | `Container`, `JPanel`, `JFrame` | `ContainerListener` | `componentAdded`, `componentRemoved` |
| `ComponentEvent` | `java.awt.event` | All `Component` subclasses | `ComponentListener` | `componentMoved`, `componentResized`, `componentShown`, `componentHidden` |
| `PaintEvent` | `java.awt.event` | (System-internal, rarely handled) | N/A | (Special purpose) |
| `InputEvent` | `java.awt.event` | (Abstract тАФ superclass of `MouseEvent` & `KeyEvent`) | N/A | (Superclass) |

### 2.2.1 Swing-Specific Event Sources (from `javax.swing.event`)

| Event Class | Source Components | Listener Interface | Handler Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ChangeEvent` | `JSlider`, `JSpinner`, `JTabbedPane`, `JProgressBar` | `ChangeListener` | `stateChanged(ChangeEvent)` |
| `ListSelectionEvent` | `JList`, `JTable` | `ListSelectionListener` | `valueChanged(ListSelectionEvent)` |
| `MenuEvent` | `JMenu` | `MenuListener` | `menuSelected`, `menuDeselected`, `menuCanceled` |
| `PopupMenuEvent` | `JPopupMenu` | `PopupMenuListener` | `popupMenuWillBecomeVisible`, etc. |
| `CaretEvent` | `JTextComponent` | `CaretListener` | `caretUpdate(CaretEvent)` |
| `HyperlinkEvent` | `JEditorPane` | `HyperlinkListener` | `hyperlinkUpdate(HyperlinkEvent)` |
| `UndoableEditEvent` | `UndoManager` | `UndoableEditListener` | `undoableEditHappened` |
| `DocumentEvent` | `Document` (Swing text) | `DocumentListener` | `insertUpdate`, `removeUpdate`, `changedUpdate` |
| `TableModelEvent` | `TableModel` | `TableModelListener` | `tableChanged` |
| `TreeExpansionEvent` | `JTree` | `TreeExpansionListener` | `treeExpanded`, `treeCollapsed` |
| `TreeSelectionEvent` | `JTree` | `TreeSelectionListener` | `valueChanged` |
| `AncestorEvent` | (Internal) | `AncestorListener` | `ancestorAdded`, etc. |
| `InternalFrameEvent` | `JInternalFrame` | `InternalFrameListener` | `internalFrameOpened`, etc. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Reference Rule:** *If a component has an `addXXXListener()` method, it is an event source for `XXXEvent`.* The naming convention is strictly **1:1 тАФ Listener тЖФ Event**.

## 2.3 The Event Class Hierarchy (Rooted at `java.util.EventObject`)

```
java.util.EventObject                              тЖР Root (Serializable)
   тФФтФАтФА java.awt.AWTEvent                           тЖР All AWT events
          тФЬтФАтФА ActionEvent
          тФЬтФАтФА AdjustmentEvent
          тФЬтФАтФА ComponentEvent
          тФВ      тФЬтФАтФА FocusEvent
          тФВ      тФЬтФАтФА PaintEvent
          тФВ      тФЬтФАтФА WindowEvent
          тФВ      тФФтФАтФА (others)
          тФЬтФАтФА ContainerEvent
          тФЬтФАтФА InputEvent                            тЖР Abstract
          тФВ      тФЬтФАтФА KeyEvent
          тФВ      тФФтФАтФА MouseEvent
          тФВ             тФФтФАтФА MouseWheelEvent
          тФЬтФАтФА ItemEvent
          тФЬтФАтФА TextEvent
          тФФтФАтФА HierarchyEvent
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Every event object in Java ultimately inherits from `java.util.EventObject`, guaranteeing that all events are **Serializable** and expose `getSource()` and `getID()` methods. This unified hierarchy simplifies generic event-handling frameworks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of event sources is foundational to:
- **GUI desktop applications** (Swing/JavaFX, Eclipse RCP plugins).
- **Internet of Things (IoT) dashboards** where sensor widgets are event sources.
- **SCADA systems** in industrial automation.
- **Web backends** (e.g., Spring's `ApplicationEventPublisher` mirrors the same source-listener pattern).
- **Real-time monitoring tools** in network management and observability.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Syntax Cheat Sheet (for source registration)

| Syntax Pattern | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `ComponentName obj = new ComponentName();` | Instantiate the source | `JButton b = new JButton("OK");` |
| `obj.addXXXListener(listenerObj);` | Register a listener (makes source active) | `b.addActionListener(this);` |
| `obj.removeXXXListener(listenerObj);` | Unregister a listener | `b.removeActionListener(this);` |
| `obj.getListeners(XXXListener.class);` | Retrieve all registered listeners (Java 6+) | `b.getListeners(ActionListener.class);` |
| `obj.setEnabled(false);` | Disable the source (no events fire) | `b.setEnabled(false);` |
| `obj.processXXXEvent(e);` | Manually trigger an event (advanced) | `b.processActionEvent(new ActionEvent(...));` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Source of Common Errors:** Forgetting to register a listener (`addActionListener`) is the **#1 reason** a Java GUI application "does not respond" to user actions. Always verify listener registration when debugging event-driven code.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix тАФ Event Source Mechanics

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is conceptually driven (not a mathematical derivation), the "derivation" here is presented as a **structured logical walkthrough** of how a source generates and dispatches events, broken into KTU-style analytical steps. Every Java syntax element is treated as an "equation transition" with full explanation.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Walkthrough: How a `JButton` Becomes an Active `ActionEvent` Source

### Step 1: Component Declaration (the latent source)
```java
JButton submitBtn = new JButton("Submit");
```
**Explanation:** A `JButton` object is instantiated. At this stage, it is a *potential* event source тАФ it has the *capability* to fire `ActionEvent`, but no listener is registered, so any click would be silently ignored. The component's internal listener list is empty.

### Step 2: Listener Interface Implementation
```java
public class MyForm extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    // ...
}
```
**Explanation:** A class declares that it implements the `ActionListener` interface. This obligates the class to override the `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` method. The class is now a *qualified listener*.

### Step 3: Listener Registration (the source becomes "live")
```java
submitBtn.addActionListener(this);
```
**Explanation:** The `this` object (the `MyForm` instance) is added to `submitBtn`'s internal listener registry. Internally, AWT stores this in a `EventListener listener = ActionListener.class.cast(this);` style collection. **Now the source is active.**

### Step 4: User Interaction Triggers the Event
```java
// User clicks the "Submit" button at runtime
```
**Explanation:** The AWT event-dispatch thread (EDT) detects the mouse click. The button's internal `processActionEvent(ActionEvent e)` method is invoked. This method:
- Constructs an `ActionEvent` object:  
  `new ActionEvent(source, id, command, modifiers, when)`
- Iterates through all registered `ActionListener` instances.
- Invokes `actionPerformed(event)` on each.

### Step 5: Listener Callback Executes
```java
@Override
public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
    if (e.getSource() == submitBtn) {
        System.out.println("Submit clicked at " + e.getWhen());
    }
}
```
**Explanation:** The listener's overridden method executes. Note that `e.getSource()` returns the original `submitBtn` object тАФ confirming the **EventObject contract**.

### Step 6: Event Disposal
The `ActionEvent` object becomes eligible for **garbage collection** once `actionPerformed` returns. No further references are held by AWT.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Source тЖТ Event тЖТ Listener Triplets (the KTU 14-Mark Backbone)

| Source Object | Method Called Internally by AWT/Swing | Event Class Created | Listener Interface Required | Handler Method Signature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `JButton btn` | `processActionEvent()` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| `JCheckBox chk` | `processItemEvent()` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |
| `JTextField tf` | `processActionEvent()` & `processKeyEvent()` | `ActionEvent`/`KeyEvent` | `ActionListener`/`KeyListener` | `actionPerformed` / `keyPressed` etc. |
| `JList list` | `processActionEvent()` & value-change events | `ActionEvent` / `ListSelectionEvent` | `ActionListener` / `ListSelectionListener` | `actionPerformed` / `valueChanged` |
| `JFrame frm` | `processWindowEvent()` | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowClosing(WindowEvent e)` etc. |
| `JSlider sld` | `processMouseEvent()` & Swing change event | `ChangeEvent` (Swing) | `ChangeListener` | `void stateChanged(ChangeEvent e)` |
| `JScrollBar sb` | `processAdjustmentEvent()` | `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | `void adjustmentValueChanged(AdjustmentEvent e)` |
| `Container c` | `processContainerEvent()` | `ContainerEvent` | `ContainerListener` | `componentAdded` / `componentRemoved` |
| `JTree tr` | (Swing change) | `TreeSelectionEvent` | `TreeSelectionListener` | `void valueChanged(TreeSelectionEvent e)` |
| `JTable tbl` | (Swing model) | `TableModelEvent` | `TableModelListener` | `void tableChanged(TableModelEvent e)` |

## 3.3 Adaptation Classes тАФ A Special Case in Event Source Analysis

For listeners with **multiple methods** (e.g., `WindowListener` has 7, `MouseListener` has 5), Java provides **Adapter classes** (e.g., `WindowAdapter`, `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`). These are **abstract classes with empty implementations** of all listener methods, so a programmer can override only the methods of interest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Adapters Exist:** To avoid forcing the programmer to implement *every* method of a multi-method listener interface (a tedious and error-prone task). The adapter is an *implementation convenience*, not a change to the event source semantics.

| Listener Interface (many methods) | Adapter Class (empty defaults) |
|---|---|
| `WindowListener` (7 methods) | `WindowAdapter` |
| `MouseListener` (5 methods) | `MouseAdapter` |
| `MouseMotionListener` (2 methods) | `MouseMotionAdapter` |
| `KeyListener` (3 methods) | `KeyAdapter` |
| `FocusListener` (2 methods) | `FocusAdapter` |
| `ComponentListener` (4 methods) | `ComponentAdapter` |
| `ContainerListener` (2 methods) | `ContainerAdapter` |
| `InternalFrameListener` (6 methods) | `InternalFrameAdapter` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** If a listener has only **one method** (e.g., `ActionListener.actionPerformed`, `ChangeListener.stateChanged`), Java does **not** provide an adapter тАФ there is no need, as the programmer must implement only that one method.

## 3.4 Multiple Sources, Single Listener тАФ Real-World Pattern

A common KTU question pattern: *Can one listener handle multiple sources?*  
**Answer:** Yes. The same listener instance can be registered to multiple event sources. The listener differentiates sources using `e.getSource()` or `e.getActionCommand()`.

```java
JButton b1 = new JButton("Save");
JButton b2 = new JButton("Cancel");
b1.addActionListener(this);
b2.addActionListener(this);
// In actionPerformed:
if (e.getSource() == b1) { /* save logic */ }
else if (e.getSource() == b2) { /* cancel logic */ }
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Tip:** Always show `e.getSource() instanceof Component` checks and `e.getActionCommand()` comparisons in your model answers to demonstrate mastery of source differentiation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Event Delegation Model тАФ High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Action Click or Key Press] --> B[Event Source e.g. JButton]
    B --> C{Listeners Registered?}
    C -- No --> Z[Event Discarded Silently]
    C -- Yes --> D[Create Event Object e.g. ActionEvent]
    D --> E[Invoke Callback on Each Listener]
    E --> F[Listener1 Handler]
    E --> G[Listener2 Handler]
    E --> H[Listener3 Handler]
    F --> I[Application Logic Response]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.2 Event Class Hierarchy тАФ Source-Centric View

```mermaid
graph TD
    EVTROOT[EventObject from java.util] --> AWTEVT[AWTEvent from java.awt]
    AWTEVT --> ACTEVT[ActionEvent]
    AWTEVT --> ITMEVT[ItemEvent]
    AWTEVT --> INPEVT[InputEvent]
    INPEVT --> KEYEVT[KeyEvent]
    INPEVT --> MOUEVT[MouseEvent]
    MOUEVT --> WHELEVT[MouseWheelEvent]
    AWTEVT --> COMPEVT[ComponentEvent]
    COMPEVT --> FOCEVT[FocusEvent]
    COMPEVT --> WINMEVT[WindowEvent]
    COMPEVT --> PNTMEVT[PaintEvent]
    AWTEVT --> CONTNRT[ContainerEvent]
    AWTEVT --> ADJEVT[AdjustmentEvent]
    AWTEVT --> TXTEVT[TextEvent]
```

## 4.3 SourceтАУListenerтАУAdapter Triangular Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[GUI Component Source] -->|addWindowListener| LST[WindowListener Interface]
    LST -->|implemented by| CLS[Your Handler Class]
    LST -.->|empty defaults provided by| ADP[WindowAdapter Class]
    ADP -->|extended by| CLS
    CLS -->|overrides only needed method| MTHD[windowClosing Method]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Event Flow in a Java Application

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UI_LAYER["Presentation Layer UI"]
        A1[JButton]
        A2[JTextField]
        A3[JCheckBox]

    end

    subgraph EVENT_DISPATCH["Event Dispatch Subsystem AWT Event Queue"]
        B1[EDT Event Dispatch Thread]
        B2[Event Object Constructor]

    end

    subgraph LOGIC_LAYER["Business Logic Layer Listeners"]
        C1[ActionListener Impl]
        C2[ItemListener Impl]
        C3[WindowListener Impl]
        C4[KeyListener Impl]
    end

    A1 -->|click triggers| B1
    A2 -->|enter key triggers| B1
    A3 -->|state change triggers| B1
    A4 -->|window activity triggers| B1
    A5 -->|selection triggers| B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 -->|dispatches to| C1
    B3 -->|dispatches to| C2
    B3 -->|dispatches to| C3
    B3 -->|dispatches to| C4
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an event source in Java. Give two examples. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **event source** is a GUI component (from AWT or Swing) that generates or "fires" an event in response to user interaction (such as mouse click, key press) or system-triggered state change, and dispatches the event object to all registered listeners.

**Two Examples:**
1. `JButton` тАФ source of `ActionEvent` (fires when clicked).
2. `JCheckBox` тАФ source of `ItemEvent` (fires when checked/unchecked).

(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for each example = **3 marks**)

### Question 2: List any three methods of the `MouseListener` interface. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `MouseListener` interface declares the following five methods:
1. `void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e)`
2. `void mousePressed(MouseEvent e)`
3. `void mouseReleased(MouseEvent e)`
4. `void mouseEntered(MouseEvent e)`
5. `void mouseExited(MouseEvent e)`

(Any three for 3 marks: **1 mark each**)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Sources of Events тАФ Comprehensive Analysis `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the Event Delegation Model in Java. Identify its three principal actors and describe the role of each with a suitable diagram.

**Model Solution:**

The **Event Delegation Model** is Java's modern (post-JDK 1.1) mechanism for handling GUI events. It decouples event generation from event processing, allowing flexible and reusable code.

**Three Principal Actors:**

1. **Event Source:** The GUI component (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`) that generates events. It maintains a list of registered listeners and provides `addXXXListener()` and `removeXXXListener()` methods.
2. **Event Object/Listener:** A listener is an object implementing a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`). It "waits" for events from one or more sources.
3. **Event Object:** The state-bearing object (e.g., `ActionEvent`) created by the source and passed to the listener's callback method.

**Process Flow:**

```
User Interaction тЖТ Source detects тЖТ Source creates Event Object
              тЖТ Source calls listener.actionPerformed(Event)
              тЖТ Listener's business logic executes
```

**Diagram (textual):**

```
[JButton: Source] ---fires---> [ActionEvent Object] --delivered to--> [ActionListener.actionPerformed()]
```

**Key Advantages:**
- Separation of UI and business logic.
- One source can notify multiple listeners.
- One listener can handle multiple sources.
- No inheritance dependency between source and listener.

(7 marks: 1 mark per actor ├Ч 3 = 3, 2 marks for process flow, 2 marks for advantages)

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Write a Java program that creates a `JFrame` with a `JButton` labeled "Greet". When the button is clicked, display "Hello, KTU Student!" in a `JLabel`. Identify the event source, event class, and listener interface used.

**Model Solution:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class GreetApp extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JLabel label;
    JButton greetBtn;

    public GreetApp() {
        setTitle("Greet Application");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        greetBtn = new JButton("Greet");
        label = new JLabel("Click the button to greet.");

        greetBtn.addActionListener(this);   // Source registration

        add(greetBtn);
        add(label);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        label.setText("Hello, KTU Student!");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new GreetApp();
    }
}
```

**Identification:**
- **Event Source:** `JButton greetBtn`
- **Event Class:** `ActionEvent` (from `java.awt.event`)
- **Listener Interface:** `ActionListener` (with handler `actionPerformed`)

(7 marks: 2 marks for correct class structure, 2 marks for proper listener registration, 2 marks for handler logic, 1 mark for correct identification)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Differentiate between Event Source, Event Listener, and Event Object with examples. State the role of Adapter classes in event handling.

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Event Source | Event Listener | Event Object |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Component that generates events | Interface-implementing object that handles events | Object encapsulating event details |
| **Examples** | `JButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JTextField` | Class implementing `ActionListener`, `MouseListener` | `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent` |
| **Role** | Maintains listener list, fires events | Receives event and performs response | Carries source, ID, timestamp, etc. |
| **Method** | `addXXXListener()`, `removeXXXListener()` | `actionPerformed()`, `mouseClicked()` etc. | `getSource()`, `getID()` |

**Role of Adapter Classes:**

Adapter classes (e.g., `WindowAdapter`, `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`) are **abstract classes** that provide empty default implementations of all methods of a multi-method listener interface. They are used to:

1. **Avoid boilerplate code:** The programmer can override only the methods of interest, leaving the rest as no-ops.
2. **Simplify event handling:** Especially useful for interfaces with many methods (e.g., `WindowListener` has 7 methods).

**Example:**
```java
frame.addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
    @Override
    public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
        System.exit(0);
    }
    // Other 6 methods are inherited as empty implementations
});
```

(7 marks: 4 marks for differentiation table, 3 marks for adapter explanation with example)

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Write a Java program demonstrating `ItemListener` with two `JRadioButton`s ("Male" and "Female") and a `JLabel` that displays the selected gender.

**Model Solution:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class GenderApp extends JFrame implements ItemListener {
    JLabel resultLabel;
    JRadioButton maleBtn, femaleBtn;

    public GenderApp() {
        setTitle("Gender Selector");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        maleBtn = new JRadioButton("Male");
        femaleBtn = new JRadioButton("Female");
        resultLabel = new JLabel("Select your gender.");

        // Group the radio buttons for mutual exclusion
        ButtonGroup bg = new ButtonGroup();
        bg.add(maleBtn);
        bg.add(femaleBtn);

        // Register the ItemListener on both sources
        maleBtn.addItemListener(this);
        femaleBtn.addItemListener(this);

        add(maleBtn);
        add(femaleBtn);
        add(resultLabel);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) {
        if (e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED) {
            if (e.getSource() == maleBtn) {
                resultLabel.setText("Gender: Male");
            } else if (e.getSource() == femaleBtn) {
                resultLabel.setText("Gender: Female");
            }
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new GenderApp();
    }
}
```

**Identification:**
- **Event Source:** `JRadioButton maleBtn` and `JRadioButton femaleBtn`
- **Event Class:** `ItemEvent`
- **Listener Interface:** `ItemListener` (handler: `itemStateChanged`)

(7 marks: 2 marks for frame setup, 2 marks for ButtonGroup usage, 2 marks for `itemStateChanged` logic, 1 mark for output handling)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `addXXXListener()`:** Without listener registration, the component is NOT an active event source тАФ this is the most common reason for "non-responsive" GUIs. (Lose 1-2 marks.)
> 2. **Wrong listener interface pairing:** A `JButton` uses `ActionListener`, NOT `MouseListener` for click handling. Use the right interface! (Lose up to 3 marks.)
> 3. **Confusing Adapter with Listener:** `WindowAdapter` is NOT a listener itself тАФ it must be subclassed (anonymous or named) and registered.
> 4. **Not using `e.getSource()`:** When one listener handles multiple sources, failing to differentiate using `e.getSource()` or `e.getActionCommand()` results in monolithic, untestable code.
> 5. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation`:** The window will not close on the close button without this тАФ students often debug for hours on this.
> 6. **Importing wrong package:** `ActionEvent` is in `java.awt.event`, not `javax.swing`. Wrong import = compile-time error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- тЬЕ An **Event Source** is a GUI component that generates events; it must have at least one listener registered via `addXXXListener()` to be "active."
- тЬЕ The **Event Delegation Model** has three actors: **Source, Event, Listener** тАФ decoupled for reusability.
- тЬЕ Every event class inherits from `java.util.EventObject` (via `java.awt.AWTEvent`), guaranteeing `getSource()` and `getID()`.
- тЬЕ **Naming convention is strict:** `XXXEvent` тЖФ `XXXListener` тЖФ `addXXXListener()` / `removeXXXListener()`.
- тЬЕ **12 AWT event types + 13 Swing event types** (approximate count) form the comprehensive event taxonomy.
- тЬЕ **Adapter classes** exist only for multi-method listener interfaces (e.g., `WindowAdapter`, `MouseAdapter`); single-method listeners (`ActionListener`, `ChangeListener`) have no adapter.
- тЬЕ One **listener can be registered to multiple sources**; one **source can have multiple listeners** тАФ both are valid.
- тЬЕ Use `e.getSource()` to identify the firing source, and `e.getActionCommand()` for button text identification.
- тЬЕ The **AWT Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** is the single thread responsible for event processing тАФ long-running tasks should be offloaded to worker threads.
- тЬЕ **Swing-specific events** (e.g., `ChangeEvent`, `ListSelectionEvent`) live in `javax.swing.event`, not `java.awt.event`.
- тЬЕ **Common pitfall:** Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` results in a non-closable window.
- тЬЕ **Common pitfall:** Not grouping `JRadioButton`s in a `ButtonGroup` allows multiple simultaneous selections.
- тЬЕ For 14-mark answers, always include: (1) import statements, (2) class declaration with listener interfaces, (3) constructor with GUI setup, (4) overridden handler method, (5) `main` method, and (6) identification of source/event/listener triple.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Listener Interfaces, Swings Fundamentals - Swing Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Listener Interfaces, Swings Fundamentals - Swing Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Event-Driven Programming in Java

**Event-Driven Programming** is a programming paradigm in which the flow of the program is determined by events such as user actions (mouse clicks, key presses), sensor outputs, or messages from other programs. In Java, especially within **AWT** and **Swing**, this model is formalized through the **Delegation Event Model**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Delegation Event Model (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> A source (component) generates an event and forwards (delegates) it to a registered listener. The listener contains the handler method that processes the event. The source and listener remain loosely coupled.

## 1.2 Event Listener Interfaces тАФ Formal Definition

An **Event Listener Interface** in Java is an interface from the `java.awt.event` and `javax.swing.event` packages that defines one or more abstract methods (event handlers) which must be implemented by a class that wishes to receive and process specific types of events.

Common listener interfaces include:
- `ActionListener` тЖТ handles `ActionEvent` (button click, menu selection)
- `MouseListener` тЖТ handles `MouseEvent` (clicks, enters, exits)
- `KeyListener` тЖТ handles `KeyEvent` (key press, release, type)
- `WindowListener` тЖТ handles `WindowEvent` (open, close, iconify)
- `ItemListener` тЖТ handles `ItemEvent` (checkbox, radio button toggles)
- `TextListener` тЖТ handles `TextEvent` (text value changes)
- `FocusListener` тЖТ handles `FocusEvent` (gain/lose focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** A listener interface has its corresponding **event class** (e.g., `ActionListener` тЖФ `ActionEvent`). The interface methods take the event object as their parameter.

## 1.3 Swing Fundamentals тАФ Formal Definition

**Swing** is a GUI (Graphical User Interface) toolkit in Java that is part of the **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)**. It is built on top of the AWT API and provides a richer set of components that are **lightweight** (rendered entirely by Java, not relying on the underlying OS).

**Key characteristics of Swing:**
- **Platform Independent:** Same appearance across operating systems (uses Pluggable Look and Feel).
- **Lightweight Components:** Written entirely in Java (prefixed with `J`, e.g., `JButton`, `JFrame`).
- **MVC Architecture:** Model-View-Controller design separates data, presentation, and control logic.
- **Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF):** Customizable appearance via `UIManager.setLookAndFeel()`.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Bell System**
>
> Imagine a **restaurant**:
> - The **customer** is the *Event Source* (e.g., a `JButton`).
> - The **waiter assigned to that table** is the *Event Listener* (e.g., `ActionListener`).
> - The **order slip** is the *Event Object* (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
> - The **act of placing the order** is the *Event* itself.
>
> When the customer calls (clicks a button), the waiter (listener) takes the order (event) to the kitchen (handler method). The customer never needs to know *how* the kitchen works тАФ they just delegate. This is the **Delegation Event Model**!

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Package for AWT events:** `java.awt.event` (**bold** mandatory import)
- **Package for Swing events:** `javax.swing.event`
- **Root container class:** `java.awt.Container` (extended by `JFrame`, `JPanel`, `JDialog`)
- **Lightweight prefix:** All Swing components begin with the letter `J`
- **Default layout for `JFrame` content pane:** `BorderLayout`
- **Default layout for `JPanel`:** `FlowLayout`

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Delegation Event Model тАФ How It Works

The event-handling mechanism in Java follows four structured steps:

1. **Event Source Creation:** A GUI component (button, text field) is instantiated. It *knows* it can generate events but has no built-in reaction logic.
2. **Listener Registration:** The event source provides a method to register a listener, typically:
   - `addActionListener(ActionListener l)` for `ActionEvent`
   - `addMouseListener(MouseListener l)` for `MouseEvent`
   - `addKeyListener(KeyListener l)` for `KeyEvent`
3. **Event Generation:** When the user interacts (clicks, types), the component creates an event object encapsulating details (source, ID, timestamp).
4. **Event Delegation:** The source invokes the appropriate method on every registered listener (e.g., `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Delegation"?** Because the source *delegates* the task of handling the event to a separate listener object. This decouples the GUI code from the event-handling logic, supporting cleaner OOP design.

## 2.2 Adapter Classes тАФ Convenience Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> Many listener interfaces (e.g., `MouseListener`, `WindowListener`) have **multiple methods**, even if you only need one. Implementing all of them with empty bodies is tedious.

Java provides **Adapter Classes** that implement listener interfaces with empty (no-op) methods. You can extend an adapter and override only the methods you need.

| Adapter Class | Implements | Key Methods to Override |
|---|---|---|
| `MouseAdapter` | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseClicked()`, `mousePressed()` |
| `KeyAdapter` | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped()`, `keyPressed()` |
| `WindowAdapter` | `WindowListener` | `windowClosing()`, `windowOpened()` |

## 2.3 Swing Component Hierarchy

```
java.lang.Object
   тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Component
          тФЬтФАтФА java.awt.Container
          тФВ      тФЬтФАтФА javax.swing.JComponent (abstract)
          тФВ      тФВ      тФЬтФАтФА JButton, JLabel, JTextField
          тФВ      тФВ      тФЬтФАтФА JCheckBox, JRadioButton
          тФВ      тФВ      тФЬтФАтФА JComboBox, JList, JTable
          тФВ      тФВ      тФЬтФАтФА JPanel, JScrollPane, JTabbedPane
          тФВ      тФВ      тФФтФАтФА JMenuBar, JMenu, JMenuItem
          тФВ      тФЬтФАтФА JFrame, JDialog, JApplet
```

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet тАФ Swing Controls

| Component | Class Name | Purpose | Key Event(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top-level Window | `JFrame` | Main application window | `WindowEvent` |
| Pressable Button | `JButton` | Triggers an action | `ActionEvent` |
| Text Display | `JLabel` | Shows read-only text/image | None (passive) |
| Single-line Input | `JTextField` | User text entry | `ActionEvent`, `KeyEvent` |
| Multi-line Input | `JTextArea` | Multi-line text editor | `TextEvent` |
| Toggle Box | `JCheckBox` | Independent on/off choice | `ItemEvent` |
| Mutually Exclusive | `JRadioButton` | Group-based choice (with `ButtonGroup`) | `ItemEvent` |
| Drop-down List | `JComboBox` | Select from a list | `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent` |
| Scrollable List | `JList` | Display a list of items | `ListSelectionEvent` |
| Tabular Data | `JTable` | Display data in rows/columns | `TableModelEvent` |
| Layered Pane | `JLayeredPane` | Overlapping components | N/A |
| Panel | `JPanel` | Container for grouping | N/A |
| Scroll Container | `JScrollPane` | Adds scrollbars to components | N/A |
| Menu | `JMenuBar`, `JMenu`, `JMenuItem` | Menu bar system | `ActionEvent`, `MenuEvent` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** `JButton` + `ActionListener`, `JCheckBox`/`JRadioButton` + `ItemListener`, `JTextField` + `ActionListener` (triggered only when Enter is pressed).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Swing-based GUIs are used in:
- **Enterprise Tools:** IDEs (Eclipse, IntelliJ originally used Swing) for plugin dialogs.
- **Banking Software:** Internal administrative dashboards.
- **Scientific Applications:** Data visualization (with JFreeChart overlays).
- **Educational Tools:** KTU lab mini-projects frequently use Swing for calculator, student record, and login form applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Event Listener Registration

**Problem:** Create a `JFrame` containing a `JButton`. When the button is clicked, display the message *"Hello KTU!"* in a `JLabel`.

### Step 1: Import the required packages
```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;
```

### Step 2: Create the main class and extend `JFrame` (composition is also acceptable)
```java
public class HelloKtuApp extends JFrame {
```

### Step 3: Declare GUI components as instance variables
```java
    private JButton clickButton;
    private JLabel messageLabel;
```

### Step 4: Constructor тАФ set up the frame
```java
    public HelloKtuApp() {
        setTitle("KTU Event Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
```

### Step 5: Instantiate components
```java
        clickButton = new JButton("Click Me");
        messageLabel = new JLabel("Press the button above.");
```

### Step 6: Register the event listener
The source (`clickButton`) delegates events to the listener (`this`).
```java
        clickButton.addActionListener(this);
```

### Step 7: Add components and make visible
```java
        add(clickButton);
        add(messageLabel);
        setVisible(true);
    }
```

### Step 8: Implement the listener interface and override handler
```java
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == clickButton) {
            messageLabel.setText("Hello KTU!");
        }
    }
```

### Step 9: `main` method
```java
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new HelloKtuApp();
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Learning:** The method `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` is the contract defined by the `ActionListener` interface. The source button calls this method when activated, passing the event object `e`.

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: ItemListener with Checkbox and Radio Buttons

**Problem:** Build a small form using `JCheckBox` (subscribe newsletter) and `JRadioButton` (gender selection). Display the current selections dynamically.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class FormDemo extends JFrame implements ItemListener {
    private JCheckBox subscribeBox;
    private JRadioButton maleBtn, femaleBtn;
    private JLabel statusLabel;

    public FormDemo() {
        setTitle("Form with Item Events");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        subscribeBox = new JCheckBox("Subscribe to Newsletter");
        maleBtn   = new JRadioButton("Male");
        femaleBtn = new JRadioButton("Female");
        statusLabel = new JLabel("Status: Waiting for input...");

        // Group the radio buttons
        ButtonGroup genderGroup = new ButtonGroup();
        genderGroup.add(maleBtn);
        genderGroup.add(femaleBtn);

        // Register ItemListener
        subscribeBox.addItemListener(this);
        maleBtn.addItemListener(this);
        femaleBtn.addItemListener(this);

        add(subscribeBox);
        add(maleBtn);
        add(femaleBtn);
        add(statusLabel);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) {
        String msg = "Status: ";
        if (e.getSource() == subscribeBox) {
            msg += subscribeBox.isSelected() ? "Subscribed" : "Unsubscribed";
        } else if (e.getSource() == maleBtn && maleBtn.isSelected()) {
            msg += "Gender: Male";
        } else if (e.getSource() == femaleBtn && femaleBtn.isSelected()) {
            msg += "Gender: Female";
        }
        statusLabel.setText(msg);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new FormDemo();
    }
}
```

### Event-Flow Matrix

| User Action | Source Component | Event Class | Listener Method Triggered |
|---|---|---|---|
| Click button | `JButton` | `ActionEvent` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| Check/uncheck box | `JCheckBox` | `ItemEvent` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |
| Select radio | `JRadioButton` | `ItemEvent` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |
| Press Enter in text field | `JTextField` | `ActionEvent` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| Click on list item | `JList` | `ListSelectionEvent` | `valueChanged(ListSelectionEvent e)` |
| Click on table cell | `JTable` | `TableModelEvent` | `tableChanged(TableModelEvent e)` |
| Close window | `JFrame` | `WindowEvent` | `windowClosing(WindowEvent e)` |
| Press keyboard key | Any focusable component | `KeyEvent` | `keyPressed/keyReleased/keyTyped` |

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough: Adapter Class with Mouse Events

**Problem:** Track only `mouseClicked` events on a `JPanel` without writing empty methods for the other four methods of `MouseListener`.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class MouseClickDemo extends JFrame {
    private JLabel coordLabel;

    public MouseClickDemo() {
        setTitle("Adapter Class Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());

        JPanel canvas = new JPanel();
        coordLabel = new JLabel("Click anywhere in the panel.");

        // Register MouseAdapter (instead of implementing MouseListener)
        canvas.addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
                coordLabel.setText("Clicked at (" + e.getX() + ", " + e.getY() + ")");
            }
        });

        add(canvas, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        add(coordLabel, BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new MouseClickDemo();
    }
}
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks "implement event handling without overriding all methods", the answer must use an **Adapter Class**. The signature `new MouseAdapter() { ... }` is an **anonymous inner class** overriding only `mouseClicked`.

## 3.4 Lambda Expression Shorthand (Modern Java 8+)

Since `ActionListener` has a single abstract method, it is a *functional interface* and can be replaced with a lambda:

```java
clickButton.addActionListener(e -> messageLabel.setText("Hello KTU!"));
```

This single line replaces the entire `actionPerformed` method тАФ extremely useful for quick event wiring.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Delegation Event Model in Java with a suitable diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Delegation Event Model has four participants:
1. **Event Source:** The GUI component (e.g., `JButton`) that generates an event.
2. **Event Object:** Encapsulates event details тАФ source, type, timestamp.
3. **Event Listener:** An object implementing a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`).
4. **Event Handler:** The overridden method (e.g., `actionPerformed`) inside the listener.

The source *delegates* the event to a registered listener using methods like `addActionListener()`. The source does **not** know what the listener will do тАФ they are loosely coupled, following OOP principles.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between AWT and Swing components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | AWT | Swing |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Component Type | Heavyweight (uses OS peers) | Lightweight (pure Java) |
| Class Prefix | No prefix | `J` prefix (e.g., `JButton`) |
| Look and Feel | OS-dependent | Pluggable (PLAF) |
| Components | Limited (Button, TextField) | Rich (JTable, JTree, JTabbedPane) |
| MVC | No | Yes |

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various Event Listener Interfaces available in Java AWT and Swing. List at least five with their corresponding event classes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java Swing program to create a frame with a text field and a button. When the button is clicked, the text entered in the text field should be displayed in a label.

---

### Model Solution тАФ Question A

#### Part (a) Solution:

Event Listener Interfaces are declared in `java.awt.event` and `javax.swing.event`. They define callback methods invoked when a particular event occurs.

1. **ActionListener** тАФ `java.awt.event`
   - Method: `void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`
   - Triggered by: `JButton`, `JMenuItem`, `JTextField` (on Enter)
   - Event Class: `ActionEvent`

2. **MouseListener** тАФ `java.awt.event`
   - Methods: `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited`
   - Triggered by: Mouse interactions on any component
   - Event Class: `MouseEvent`
   - Adapter: `MouseAdapter`

3. **KeyListener** тАФ `java.awt.event`
   - Methods: `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`
   - Event Class: `KeyEvent`
   - Adapter: `KeyAdapter`

4. **ItemListener** тАФ `java.awt.event`
   - Method: `void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)`
   - Triggered by: `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox`
   - Event Class: `ItemEvent`

5. **WindowListener** тАФ `java.awt.event`
   - Methods: `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated`
   - Event Class: `WindowEvent`
   - Adapter: `WindowAdapter`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For each listener interface, the exam answer must mention the **event class** and the **method name** to score full marks.

#### Part (b) Solution тАФ Complete Java Program:

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class TextDisplayApp extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    private JTextField inputField;
    private JButton showButton;
    private JLabel outputLabel;

    public TextDisplayApp() {
        setTitle("KTU Text Display Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        inputField = new JTextField(15);
        showButton = new JButton("Show Text");
        outputLabel = new JLabel("Result will appear here");

        // Register listener with the source
        showButton.addActionListener(this);
        // Also listen for Enter key in the text field
        inputField.addActionListener(this);

        add(new JLabel("Enter text: "));
        add(inputField);
        add(showButton);
        add(outputLabel);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String userText = inputField.getText();
        outputLabel.setText("You entered: " + userText);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new TextDisplayApp();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines (for 7 marks):**
- `implements ActionListener` тАФ registers the class as a listener.
- `addActionListener(this)` тАФ source delegates events to this listener.
- `actionPerformed` тАФ handler method defined by the interface.
- `getText()` and `setText()` тАФ read input and update the label.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are Adapter Classes in Java? Explain with an example using `WindowAdapter`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program using Swing to create a frame containing a `JCheckBox` and a `JRadioButton` group. Display the current selection in a `JLabel` using the `ItemListener` interface.

---

### Model Solution тАФ Question B

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Adapter Classes** are convenience classes in `java.awt.event` that provide empty implementations of all methods in a listener interface. They allow developers to override only the methods of interest.

Example: `WindowListener` has 7 methods. Implementing it directly requires 7 empty methods. Using `WindowAdapter`, you override only `windowClosing`:

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class WindowAdapterDemo extends JFrame {
    public WindowAdapterDemo() {
        setTitle("Adapter Demo");
        setSize(300, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE);

        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                int confirm = JOptionPane.showConfirmDialog(
                    null, "Are you sure you want to exit?",
                    "Exit Confirmation", JOptionPane.YES_NO_OPTION);
                if (confirm == JOptionPane.YES_OPTION) {
                    System.exit(0);
                }
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new WindowAdapterDemo();
    }
}
```

#### Part (b) Solution тАФ Complete Java Program:

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ItemListenerDemo extends JFrame implements ItemListener {
    private JCheckBox hobbyBox;
    private JRadioButton cBtn, javaBtn, pythonBtn;
    private JLabel displayLabel;

    public ItemListenerDemo() {
        setTitle("ItemListener Demo");
        setSize(380, 220);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        hobbyBox = new JCheckBox("Programming as Hobby");
        cBtn = new JRadioButton("C");
        javaBtn = new JRadioButton("Java");
        pythonBtn = new JRadioButton("Python");
        displayLabel = new JLabel("Select options above");

        ButtonGroup langGroup = new ButtonGroup();
        langGroup.add(cBtn);
        langGroup.add(javaBtn);
        langGroup.add(pythonBtn);

        hobbyBox.addItemListener(this);
        cBtn.addItemListener(this);
        javaBtn.addItemListener(this);
        pythonBtn.addItemListener(this);

        add(hobbyBox);
        add(cBtn); add(javaBtn); add(pythonBtn);
        add(displayLabel);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) {
        String result = "Hobby: " + (hobbyBox.isSelected() ? "Yes" : "No");
        if (cBtn.isSelected())       result += " | Lang: C";
        else if (javaBtn.isSelected())  result += " | Lang: Java";
        else if (pythonBtn.isSelected()) result += " | Lang: Python";
        displayLabel.setText(result);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ItemListenerDemo();
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** тАФ The frame will not appear even if all code is correct. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Not setting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** тАФ The JVM will not terminate when the window is closed. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing `ActionEvent` with `ItemEvent`** тАФ Buttons fire `ActionEvent` (use `ActionListener`); checkboxes/radios fire `ItemEvent` (use `ItemListener`). Mismatching these is a -2 mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting to group radio buttons with `ButtonGroup`** тАФ All radio buttons will behave like independent checkboxes, breaking the mutually-exclusive logic. (-2 marks)
> 5. **Implementing `MouseListener` directly with all 5 empty methods** тАФ Use `MouseAdapter` to avoid penalty for redundant code. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Writing `addActionListener(new ActionListener() { ... })` without using lambda** тАФ Both are accepted, but lambda is cleaner. Examiners do NOT penalize the verbose version, but it costs writing time.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ KTU Module 4: Event Listeners & Swing**

- **Delegation Event Model** has 4 parts: Source тЖТ Event тЖТ Listener тЖТ Handler.
- **All Swing components start with `J`** (`JButton`, `JFrame`, `JPanel`).
- **Swing is lightweight**, **AWT is heavyweight**.
- `ActionListener` тЖТ `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` for buttons, menu items, text fields (Enter key).
- `ItemListener` тЖТ `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` for checkboxes, radio buttons, combo boxes.
- `MouseListener` has **5 methods**; use `MouseAdapter` to avoid empty implementations.
- `WindowListener` has **7 methods**; use `WindowAdapter` similarly.
- `KeyListener` has **3 methods**: `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped`.
- **Adapter Classes** end with `Adapter` suffix and provide default no-op implementations.
- **Always call `setVisible(true)`** at the end of the constructor to display the frame.
- **Always call `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** to terminate JVM on close.
- **Lambda expressions** can replace single-method listener interfaces since Java 8.
- **Anonymous inner classes** are commonly used for one-off event handling (`new ActionListener() { ... }`).
- `ButtonGroup` is **mandatory** to make `JRadioButton`s mutually exclusive.
- **JTable, JTree, JTabbedPane, JMenuBar** are advanced Swing components frequently asked in 14-mark questions.
- **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT):** All Swing component updates should ideally happen on the EDT for thread safety.
- **Pluggable Look and Feel:** `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(UIManager.getSystemLookAndFeelClassName());`
- **Default layout for `JFrame` content pane** is `BorderLayout`; for `JPanel` it is `FlowLayout`.
- **Packages to remember:** `java.awt`, `java.awt.event`, `javax.swing`, `javax.swing.event`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Swing Packages
