> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Swing Packages** constitute a comprehensive set of Java class libraries housed under the root package **`javax.swing`** and its nested sub-packages. They provide a rich, **platform-independent**, **lightweight** component framework used to build **Graphical User Interfaces (GUI)** for desktop applications in Java. Swing was introduced as part of **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)** with JDK 1.2 (1998) and supersedes the legacy **java.awt** toolkit.

The full set of officially recognized KTU-relevant Swing packages is:

| Package | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `javax.swing` | Core components: `JFrame`, `JPanel`, `JButton`, `JLabel`, etc. |
| `javax.swing.event` | Event listener interfaces (`ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, etc.) |
| `javax.swing.border` | Borders for components (`LineBorder`, `TitledBorder`, `EtchedBorder`) |
| `javax.swing.plaf` | Pluggable Look-And-Feel (PLAFAF) architecture |
| `javax.swing.table` | `JTable` model and renderer classes |
| `javax.swing.tree` | `JTree` model and renderer classes |
| `javax.swing.text` | Text components and document models |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy тАФ "The Modular Furniture Showroom"** ЁЯЫЛя╕П
>
> Imagine building a house. The older **AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit)** is like a carpenter who hand-cuts every piece of wood using the local workshop's tools тАФ the windows look slightly different on every operating system because they depend on the underlying OS.
>
> **Swing**, on the other hand, is like a **modular furniture showroom** where every component (button, label, text-field) is built entirely in pure Java, painted pixel-by-pixel by Java itself. The same sofa looks **identical** in every house, on every continent тАФ be it Windows, Linux, or macOS. That is **platform independence**.

### Key Characteristics of Swing Components

- **100% Pure Java**: No reliance on native OS peers (unlike AWT).
- **Lightweight Components**: Rendered entirely by Java тАФ they don't grab OS resources.
- **Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF)**: A single component can mimic Windows, Motif, GTK, or Nimbus appearance at runtime.
- **MVC Architecture**: ModelтАУViewтАУController separation allows data, display, and control logic to evolve independently.
- **Replaceable Delegation**: Each Swing component delegates its UI rendering to a separate `ComponentUI` object (this is the actual *pluggable* piece).

---

## 1.3 The Swing Class Hierarchy (Top-Level)

```
java.lang.Object
   тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Component
         тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Container
               тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JComponent
                     тФЬтФАтФА JLabel
                     тФЬтФАтФА JButton
                     тФЬтФАтФА JTextField
                     тФЬтФАтФА JTextArea
                     тФЬтФАтФА JCheckBox
                     тФЬтФАтФА JRadioButton
                     тФЬтФАтФА JComboBox
                     тФЬтФАтФА JList
                     тФЬтФАтФА JPanel
                     тФЬтФАтФА JScrollPane
                     тФЬтФАтФА JTabbedPane
                     тФФтФАтФА ...
               тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JFrame  (top-level window)
               тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JDialog (dialog window)
               тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JApplet (deprecated)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`JComponent`** is the *abstract base class* for almost all Swing components. It is a subclass of `java.awt.Container` and inherits all AWT capabilities (layout, focus, painting) while extending them with pluggable UI, borders, tooltips, double-buffering, and key bindings.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Heavyweight vs Lightweight Components

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **high-frequency KTU question**.

| Property | Heavyweight (AWT) | Lightweight (Swing) |
|---|---|---|
| Rendering | Delegated to the **native OS** | Drawn entirely by **Java code** |
| Performance | Faster on host OS | Slightly slower, but more consistent |
| Look & Feel | Tied to the OS | **Pluggable** (changeable at runtime) |
| Transparency | Not supported | Supported (`setOpaque(false)`) |
| Z-order conflicts | Always on top of lightweight | Must manage with `setComponentZOrder` |
| Examples | `Button`, `TextField`, `Frame` | `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JFrame` |
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |

A **`JFrame`** is a *hybrid* тАФ it is a Swing top-level container but it **inherits** from `java.awt.Frame`, so it is technically a **heavyweight** container. All components placed *inside* the `JFrame` are lightweight.

---

## 2.2 Top-Level Containers (The Five Official Swing Containers)

```java
JFrame      // Standard window with title, icon, and menu bar
JDialog     // Pop-up dialog window (modal or modeless)
JApplet     // Browser-based (deprecated since JDK 9)
JWindow     // Bare title-less window (rare)
JInternalFrame // Frame confined inside another window (MDI apps)
```

### 2.2.1 The JFrame Anatomy

A `JFrame` uses **four layered panes** (from back to front):

1. **Root Pane** (`JRootPane`) тАФ the master container inside the frame.
2. **Glass Pane** тАФ invisible layer above everything; used for intercepting mouse events.
3. **Layered Pane** тАФ holds the content pane and the optional menu bar at specific depths (`-300` to `+400`).
4. **Content Pane** (`getContentPane()`) тАФ the visible area where you add components.

```java
JFrame frame = new JFrame("My App");        // Title
frame.setSize(400, 300);
frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
frame.setLayout(new FlowLayout());          // default is BorderLayout

JButton btn = new JButton("Click Me");
frame.add(btn);                             // Goes to content pane automatically (since JDK 5)

frame.setVisible(true);
```

---

## 2.3 Common Swing Components (KTU Reference Table)

| Component | Class | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Label | `JLabel` | Displays read-only text or icon |
| Button | `JButton` | Triggers an action on click |
| Text Field | `JTextField` | Single-line text input |
| Password Field | `JPasswordField` | Single-line masked input |
| Text Area | `JTextArea` | Multi-line plain text input |
| Check Box | `JCheckBox` | Multi-select toggle |
| Radio Button | `JRadioButton` | Mutually exclusive toggle (inside `ButtonGroup`) |
| Combo Box | `JComboBox` | Drop-down selection |
| List | `JList` | Scrollable list of selections |
| Slider | `JSlider` | Continuous or stepped numeric value |
| Progress Bar | `JProgressBar` | Visual completion indicator |
| Table | `JTable` | Two-dimensional tabular display |
| Tree | `JTree` | Hierarchical data display |
| Tabbed Pane | `JTabbedPane` | Multiple tabs in one area |
| Menu Bar | `JMenuBar` | Top-level menu container |
| Tool Tip | `setToolTipText(...)` | Hover help text |

---

## 2.4 Layout Managers (Cheat Sheet)

A **Layout Manager** controls the size and position of components inside a container. Swing containers use a `LayoutManager` object assigned via `setLayout()`.

| Manager | Class | Arrangement |
|---|---|---|
| Border | `BorderLayout` | North, South, East, West, Center (5 regions) |
| Flow | `FlowLayout` | Left-to-right, wraps to next line |
| Grid | `GridLayout` | Equal-sized rectangular cells (rows ├Ч cols) |
| Box | `BoxLayout` | Single-axis (X_LINE_AXIS or Y_LINE_AXIS) |
| Grid Bag | `GridBagLayout` | Variable-sized cells with constraints |
| Card | `CardLayout` | Stack of panels, one visible at a time |
| Group | `GroupLayout` | Used by NetBeans GUI Builder (JDK 6+) |
| Spring | `SpringLayout` | Constraint-based positioning |

---

## 2.5 Event Handling in Swing

Swing uses the **Delegation Event Model**, identical to AWT. Three participants:

1. **Event Source** тАФ the component that fires the event (e.g., `JButton`).
2. **Event Object** тАФ encapsulates what happened (`ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `WindowEvent`, etc.).
3. **Event Listener / Handler** тАФ implements an interface (`ActionListener`, etc.) and receives the event.

### 2.5.1 Common Event Listeners

| Event Type | Listener Interface | Adapter Class |
|---|---|---|
| Action (click, Enter) | `ActionListener` | тАФ |
| Mouse | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener` | `MouseAdapter` |
| Key | `KeyListener` | `KeyAdapter` |
| Window | `WindowListener` | `WindowAdapter` |
| Focus | `FocusListener` | `FocusAdapter` |
| Item (toggle) | `ItemListener` | тАФ |
| List selection | `ListSelectionListener` | тАФ |

```java
JButton btn = new JButton("Save");
btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Save clicked");
    }
});

// JDK 8+ lambda shorthand:
btn.addActionListener(e -> System.out.println("Save clicked"));
```

---

## 2.6 Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF)

```java
UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");
// or
UIManager.setLookAndFeel(UIManager.getSystemLookAndFeelClassName());

SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(frame);  // Apply the new LAF
```

Available LAFs in standard JDK:
- `MetalLookAndFeel` (default, cross-platform "Java look")
- `NimbusLookAndFeel` (recommended modern default since JDK 7)
- `WindowsLookAndFeel` (Windows only)
- `MotifLookAndFeel` (UNIX CDE)
- `GTKLookAndFeel` (Linux native)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PLAF matters:** A single JAR compiled with Swing looks *native* on every OS without recompilation тАФ this is the **Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA)** principle in action.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

Swing is the **canonical teaching vehicle** for event-driven programming, MVC separation, and design patterns like **Observer**, **Strategy**, and **Composite**. While superseded in modern enterprise software by JavaFX and web frameworks, Swing remains essential for:
- Internal enterprise tooling (NetBeans IDE was built with Swing).
- Embedded kiosk applications using compact JREs.
- Educational demonstration of GUI principles.
- Quick desktop utilities where the overhead of a browser-based stack is unwarranted.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example тАФ A Complete Swing Registration Form

Below is a **fully annotated**, exam-style program that combines **six** major Swing concepts:

1. `JFrame` top-level container
2. `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JPasswordField`, `JButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`
3. `BorderLayout` and `GridLayout` composition
4. `JPanel` sub-containers
5. `ActionListener` event handling
6. `JOptionPane` for output

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class RegistrationForm extends JFrame implements ActionListener {

    // --- 1. Declare components as instance fields ---
    JTextField nameField;
    JPasswordField passField;
    JCheckBox javaBox, pythonBox, cppBox;
    JRadioButton maleBtn, femaleBtn;
    JButton submitBtn, resetBtn;

    // --- 2. Constructor builds the UI ---
    RegistrationForm() {
        setTitle("Student Registration");          // Window title
        setSize(420, 360);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10));      // gaps between regions

        // --- NORTH panel: name + password ---
        JPanel northPanel = new JPanel(new GridLayout(2, 2, 5, 5));
        northPanel.add(new JLabel("Name:"));
        nameField = new JTextField();
        northPanel.add(nameField);
        northPanel.add(new JLabel("Password:"));
        passField = new JPasswordField();
        northPanel.add(passField);
        add(northPanel, BorderLayout.NORTH);

        // --- CENTER panel: languages (checkboxes) ---
        JPanel centerPanel = new JPanel(new FlowLayout());
        centerPanel.setBorder(BorderFactory.createTitledBorder("Languages"));
        javaBox   = new JCheckBox("Java");
        pythonBox = new JCheckBox("Python");
        cppBox    = new JCheckBox("C++");
        centerPanel.add(javaBox);
        centerPanel.add(pythonBox);
        centerPanel.add(cppBox);
        add(centerPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        // --- EAST panel: gender (radio buttons in a group) ---
        JPanel eastPanel = new JPanel(new GridLayout(2, 1));
        eastPanel.setBorder(BorderFactory.createTitledBorder("Gender"));
        maleBtn   = new JRadioButton("Male");
        femaleBtn = new JRadioButton("Female");
        ButtonGroup genderGroup = new ButtonGroup();
        genderGroup.add(maleBtn);
        genderGroup.add(femaleBtn);
        eastPanel.add(maleBtn);
        eastPanel.add(femaleBtn);
        add(eastPanel, BorderLayout.EAST);

        // --- SOUTH panel: submit & reset buttons ---
        JPanel southPanel = new JPanel();
        submitBtn = new JButton("Submit");
        resetBtn  = new JButton("Reset");
        submitBtn.addActionListener(this);         // self-listener
        resetBtn.addActionListener(this);
        southPanel.add(submitBtn);
        southPanel.add(resetBtn);
        add(southPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        setLocationRelativeTo(null);                // Center on screen
        setVisible(true);                           // Make it appear
    }

    // --- 3. Event handler (callback) ---
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == submitBtn) {
            String name    = nameField.getText();
            String pass    = new String(passField.getPassword());
            String gender  = maleBtn.isSelected() ? "Male" : "Female";
            StringBuilder langs = new StringBuilder();
            if (javaBox.isSelected())   langs.append("Java ");
            if (pythonBox.isSelected()) langs.append("Python ");
            if (cppBox.isSelected())    langs.append("C++ ");

            JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this,
                "Name: "    + name    + "\n" +
                "Password: " + pass    + "\n" +
                "Gender: "  + gender  + "\n" +
                "Languages: " + langs);
        } else if (e.getSource() == resetBtn) {
            nameField.setText("");
            passField.setText("");
            javaBox.setSelected(false);
            pythonBox.setSelected(false);
            cppBox.setSelected(false);
            genderGroup.clearSelection();
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new RegistrationForm();
    }
}
```

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Walk-Through

| Step | Action | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `extends JFrame implements ActionListener` | Inherit windowing + provide event handler in the same class. |
| 2 | `setLayout(new BorderLayout(10,10))` | Divide frame into 5 regions with **10 px** gaps. |
| 3 | Create 4 `JPanel` sub-containers | Group related components and apply independent layouts. |
| 4 | `BorderFactory.createTitledBorder("Languages")` | Add a labelled, etched border around a panel. |
| 5 | `ButtonGroup` for radio buttons | Enforces **mutual exclusion** (only one gender selectable). |
| 6 | `addActionListener(this)` | Register the *current* frame as the event handler. |
| 7 | `actionPerformed(...)` | Callback invoked by the JVM on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**. |
| 8 | `JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(...)` | Modal pop-up dialog; blocks until OK clicked. |
| 9 | `genderGroup.clearSelection()` | Resets both `maleBtn` and `femaleBtn` simultaneously. |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix тАФ "Which Component Do I Use?"

| Requirement | Best Component | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Display static text | `JLabel` | Not selectable, lightweight |
| Trigger a single command | `JButton` | Fires `ActionEvent` on click |
| Multi-line editable text | `JTextArea` inside `JScrollPane` | Native scrolling support |
| Single-line sensitive input | `JPasswordField` | Echoes `тАв` instead of text |
| Independent yes/no toggles | Multiple `JCheckBox` | Each state is independent |
| Pick one from 3тАУ5 options | `JComboBox` | Saves screen space |
| Display 50+ choices | `JList` inside `JScrollPane` | Vertical scroll, multi-select optional |
| Numeric range input | `JSlider` | Continuous/Discrete with ticks |
| Tabbed interface | `JTabbedPane` | Multiple panels share one window |
| Tooltip on hover | `setToolTipText(...)` | Inherited from `JComponent` |
| Top-level window | `JFrame` | Only Swing top-level with title bar & menus |
| Modal pop-up | `JDialog` or `JOptionPane` | Blocks other windows until dismissed |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step тАФ Switching the Look and Feel

```java
import javax.swing.*;

public class LAFDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            // Step 1: Choose the LAF class
            UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.err.println("LAF not available");
        }

        // Step 2: Create UI (LAF applied only to components created AFTER this)
        JFrame f = new JFrame("LAF Demo");
        f.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        f.add(new JButton("I am a Nimbus Button"));
        f.pack();
        f.setVisible(true);

        // Step 3: If UI already exists, refresh it:
        SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(f);
    }
}
```

The three required steps for a successful LAF switch are therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{setLookAndFeel} \;\rightarrow\; \text{(re)build UI} \;\rightarrow\; \text{updateComponentTreeUI}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Swing Class Hierarchy (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Object java lang Object] --> B[Component java awt Component]
    B --> C[Container java awt Container]
    C --> D[JComponent javax swing JComponent]
    C --> E[JFrame javax swing JFrame]
    C --> F[JDialog javax swing JDialog]
    D --> G[JLabel]
    D --> H[JButton]
    D --> I[JTextField]
    D --> J[JTextArea]
    D --> K[JCheckBox]
    D --> L[JRadioButton]
    D --> M[JComboBox]
    D --> N[JList]
    D --> O[JPanel]
    D --> P[JScrollPane]
    D --> Q[JTabbedPane]
    D --> R[JMenuBar]
    D --> S[JTable]
    D --> T[JTree]
```

---

## 4.2 Event Delegation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor User
    participant Button as JButton Source
    participant JVM as Event Queue
    participant EDT as Event Dispatch Thread
    participant Listener as ActionListener

    User->>Button: Click with mouse
    Button->>JVM: Fires ActionEvent
    JVM->>EDT: Posts event to EDT
    EDT->>Listener: invoke actionPerformed event
    Listener-->>EDT: Updates UI state
    EDT-->>User: Visual feedback repaint
```

---

## 4.3 JFrame Internal Pane Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[JFrame Heavyweight Window] --> B[JRootPane]
    B --> C[Glass Pane Invisible]
    B --> D[Layered Pane]
    D --> E[Content Pane Add your components here]
    D --> F[Menu Bar Optional top strip]
    E --> G[JPanel sub container]
    G --> H[JLabel]
    G --> I[JButton]
    G --> J[JTextField]
```

---

## 4.4 MVC Architecture in Swing (Using JList as Example)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph M [Model]
        M1[DefaultListModel]
        M2[Stores raw data]
    end
    subgraph V [View]
        V1[JList Component]
        V2[CellRenderer]
    end
    subgraph C [Controller]
        C1[ListSelectionListener]
        C2[Event handlers]
    end
    M --> V
    V --> C
    C --> M
    V --> V1
    V --> V2
    C --> C1
    C --> C2
```

---

## 4.5 Layout Manager Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Need to arrange components] --> Q1{How many regions?}
    Q1 -->|Five fixed regions| Border[Use BorderLayout]
    Q1 -->|Equal grid cells| Grid[Use GridLayout]
    Q1 -->|Single row or column| Box[Use BoxLayout]
    Q1 -->|Stacked cards| Card[Use CardLayout]
    Q1 -->|Variable cells| Bag[Use GridBagLayout]
    Q1 -->|Auto wrap flow| Flow[Use FlowLayout]
    Start --> Note[All managed by java awt LayoutManager interface]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]** Differentiate between AWT and Swing. List any four differences.

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | AWT (`java.awt`) | Swing (`javax.swing`) |
|---|---|---|
| Components | Heavyweight | Lightweight |
| Platform dependency | Native peers тАФ looks different per OS | Pure Java тАФ identical across OS |
| Pluggable LAF | Not supported | Supported via `UIManager` |
| MVC | Partial | Full MVC separation |
| Top-level container | `Frame` | `JFrame` |
| Number of classes | Fewer | Larger, richer component set |
| Rendering | OS-controlled | Java-controlled pixel rendering |

*(Award 0.5 marks per correct comparison; pick any four.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]** Explain the term **pluggable look-and-feel** in Swing.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pluggable Look-And-Feel (PLAFF) is the architectural feature that allows a Swing application's appearance to be **changed at runtime** without modifying or recompiling the source code. Every Swing component delegates its painting to a `ComponentUI` object that can be swapped using `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(className)`. Built-in LAFs include **Metal**, **Nimbus**, **Windows**, **Motif**, and **GTK**.

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. We provide **Option A** and **Option B** as alternative selections. Each option is split into a **(a)** sub-question worth 7 marks and a **(b)** sub-question worth 7 marks.

---

### ЁЯУШ Question A тАФ Total 14 Marks

#### Q-A (a) **[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2023 тАФ 7 Marks]**

Explain the **Swing class hierarchy** starting from `java.lang.Object` down to `JButton`. Mention the significance of `JComponent`.

**Model Solution:**

```
java.lang.Object
   тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Component
         тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Container
               тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JComponent
                     тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.AbstractButton
                           тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JButton
```

- **`Object`** тАФ root of the class hierarchy; provides `toString`, `equals`, etc.
- **`Component`** тАФ abstract class for anything that can be displayed on screen; provides `setSize`, `setLocation`, `paint`, `repaint`.
- **`Container`** тАФ subclass of `Component` that can hold other components; provides `add`, `remove`, `setLayout`.
- **`JComponent`** тАФ base class for **all Swing components**; provides:
  - **Pluggable UI** via the `ComponentUI` delegate.
  - **Tool-tips** via `setToolTipText()`.
  - **Borders** via `setBorder()`.
  - **Double-buffering** for flicker-free painting.
  - **Key bindings** for keyboard-driven UI.
  - **Autoscrolling** and **accessible** properties.
- **`AbstractButton`** тАФ provides shared behaviour for all button types (`JButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JMenuItem`); defines `addActionListener`, `setText`, `setIcon`, `setMnemonic`.
- **`JButton`** тАФ the concrete push-button component. 

*(7 marks: 1 for diagram, 1 each for role of Component/Container, 2 for JComponent features, 1 for AbstractButton, 1 for JButton.)*

---

#### Q-A (b) **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2022 тАФ 7 Marks]**

Write a Java Swing program to create a `JFrame` containing a `JTextField`, a `JButton` labelled "Greet", and a `JLabel`. When the button is clicked, the label should display `"Hello, <name>"` where `<name>` is the text typed in the text field. Use lambda expression for the event handler.

**Model Solution:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class GreetApp extends JFrame {

    JTextField nameField;
    JLabel greetLabel;

    GreetApp() {
        setTitle("Greeter");
        setSize(350, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 20));

        add(new JLabel("Enter name:"));
        nameField = new JTextField(15);
        add(nameField);

        JButton greetBtn = new JButton("Greet");
        add(greetBtn);

        greetLabel = new JLabel(" ");
        greetLabel.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 16));
        add(greetLabel);

        // JDK 8+ lambda replaces the anonymous inner class
        greetBtn.addActionListener(e -> 
            greetLabel.setText("Hello, " + nameField.getText() + "!"));

        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new GreetApp();
    }
}
```

**Explanation (for valuation):**

1. Class extends `JFrame` to inherit a top-level window.
2. `FlowLayout(CENTER, 10, 20)` arranges components left-to-right with **10 px** horizontal and **20 px** vertical gaps.
3. The lambda `e -> greetLabel.setText(...)` is shorthand for a single-method `ActionListener`.
4. `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` centers the frame on the screen.
5. `setVisible(true)` triggers painting тАФ must be called **after** all components are added.

*(7 marks: 1 imports/extends, 1 layout, 1 components, 2 lambda listener, 1 setVisible, 1 output explanation.)*

---

### ЁЯУЧ Question B тАФ Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

#### Q-B (a) **[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024 тАФ 7 Marks]**

Explain the **Delegation Event Model** used in Swing with a neat diagram. List any **four** event listener interfaces from `javax.swing.event` and the corresponding adapter classes from `java.awt.event`.

**Model Solution:**

The **Delegation Event Model** decouples the event-generating component (source) from the event-handling code (listener). Steps:

1. A user action (click, key press) on a component generates an **Event Object**.
2. The source component stores a list of registered listeners.
3. The Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) invokes the appropriate method on each listener.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant SRC as JButton Source
    participant E as ActionEvent Object
    participant L as ActionListener

    U->>SRC: Click
    SRC->>E: Creates ActionEvent
    SRC->>L: actionPerformed event
    L-->>SRC: Update UI
```

**Listeners and Adapters:**

| Listener Interface (in `javax.swing.event` / `java.awt.event`) | Adapter Class (in `java.awt.event`) |
|---|---|
| `MouseListener` | `MouseAdapter` |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `MouseMotionAdapter` |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyAdapter` |
| `WindowListener` | `WindowAdapter` |
| `FocusListener` | `FocusAdapter` |

> **Note:** `ActionListener` and `ItemListener` have **no adapter** because each has only one abstract method.

*(7 marks: 3 for explanation + diagram, 2 for the four listener rows, 1 for the no-adapter note, 1 for naming the EDT.)*

---

#### Q-B (b) **[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2023 тАФ 7 Marks]**

Compare **`BorderLayout`**, **`FlowLayout`**, and **`GridLayout`** with code-level examples showing how each arranges five buttons.

**Model Solution:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class LayoutDemo extends JFrame {

    LayoutDemo(String title, LayoutManager lm) {
        setTitle(title);
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(lm);                            // тЖР Different layout per demo
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++)
            add(new JButton("Btn-" + i));
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new LayoutDemo("BorderLayout", new BorderLayout(5, 5));
        new LayoutDemo("FlowLayout",    new FlowLayout());
        new LayoutDemo("GridLayout",    new GridLayout(2, 3, 5, 5));  // 2 rows ├Ч 3 cols
    }
}
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | `BorderLayout` | `FlowLayout` | `GridLayout` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Regions | 5 fixed (N, S, E, W, C) | Sequential row, wraps | Equal cells in rows ├Ч cols |
| Default of `JFrame` | тЬЕ Yes | No | No |
| Component stretching | Components fill region | Native preferred size | All cells equal |
| Effect of 5 buttons | Last 5th goes to Center; one button per region only | One row if width permits, otherwise wraps | Arranged in 2├Ч3 grid (one cell empty) |
| Constructor | `BorderLayout(hgap, vgap)` | `FlowLayout(align, hgap, vgap)` | `GridLayout(rows, cols, hgap, vgap)` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In `BorderLayout`, adding the **6th component without specifying a region** silently overwrites the *Center*. Many students lose **2 marks** here.
> 2. `GridLayout(rows, cols, ...)` treats **`rows` or `cols` set to 0** as "as many as needed." Students often hard-code both and then complain the layout overflows.
> 3. `FlowLayout` does **not** expand components vertically тАФ components stay at their **preferred height**.
> 4. Mixing layouts requires **nested `JPanel`s**; do not call `setLayout` on a panel that already has a parent layout active.

*(7 marks: 3 code, 3 comparison table, 1 additional insight/limitation.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- тЬЕ **Swing is 100% pure Java**, lives under `javax.swing.*`, and supersedes `java.awt.*` for new GUI development.
- тЬЕ **`JComponent`** is the abstract base class for almost every Swing widget тАФ know its six headline features: borders, tooltips, double-buffering, key bindings, autoscroll, pluggable UI.
- тЬЕ A **`JFrame`** is technically a **heavyweight** hybrid (inherits `java.awt.Frame`) but its content is **lightweight**.
- тЬЕ Use **content pane** (`getContentPane()`) to add components; before JDK 5 you had to call it explicitly.
- тЬЕ Always end Swing initialisation with `setVisible(true)`.
- тЬЕ **Delegation Event Model** тАФ three roles: *Source*, *Event*, *Listener*. Listeners register with `addXxxListener`, fire methods named `xxxPerformed` or `xxxChanged`.
- тЬЕ Adapter classes (`MouseAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `FocusAdapter`) provide empty default implementations so you override **only the events you care about**.
- тЬЕ `ButtonGroup` enforces **mutual exclusion** for `JRadioButton`s тАФ without it, multiple radio buttons can be selected simultaneously.
- тЬЕ **Layout managers** are mandatory; setting `setLayout(null)` is discouraged because components will not respond to window resize.
- тЬЕ **PLAF switch** = `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)` тЖТ rebuild UI тЖТ `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(...)`.
- тЬЕ Long-running work must be done in a **`SwingWorker`** thread, not the EDT, otherwise the UI **freezes**.
- тЬЕ `JOptionPane.showMessageDialog`, `showConfirmDialog`, `showInputDialog` are the fastest ways to produce modal pop-ups.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Pitfalls (re-read before submitting):**
> - Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` тАФ the window won't terminate the JVM.
> - Calling `setVisible(true)` *before* `add(...)` тАФ components won't appear.
> - Comparing strings with `==` instead of `.equals(...)` inside `actionPerformed`.
> - Using `TextField` (AWT) instead of `JTextField` (Swing) тАФ silently compiles but breaks PLAF.
> - Declaring `JButton btn = new JButton();` *after* `frame.setVisible(true)` тАФ the button will not be rendered.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Handling in Swings
Swing Layout Managers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Layout Managers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Layout Manager** in Java Swing is an object that implements the `java.awt.LayoutManager` (or its sub-interface `LayoutManager2`) contract and dictates the **size, position, and arrangement of components** inside a `Container` (such as a `JFrame`, `JPanel`, or `JApplet`). It abstracts away the raw pixel-coordinate calculations from the programmer, thereby enforcing the **platform-independent Look-And-Feel** paradigm mandated by the Java Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT) and its Swing extension.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *"A Layout Manager is a Java object responsible for arranging the GUI components in a container such that the GUI retains its visual integrity across different operating systems and window resizes."*

Every `Container` in Swing has a **default layout manager** assigned at construction time:
- `JFrame` тЖТ `BorderLayout`
- `JPanel` тЖТ `FlowLayout`
- `JApplet` тЖТ `FlowLayout`
- `JDialog` тЖТ `BorderLayout`

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ "The Furniture Fitter"

Imagine you are moving into a new **room** (the `Container`). You have a **bed, table, chair, and wardrobe** (the GUI components like `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`). You have three options:

1. **Manual Placement (Absolute Layout / `null` layout):** You measure the room with a tape, mark exact wall distances, and place each item at hard-coded coordinates. If the room changes shape (window resize) тАФ your furniture floats in mid-air or overlaps.
2. **The Furniture Fitter (Layout Manager):** You call a professional fitter and say, *"Put the bed in the North, the table in the Center, and arrange the chairs in a row."* The fitter recalculates positions every time the room dimensions change.
3. **Custom Fitter with Constraints (e.g., `GridBagLayout`):** You provide detailed instructions тАФ *"The bed should occupy 60% of the room width, table exactly in the center, chairs evenly distributed."*

The **Layout Manager** is the Furniture Fitter. It reacts to resize events automatically.

### 1.3 Why Layout Managers? (The Core "Why")

| Problem with Absolute Positioning | How Layout Manager Solves It |
|---|---|
| Breaks on different screen resolutions | Uses **preferred/minimum/maximum size** properties |
| Ugly on window resize | Components re-flow dynamically |
| Platform-specific look (Windows vs Mac vs Linux) | DPI-aware, font-aware positioning |
| Heavy maintenance code | Declarative style тАФ write layout, not coordinates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Golden Rules of Swing Layouts (from KTU Board perspective):**
> 1. Never use `setLayout(null)` unless the examiner specifically demands it.
> 2. Always nest `JPanel` containers тАФ outer layout for the big picture, inner layouts for grouped widgets.
> 3. The order of `add(component, constraint)` matters in BorderLayout, but not in FlowLayout or GridLayout.

### 1.4 The `LayoutManager` Interface тАФ Core Contract

The interface (in `java.awt`) declares the following abstract methods that every layout manager must implement:

$$\text{void addLayoutComponent(String name, Component comp)}$$

$$\text{void removeLayoutComponent(Component comp)}$$

$$\text{Dimension preferredLayoutSize(Container parent)}$$

$$\text{Dimension minimumLayoutSize(Container parent)}$$

$$\text{void layoutContainer(Container parent)}$$

The extended interface `LayoutManager2` adds support for **constraints** (used by `BorderLayout`, `GridBagLayout`, `CardLayout`):

$$\text{void addLayoutComponent(Component comp, Object constraints)}$$

$$\text{Dimension maximumLayoutSize(Container target)}$$

$$\text{float getLayoutAlignmentX(Container target)}$$

$$\text{float getLayoutAlignmentY(Container target)}$$

$$\text{void invalidateLayout(Container target)}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow тАФ "How a Layout Manager Works"

When a `Container` is displayed, the following lifecycle executes:

1. **Container Validation:** The AWT/Swing event-dispatching thread calls `validate()` (or `revalidate()`).
2. **Preferred Size Query:** The container asks each child for its `getPreferredSize()`.
3. **Layout Calculation:** The `LayoutManager.layoutContainer(parent)` method is invoked. The manager calculates `x, y, width, height` for each child based on:
   - The parent's current size (`parent.getWidth()`, `parent.getHeight()`)
   - The child's preferred/minimum/maximum size
   - The layout's own algorithm
4. **Component Placement:** Each child's `setBounds(x, y, w, h)` is called.
5. **Paint Cycle:** The freshly-positioned components are rendered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mathematical goal of any layout manager can be generalized as:
> $$S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_{\text{preferred}}^{(i)} + \sum_{j} G_j$$
> where $S_{\text{total}}$ is the available space, $S_{\text{preferred}}^{(i)}$ is the preferred size of the $i$-th component, and $G_j$ represents the gaps (insets, padding, hgap, vgap).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet тАФ Quick-Reference Table

| Layout Manager | Constructor Signature | Key Constraint / Property | Default Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| `FlowLayout` | `new FlowLayout(int align, int hgap, int vgap)` | Alignment: `LEFT`, `CENTER` (default), `RIGHT` | Wraps to next line when full |
| `BorderLayout` | `new BorderLayout(int hgap, int vgap)` | Regions: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER` | 5 regions; CENTER grabs leftovers |
| `GridLayout` | `new GridLayout(int rows, int cols, int hgap, int vgap)` | Uniform cell sizes | All cells equal size |
| `BoxLayout` | `new BoxLayout(Container target, int axis)` | Axis: `X_AXIS` (horizontal), `Y_AXIS` (vertical) | Stacks components linearly |
| `GridBagLayout` | `new GridBagLayout()` | `GridBagConstraints` object | Cells can vary in size |
| `CardLayout` | `new CardLayout(int hgap, int vgap)` | Card name (String identifier) | Shows one card at a time |
| `GroupLayout` | `new GroupLayout(Container host)` | `Group` (sequential/parallel) | Used by NetBeans GUI Builder |
| `SpringLayout` | `new SpringLayout()` | `Spring` object (edge constraints) | Spring physics for positioning |

### 2.3 BorderLayout тАФ Deep Theoretical Notes

$$\text{Available Height} = H_{\text{parent}} - H_{\text{NORTH}} - H_{\text{SOUTH}}$$

$$\text{Available Width} = W_{\text{parent}} - W_{\text{EAST}} - W_{\text{WEST}}$$

The **CENTER** component is the only one that stretches on both axes. If you add a component without specifying a constraint, it defaults to `CENTER`, and subsequent components **overwrite** the previous one.

### 2.4 GridBagLayout тАФ The "Mathematical Heavyweight"

This layout uses a **zero-based grid coordinate system** where each cell can be occupied by one or more grid cells. The critical formula is:

$$W_{\text{component}} = \sum_{k=\text{gridx}}^{\text{gridx}+\text{gridwidth}-1} C_k \cdot w_k + \text{ipady} \cdot 2$$

where $C_k$ is the **weight-x** of column $k$ and $w_k$ is its base width. The `weightx` and `weighty` fields (0.0 to 1.0) determine how **extra space** is distributed:

$$\text{Extra}_x^{(i)} = \frac{\text{weightx}_i}{\sum_{k} \text{weightx}_k} \cdot E_x$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** GridBagLayout is the de-facto choice for **Enterprise data-entry forms** (J2EE Swing clients, NetBeans IDE, IntelliJ UI) because it precisely mimics HTML/CSS table rendering behavior.

### 2.5 CardLayout тАФ Tab-Style Switching

Used to build **wizard dialogs** and **tabbed interfaces** before `JTabbedPane` existed. The card display logic uses:

$$\text{Visible Card}_i \Rightarrow \text{show(Container parent, String name)}_i$$

Cards are stacked like a deck; only the top card is visible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: BorderLayout Resize Calculation

**Problem Setup:**
A `JFrame` of size $W = 600, H = 400$ uses `BorderLayout(10, 10)` (hgap=10, vgap=10). Five components are added with the following preferred sizes:

| Component | Region | Preferred (W ├Ч H) |
|---|---|---|
| `lblTitle` | NORTH | 600 ├Ч 40 |
| `btnOK` | SOUTH | 100 ├Ч 30 |
| `txtLeft` | WEST | 150 ├Ч 100 |
| `txtRight` | EAST | 150 ├Ч 100 |
| `txtArea` | CENTER | ? |

**Step 1: Reserve space for NORTH**

$$H_{\text{NORTH}} = 40$$

**Step 2: Reserve space for SOUTH**

$$H_{\text{SOUTH}} = 30$$

**Step 3: Compute remaining vertical space (with vgap deducted)**

$$H_{\text{remaining}} = H - H_{\text{NORTH}} - H_{\text{SOUTH}} - 2 \cdot v_{\text{gap}}$$

$$H_{\text{remaining}} = 400 - 40 - 30 - 2 \cdot 10 = 320$$

**Step 4: WEST region takes its full preferred width**

$$W_{\text{WEST}} = 150$$

**Step 5: EAST region takes its full preferred width**

$$W_{\text{EAST}} = 150$$

**Step 6: Compute remaining horizontal space (with hgap deducted)**

$$W_{\text{remaining}} = W - W_{\text{WEST}} - W_{\text{EAST}} - 2 \cdot h_{\text{gap}}$$

$$W_{\text{remaining}} = 600 - 150 - 150 - 2 \cdot 10 = 280$$

**Step 7: CENTER region gets the leftovers**

$$W_{\text{CENTER}} = 280, \quad H_{\text{CENTER}} = 320$$

**Result:** The `txtArea` (CENTER) is rendered at position $(170, 50)$ with dimensions $280 \times 320$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix тАФ Choosing the Right Layout (Board Exam Decision Tree)

| Scenario | Recommended Layout | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Simple toolbar of equal buttons | `GridLayout(1, n)` | Equal-size cells, no logic needed |
| OK / Cancel button row at the bottom | `FlowLayout(RIGHT)` | Natural right-alignment of action buttons |
| Form with labels and inputs in 2 columns | `GridLayout(n, 2)` | Pairs align perfectly |
| Complex form with variable field widths | `GridBagLayout` | Most flexible constraint system |
| Wizard with multiple steps | `CardLayout` | One screen at a time |
| Toolbar + status bar + central editor | `BorderLayout` | Classic editor pattern (Eclipse, VS Code style) |
| Vertical stack of panels | `BoxLayout(Y_AXIS)` | Linear, predictable stacking |
| NetBeans drag-and-drop GUIs | `GroupLayout` | Auto-generated; horizontal+vertical groups |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Java Code тАФ Demonstrating FlowLayout тЖТ BorderLayout Transition

**Step 1: Default JPanel uses FlowLayout**

```java
JPanel p = new JPanel();  // implicitly: new FlowLayout()
p.add(new JButton("One"));
p.add(new JButton("Two"));
```

**Step 2: Override with GridLayout**

```java
p.setLayout(new GridLayout(2, 2, 5, 5));
```

**Step 3: Add multiple components тАФ they fill the 2x2 grid cell-by-cell**

```java
p.add(new JButton("A"));
p.add(new JButton("B"));
p.add(new JButton("C"));
p.add(new JButton("D"));
```

**Step 4: Full BorderLayout demo in a JFrame**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class BorderDemo extends JFrame {
    public BorderDemo() {
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10));
        add(new JButton("NORTH"), BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(new JButton("SOUTH"), BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        add(new JButton("EAST"),  BorderLayout.EAST);
        add(new JButton("WEST"),  BorderLayout.WEST);
        add(new JTextArea("CENTER"), BorderLayout.CENTER);
        setSize(500, 400);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new BorderDemo();
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each `add(component, constraint)` call uses the `LayoutManager2.addLayoutComponent(comp, constraints)` method overload. This is why `BorderLayout` and `GridBagLayout` *require* the constraint argument тАФ they implement the `LayoutManager2` interface, not the basic `LayoutManager`.

### 3.4 BoxLayout тАФ Spring-Based Component Sizing

The `BoxLayout` uses `Box.createHorizontalBox()` and `Box.createVerticalBox()` factory methods. It supports three flexible components:

| Component | Type | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `Box.createGlue()` | Invisible | Pushes components apart |
| `Box.createHorizontalStrut(n)` | Invisible | Forces minimum $n$ pixels horizontal gap |
| `Box.createRigidArea(new Dimension(w,h))` | Invisible | Forces fixed $(w,h)$ gap |

These act as invisible "spacers" in the layout equation:

$$D_{\text{component}} = P_{\text{preferred}} + \sum_{k} S_k$$

where $S_k$ are the adjacent glue/strut contributions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram тАФ Swing Layout Manager Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Interface LayoutManager] --> A1[Method preferredLayoutSize]
    A0 --> A2[Method minimumLayoutSize]
    A0 --> A3[Method layoutContainer]
    A0 --> A4[Method addLayoutComponent String]
    A0 --> A5[Method removeLayoutComponent]
    A1Z[Interface LayoutManager2] --> A1
    A1Z --> A2
    A1Z --> A3
    A1Z --> A4B[Method addLayoutComponent Object]
    A1Z --> A6[Method maximumLayoutSize]
    A1Z --> A7[Method getLayoutAlignmentX]
    A1Z --> A8[Method getLayoutAlignmentY]
    A1Z --> A9[Method invalidateLayout]
    A0 --> A4B
    A1Z --> A5
    A1Z --> A4
    A0 --> A6
    A0 --> A7
    A0 --> A8
    A0 --> A9
    B0[Class FlowLayout] --> A0
    B1[Class BorderLayout] --> A1Z
    B2[Class GridLayout] --> A0
    B3[Class BoxLayout] --> A1Z
    B4[Class GridBagLayout] --> A1Z
    B5[Class CardLayout] --> A1Z
    B6[Class GroupLayout] --> A1Z
    B7[Class SpringLayout] --> A0
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram тАФ BorderLayout Region Distribution

```mermaid
graph TD
    N0[Container Parent WxH] --> N1[NORTH Region full width preferred height]
    N0 --> N2[SOUTH Region full width preferred height]
    N0 --> N3[EAST Region preferred width full height]
    N0 --> N4[WEST Region preferred width full height]
    N0 --> N5[CENTER Region leftover space both axes]
    N1 --> M1[Stretches horizontally only]
    N2 --> M2[Stretches horizontally only]
    N3 --> M3[Stretches vertically only]
    N4 --> M4[Stretches vertically only]
    N5 --> M5[Stretches both axes grabs extras]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram тАФ Layout Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid

    Q1[Need a Swing layout] --> Q2{Equal sized cells}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3[Use GridLayout]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{North South East West Center pattern}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5[Use BorderLayout]
    Q4 -->|No| Q6{Linear stack horizontal or vertical}
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q7[Use BoxLayout]
    Q6 -->|No| Q8{One card visible at a time}

```

```mermaid

    L0[Container is created] --> L1[LayoutManager assigned via setLayout]
    L1 --> L2[Components added via add component constraint]

    L4 --> L5[For each child call setBounds xywh]
    L5 --> L6[Paint cycle renders components]

    L7 -->|No| L8[GUI Idle state]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАФ June 2023]**
*Explain the concept of a Layout Manager in Java. Why is it preferred over absolute positioning?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Layout Manager is an object that implements the `java.awt.LayoutManager` interface and is responsible for arranging GUI components within a container such as a `JFrame` or `JPanel`. It dynamically calculates the position and size of each component based on the container's current dimensions. It is preferred over absolute positioning (using `setLayout(null)` and `setBounds()`) because: (i) it provides platform-independent rendering, (ii) components re-position themselves automatically when the window is resized, and (iii) it reduces hard-coded pixel calculations, improving maintainability.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2022]**
*List any three Layout Managers available in Java Swing and state one specific use case for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **`BorderLayout`** тАФ Used to divide the container into five regions (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, CENTER); ideal for application shells with a toolbar, status bar, and central working area.
2. **`GridLayout`** тАФ Used to arrange components in a matrix of equal-sized cells; ideal for calculator-style numeric keypads.
3. **`CardLayout`** тАФ Used to display only one of several panels at a time; ideal for wizard dialogs and tab-style step-by-step forms.

---

### 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A тАФ 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the `BorderLayout` manager in detail. Draw a diagram showing all five regions and describe the resizing behavior of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program using `GridLayout` to create a calculator-like numeric keypad (buttons 0тАУ9, +, тИТ, ├Ч, ├╖, and "=") arranged in a 4├Ч4 grid. Handle the button clicks to display the pressed value in a `JTextField` at the top. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BorderLayout Explanation (7 marks):**

`BorderLayout` is the default layout manager for `JFrame`, `JDialog`, and `JWindow` (in Java 2+). It divides the container into five distinct regions: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, and `CENTER`.

*Resizing Behavior:*
- `NORTH` and `SOUTH` components stretch **horizontally** to fill the container's full width but maintain their **preferred height**. They appear at the topmost and bottommost positions respectively.
- `EAST` and `WEST` components maintain their **preferred width** but stretch **vertically** to fill the remaining height (after NORTH and SOUTH are placed).
- `CENTER` occupies whatever space remains after the other four regions claim their preferred sizes. It stretches on **both axes**.

*Constructor:* `BorderLayout(int hgap, int vgap)` тАФ `hgap` is horizontal gap between regions, `vgap` is vertical gap. Default is no gap.

```java
Container c = getContentPane();
c.setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10));
c.add(new JButton("Top"), BorderLayout.NORTH);
c.add(new JButton("Bottom"), BorderLayout.SOUTH);
c.add(new JButton("Right"), BorderLayout.EAST);
c.add(new JButton("Left"), BorderLayout.WEST);
c.add(new JTextArea("Center"), BorderLayout.CENTER);
```

If two components are added to the same region (e.g., both to `NORTH`), only the **last-added** component is visible.

**(b) Calculator Keypad Program (7 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CalculatorKeypad extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JTextField display;
    JButton[] buttons = new JButton[16];
    String[] labels = {"7","8","9","/",
                       "4","5","6","*",
                       "1","2","3","-",
                       "C","0","=","+"};

    public CalculatorKeypad() {
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());
        display = new JTextField();
        add(display, BorderLayout.NORTH);

        JPanel panel = new JPanel();
        panel.setLayout(new GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5));
        for (int i = 0; i < 16; i++) {
            buttons[i] = new JButton(labels[i]);
            buttons[i].addActionListener(this);
            panel.add(buttons[i]);
        }
        add(panel, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        setSize(300, 400);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String cmd = e.getActionCommand();
        if (cmd.equals("C")) display.setText("");
        else display.setText(display.getText() + cmd);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new CalculatorKeypad();
    }
}
```

*Explanation:* A `JTextField` is placed in the NORTH region of a `BorderLayout`. A `JPanel` with a 4├Ч4 `GridLayout` is placed in the CENTER. The button array iterates through the labels, creating 16 buttons in row-major order. The `actionPerformed` method appends each label to the display field, except the "C" (clear) button which resets the text.

---

#### Question B тАФ 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Compare `FlowLayout`, `BorderLayout`, and `GridLayout` with respect to: (i) default alignment, (ii) number of components supported, (iii) handling of resize events. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain `GridBagLayout` with a suitable example. What is the role of `weightx` and `weighty` in `GridBagConstraints`? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Criterion | FlowLayout | BorderLayout | GridLayout |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Default Alignment | CENTER (horizontal), TOP (vertical) | None тАФ 5 fixed regions | None тАФ grid is uniform |
| (ii) Components Supported | Unlimited; wraps to next line | Exactly 5 regions; extra components overwrite | Exactly $rows \times cols$ slots; more overwrite |
| (iii) Resize Behavior | Components stay at preferred size, centered; extra space is gap-filler | NORTH/SOUTH stretch horizontally; EAST/WEST stretch vertically; CENTER stretches on both | All cells resize **uniformly** to fill the container; components inside stretch too |
| Default Container | `JPanel`, `JApplet` | `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow` | None (must be set explicitly) |
| Gap Property | `hgap`, `vgap` | `hgap`, `vgap` | `hgap`, `vgap` |

*Resize Notes:*
- In **FlowLayout**, components never resize тАФ they only reflow to the next line.
- In **BorderLayout**, the CENTER component is the "elastic" one that grows/shrinks.
- In **GridLayout**, all cells resize proportionally with the container.

**(b) GridBagLayout Explanation (7 marks):**

`GridBagLayout` is the most flexible and complex layout manager in AWT/Swing. It places components in a **virtual grid** where each cell can be of variable size. Each component is associated with a `GridBagConstraints` object that specifies:

- `gridx`, `gridy` тАФ top-left cell coordinate of the component
- `gridwidth`, `gridheight` тАФ number of cells the component spans
- `fill` тАФ `NONE`, `HORIZONTAL`, `VERTICAL`, or `BOTH`
- `anchor` тАФ alignment within the cell when `fill = NONE`
- `insets` тАФ external padding (top, left, bottom, right)
- `ipadx`, `ipady` тАФ internal padding (adds to component's preferred size)
- **`weightx`, `weighty`** тАФ extra-space distribution factors

**Role of `weightx` and `weighty`:**
These are floating-point values in the range [0.0, 1.0] that control how **surplus space** is distributed among components when the container is enlarged beyond the sum of preferred sizes. If all `weightx = 0`, the components are clustered together (default), and extra horizontal space appears as a gap on the right. If `weightx = 1.0` for one component and 0.0 for others, that component absorbs **all** the extra horizontal space.

*Example:*

```java
JPanel panel = new JPanel(new GridBagLayout());
GridBagConstraints gbc = new GridBagConstraints();

gbc.gridx = 0; gbc.gridy = 0;
gbc.weightx = 1.0; gbc.weighty = 1.0;
gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.BOTH;
panel.add(new JButton("Full"), gbc);

gbc.gridx = 1; gbc.gridy = 0;
gbc.weightx = 0.0; gbc.weighty = 0.0;
gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.NONE;
panel.add(new JButton("Fixed"), gbc);
```

The first button stretches to fill all available space; the second button remains at its preferred size.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `revalidate()`:** After dynamically adding/removing components at runtime, students forget to call `panel.revalidate()` and `panel.repaint()`. The GUI appears broken. Loss: 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing LayoutManager and LayoutManager2:** Using `add(component)` (no constraint) with `BorderLayout` defaults to `CENTER`, overwriting previous components. Students mistakenly think the layout is buggy. Loss: 1тАУ2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing `setPreferredSize` with `setSize`:** On a `JPanel` with a layout manager, `setSize()` is ignored. Students must use `setPreferredSize()` for the layout to honor the size hint. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **Threading Violation:** Creating GUI components in `main()` without `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` works for simple demos but fails in production code. Examiner will deduct 1 mark if the question explicitly mentions thread safety.
> 5. **Mis-spelled `BorderLayout.EAST`:** Java constants are case-sensitive. Typing `BorderLayout.east` causes a compile error. Loss: full mark for that sub-part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 4):**

- **Definition:** A Layout Manager arranges components in a container; it is an implementation of the `LayoutManager` (or `LayoutManager2`) interface.
- **Default Layouts:** `JFrame` тЖТ `BorderLayout`; `JPanel` тЖТ `FlowLayout`; `JDialog` тЖТ `BorderLayout`.
- **FlowLayout:** Linear, wraps, uses `hgap`/`vgap`, alignment `LEFT/CENTER/RIGHT`.
- **BorderLayout:** Five regions тАФ `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`. CENTER is the only one that stretches on both axes. Extra components in same region overwrite previous.
- **GridLayout:** Uniform $rows \times cols$ matrix; all cells equal size; components fill cell-by-cell in row-major order.
- **BoxLayout:** Single-axis (`X_AXIS` or `Y_AXIS`) linear stacking; supports `Glue`, `Strut`, and `RigidArea` invisible spacers.
- **GridBagLayout:** Most powerful; uses `GridBagConstraints` with `gridx`, `gridy`, `gridwidth`, `gridheight`, `fill`, `anchor`, `weightx`, `weighty`, `insets`, `ipadx`, `ipady`.
- **CardLayout:** Stack of panels; only one visible at a time; methods `show()`, `next()`, `previous()`, `first()`, `last()`.
- **GroupLayout:** Used by NetBeans GUI Builder; defines Horizontal and Vertical groups separately.
- **SpringLayout:** Spring-physics-based; rarely used manually.
- **Key Methods:** `setLayout(LayoutManager)`, `Container.validate()` / `revalidate()`, `Component.setPreferredSize(Dimension)`.
- **Golden Rules:** Never use `null` layout; always nest `JPanel`s; remember to call `revalidate()` after dynamic changes.
- **Exam Favorites:** Comparison of layouts (very common 7-mark question); complete program demonstrating one layout (common 7-mark question); explanation of GridBagConstraints (favorite 14-mark question).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exploring Swings - JFrame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Exploring Swings - JFrame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** `JFrame` is a top-level container class provided by the **`javax.swing`** package in Java. It is the **Swing equivalent of AWT's `Frame`** and is used to create a **standard window** (with title bar, border, minimize/maximize/close buttons) that acts as the **primary top-level container** for hosting Swing-based GUI components.

**Package:** `javax.swing.JFrame`

**Class Declaration:**
```java
public class JFrame extends Frame implements WindowConstants, Accessible, RootPaneContainer
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy Insight (KTU High-Yield):**
> `java.lang.Object` тЖТ `java.awt.Component` тЖТ `java.awt.Container` тЖТ `java.awt.Window` тЖТ `java.awt.Frame` тЖТ **`javax.swing.JFrame`**
>
> Notice that `JFrame` is a **direct child of AWT's `Frame`**, which is why it is *lightweight* on AWT but *heavyweight* on Swing components (it is one of the few **heavyweight** Swing classes because it relies on the underlying OS window).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **`JFrame`** as the **wooden frame of a house window**:

- The **outer wooden frame** = the `JFrame` (the visible window on your desktop).
- The **glass pane** inside the frame = the **Content Pane** (where you place buttons, text fields, labels).
- The **locks/handles on the frame** = the title bar buttons (minimize, maximize, close).
- The **room inside** = your Java application logic.

Just as you cannot put furniture *inside* the wooden frame itself but on the **glass pane area**, you cannot directly add components to a `JFrame`; you must add them to its **Content Pane** (or use helper methods that do it for you).

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric:** A default `JFrame` in Java has a size of **0├Ч0 pixels** until you explicitly call `setSize()` or `pack()`. Its initial location is the **top-left corner of the screen (0,0)** unless `setLocation()` is invoked.

## 1.3 Why JFrame Instead of AWT Frame?

| Feature | AWT `Frame` | Swing `JFrame` |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Pluggable Look & Feel | No | **Yes** |
| Lightweight Components | No | **Yes (most Swing components)** |
| Root Pane Support | No | **Yes (uses `JRootPane`)** |
| Default Close Operation | Manual | **`WindowConstants`** enum support |
| Double Buffering | Manual | **Built-in** |
| Mnemonic / Keyboard Support | Limited | **Full** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Whenever a question says "create a window in Java using Swing", the **correct answer is `JFrame`**, NOT `Frame` from AWT.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of JFrame

A `JFrame` is composed of **four layered panes** stacked from back to front:

1. **Root Pane** (`JRootPane`) тАУ The master container that manages all other panes.
2. **Layered Pane** (`JLayeredPane`) тАУ Holds the menu bar and content pane at different layers (e.g., `DEFAULT_LAYER`, `PALETTE_LAYER`, `MODAL_LAYER`).
3. **Glass Pane** тАУ A transparent pane on top of everything, used for intercepting mouse events or drawing over the entire frame (invisible by default).
4. **Content Pane** тАУ The pane where you actually add your visible components (buttons, labels, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** When you call `frame.add(component)`, Java internally routes it to the **Content Pane** thanks to the overridden `add()` method in `JFrame`. So you do *not* need to call `getContentPane().add()` explicitly in modern code, but it is **good practice** for KTU exams.

## 2.2 Key Constructors of JFrame

| Constructor | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `JFrame()` | Creates a frame with **no title** and not visible. |
| `JFrame(String title)` | Creates a frame with the specified **title bar text**. |
| `JFrame(GraphicsConfiguration gc)` | Creates a frame with a specific **graphics config** (multi-monitor support). |
| `JFrame(String title, GraphicsConfiguration gc)` | Combines both above. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Method Cheat Sheet

| Method | Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `setTitle(String)` | `void` | Sets the title bar text. |
| `setSize(int w, int h)` | `void` | Sets the **frame size in pixels**. |
| `setLocation(int x, int y)` | `void` | Sets the **top-left position** on screen. |
| `setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` | `void` | Combines `setLocation` + `setSize`. |
| `setDefaultCloseOperation(int)` | `void` | Defines behaviour on close click. |
| `setVisible(boolean)` | `void` | **Makes the frame visible** (`true`) or invisible (`false`). |
| `setLayout(LayoutManager)` | `void` | Sets the **layout manager** of the content pane. |
| `getContentPane()` | `Container` | Returns the content pane object. |
| `add(Component)` | `Component` | Adds a component (auto-routed to content pane). |
| `setResizable(boolean)` | `void` | Allows/blocks window resizing. |
| `setIconImage(Image)` | `void` | Sets the **taskbar/title icon**. |
| `pack()` | `void` | Sizes the frame based on **preferred sizes** of components. |
| `setJMenuBar(JMenuBar)` | `void` | Attaches a menu bar. |

## 2.4 `setDefaultCloseOperation` Constants

These are the **four integer constants** from the `WindowConstants` interface:

| Constant | Value | Behaviour on Close |
|---|---|---|
| `DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` | `0` | Ignores the close click. |
| `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` | `1` | (Default for `JFrame`) Hides the window. |
| `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` | `2` | Releases resources, closes window. |
| `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` | `3` | **Terminates the entire JVM** via `System.exit(0)`. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write `frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`. While this compiles, the **preferred KTU-board-safe** form is `frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(WindowConstants.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` or simply `3`. Always import `javax.swing.WindowConstants` for clarity.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

`JFrame` is the **foundation of every desktop Java application** тАФ from simple calculators to enterprise tools like **IntelliJ IDEA**, **Eclipse** (SWT aside), and **NetBeans**. It is also the entry point for Java-based **GUI simulation software** in engineering, e.g., traffic control UIs, circuit simulators, and SCADA dashboards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Code Walkthroughs

## 3.1 The Minimal JFrame Program тАФ Line-by-Line Analysis

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.WindowConstants;

public class FirstSwingFrame {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // STEP 1: Instantiate the JFrame
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("My First Swing Window");

        // STEP 2: Set size (width, height) in pixels
        frame.setSize(400, 300);

        // STEP 3: Define what happens on clicking the 'X' button
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(WindowConstants.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // STEP 4: Make the frame visible on screen
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logic

**STEP 1 тАФ Instantiation**
The `new` operator allocates memory in the **heap**. A `JFrame` object is created with a title `"My First Swing Window"`. Internally, `JRootPane`, `JLayeredPane`, `GlassPane`, and `ContentPane` are all created and wired together.

**STEP 2 тАФ Setting Size**
`setSize(400, 300)` sets the frame's dimensions to **400 pixels wide ├Ч 300 pixels tall**. The size includes the **title bar and borders**.

**STEP 3 тАФ Default Close Operation**
Setting it to `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` ensures that clicking the close (├Ч) button terminates the entire Java application via `System.exit(0)`. Without this line, the default behaviour is `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` and the JVM keeps running invisibly.

**STEP 4 тАФ Visibility**
A `JFrame` is **invisible by default** (unlike AWT `Frame`). Calling `setVisible(true)` invokes the underlying native OS to display the window. The `paint()` method is triggered automatically.

## 3.2 Adding Components тАФ Derivation of the Content Pane Flow

```java
import javax.swing.*;

public class FrameWithButton {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("Frame with a Button");
        JButton button = new JButton("Click Me!");

        // Method 1 (Modern, Recommended)
        frame.add(button);

        // Method 2 (Explicit, KTU-Exam Preferred)
        // frame.getContentPane().add(button);

        frame.setSize(300, 200);
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

### Conversion Logic Explained

When you call `frame.add(button)`, the JVM looks up the **`add()` method in `JFrame`**. Since `JFrame` overrides `add()`, it **delegates the call internally** to the content pane:

$$\text{frame.add}(C) \;\equiv\; \text{frame.getContentPane().add}(C)$$

Mathematically expressed:

$$\forall C \in \text{Component}: \quad \text{add}_{\text{JFrame}}(C) \;\mapsto\; \text{add}_{\text{ContentPane}}(C)$$

This delegation exists because `JFrame` is essentially a `RootPaneContainer`, and all visible components **must** reside inside the content pane (not on the layered/glass panes) for standard rendering.

## 3.3 The `pack()` vs `setSize()` Decision Rule

| Scenario | Use |
|---|---|
| Fixed window size needed (e.g., splash screen) | `setSize(w, h)` |
| Window should size itself to fit components automatically | `pack()` |
| Window with dynamic content where size should adapt | `pack()` then optionally `setSize` |

### Worked Example Using `pack()`

```java
import javax.swing.*;

public class PackDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("Pack Demo");
        frame.add(new JButton("Small"));
        frame.add(new JLabel("A label"));

        // pack() sizes the frame to fit the preferred size of its components
        frame.pack();
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        frame.setLocationRelativeTo(null);   // Centers on screen
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **`setLocationRelativeTo(null)`** is a KTU-favourite one-liner that **centers the frame on the screen**. The argument `null` refers to the *default screen* rather than another component.

## 3.4 Mathematical Justification of the Layered Coordinate System

Every Swing window uses a **top-left origin (0,0)** coordinate system where:

$$X_{\text{pixel}} = X_{\text{window-left}} + \Delta x, \quad Y_{\text{pixel}} = Y_{\text{window-top}} + \Delta y$$

with **Y-axis pointing downward** (unlike mathematical convention). When you set `setLocation(100, 50)`, the window's top-left corner is placed **100 pixels right** and **50 pixels down** from the screen's top-left origin $(0_{\text{screen}}, 0_{\text{screen}})$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
