# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between `java.awt.Frame` and `javax.swing.JFrame`. Mention any three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | `java.awt.Frame` | `javax.swing.JFrame` |
|---|---|---|
| **Package** | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| **Pluggable Look & Feel** | Not supported | Supported |
| **Default Close Operation** | Not available (manual handling) | Built-in via `WindowConstants` |
| **Lightweight Components** | No (Heavyweight only) | Yes (supports lightweight Swing components) |
| **Root Pane** | Absent | Present (uses `JRootPane`) |
| **Double Buffering** | Manual | Automatic |

**Conclusion:** `JFrame` is the **modern, preferred** top-level container in Swing due to its pluggable L&F, built-in root pane, and richer component support. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the role of the `Content Pane` in a `JFrame`? Why can't we add components directly to the `JFrame`?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Content Pane** is a `Container` (typically of type `JPanel`) that holds all the **visible GUI components** (buttons, labels, text fields) of a `JFrame`. It is one of the four layered panes inside the `JRootPane`.

Components cannot be added **directly** to the `JFrame` because the `JFrame` is, by design, a **top-level container with a layered architecture** (`JRootPane` тЖТ `JLayeredPane` тЖТ `ContentPane`/`GlassPane`). Adding a component to the `JFrame` would place it on the wrong layer, leading to **rendering anomalies**.

Hence, the `add()` method of `JFrame` is **overridden** to delegate calls automatically to the `ContentPane` (or you may explicitly call `getContentPane().add()`). **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam тАУ Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the class hierarchy of `JFrame` in Java Swing. List at least four important methods of the `JFrame` class with their purpose. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program to create a `JFrame` titled **"Student Registration"** of size **500├Ч400** pixels, containing a `JLabel` displaying **"Enter Name:"** and a `JTextField`. The window should terminate the application when closed. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Class Hierarchy:**

`java.lang.Object` тЖТ `java.awt.Component` тЖТ `java.awt.Container` тЖТ `java.awt.Window` тЖТ `java.awt.Frame` тЖТ **`javax.swing.JFrame`**

This shows that `JFrame` inherits all the features of AWT's `Frame` (window management) but extends it with **Swing-specific capabilities** like the root pane, pluggable look-and-feel, and double buffering.

**Four Important Methods:**

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `setSize(int w, int h)` | Sets the dimensions of the frame in pixels. |
| `setTitle(String s)` | Sets the text shown in the title bar. |
| `setDefaultCloseOperation(int op)` | Defines the close-button behaviour. |
| `setVisible(boolean b)` | Shows/hides the frame on screen. |

(Additional valid methods: `setLayout()`, `add()`, `getContentPane()`, `pack()`, `setLocationRelativeTo()`.) **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (b) (7 Marks)

```java
import javax.swing.*;

public class StudentRegistration {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Create the frame
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("Student Registration");

        // Step 2: Create components
        JLabel label = new JLabel("Enter Name:");
        JTextField textField = new JTextField(15);

        // Step 3: Set layout and add components
        frame.setLayout(new java.awt.FlowLayout());
        frame.add(label);
        frame.add(textField);

        // Step 4: Configure the frame
        frame.setSize(500, 400);
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Steps:**

1. **Frame Creation:** A `JFrame` is instantiated with the title `"Student Registration"`.
2. **Component Creation:** A `JLabel` (text display) and `JTextField` (user input) are created.
3. **Layout & Addition:** A `FlowLayout` is set (components arranged left-to-right). Both components are added to the content pane.
4. **Configuration:** Size is set to 500├Ч400, the close button is set to terminate the JVM, and the frame is made visible. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam тАУ Model Paper 2024 тАФ Alternative Choice]

**Q: (a)** What is a `JFrame`? Explain the significance of the following methods: `setSize()`, `setDefaultCloseOperation()`, `setLayout()`, `pack()`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program using `JFrame` that displays a window titled **"Calculator UI"** with a `JTextField` at the top and a **4├Ч4 grid of `JButton`s** (digits 0тАУ9 and operators +, тИТ, *, /, =, C). Use `GridLayout` and `pack()` instead of `setSize()`. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Definition:** `JFrame` is a top-level container from the `javax.swing` package, used to create the main application window in Swing-based GUI programs. It extends AWT's `Frame` and adds Swing features like the root pane, pluggable look-and-feel, and lightweight component support.

**Significance of Methods:**

- **`setSize(int w, int h)`** тАУ Explicitly sets the frame's width and height in pixels. Useful when the size is predetermined.
- **`setDefaultCloseOperation(int op)`** тАУ Determines the response when the user clicks the **X (close) button**. Common value: `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` (terminates JVM).
- **`setLayout(LayoutManager m)`** тАУ Assigns a layout manager to the content pane (e.g., `BorderLayout`, `FlowLayout`, `GridLayout`). Defines how components are arranged.
- **`pack()`** тАУ Auto-sizes the frame based on the **preferred sizes** of all contained components. Highly recommended for responsive UI design. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (b) (7 Marks)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.GridLayout;

public class CalculatorUI {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("Calculator UI");

        // Top display
        JTextField display = new JTextField();
        frame.add(display);

        // 4x4 grid of buttons
        JPanel panel = new JPanel();
        panel.setLayout(new GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5));

        String[] labels = {
            "7", "8", "9", "/",
            "4", "5", "6", "*",
            "1", "2", "3", "-",
            "0", "C", "=", "+"
        };

        for (String lbl : labels) {
            panel.add(new JButton(lbl));
        }

        frame.add(panel);

        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        frame.pack();                                  // Auto-size
        frame.setLocationRelativeTo(null);             // Center on screen
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**

- The `JTextField` is added first (default `BorderLayout.CENTER`).
- A `JPanel` with `GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5)` holds the 16 buttons in a **4-row ├Ч 4-column grid** with 5px gaps.
- `pack()` resizes the window snugly around its components.
- `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` centers the window on screen. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** тАУ Frame remains invisible, leading to a "program runs but nothing shows" complaint. **тИТ2 marks**.
> 2. **Not importing `javax.swing.*`** тАУ Causes *compilation errors* on `JFrame`/`JButton`. **тИТ1 to тИТ3 marks**.
> 3. **Using `frame.setLayout(null)` for absolute positioning** тАУ Accepted, but components must have `setBounds()` for each. If you forget, the components overlap at (0,0). **тИТ2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** тАУ The window closes, but the JVM keeps running (only hidden), so a console test will appear "stuck". **тИТ1 mark**.
> 5. **Calling `pack()` AND `setSize()`** тАУ The last call wins. Don't do both; the examiner may deduct marks for inconsistent logic. **тИТ1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for JFrame:**

- ЁЯУж **Package:** `javax.swing` (class `JFrame`).
- ЁЯзм **Inherits from:** `java.awt.Frame` (heavyweight top-level container).
- ЁЯкЯ **Default Visibility:** `false` тАФ must call `setVisible(true)`.
- ЁЯУР **Default Size:** `0├Ч0` тАФ must call `setSize()` or `pack()`.
- ЁЯОп **Default Location:** `(0,0)` (top-left) тАФ use `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` to center.
- ЁЯз▒ **Components must go to Content Pane** (auto-routed via overridden `add()`).
- ЁЯПЫя╕П **Four Panes:** Root тЖТ Layered тЖТ (Glass + Content) тЖТ Menu Bar.
- тЭМ **Default Close Op:** `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (1). Use `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` (3) for full termination.
- ЁЯз░ **Essential Methods:** `setTitle`, `setSize`, `setBounds`, `setLayout`, `setLocation`, `setLocationRelativeTo`, `setVisible`, `setDefaultCloseOperation`, `pack`, `setResizable`, `setJMenuBar`, `getContentPane`, `add`.
- ЁЯзк **Best Practice:** Use **`pack()` + `setLocationRelativeTo(null)`** for cross-platform responsive windows.
- тЪая╕П **Heavyweight Alert:** `JFrame` is one of the few *non-lightweight* Swing classes тАФ mixing it with lightweight Swing components in mixed-event scenarios may need `getGlassPane()` trickery.
- ЁЯЖЪ **AWT vs Swing Frame:** Always prefer `JFrame` in modern Swing applications.
- ЁЯФБ **Event Loop:** `setVisible(true)` triggers `addNotify()` тЖТ peer creation тЖТ `paint()` chain.
- ЁЯза **Memorize Constants:** `EXIT_ON_CLOSE = 3`, `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE = 2`, `HIDE_ON_CLOSE = 1`, `DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE = 0`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: JLabel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** JLabel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### ЁЯУШ Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Java Swing API (under the package `javax.swing`), **`JLabel`** is a fundamental **passive (lightweight) GUI component** designed to display a **single line of read-only text**, an **image (icon)**, or **both simultaneously**. It extends the class `JComponent` and implements the interfaces `SwingConstants` and `Accessible`. Because a `JLabel` cannot receive keyboard focus by default, it is primarily used as a **descriptive label** for other interactive components (such as `JTextField`, `JButton`, etc.) through the use of mnemonics, accessibility labels, and the `setLabelFor()` mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** As per the revised syllabus under **CO4 (Develop GUI-based applications using Java Swing and event handling)**, `JLabel` is grouped under **"Display Components"** and is a prerequisite for understanding advanced layout managers and `JTextField` design patterns.

### ЁЯза Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a `JLabel` as a **name plate or a caption sticker** that you stick next to an input field on a paper form.

Imagine you have a physical form with blank boxes. Without labels like *"Name:"*, *"Age:"*, or *"Address:"*, the user wouldn't know what to write. The label does **not** collect any data itself тАФ it just **describes** what another component does.

- The **text box** = `JTextField` (interactive)
- The **text next to the box** = `JLabel` (descriptive, non-interactive)

Just as a name plate can also have a small icon (like a hospital cross next to a medical form field), a `JLabel` can show an **icon AND text together**, with full control over their relative positions.

### ЁЯП╖я╕П Key Standard Metrics & Properties

| Property | Default Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Text Alignment** | `LEADING` | Position of content inside the label's bounding box |
| **Vertical Alignment** | `CENTER` | Vertically centers content |
| **Text Position (relative to icon)** | `TRAILING` | Icon-text layout direction |
| **Icon-Text Gap** | **4 pixels** | Default spacing between icon and text |
| **Focusability** | `false` (by default) | Cannot receive keyboard focus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why default gap is 4 pixels?** This conforms to the **Java Look and Feel (L&F) Design Guidelines** for optical balance, ensuring that icons and text are not visually crammed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### ЁЯФН Class Hierarchy Breakdown

The inheritance path of `JLabel` in `javax.swing` is:

$$\text{java.lang.Object} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Component} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Container} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JComponent} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JLabel}$$

This is significant because `JLabel` inherits capabilities from:
- **`Component`**: Basic rendering, positioning (`setLocation`, `setSize`).
- **`Container`**: Can technically contain other components (though rare in practice).
- **`JComponent`**: Swing-specific features like tooltips (`setToolTipText()`), borders (`setBorder()`), and pluggable look-and-feel.

### ЁЯзй Core Constructors (KTU Frequently Asked)

1. `JLabel()` тАФ Creates an empty label with no text and no icon.
2. `JLabel(String text)` тАФ Creates a label with the given text string.
3. `JLabel(Icon image)` тАФ Creates a label with the given image icon.
4. `JLabel(String text, Icon icon, int horizontalAlignment)` тАФ Creates a label with both, where `horizontalAlignment` can be `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`, `LEADING`, or `TRAILING`.

### ЁЯУЛ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Method Signature | Return Type | Purpose / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| `setText(String text)` | `void` | Updates the label's display text dynamically |
| `getText()` | `String` | Retrieves current text (used in event handling) |
| `setIcon(Icon icon)` | `void` | Assigns an image (ImageIcon) to the label |
| `getIcon()` | `Icon` | Returns the current icon |
| `setHorizontalAlignment(int)` | `void` | Aligns content horizontally inside the label bounds |
| `setVerticalAlignment(int)` | `void` | Aligns content vertically |
| `setHorizontalTextPosition(int)` | `void` | Positions text **relative to the icon** (left/right of it) |
| `setVerticalTextPosition(int)` | `void` | Positions text above/below the icon |
| `setIconTextGap(int)` | `void` | Sets pixel gap between icon and text (default 4) |
| `setLabelFor(Component c)` | `void` | Associates label with another component (mnemonic target) |
| `setDisplayedMnemonic(char)` | `void` | Sets the keyboard shortcut (e.g., `'N'` for Name) |
| `setDisabledIcon(Icon)` | `void` | Icon shown when label is disabled |

### ЁЯМН Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry-grade Java applications, `JLabel` is used in:
- **Banking software** for form field captions (Account Number, IFSC, etc.).
- **Medical record systems** for diagnostic labels next to vital sign inputs.
- **IDE tools** (e.g., NetBeans, IntelliJ's Swing UI Designer output) for static descriptive text.
- **SCADA dashboards** for sensor readings with associated icons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Note:** In MVC (Model-View-Controller) pattern, a `JLabel` belongs to the **View** layer. It receives updates from the **Model** through a Controller when state changes (e.g., `label.setText("Status: Connected")`).

### ЁЯза The "Why" Behind Each Critical Method

- **Why `setLabelFor()`?** This makes the label **clickable** in such a way that clicking it transfers focus to the target component (e.g., the text field). It is essential for **accessibility (screen readers)** and **mnemonic-based navigation** (pressing `Alt + N` jumps focus to the Name field).
- **Why two alignment sets (`setHorizontalAlignment` vs. `setHorizontalTextPosition`)?**
  - The **first** positions the entire content block (icon+text combined) **inside the label's area**.
  - The **second** positions the text **relative to the icon within that combined block**.
  
  This dual-control is what gives Swing its fine-grained layout power.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### ЁЯЫая╕П Analytical Walkthrough: Building a Labeled Form

Let's derive the complete code logic for a typical **"Login Form"** with two labeled fields, following step-by-step engineering reasoning.

#### **Step 1: Import the Required Packages**

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JLabel;
import javax.swing.JTextField;
import javax.swing.ImageIcon;
import javax.swing.SwingConstants;
import java.awt.FlowLayout;
```

**Logic Explanation:** `javax.swing.*` contains all Swing components. `SwingConstants` is needed because `JLabel` implements this interface to inherit alignment constants like `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`, `LEADING`, `TRAILING`.

#### **Step 2: Define the Class and Main Method**

```java
public class LoginFormDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 3 onwards goes here
    }
}
```

**Logic Explanation:** The `main` method serves as the **entry point** of the Swing application. In real-world engineering, GUI launches are often delegated to `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` for thread safety, but for academic purposes, direct instantiation is acceptable.

#### **Step 3: Create the JFrame Container**

```java
JFrame frame = new JFrame("Login - KTU Demo");
frame.setSize(400, 200);
frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
frame.setLayout(new FlowLayout());
```

**Logic Explanation:** The `JFrame` is the top-level window. `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` ensures the JVM terminates on window close (important in KTU lab exams). `FlowLayout` arranges components left-to-right.

#### **Step 4: Create and Configure a JLabel for "Username"**

```java
JLabel userLabel = new JLabel("Username:");
userLabel.setDisplayedMnemonic('U');          // Alt+U activates the field
userLabel.setHorizontalAlignment(SwingConstants.LEFT);
```

**Logic Explanation:**
- `setDisplayedMnemonic('U')` тАФ The letter 'U' will be **underlined** in the displayed text (`Us<u>e</u>rname:` тЖТ actually `U<sername</u>:` based on first occurrence of 'U').
- This sets up **keyboard accessibility** for the form.

#### **Step 5: Create the Target JTextField and Link the Label**

```java
JTextField userField = new JTextField(15);
userLabel.setLabelFor(userField);   // Critical: Links label -> text field
```

**Logic Explanation:** `setLabelFor(userField)` establishes the **mnemonic target**. Now, pressing `Alt+U` will move keyboard focus to `userField`. Without this call, the mnemonic has no effect.

#### **Step 6: Create a Second JLabel with an Icon (for Password)**

```java
ImageIcon lockIcon = new ImageIcon("lock.png");
JLabel passLabel = new JLabel("Password:", lockIcon, SwingConstants.LEFT);
passLabel.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.RIGHT);  // Text to right of icon
passLabel.setIconTextGap(10);                              // 10-pixel spacing
```

**Logic Explanation:**
- The 3-argument constructor `JLabel(String, Icon, int)` sets text, icon, and horizontal alignment in a single line (compact, KTU-friendly).
- `RIGHT` here means text appears to the **right of the icon** (note: this is the **text position** constant, not the alignment constant).
- `setIconTextGap(10)` overrides the default 4-pixel gap.

#### **Step 7: Assemble All Components and Display**

```java
JTextField passField = new JTextField(15);

frame.add(userLabel);
frame.add(userField);
frame.add(passLabel);
frame.add(passField);
frame.setVisible(true);
```

**Logic Explanation:** Components are added in order. `setVisible(true)` triggers Swing's painting pipeline (a combination of AWT's heavyweight peer system and Swing's lightweight rendering).

### ЁЯУК Analytical Matrix: Alignment Constants Reference

| Constant Name | Value | Direction | Used In Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| `SwingConstants.LEFT` | 2 | West | `setHorizontalAlignment` & `setHorizontalTextPosition` |
| `SwingConstants.CENTER` | 0 | Center | Both alignment and text position |
| `SwingConstants.RIGHT` | 4 | East | Both |
| `SwingConstants.LEADING` | 10 | Start of text flow (LTR = LEFT) | `setHorizontalAlignment` only |
| `SwingConstants.TRAILING` | 11 | End of text flow (LTR = RIGHT) | `setHorizontalAlignment` only |
| `SwingConstants.TOP` | 1 | North | `setVerticalAlignment` & `setVerticalTextPosition` |
| `SwingConstants.BOTTOM` | 3 | South | Both vertical methods |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Use `LEADING` and `TRAILING` instead of `LEFT`/`RIGHT` for **internationalization (i18n)** support тАФ they automatically flip for Right-to-Left languages like Arabic.

### ЁЯФв Complete Compilable Program

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class JLabelCompleteDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("KTU JLabel Demo");
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        frame.setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.LEFT, 10, 15));

        JLabel lbl1 = new JLabel("Name:");
        lbl1.setDisplayedMnemonic('N');
        JTextField tf1 = new JTextField(12);
        lbl1.setLabelFor(tf1);

        ImageIcon icon = new ImageIcon("user.png");
        JLabel lbl2 = new JLabel("Email:", icon, SwingConstants.LEFT);
        lbl2.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.RIGHT);
        lbl2.setIconTextGap(8);
        JTextField tf2 = new JTextField(12);
        lbl2.setLabelFor(tf2);

        JLabel status = new JLabel("Status: Ready", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        status.setVerticalAlignment(SwingConstants.BOTTOM);

        frame.add(lbl1); frame.add(tf1);
        frame.add(lbl2); frame.add(tf2);
        frame.add(status);
        frame.setSize(350, 200);
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### ЁЯУК Diagram 1: JLabel Class Inheritance & Interface Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["java lang Object"] --> B["java awt Component"]
    B --> C["java awt Container"]
    C --> D["javax swing JComponent"]
    D --> E["javax swing JLabel"]
    F["Interface SwingConstants"] -.-> E
    G["Interface Accessible"] -.-> E
    H["Interface Serializable"] -.-> B
    I["Interface ImageObserver"] -.-> B
    J["Interface MenuContainer"] -.-> C
```

### ЁЯФД Diagram 2: JLabel Functional Block Architecture (Label-Field Association Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["USER INTERACTION LAYER"]
        A1["User clicks JLabel text"]
        A2["User presses Alt plus Mnemonic key"]
        A3["Screen reader reads label"]
    end

    subgraph LOGIC["JLABEL INTERNAL ROUTING"]
        B1["setLabelFor method stores target Component reference"]
        B2["setDisplayedMnemonic stores char keycode"]
        B3["Accessible Context exposes name and description"]
    end

    subgraph TARGET["ASSOCIATED COMPONENT"]
        C1["JTextField receives focus"]
        C2["JButton triggers action"]
        C3["Screen reader announces label then component"]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> C1
    A2 --> B2 --> C1
    A3 --> B3 --> C3
```

### ЁЯзн Diagram 3: Icon + Text Positioning Matrix (9 Possible Layouts)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["JLabel Content Block"] --> Q{"setHorizontalTextPosition"}
    Q -->|LEFT| R1["Text LEFT of Icon"]
    Q -->|CENTER| R2["Text OVERLAPS Icon at CENTER"]
    Q -->|RIGHT| R3["Text RIGHT of Icon"]
    P --> S{"setVerticalTextPosition"}
    S -->|TOP| T1["Text ABOVE Icon"]
    S -->|CENTER| T2["Text OVERLAPS Icon vertically at CENTER"]
    S -->|BOTTOM| T3["Text BELOW Icon"]
    R1 --> U["Final Visual: e.g. Email ICON"]
    R2 --> V["Final Visual: e.g. E-m-a-i-l ICON"]
    R3 --> W["Final Visual: e.g. ICON Email"]
    T1 --> X["Final Visual: Text on top of ICON"]
    T2 --> Y["Final Visual: Text centered with ICON"]
    T3 --> Z["Final Visual: ICON with Text below"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** These Mermaid schematics map the **static class relationships**, **runtime event flow**, and **visual layout options** of `JLabel`. The dotted lines (`-.->`) represent interface implementation, while solid arrows represent class inheritance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### ЁЯУЭ Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024, Model]**  
*Explain the purpose of the `JLabel` class in Java Swing. Why is it called a "passive" component?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

`JLabel` is a Swing component (defined in `javax.swing`) used to display a **short text string, an image, or both**. It extends `JComponent` and implements `SwingConstants`. It is called a **"passive" component** because it **cannot receive keyboard focus** by default and does not generate any user interaction events (like `ActionEvent`). Its primary role is to **describe or label** other interactive components (such as `JTextField`) through the `setLabelFor()` method and mnemonics. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for extending JComponent, 1 mark for passive nature with example)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Model]**  
*List any three constructors of `JLabel` class and write the syntax of any one method to associate a label with a text field.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Three constructors of `JLabel`:
1. `JLabel()` тАФ *(1 mark)*
2. `JLabel(String text)` тАФ *(1 mark)*
3. `JLabel(String text, Icon icon, int horizontalAlignment)` тАФ *(1 mark)*

Association syntax: `labelObj.setLabelFor(textFieldObj);` тАФ *(Bonus point for setting mnemonic: `labelObj.setDisplayedMnemonic('N');`)*

---

### ЁЯУЭ Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each тАУ Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** тАФ *Concept + Code-based*

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the class hierarchy of `JLabel`. Explain the difference between `setHorizontalAlignment()` and `setHorizontalTextPosition()` with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program using `JLabel` and `JTextField` to create a **Student Registration Form** with fields: Name, Roll Number, and Email. Each label should have a **displayed mnemonic** and must be **linked to its corresponding text field**. The form should also display a status label at the bottom showing "Registration Open". *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Class Hierarchy and Alignment Difference (7 marks):**

```
java.lang.Object
   тЖТ java.awt.Component
      тЖТ java.awt.Container
         тЖТ javax.swing.JComponent
            тЖТ javax.swing.JLabel
```
*(2 marks for hierarchy)*

**Difference between the two methods:**

| Aspect | `setHorizontalAlignment(int)` | `setHorizontalTextPosition(int)` |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Positions the **entire content block** within the label's bounding area | Positions the **text relative to the icon** inside the content block |
| Default value | `LEADING` | `TRAILING` |
| Example | `label.setHorizontalAlignment(CENTER)` тЖТ centers icon+text together inside the label | `label.setHorizontalTextPosition(LEFT)` тЖТ places text to the LEFT of the icon |
| Use case | Controls margin/positioning within layout cell | Controls icon-text visual relationship |

*(3 marks for comparison table with example)*

**Example Code Snippet:**
```java
JLabel lbl = new JLabel("Hello", new ImageIcon("hi.png"), SwingConstants.CENTER);
lbl.setHorizontalAlignment(SwingConstants.RIGHT);    // Moves whole block right
lbl.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.LEFT);  // Text appears left of icon
```
*(2 marks for the working example)*

**(b) Student Registration Form (7 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class StudentRegistration extends JFrame {
    public StudentRegistration() {
        setTitle("Student Registration");
        setSize(400, 250);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.LEFT, 10, 12));

        JLabel lblName = new JLabel("Name:");
        lblName.setDisplayedMnemonic('N');
        JTextField tfName = new JTextField(15);
        lblName.setLabelFor(tfName);

        JLabel lblRoll = new JLabel("Roll Number:");
        lblRoll.setDisplayedMnemonic('R');
        JTextField tfRoll = new JTextField(15);
        lblRoll.setLabelFor(tfRoll);

        JLabel lblEmail = new JLabel("Email:");
        lblEmail.setDisplayedMnemonic('E');
        JTextField tfEmail = new JTextField(15);
        lblEmail.setLabelFor(tfEmail);

        JLabel status = new JLabel("Status: Registration Open", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        status.setForeground(Color.BLUE);

        add(lblName);  add(tfName);
        add(lblRoll);  add(tfRoll);
        add(lblEmail); add(tfEmail);
        add(status);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new StudentRegistration();
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution for Part (b):**
- Correct imports and class declaration тАФ *1 mark*
- Three labels with mnemonics тАФ *2 marks (1 mark partial for each mnemonic set)*
- `setLabelFor()` linking тАФ *2 marks*
- Status label + JFrame setup + visibility тАФ *2 marks*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** тАФ *Alternative Choice (Application-based)*

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** What is the role of `setDisplayedMnemonic()` and `setLabelFor()` methods? How do they work together to enhance **GUI accessibility**? Write a code snippet demonstrating both. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a Swing application that displays a **"Search Engine"** interface with:
   - A `JLabel` titled "Search Query:" with mnemonic 'S'
   - A `JTextField` linked to the above label
   - A `JLabel` displaying a magnifying glass `ImageIcon` to the LEFT of the text "Search Here"
   - A status `JLabel` at the bottom showing horizontal center alignment
   - All components should be in a single `JFrame` with `FlowLayout`. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Mnemonic and LabelFor Methods (7 marks):**

- **`setDisplayedMnemonic(char mnemonic)`**: This method assigns a **keyboard shortcut** to the label. The character passed is **underlined** in the label's text, indicating to the user that pressing `Alt + that character` will trigger an action. *(2 marks)*
- **`setLabelFor(Component c)`**: This method **associates** the label with another component (usually a `JTextField`, `JButton`, etc.). It tells the L&F and accessibility framework: *"I am the label for this component."* *(2 marks)*
- **Working Together**: When the user presses `Alt + <mnemonic>`, the **focus is automatically transferred** to the component registered via `setLabelFor()`. This is critical for **keyboard-only navigation** and **screen reader compatibility** (the screen reader reads the label's text when the focus reaches the target component). *(2 marks)*
- **Code Snippet** *(1 mark)*:
```java
JLabel lbl = new JLabel("Username:");
lbl.setDisplayedMnemonic('U');  // Alt+U
JTextField tf = new JTextField(10);
lbl.setLabelFor(tf);             // Focus jumps to tf
```

**(b) Search Engine Interface (7 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class SearchEngineUI extends JFrame {
    SearchEngineUI() {
        setTitle("Search Engine - KTU");
        setSize(420, 180);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 12, 15));

        // Search Query label with mnemonic
        JLabel queryLbl = new JLabel("Search Query:");
        queryLbl.setDisplayedMnemonic('S');
        JTextField queryField = new JTextField(18);
        queryLbl.setLabelFor(queryField);

        // Icon + Text label
        ImageIcon searchIcon = new ImageIcon("search.png");
        JLabel hintLbl = new JLabel("Search Here", searchIcon, SwingConstants.LEFT);
        hintLbl.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.RIGHT);
        hintLbl.setIconTextGap(8);

        // Status label
        JLabel status = new JLabel("Ready to search...", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        status.setForeground(new Color(0, 100, 0));

        add(queryLbl);
        add(queryField);
        add(hintLbl);
        add(status);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SearchEngineUI();
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution for Part (b):**
- Mnemonic setup + label-for linking тАФ *2 marks*
- ImageIcon with text positioning тАФ *2 marks*
- Status label with CENTER alignment тАФ *1 mark*
- JFrame, FlowLayout, main method тАФ *2 marks*

---

### тЪая╕П KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing `setHorizontalAlignment` with `setHorizontalTextPosition`**: Many students write `setHorizontalTextPosition(LEADING)` тАФ but `LEADING` is **only valid** for `setHorizontalAlignment`. This causes a **compilation error** and results in **0 marks** for that part.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting `setLabelFor()`**: Setting only the mnemonic without linking to a target component makes the mnemonic **non-functional**. Examiners specifically test this тАФ losing 2тАУ3 marks.
> 
> 3. **Using `ImageIcon` without quoting the path correctly**: Always use double quotes: `new ImageIcon("image.png")`. Using a `char` or variable name without quotes is a common slip.
> 
> 4. **Missing `setVisible(true)`**: The window will compile but not display. Examiners may deduct 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Importing `java.awt.Label` (AWT) instead of `javax.swing.JLabel`**: This is a **fatal import error** in Swing-based questions тАФ no partial credit is given for AWT components in Swing questions.

---

### ЁЯОп Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ЁЯФ╣ `JLabel` is a **passive display component** in `javax.swing` used for **read-only text, icons, or both**.
- ЁЯФ╣ It **extends `JComponent`** and **implements `SwingConstants` & `Accessible`**.
- ЁЯФ╣ It is **non-focusable by default** but can transfer focus via **`setLabelFor()`** + **`setDisplayedMnemonic()`**.
- ЁЯФ╣ Two alignment systems exist: **content alignment** (`setHorizontalAlignment`) and **text-to-icon position** (`setHorizontalTextPosition`).
- ЁЯФ╣ The **default icon-text gap is 4 pixels**, customizable via `setIconTextGap(int)`.
- ЁЯФ╣ Use **`LEADING`/`TRAILING`** for internationalization (RTL/LTR language support).
- ЁЯФ╣ The 4-argument constructor `JLabel(String, Icon, int)` is the most versatile and is **frequently tested in KTU exams**.
- ЁЯФ╣ For KTU lab exams, remember to include `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` and `setVisible(true)`.
- ЁЯФ╣ Accessibility tip: **Always pair labels with input fields using `setLabelFor()`** тАФ this is a **best practice** and a **mark-bearing expectation** in KTU 2024 evaluations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing Buttons
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Buttons

# ЁЯФШ Swing Buttons тАФ Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEERT416 тАФ Object Oriented Programming using Java)**, a **Swing Button** is defined as a concrete instance of the `javax.swing.AbstractButton` subclass hierarchy that provides a graphical push-button, toggle-state, or selection-state user interface component. The **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)** Swing component library, built on top of the older AWT (`java.awt`) framework, encapsulates these interactive controls as **lightweight, pluggable Look-And-Feel (LAF)** components governed by the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** design pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 тАФ GUI Programming)**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mandates the study of `JButton`, `JToggleButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `ButtonGroup`, and the associated `ActionListener` / `ItemListener` event-handling mechanisms. Mastery of constructors, mutator methods (e.g., `setText()`, `setIcon()`), and event registration via `addActionListener()` is **mandatory** for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### The Four Canonical Swing Button Variants

| S/No. | Class | Behaviour | State Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `JButton` | A standard push-button that "fires" once on click | Stateless (transient click event) |
| 2 | `JToggleButton` | A two-state (pressed/released) sticky button | `ButtonModel` (boolean `selected`) |
| 3 | `JCheckBox` | A toggle button paired with a label, often used in groups | `ButtonModel` (boolean `selected`) |
| 4 | `JRadioButton` | A mutually-exclusive toggle, requires `ButtonGroup` | `ToggleButtonModel` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Inheritance Note**
> All four classes inherit from `javax.swing.AbstractButton`, which itself extends `javax.swing.JComponent` тЖТ `java.awt.Container` тЖТ `java.awt.Component`. This means every Swing button inherently possesses painting, sizing, focus, and accessibility (keyboard navigation) capabilities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **television remote control** sitting on your coffee table. The remote is your *Container* (the physical casing). On it sit several different types of "buttons":

- **Power / Volume-Up buttons** тЖТ these are like `JButton`. You press them, they perform an action, and they return to their original state. They don't "stick."
- **Mute button** тЖТ this is like `JToggleButton`. Press it once, it stays pressed (red light on); press it again, it pops back out. The state is *sticky* and *toggles*.
- **Subtitle ON/OFF checkbox in YouTube settings** тЖТ this is like `JCheckBox`. You can independently turn subtitles ON, captions ON, and audio descriptions ON all at the same time. They are *non-mutually-exclusive*.
- **AM / FM / SW radio station selector** тЖТ this is like `JRadioButton`. You can listen to *only one* station at a time. Selecting FM automatically deselects AM. They are *mutually-exclusive*, and you group them using a `ButtonGroup` (think of it as the "selector dial" that enforces this exclusivity).

> [!TIP]
> **Student Memory Trick:** "Push = JButton, Stick = JToggle, Box = JCheck (multi-select), Radio = JRadio (single-select)." The words themselves hint at their behaviour!

## 1.3 The AbstractButton Superclass тАФ The Common Engine

The `javax.swing.AbstractButton` class is the **abstract backbone** that supplies all the shared behaviour. Every Swing button inherits the following capabilities *for free*:

1. **Textual Content** тАФ `setText(String)`, `getText()`
2. **Iconographic Content** тАФ `setIcon(Icon)`, `setPressedIcon(Icon)`, `setRolloverIcon(Icon)`, `setDisabledIcon(Icon)`
3. **Mnemonics (Keyboard Shortcuts)** тАФ `setMnemonic(int)` (e.g., `KeyEvent.VK_A` for **Alt + A**)
4. **Action Command** тАФ `setActionCommand(String)` (a logical name sent to listeners)
5. **Enabled/Disabled State** тАФ `setEnabled(boolean)`
6. **Selected State** тАФ `setSelected(boolean)` (for toggle-capable buttons)
7. **Horizontal/Vertical Alignment** тАФ `setHorizontalAlignment(int)`, `setVerticalAlignment(int)`
8. **Margin** тАФ `setMargin(Insets)`
9. **Border** тАФ `setBorder(Border)` (default: `BorderFactory.createEmptyBorder()`)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake**
> Students often try to call `setSelected(true)` on a plain `JButton`. This will compile (because `JButton` inherits the method), but it has **no visual effect** because `JButton` uses a `DefaultButtonModel` that does not maintain a selected state. The method is meaningful only for `JToggleButton`, `JCheckBox`, and `JRadioButton`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# тЪЩя╕П Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The MVC Architecture Under the Hood

Every Swing button strictly obeys the **Model-View-Controller** triad. This is not a stylistic choice тАФ it is enforced by the `AbstractButton` class itself.

```
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ                       AbstractButton (View + Controller)        тФВ
тФВ                                                                 тФВ
```

### 2.1.1 The ButtonModel Interface тАФ The Data Layer

The model stores four critical boolean state flags:

| State Flag | Meaning | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `isArmed()` | Mouse pressed over the button, not yet released | `false` |
| `isPressed()` | Mouse button currently down on the component | `false` |
| `isSelected()` | The button is in its "on" state (toggleable only) | `false` |
| `isEnabled()` | The button can receive input events | `true` |
| `isRollover()` | Mouse cursor is hovering over the button | `false` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The State Transition Lifecycle of a JButton**
> 1. **IDLE** тЖТ mouse enters тЖТ **ROLLOVER**
> 2. **ROLLOVER** тЖТ mouse pressed тЖТ **ARMED + PRESSED**
> 3. **ARMED** тЖТ mouse released inside button тЖТ **FIRES ActionEvent** тЖТ **IDLE**
> 4. **ARMED** тЖТ mouse dragged out and released тЖТ **IDLE** (no event fired)

## 2.2 Event Handling тАФ The Listener Interfaces

Swing buttons fire events through three standard `java.awt.event` listener interfaces:

| Listener Interface | Method to Implement | When It Fires | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | A "click" (press + release inside) | Execute commands, submit forms |
| `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` | A toggleable button's `selected` state changes | React to check-box / radio state |
| `ChangeListener` | `stateChanged(ChangeEvent e)` | Any model property change (armed, pressed, etc.) | Custom UI feedback, animations |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For `JButton`, use `ActionListener`. For `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton`, use `ItemListener` (or `ActionListener` тАФ both work, but `ItemListener` is semantically correct for state changes).

### 2.2.1 The ActionEvent Object тАФ What You Receive

When a listener method is invoked, the runtime passes an `ActionEvent` object containing:

- `e.getActionCommand()` тЖТ the String set by `setActionCommand()` (default = button text)
- `e.getSource()` тЖТ the originating `Object` (cast it to the button class)
- `e.getWhen()` тЖТ timestamp of the event
- `e.getModifiers()` тЖТ modifier keys held (Shift, Ctrl, Alt, Meta)

## 2.3 The ButtonGroup Class тАФ Enforcing Mutual Exclusivity

`javax.swing.ButtonGroup` is **not a visual container** (it does not derive from `java.awt.Container`). It is a *logical grouping mechanism* that ensures only one `AbstractButton` within it is selected at any time.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Mistake:** Adding buttons to a `ButtonGroup` does **NOT** add them to the GUI. You must add them to a real container (like a `JPanel`) AND to the `ButtonGroup` separately. The `ButtonGroup` only manages the state; the `JPanel` (or `JFrame`) handles the layout.

### 2.3.1 Canonical ButtonGroup Setup Pattern

```java
ButtonGroup bg = new ButtonGroup();
bg.add(radioBtn1);
bg.add(radioBtn2);
bg.add(radioBtn3);
// Note: No layout impact. Add buttons to JPanel separately.
```

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet тАФ Swing Button Reference Table

| Method Signature | Return Type | Applicable To | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `JButton()` | тАФ | `JButton` | Default constructor, no text or icon |
| `JButton(String text)` | тАФ | `JButton` | Constructor with label |
| `JButton(Icon icon)` | тАФ | `JButton` | Constructor with image |
| `JButton(String text, Icon icon)` | тАФ | `JButton` | Both text and icon |
| `setText(String text)` | `void` | All buttons | Change label |
| `getText()` | `String` | All buttons | Retrieve label |
| `setIcon(Icon icon)` | `void` | All buttons | Set main icon |
| `setPressedIcon(Icon icon)` | `void` | All buttons | Icon shown while pressed |
| `setRolloverIcon(Icon icon)` | `void` | All buttons | Icon shown on mouse hover |
| `setMnemonic(int keyCode)` | `void` | All buttons | Set Alt + key shortcut |
| `setActionCommand(String cmd)` | `void` | All buttons | Logical event identifier |
| `setEnabled(boolean enabled)` | `void` | All buttons | Grey-out if `false` |
| `setSelected(boolean selected)` | `void` | Toggle/Check/Radio | Force on/off state |
| `isSelected()` | `boolean` | Toggle/Check/Radio | Query state |
| `addActionListener(ActionListener l)` | `void` | All buttons | Register click handler |
| `addItemListener(ItemListener l)` | `void` | Toggle/Check/Radio | Register state-change handler |
| `setHorizontalAlignment(int align)` | `void` | All buttons | `SwingConstants.LEFT/CENTER/RIGHT` |
| `setMargin(Insets m)` | `void` | All buttons | Space between border & content |
| `ButtonGroup()` | тАФ | `ButtonGroup` | Default grouping object |
| `ButtonGroup.add(AbstractButton b)` | `void` | `ButtonGroup` | Add to exclusive group |
| `ButtonGroup.getSelection()` | `ButtonModel` | `ButtonGroup` | Get the currently selected model |
| `ButtonGroup.clearSelection()` | `void` | `ButtonGroup` | Deselect all in group |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Key Codes:** Use constants from `java.awt.event.KeyEvent`, e.g., `KeyEvent.VK_S` for the **S** key, `KeyEvent.VK_ENTER` for Enter, `KeyEvent.VK_F1` for F1.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Swing buttons are not merely academic curiosities. They are the **frontline of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** in:

- **Enterprise Java Applications:** Internal banking dashboards, CRM tools, ERP systems
- **Scientific Visualization:** Lab instrument control panels (e.g., JMRI, ImageJ)
- **Educational Software:** IDEs like NetBeans, BlueJ, jGRASP use Swing extensively
- **Standalone Utility Tools:** Apache JMeter, Burp Suite Community Edition
- **Legacy Industrial Systems:** SCADA frontends, medical imaging viewers

Even in 2026, the AWT/Swing skill-set remains in the **banking and government sectors** where Java's "write once, run anywhere" portability and Swing's zero-dependency footprint beat modern JavaFX for stability.

# ЁЯФм Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1 тАФ Building a Standard `JButton` with `ActionListener`

This is the most common KTU board question pattern. We will derive a working program from first principles.

### 3.1.1 The Problem Statement

*"Write a Java Swing program to create a window with a JButton labeled 'Click Me'. When the user clicks the button, display the number of times it has been clicked in a JLabel."*

### 3.1.2 The Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 тАФ Import the required packages.**

```java
import javax.swing.*;        // JFrame, JButton, JLabel, JPanel
import java.awt.*;           // BorderLayout, FlowLayout, Font
import java.awt.event.*;     // ActionListener, ActionEvent
```

*Reasoning:* `javax.swing.*` provides all the GUI components; `java.awt.*` provides the layout managers and base `Font` class; `java.awt.event.*` provides the event-handling interfaces.

**Step 2 тАФ Declare the class extending `JFrame` and implement `ActionListener`.**

```java
public class ClickCounter extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
```

*Reasoning:* Extending `JFrame` gives us a top-level window. Implementing `ActionListener` lets a single class handle its own button events, simplifying the code (instead of using a separate inner class or lambda).

**Step 3 тАФ Declare the instance variables for the components we need to reference later.**

```java
    private JButton clickButton;
    private JLabel countLabel;
    private int count = 0;   // Counter, starts at zero
```

*Reasoning:* The `count` must persist across multiple clicks. If declared inside `actionPerformed`, it would reset to 0 every click. Hence, it is an **instance variable** (a "field" of the class).

**Step 4 тАФ Write a constructor that builds the UI.**

```java
    public ClickCounter() {
        setTitle("Click Counter Application");
        setSize(400, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 20, 20));

        // --- Initialize the label ---
        countLabel = new JLabel("Clicks: 0");
        countLabel.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 18));
        add(countLabel);

        // --- Initialize the button ---
        clickButton = new JButton("Click Me");
        clickButton.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.PLAIN, 16));
        clickButton.setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_C);   // Alt + C shortcut
        clickButton.addActionListener(this);      // 'this' = current object
        add(clickButton);

        setLocationRelativeTo(null);  // Centre on screen
        setVisible(true);             // Make window appear
    }
```

*Reasoning:* The `FlowLayout` arranges components left-to-right. `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` ensures the program terminates when the window is closed (instead of just hiding). The `addActionListener(this)` line **registers** the current object as the event handler.

**Step 5 тАФ Implement the `actionPerformed` method (the event handler).**

```java
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        // Identify which button (we only have one, but it's good practice)
        if (e.getSource() == clickButton) {
            count++;
            countLabel.setText("Clicks: " + count);
        }
    }
```

*Reasoning:* Each click increments `count` and updates the JLabel's text using `setText()`. The `@Override` annotation signals to the compiler that we are fulfilling the contract of the `ActionListener` interface.

**Step 6 тАФ Add the `main` method to launch the program.**

```java
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new ClickCounter());
    }
}
```

*Reasoning:* `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` schedules the GUI construction on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**. This is the **thread-safe** way to create Swing components, preventing race conditions and visual glitches.

### 3.1.3 The Complete, Compilable Program

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ClickCounter extends JFrame implements ActionListener {

    private JButton clickButton;
    private JLabel countLabel;
    private int count = 0;

    public ClickCounter() {
        setTitle("Click Counter Application");
        setSize(400, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 20, 20));

        countLabel = new JLabel("Clicks: 0");
        countLabel.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 18));
        add(countLabel);

        clickButton = new JButton("Click Me");
        clickButton.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.PLAIN, 16));
        clickButton.setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_C);
        clickButton.addActionListener(this);
        add(clickButton);

        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == clickButton) {
            count++;
            countLabel.setText("Clicks: " + count);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new ClickCounter());
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2 тАФ `JCheckBox` and `ButtonGroup` with `JRadioButton`

### 3.2.1 The Problem Statement

*"Design a Swing form with three checkboxes (Java, Python, C++) representing skills known, and a `ButtonGroup` of three radio buttons (Fresher, Intermediate, Expert) representing experience level. Display the user's selections on clicking a Submit JButton."*

### 3.2.2 The Analytical Matrix тАФ How the Components Behave

| Component | Action | State After Action | Event Fired |
|---|---|---|---|
| `chkJava` clicked | Toggle | `isSelected()` flips | `ItemEvent` + `ActionEvent` |
| `radIntermediate` clicked in group | Select this, deselect siblings | Only one radio is `true` | `ItemEvent` + `ActionEvent` |
| `btnSubmit` clicked | No state change | Unchanged | `ActionEvent` only |

### 3.2.3 The Implementation

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SurveyForm extends JFrame implements ActionListener {

    private JCheckBox chkJava, chkPython, chkCpp;
    private JRadioButton radFresher, radIntermediate, radExpert;
    private JButton btnSubmit;
    private JTextArea resultArea;

    public SurveyForm() {
        setTitle("Skill Survey");
        setSize(450, 350);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.LEFT, 10, 10));

        // --- Skills checkboxes ---
        add(new JLabel("Skills Known:"));
        chkJava   = new JCheckBox("Java");
        chkPython = new JCheckBox("Python");
        chkCpp    = new JCheckBox("C++");
        add(chkJava);
        add(chkPython);
        add(chkCpp);

        // --- Experience radio buttons grouped ---
        add(new JLabel("Experience Level:"));
        radFresher      = new JRadioButton("Fresher");
        radIntermediate = new JRadioButton("Intermediate");
        radExpert       = new JRadioButton("Expert");
        radFresher.setSelected(true);  // default

        ButtonGroup expGroup = new ButtonGroup();
        expGroup.add(radFresher);
        expGroup.add(radIntermediate);
        expGroup.add(radExpert);
        add(radFresher);
        add(radIntermediate);
        add(radExpert);

        // --- Submit button ---
        btnSubmit = new JButton("Submit");
        btnSubmit.addActionListener(this);
        add(btnSubmit);

        // --- Result display ---
        resultArea = new JTextArea(5, 35);
        resultArea.setEditable(false);
        add(new JScrollPane(resultArea));

        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == btnSubmit) {
            StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Selected Skills: ");
            if (chkJava.isSelected())   sb.append("Java  ");
            if (chkPython.isSelected()) sb.append("Python  ");
            if (chkCpp.isSelected())    sb.append("C++  ");

            String level = "Fresher";
            if (radIntermediate.isSelected()) level = "Intermediate";
            else if (radExpert.isSelected()) level = "Expert";

            sb.append("\nExperience: ").append(level);
            resultArea.setText(sb.toString());
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new SurveyForm());
    }
}
```

### 3.2.4 The Key Insight тАФ Why `ButtonGroup` Does Not Display

`ButtonGroup` operates purely at the **model level**. It receives a `ChangeEvent` whenever one of its members changes state and forces all other members to deselect. However, the components are still positioned by the layout manager of the **parent container** (here, the `JFrame` with `FlowLayout`). If you forget the `add(radFresher)` etc. calls, the radio buttons will exist logically but will be invisible on screen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Track Registration Rule:** For every `JRadioButton`, you need **two** `add()` calls: one for the `ButtonGroup` (state coordination) and one for the container (visual display). Missing either one causes a silent failure.

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3 тАФ `JToggleButton` тАФ The Standalone Sticky Button

A `JToggleButton` is essentially a `JCheckBox` without the visual checkbox glyph. It maintains a `selected` state and can be queried via `isSelected()`.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ToggleDemo extends JFrame {
    public ToggleDemo() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setSize(300, 120);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        JToggleButton toggle = new JToggleButton("OFF");
        toggle.addItemListener(e -> {
            if (toggle.isSelected()) toggle.setText("ON");
            else                     toggle.setText("OFF");
        });

        add(toggle);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new ToggleDemo());
    }
}
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common Part A question is *"Distinguish between JButton and JToggleButton."* The model answer is: *"`JButton` is stateless тАФ it returns to its initial appearance after release. `JToggleButton` retains its pressed/released state across clicks and exposes the `isSelected()` method."*

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix тАФ When to Use Which Button

| Use Case | Recommended Button | Listener | Visual Cue |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Save" or "Submit" command | `JButton` | `ActionListener` | Rectangular push-button |
| "Mute" / "Caps Lock" sticky state | `JToggleButton` | `ItemListener` | Stays pressed in |
| "I agree to terms" multi-option | `JCheckBox` | `ItemListener` | Square box with check |
| "Gender" / "Payment Method" single-choice | `JRadioButton` + `ButtonGroup` | `ItemListener` | Round dot |
| Toolbar action with icon | `JButton` with `setIcon()` | `ActionListener` | Icon-only rectangle |

# ЁЯЧ║я╕П Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy Diagram тАФ The Inheritance Tree

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Object {
        <<java.lang>>
    }
    class Component {
        <<java.awt>>
        +setSize(int, int)
        +setLocation(int, int)
        +setEnabled(boolean)
    }
    class Container {
        <<java.awt>>
        +add(Component)
        +setLayout(LayoutManager)
    }
    class JComponent {
        <<javax.swing>>
        +setBorder(Border)
        +setToolTipText(String)
        +setOpaque(boolean)
    }
    class AbstractButton {
        <<javax.swing>>
        +setText(String)
        +setIcon(Icon)
        +setMnemonic(int)
        +setActionCommand(String)
        +addActionListener(ActionListener)
        +addItemListener(ItemListener)
    }
    class JButton {
        +JButton()
        +JButton(String)
        +JButton(Icon)
        +JButton(String, Icon)
    }
    class JToggleButton {
        +JToggleButton()
        +JToggleButton(String)
        +isSelected() boolean
        +setSelected(boolean)
    }
    class JCheckBox {
        +JCheckBox()
        +JCheckBox(String)
        +JCheckBox(String, boolean)
    }
    class JRadioButton {
        +JRadioButton()
        +JRadioButton(String)
    }
    class ButtonGroup {
        <<javax.swing>>
        +add(AbstractButton)
        +getSelection() ButtonModel
        +clearSelection()
    }

    Object <|-- Component
    Component <|-- Container
    Container <|-- JComponent
    JComponent <|-- AbstractButton
    AbstractButton <|-- JButton
    AbstractButton <|-- JToggleButton
    JToggleButton <|-- JCheckBox
    JToggleButton <|-- JRadioButton
    ButtonGroup o-- AbstractButton : manages
```

**Reading the diagram:** The arrows point from child to parent. Note how `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton` inherit from `JToggleButton` (not directly from `AbstractButton`), which is why they all share the `isSelected()` / `setSelected()` API. The diamond (тЧЗ) at `ButtonGroup` indicates aggregation тАФ it holds references to buttons but does not own them in the parent-child sense.

## 4.2 Event Flow Diagram тАФ The Click Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User mouse press on button] --> B[ButtonModel.isPressed = true]
    B --> C[ButtonModel.isArmed = true]
    C --> D{User releases mouse}
    D -- inside button bounds --> E[Fire ActionEvent]
    D -- outside button bounds --> F[Cancel: no event]
    E --> G[All registered ActionListeners notified]
    G --> H[actionPerformed called on each]
    H --> I[Reset armed and pressed flags]
    I --> J[Paint normal state]

    K[Toggle button only] --> L[User clicks]
    L --> M[ButtonModel.isSelected toggled]
    M --> N[Fire ItemEvent AND ActionEvent]
    N --> O[ItemListeners and ActionListeners notified]
    O --> P[Paint new visual state]
```

## 4.3 Layout Architecture тАФ Container + Group Two-Track Pattern

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:JFrame1["JFrame (Top-Level Window)"]:1
        block:JPanel1["JPanel (Visual Container)"]:3
            chkA["chkJava (JCheckBox)"]
            chkB["chkPython (JCheckBox)"]
            chkC["chkCpp (JCheckBox)"]
            radA["radFresher (JRadioButton)"]
            radB["radInter (JRadioButton)"]
            radC["radExpert (JRadioButton)"]
            btnX["btnSubmit (JButton)"]
        end
    end
    blockGroup["ButtonGroup (Logical Cluster)"]:1
        radA2["radFresher"]
        radB2["radInter"]
        radC2["radExpert"]
    end
    JPanel1 -.->|add(Component)| radA
    JPanel1 -.->|add(Component)| radB
    JPanel1 -.->|add(Component)| radC
    blockGroup ==>|add(AbstractButton)| radA2
    blockGroup ==>|add(AbstractButton)| radB2
    blockGroup ==>|add(AbstractButton)| radC2
```

**Interpretation:** The top row shows the *visual tree* (which determines where things are drawn). The bottom row shows the *logical grouping* (which determines state coordination). The arrows are not extra `add()` calls тАФ they represent the two separate registrations the developer must perform.

## 4.4 State Machine тАФ The JButton Press-Release Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> ROLLOVER : mouse enters bounds
    ROLLOVER --> ARMED : mouse pressed
    ARMED --> PRESSED : (transient sub-state)
    PRESSED --> ARMED : mouse held
    ARMED --> FIRED : mouse released inside
    ARMED --> IDLE : mouse released outside
    FIRED --> IDLE : event dispatched
    ROLLOVER --> IDLE : mouse exits
    IDLE --> DISABLED : setEnabled(false)
    ROLLOVER --> DISABLED : setEnabled(false)
    ARMED --> DISABLED : setEnabled(false)
    DISABLED --> IDLE : setEnabled(true)
    FIRED : ActionEvent sent to listeners
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All node identifiers above use alphanumeric prefixes (`chkA`, `radA`, `JFrame1`, `JPanel1`, `blockGroup`) per the Mermaid safeguard rules. No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as node names.

# ЁЯУЪ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the class hierarchy of Swing buttons. How does `JButton` differ from `JToggleButton`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

All Swing buttons inherit from `javax.swing.AbstractButton`, which extends `javax.swing.JComponent`. The hierarchy is:

$$
\text{Object} \rightarrow \text{Component (java.awt)} \rightarrow \text{Container} \rightarrow \text{JComponent} \rightarrow \text{AbstractButton} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{JButton} \\ \text{JToggleButton} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{JCheckBox} \\ \text{JRadioButton} \end{cases} \end{cases}
$$

**Key Difference:** `JButton` is a stateless push-button that fires an `ActionEvent` on each click and returns to its original appearance immediately. `JToggleButton` is a *stateful* two-state button that retains its "pressed" appearance after release. It exposes the methods `isSelected()` and `setSelected(boolean)`, which `JButton` inherits but cannot meaningfully use because its `DefaultButtonModel` does not maintain a `selected` property. *(1 mark for hierarchy diagram, 1 mark for the difference, 1 mark for the model/state distinction.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the purpose of `ButtonGroup` in Swing? Why is it not a `Container`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`ButtonGroup` is a logical clustering mechanism in `javax.swing` used to enforce **mutual exclusivity** among a set of `AbstractButton` subclasses (typically `JRadioButton` instances). When one button in the group becomes selected, all others in the same group are automatically deselected, ensuring the user can choose only one option.

`ButtonGroup` does **not** extend `java.awt.Container` because it is not intended to manage the **layout** of its members тАФ only their **state**. Buttons added to a `ButtonGroup` are not displayed by the group; they must still be added to a real container such as `JPanel` or `JFrame` for visual rendering. Separating "state coordination" from "layout management" is a deliberate design choice that adheres to the **Single Responsibility Principle**. *(1 mark for mutual exclusivity, 1 mark for state vs. layout, 1 mark for design rationale.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**
> тАв **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** тАФ Your window will close but the JVM will keep running. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> тАв **Forgetting `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()`** тАФ Your program will work, but the examiner will dock 1 mark for violating thread-safety. Always wrap GUI construction in it.
> тАв **Adding JRadioButton to ButtonGroup but not to JPanel** тАФ Buttons will exist in memory but **will not appear**. The examiner will mark "GUI not displayed" and deduct up to 3 marks.
> тАв **Using `==` instead of `.equals()` for String comparison in `getActionCommand()`** тАФ `==` compares references, not values; this can cause intermittent failures. Use `.equals()` for strings.
> тАв **Missing `@Override` annotation** тАФ Not a fatal error, but examiners consider it poor practice. Deduct 0.5 mark.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4 Choice A]`

**Part (a) тАФ 7 Marks**
*Explain the MVC architecture used by `AbstractButton`. List any **seven** commonly used methods of `AbstractButton` with their signatures and purposes.*

**Part (b) тАФ 7 Marks**
*Write a complete Java Swing program to create a window containing two `JButton`s тАФ "Red" and "Blue" тАФ and a `JPanel`. When the "Red" button is clicked, the panel's background should turn red; when "Blue" is clicked, it should turn blue. Use a single `ActionListener` for both buttons.*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A, Part (a)**

**The MVC Triad in `AbstractButton`:**

1. **Model тАФ `ButtonModel` Interface:** Stores the four state flags (`isArmed()`, `isPressed()`, `isSelected()`, `isEnabled()`) and the action command string. The concrete `DefaultButtonModel` (used by `JButton`) and `ToggleButtonModel` (used by `JToggleButton` / `JCheckBox` / `JRadioButton`) implement this.

2. **View тАФ `BasicButtonUI` and `MetalButtonUI`:** Implements the `ComponentUI` interface to render the button on screen, including painting text, icons, borders, focus rings, and the current visual state (pressed, rollover, disabled).

3. **Controller тАФ Event Listeners:** `ActionListener`, `ItemListener`, and `ChangeListener` act as controllers. They receive notifications from the model and invoke application logic. The `AbstractButton` itself acts as a mediator, forwarding model state changes to both the view (for repainting) and the registered listeners.

**Seven Commonly Used Methods:**

| # | Method | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `void setText(String text)` | Sets the button's display label |
| 2 | `String getText()` | Returns the current label |
| 3 | `void setIcon(Icon icon)` | Sets the default icon |
| 4 | `void setMnemonic(int keyCode)` | Sets Alt + key keyboard shortcut |
| 5 | `void setActionCommand(String cmd)` | Sets a logical identifier sent with the `ActionEvent` |
| 6 | `void addActionListener(ActionListener l)` | Registers a click handler |
| 7 | `void setEnabled(boolean enabled)` | Greys out the button if `false` |
| 8 (bonus) | `void setSelected(boolean selected)` | Forces the toggle state (toggleable buttons only) |

*(Distribute 7 marks as: 3 marks for the MVC explanation with state flags, 4 marks for the method table with correct signatures and purposes.)*

#### **Model Solution for Question A, Part (b)**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ColorChanger extends JFrame implements ActionListener {

    private JPanel colorPanel;
    private JButton btnRed, btnBlue;

    public ColorChanger() {
        setTitle("Color Changer");
        setSize(400, 250);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10));

        // --- The panel whose background we will change ---
        colorPanel = new JPanel();
        colorPanel.setBackground(Color.WHITE);
        colorPanel.setBorder(BorderFactory.createTitledBorder("Colored Panel"));
        add(colorPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        // --- The two buttons ---
        JPanel buttonPanel = new JPanel();
        buttonPanel.setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 20, 10));
        btnRed  = new JButton("Red");
        btnBlue = new JButton("Blue");

        // Both buttons share the SAME listener: this object.
        btnRed.addActionListener(this);
        btnBlue.addActionListener(this);

        buttonPanel.add(btnRed);
        buttonPanel.add(btnBlue);
        add(buttonPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        // Identify which button fired the event using getSource()
        if (e.getSource() == btnRed) {
            colorPanel.setBackground(Color.RED);
        } else if (e.getSource() == btnBlue) {
            colorPanel.setBackground(Color.BLUE);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new ColorChanger());
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step Explanation (Valuation Key):**

1. **Class declaration** (`extends JFrame implements ActionListener`) тАФ 1 mark. The class must implement the listener interface to handle events directly.

2. **Constructor builds the UI** тАФ 1 mark. Setting title, size, close operation, layout.

3. **JPanel creation and placement** тАФ 1 mark. The target panel is created with `BorderLayout.CENTER` and a default white background.

4. **Two JButtons created and registered** тАФ 1 mark. The crucial step is `addActionListener(this)` for **both** buttons pointing to the same handler.

5. **`actionPerformed` implementation using `e.getSource()`** тАФ 2 marks. The `==` comparison works here because `getSource()` returns the same object reference. Alternative: use `e.getActionCommand().equals("Red")`.

6. **`main` method with `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()`** тАФ 1 mark. Thread-safe GUI creation on the EDT.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4 Choice B]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**Part (a) тАФ 7 Marks**
*With a neat diagram, explain the event-delegation model used by Swing buttons. Differentiate between `ActionListener` and `ItemListener` with suitable examples.*

**Part (b) тАФ 7 Marks**
*Write a Java Swing program that uses **two `JCheckBox`**es (Pizza, Burger) and **one `ButtonGroup` of three `JRadioButton`s** (Small, Medium, Large). On clicking an "Order" `JButton`, display the bill in a `JLabel` as: Pizza = тВ╣200, Burger = тВ╣150, and sizes Small/Medium/Large = тВ╣50/тВ╣100/тВ╣150. If no checkbox is selected, show "Please select at least one item."*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B, Part (a)**

**The Event Delegation Model:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source: JButton etc] -- "fires" --> evt[Event Object: ActionEvent]
    evt -- "passed to" --> lst[Listener: ActionListener]
    lst -- "invokes" --> hnd[Handler: actionPerformed]
    hnd -- "may modify" --> ui[GUI components]
    ui -- "repaints" --> src
```

**The model works in three steps:**

1. **The user interacts** with the source component (e.g., clicks a button).
2. **The component fires an event** by creating an `EventObject` subclass (e.g., `ActionEvent`) and passing it to every registered listener via the listener's callback method.
3. **The listener executes its handler method** and may modify the GUI in response.

**ActionListener vs ItemListener тАФ Tabular Comparison:**

| Aspect | `ActionListener` | `ItemListener` |
|---|---|---|
| **Interface Location** | `java.awt.event` | `java.awt.event` |
| **Method** | `void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | `void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |
| **Trigger** | A semantic "command" occurred (button click, menu selection) | A selectable state changed (checkbox tick/untick, radio select/deselect) |
| **Applies To** | All `AbstractButton` subclasses | Only toggleable buttons (`JToggleButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`) |
| **Event Detail** | `e.getActionCommand()` returns the command string | `e.getStateChange()` returns `ItemEvent.SELECTED` or `DESELECTED` |
| **Typical Use** | "User clicked Submit тАФ validate form" | "User toggled dark mode тАФ re-theme the app" |

**Example Distinction:**

- For a `JButton` "Calculate", you use `ActionListener` because the action is "perform calculation."
- For a `JCheckBox` "Show Password", you use `ItemListener` because the state change ("checked/unchecked") is what matters; the user is toggling a property.

*(Distribute 7 marks as: 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for three-step explanation, 3 marks for the comparison table with examples.)*

#### **Model Solution for Question B, Part (b)**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class FoodOrder extends JFrame implements ActionListener {

    private JCheckBox chkPizza, chkBurger;
    private JRadioButton radSmall, radMedium, radLarge;
    private JButton btnOrder;
    private JLabel lblBill;

    private static final int PIZZA_PRICE = 200;
    private static final int BURGER_PRICE = 150;
    private static final int SMALL_PRICE  = 50;
    private static final int MEDIUM_PRICE = 100;
    private static final int LARGE_PRICE  = 150;

    public FoodOrder() {
        setTitle("Food Ordering System");
        setSize(400, 320);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.LEFT, 10, 10));

        // --- Items ---
        add(new JLabel("Select Items:"));
        chkPizza  = new JCheckBox("Pizza (тВ╣" + PIZZA_PRICE + ")");
        chkBurger = new JCheckBox("Burger (тВ╣" + BURGER_PRICE + ")");
        add(chkPizza);
        add(chkBurger);

        // --- Sizes (mutually exclusive) ---
        add(new JLabel("Select Size:"));
        radSmall  = new JRadioButton("Small (тВ╣" + SMALL_PRICE + ")");
        radMedium = new JRadioButton("Medium (тВ╣" + MEDIUM_PRICE + ")");
        radLarge  = new JRadioButton("Large (тВ╣" + LARGE_PRICE + ")");
        radMedium.setSelected(true);

        ButtonGroup sizeGroup = new ButtonGroup();
        sizeGroup.add(radSmall);
        sizeGroup.add(radMedium);
        sizeGroup.add(radLarge);
        add(radSmall);
        add(radMedium);
        add(radLarge);

        // --- Order button and bill label ---
        btnOrder = new JButton("Place Order");
        btnOrder.addActionListener(this);
        add(btnOrder);

        lblBill = new JLabel("Bill: тВ╣0");
        lblBill.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 16));
        add(lblBill);

        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == btnOrder) {
            if (!chkPizza.isSelected() && !chkBurger.isSelected()) {
                lblBill.setText("Please select at least one item.");
                return;
            }
            int total = 0;
            if (chkPizza.isSelected())  total += PIZZA_PRICE;
            if (chkBurger.isSelected()) total += BURGER_PRICE;

            if (radSmall.isSelected())       total += SMALL_PRICE;
            else if (radMedium.isSelected()) total += MEDIUM_PRICE;
            else if (radLarge.isSelected())  total += LARGE_PRICE;

            lblBill.setText("Bill: тВ╣" + total);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new FoodOrder());
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step Valuation Key (7 marks):**

1. **Class declaration with `implements ActionListener`** тАФ 0.5 mark.
2. **Constant price declarations** (good practice) тАФ 0.5 mark.
3. **Two checkboxes and three radio buttons created** тАФ 1 mark.
4. **`ButtonGroup` correctly instantiated and all three radios added** тАФ 1 mark.
5. **Both visual add to `JFrame` AND group add performed** (two-track rule) тАФ 1 mark.
6. **`actionPerformed` logic with at-least-one-item guard** тАФ 2 marks.
7. **Correct `main` method with `invokeLater` and proper layout/closure** тАФ 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap тАФ Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember for the KTU Exam**

- **Swing buttons inherit from `javax.swing.AbstractButton`**, which provides shared methods like `setText()`, `setIcon()`, `setMnemonic()`, `setActionCommand()`, and `addActionListener()`.
- **There are four main button types:** `JButton` (stateless push), `JToggleButton` (sticky two-state), `JCheckBox` (multi-select toggle), `JRadioButton` (single-select toggle, requires `ButtonGroup`).
- **`JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton` extend `JToggleButton`**, not `AbstractButton` directly. They inherit `isSelected()` / `setSelected(boolean)`.
- **`ButtonGroup` is NOT a `Container`** тАФ it manages state, not layout. Always add components to a `JPanel`/`JFrame` for visibility **AND** to a `ButtonGroup` for exclusivity.
- **Two-Track Registration Rule:** Every `JRadioButton` needs a `add()` call to both the visual container and the `ButtonGroup`.
- **Use `ActionListener` for `JButton` clicks**; use `ItemListener` for state-change events on `JCheckBox` / `JRadioButton` / `JToggleButton`. Either works for any button, but semantics matter.
- **The event-delegation model** has three actors: **Source** (the button), **Event object** (`ActionEvent` / `ItemEvent`), and **Listener** (the handler interface).
- **Always call `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** in your constructor тАФ otherwise the JVM keeps running after the window is closed.
- **Always launch GUI code via `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)`** to respect the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) and avoid race conditions.
- **Mnemonic key codes** come from `java.awt.event.KeyEvent` constants (e.g., `KeyEvent.VK_S` for S). They enable `Alt + letter` shortcuts.
- **`setActionCommand(String)`** is used to attach a logical name to the event; without it, the command defaults to the button's text. Prefer setting it explicitly for stable event identification.
- **`e.getSource() == button`** compares object references тАФ safe for Swing components because they are unique. Use `.equals()` for String comparisons of action commands.
- **Swing is lightweight and pluggable Look-And-Feel**, unlike the older heavy AWT buttons. This is the primary engineering reason Java developers prefer Swing in 2024-era curricula.
- **The `JToggleButton` does NOT require a `ButtonGroup`** тАФ multiple independent toggles can coexist. `ButtonGroup` is only needed when you want mutual exclusion.
- **Common examiner traps:** forgetting to add buttons to the container after adding to `ButtonGroup`; using `JFrame`'s default `BorderLayout` and not specifying a layout when adding multiple components; missing imports for `KeyEvent` or `BorderFactory`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: JText Field, Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) - JDBC Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** JText Field, Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) - JDBC Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 JTextField тАФ Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
