> `JTextField` is a lightweight Swing component (subclass of `javax.swing.JTextComponent`) that allows the editing of a **single line of unformatted text**. It is one of the most frequently used input controls in GUI programming under Java's Swing API, providing event-driven interaction through listeners such as `ActionListener` and `DocumentListener`.

**Class Hierarchy:**

```text
java.lang.Object
   тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Component
        тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Container
             тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JComponent
                  тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JTextComponent
                       тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JTextField
```

**Key Constructors:**

| Constructor | Description |
|---|---|
| `JTextField()` | Creates a new text field with default columns (0) |
| `JTextField(int columns)` | Creates a text field with the specified column width |
| `JTextField(String text)` | Creates a text field initialized with the given text |
| `JTextField(String text, int columns)` | Creates a text field with initial text and column width |
| `JTextField(Document doc, String text, int columns)` | Creates a text field using a custom document model |

**Commonly Used Methods (API Cheat Sheet):**

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `String getText()` | Returns the current text contained in the field |
| `void setText(String t)` | Sets the text of the field programmatically |
| `boolean isEditable()` | Checks if the field is editable |
| `void setEditable(boolean b)` | Enables or disables editing |
| `void addActionListener(ActionListener l)` | Registers listener for Enter key press |
| `void setHorizontalAlignment(int align)` | Sets text alignment (LEFT, CENTER, RIGHT) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:** Questions on `JTextField` typically focus on event handling (`ActionListener`), retrieval of values using `getText()`, and integration with `JButton` to build simple form-based GUIs.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ "The Form Box on Paper"

Imagine you walk into a bank and the clerk hands you a paper form. Each blank line on that form where you are expected to **write only one line of information** (such as your name, account number, or phone number) is a `JTextField`.

- **The line itself** = The visual area where typing happens.
- **What you write** = The *state* (text content) of the field.
- **The button "Submit"** = The trigger that fires the `ActionListener`, just as a banker presses a button after you complete the form.
- **The clerk reading it later** = A Java program calling `getText()` to read the data.

So, a `JTextField` is essentially a **single-line, rectangular, editable text area** used inside windows (JFrames/JPanels) to collect one piece of textual input from a user.

---

### 1.3 JDBC тАФ Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Java Database Connectivity (JDBC)** is a Java API (Application Programming Interface) that enables Java applications to interact with relational databases. It is part of the `java.sql` package and provides a standard set of classes and interfaces for sending SQL statements to a database and retrieving results. JDBC follows the **"Write Once, Run Anywhere"** principle of Java, abstracting database-specific details through the use of vendor-supplied drivers.

**Core Purpose of JDBC:**

1. **Establish a connection** to a database (Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, etc.).
2. **Create and execute SQL statements** (queries, updates, DDL).
3. **Retrieve and process** the results returned by the database.
4. **Close the connection** and release resources properly.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy тАФ "The Universal Translator at an International Airport"

Think of JDBC as a **universal translator** at a multilingual airport:

- **Java Application** = A English-speaking traveler.
- **Database (MySQL, Oracle, etc.)** = A local citizen speaking a regional language.
- **JDBC API** = The standardized translation framework (the rules, grammar, and protocol).
- **JDBC Driver** = The actual human translator who speaks both languages fluently.

Without the translator, the traveler cannot communicate. Without the **driver**, the JDBC API has no way to speak to a specific database. Each database vendor (Oracle, MySQL, Microsoft) provides its own driver тАФ but the **Java code you write remains unchanged** because it always talks to the JDBC API, not directly to the driver. This is the **"Plug-and-Play"** nature of JDBC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The phrase "**JDBC is database-independent, not database-agnostic**" is critical. You still need a driver, but your Java code does not change when you switch databases.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 JTextField тАФ Operational Mechanics

**Step-by-Step Working Logic:**

1. **Instantiation** тАФ A `JTextField` object is created using a constructor (e.g., `JTextField tf = new JTextField(20);`).
2. **Placement** тАФ The field is added to a container (e.g., `JFrame`, `JPanel`) using the `add()` method.
3. **Event Registration** тАФ One or more listeners are attached. The most common is `ActionListener`, which fires when the user presses the **Enter key** while focus is in the field.
4. **State Management** тАФ The text can be queried (`getText()`) or modified (`setText()`) at any time, even programmatically.
5. **Focus & Validation** тАФ Often used with `FocusListener` to validate input when the field loses focus.

**Why a Separate `ActionListener` is Required:**

Swing components use the **Delegation Event Model**. The `JTextField` itself does **not** decide what happens when text is entered тАФ instead, it *delegates* that responsibility to any registered listeners. This decouples the GUI from business logic (a core OOP principle).

**Real-World Utility in Engineering:**

- **Industrial Control Panels:** Operator inputs (setpoints, batch IDs).
- **Banking Applications:** Account number, IFSC, customer name fields.
- **Healthcare Systems:** Patient ID, dosage values.
- **IoT Dashboards:** Device IDs and configuration strings.

---

### 2.2 JDBC тАФ Architecture and Components

JDBC has **four major components**:

| # | Component | Type | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **JDBC API** | Set of classes/interfaces in `java.sql` and `javax.sql` | Provides the standard interface for Java applications |
| 2 | **JDBC Driver Manager** | `java.sql.DriverManager` class | Manages a list of database drivers; selects the correct one to establish a connection |
| 3 | **JDBC Test Suite** | Optional testing framework | Used to test JDBC drivers for compliance |
| 4 | **JDBC-ODBC Bridge** | `sun.jdbc.odbc.JdbcOdbcDriver` (deprecated since Java 8) | Allowed JDBC to access ODBC drivers (legacy Windows) |

**Two-Tier vs Three-Tier Architecture:**

- **Two-Tier:** Java App $\leftrightarrow$ JDBC Driver $\leftrightarrow$ Database (Direct, e.g., desktop tools).
- **Three-Tier:** Java App $\leftrightarrow$ Middleware (e.g., Web Server) $\leftrightarrow$ Database (Used in enterprise/web apps).

---

### 2.3 JDBC тАФ Five Mandatory Steps (The "5 Pillars")

These are the **canonical steps** for any JDBC program. KTU questions frequently test these:

| Step | Action | Key Method / Class |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Load the Driver** | `Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver")` |
| 2 | **Establish Connection** | `DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass)` |
| 3 | **Create Statement** | `connection.createStatement()` or `prepareStatement(sql)` |
| 4 | **Execute Query** | `stmt.executeQuery(sql)` (SELECT) or `stmt.executeUpdate(sql)` (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) |
| 5 | **Close Connection** | `connection.close()` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often forget Step 5 (closing connection), which causes **resource leaks** and is a frequently marked deduction in board exams.

---

### 2.4 JDBC Driver Types (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Type | Name | Description | Usage Today |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type-1 | JDBC-ODBC Bridge | Converts JDBC calls to ODBC calls | Deprecated (Java 8+) |
| Type-2 | Native-API Driver | Uses native client-side libraries | Partially used |
| Type-3 | Network Protocol Driver | Sends requests to a middleware server | Rare today |
| **Type-4** | **Thin/ Pure Java Driver** | **Converts JDBC calls directly to database-specific protocol; pure Java** | **Most widely used (MySQL Connector/J, Oracle Thin)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The **Type-4 Thin Driver** is the industry standard and is what KTU expects in practical exam answers for MySQL/Oracle connectivity.

---

### 2.5 JDBC URL Format (Reference Table)

| Database | URL Format |
|---|---|
| MySQL | `jdbc:mysql://hostname:3306/databaseName` |
| Oracle | `jdbc:oracle:thin:@hostname:1521:SID` |
| PostgreSQL | `jdbc:postgresql://hostname:5432/databaseName` |
| SQL Server | `jdbc:sqlserver://hostname:1433;databaseName=dbName` |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Code Models

### 3.1 JTextField тАФ Complete Working Example

Below is a **fully functional** `JTextField` program that demonstrates the OOP event-driven model.

**Problem Statement:** Create a Swing application that accepts a user's name in a `JTextField` and displays a greeting when a `JButton` is clicked.

**Step 1: Import the required packages**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;
```

**Step 2: Define the class extending `JFrame` (OOP inheritance)**

```java
public class GreetingApp extends JFrame {
    private JTextField nameField;
    private JLabel resultLabel;
    private JButton greetButton;
```

**Step 3: Constructor тАФ Initialize components**

```java
    public GreetingApp() {
        setTitle("KTU Greeting App");
        setSize(400, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        nameField = new JTextField(20);
        greetButton = new JButton("Greet");
        resultLabel = new JLabel("Enter your name and click Greet.");

        add(new JLabel("Name:"));
        add(nameField);
        add(greetButton);
        add(resultLabel);
```

**Step 4: Register an ActionListener (Event Delegation Model)**

```java
        greetButton.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                String name = nameField.getText();  // Retrieve text
                if (name == null || name.trim().isEmpty()) {
                    resultLabel.setText("Please enter a valid name.");
                } else {
                    resultLabel.setText("Hello, " + name + "! Welcome to KTU.");
                }
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }
```

**Step 5: Main method тАФ entry point**

```java
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(new Runnable() {
            @Override
            public void run() {
                new GreetingApp();
            }
        });
    }
}
```

**Logical Walkthrough:**

1. The `JTextField nameField` is created with **20 columns** (approximately 20 character widths wide).
2. The `ActionListener` is **registered** with the `JButton` (not the text field), since the button is the trigger.
3. Inside `actionPerformed`, `nameField.getText()` **retrieves** the current string.
4. The string is **validated** (empty check) тАФ an example of input sanitization.
5. The result is displayed in a `JLabel`, demonstrating component-to-component communication.

---

### 3.2 JDBC тАФ Complete 5-Step Program (MySQL)

**Problem Statement:** Write a JDBC program to fetch all records from a `students` table in MySQL and display them on the console.

**Step 1: Import packages**

```java
import java.sql.*;
```

**Step 2: Load the driver class**

```java
Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");
```

This dynamically loads the MySQL JDBC driver class. From **Java 6+**, this step is *technically optional* if the driver is auto-registered via the service-provider mechanism in the JAR's `META-INF/services` directory. However, **for KTU board exams, always include it explicitly**.

**Step 3: Establish the connection**

```java
String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktudb";
String user = "root";
String pass = "password";
Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass);
```

`DriverManager.getConnection()` returns a `Connection` object. The URL specifies the **protocol**, **host**, **port**, and **database name**.

**Step 4: Create and execute a statement**

```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement();
String query  = "SELECT * FROM students";
ResultSet rs   = stmt.executeQuery(query);
```

`executeQuery()` is used for **SELECT** (returns data). For **INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE**, use `executeUpdate()`, which returns an `int` (rows affected).

**Step 5: Process the ResultSet and close resources**

```java
while (rs.next()) {
    int id       = rs.getInt("id");
    String name  = rs.getString("name");
    int marks    = rs.getInt("marks");
    System.out.println(id + " | " + name + " | " + marks);
}
rs.close();
stmt.close();
con.close();
```

**Detailed Explanation of `ResultSet.next()`:**

- A `ResultSet` is conceptually a **cursor** pointing to the current row of query output.
- Initially, the cursor is positioned **before the first row**.
- `next()` shifts the cursor to the next row and returns `true` if a valid row exists.
- Column data is extracted using typed getters: `getInt()`, `getString()`, `getDouble()`, etc.

**Mathematical Representation of ResultSet Traversal:**

Let the query result contain $n$ rows, indexed as $R_0, R_1, \dots, R_{n-1}$.
The traversal loop processes them as:

$$
\text{for } i = 0 \text{ to } n-1: \quad \text{process } (R_i)
$$

This is equivalent to the while loop condition $i < n$, where $i$ is implicitly advanced by `rs.next()`.

**Complete Code (Consolidated):**

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class StudentFetcher {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");

            Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(
                "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktudb", "root", "password");

            Statement stmt = con.createStatement();
            ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM students");

            while (rs.next()) {
                System.out.println(
                    rs.getInt("id") + " " +
                    rs.getString("name") + " " +
                    rs.getInt("marks"));
            }

            rs.close();
            stmt.close();
            con.close();

        } catch (ClassNotFoundException e) {
            System.out.println("Driver not found: " + e);
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            System.out.println("SQL Error: " + e);
        }
    }
}
```

---

### 3.3 JDBC тАФ PreparedStatement (Advanced, KTU Part-B Favorite)

**Why PreparedStatement over Statement?**

| Feature | Statement | PreparedStatement |
|---|---|---|
| SQL Injection Safe | No | Yes (uses `?` placeholders) |
| Precompiled | No | Yes (compiled once, executed many times) |
| Performance (bulk inserts) | Slow | Faster |
| Readability | Moderate | High |

**Sample Code:**

```java
String sql = "INSERT INTO students(id, name, marks) VALUES (?, ?, ?)";
PreparedStatement pst = con.prepareStatement(sql);

pst.setInt(1, 101);
pst.setString(2, "Anu");
pst.setInt(3, 95);

int rowsInserted = pst.executeUpdate();
System.out.println(rowsInserted + " row(s) inserted.");

pst.close();
con.close();
```

Here, `setInt(1, 101)` binds the value `101` to the **first** `?` placeholder. Indexing is **1-based**, not 0-based тАФ a common KTU pitfall.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 JTextField Event Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User types in JTextField] --> B[Field buffer updates internally]
    B --> C{User presses Enter or clicks Button}
    C -- Enter --> D[ActionEvent fired]
    C -- Click Button --> E[ActionEvent fired on JButton]
    D --> F[ActionListener.actionPerformed called]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Program calls nameField.getText]
    G --> H[Validation logic executes]
    H --> I[Result displayed via JLabel setText]
    I --> J[GUI state updated visually]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A `JTextField` does **not** fire an `ActionEvent` simply by being typed into. The event is triggered by the **Enter key** inside the field, or by an external event source (like a `JButton`) whose listener reads the field's content.

---

### 4.2 JDBC Two-Tier Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP[Java Application]
    JDBC[JDBC API java.sql]
    DRV[Vendor JDBC Driver Type-4 Thin]
    DB[(Relational Database e.g., MySQL)]

    APP -- Method calls --> JDBC
    JDBC -- Service request --> DRV
    DRV -- SQL over TCP IP --> DB
    DB -- ResultSet returned --> DRV
    DRV -- Java objects --> JDBC
    JDBC -- Return to programmer --> APP
```

---

### 4.3 JDBC 5-Step Program Flow (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Class.forName loads driver] --> S2[Step 2: DriverManager.getConnection establishes Connection]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Connection.createStatement or prepareStatement]
    S3 --> S4a[executeQuery for SELECT returns ResultSet]
    S3 --> S4b[executeUpdate for INSERT UPDATE DELETE returns int]
    S4a --> S5[Process rows using rs.next and getters]
    S4b --> S5b[Confirm rows affected]
    S5 --> S6[Step 5: Close ResultSet Statement Connection in finally block]
    S5b --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Application continues or terminates]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The order **close ResultSet тЖТ close Statement тЖТ close Connection** is the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** best practice and is often asked as a short question in KTU exams.

---

### 4.4 JDBC Driver Types тАФ Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1[Type 1 JDBC ODBC Bridge]
        A1[Java App] --> A2[JDBC] --> A3[ODBC Driver] --> A4[(Database)]
    end
    subgraph T2[Type 2 Native API]
        B1[Java App] --> B2[JDBC] --> B3[Vendor Native Client Lib] --> B4[(Database)]
    end
    subgraph T4[Type 4 Thin Pure Java]
        C1[Java App] --> C2[JDBC] --> C3[Pure Java Driver] --> C4[(Database)]
    end
```

**Observation from the diagram:** Type-1 and Type-2 require an *intermediate* layer (ODBC or native code), while **Type-4 communicates directly** with the database using its native protocol тАФ entirely in pure Java.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of `JTextField` in Java Swing. List any two of its constructors with their purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

`JTextField` is a Swing component in the `javax.swing` package that allows a user to enter a **single line of unformatted text**. It is a subclass of `JTextComponent` and supports event-based interaction through `ActionListener`.

Two constructors:
1. `JTextField(int columns)` тАФ Creates an empty text field with the specified number of columns (visible width).
2. `JTextField(String text)` тАФ Creates a text field pre-initialized with the given string.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU key):**
- Definition тАФ **1 mark**
- Constructors тАФ **2 marks** (1 each)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]**
**What is JDBC? Mention the role of `DriverManager` class.**

**Model Answer:**

**JDBC (Java Database Connectivity)** is a Java API provided in the `java.sql` package that enables Java applications to send SQL statements to relational databases and retrieve results in a database-independent manner.

**Role of `DriverManager`:** The `java.sql.DriverManager` class acts as a service-provider. It:
- Maintains a list of available JDBC drivers.
- Selects the appropriate driver based on the database URL.
- Provides the static method `getConnection(url, user, password)` to establish a connection to the database.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition of JDBC тАФ **2 marks**
- `DriverManager` role тАФ **1 mark**

---

### Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Internal Choice Convention:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** тАФ [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Write a Java Swing program using `JTextField` and `JButton` to accept two integers and display their sum on a `JLabel` when the button is clicked. Explain the event-handling mechanism used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **JDBC architecture** with a neat diagram. Differentiate between `Statement` and `PreparedStatement` with examples. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SumApp extends JFrame {
    JTextField tf1, tf2;
    JLabel result;

    SumApp() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setSize(350, 150);
        setTitle("Sum Calculator");
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        tf1 = new JTextField(10);
        tf2 = new JTextField(10);
        JButton btn = new JButton("Add");
        result = new JLabel("Result will appear here");

        add(new JLabel("Num1:")); add(tf1);
        add(new JLabel("Num2:")); add(tf2);
        add(btn); add(result);

        btn.addActionListener(e -> {
            try {
                int a = Integer.parseInt(tf1.getText());
                int b = Integer.parseInt(tf2.getText());
                result.setText("Sum = " + (a + b));
            } catch (NumberFormatException ex) {
                result.setText("Invalid input. Enter integers.");
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SumApp();
    }
}
```

**Event-Handling Mechanism Explanation:**

- Swing follows the **Delegation Event Model** defined in `java.awt.event`.
- The `JButton` does not process its own click; it **delegates** the event to its registered `ActionListener`.
- When the button is clicked, the JVM constructs an `ActionEvent` object and passes it to the `actionPerformed` method of the listener.
- The lambda expression `e -> { ... }` is syntactic sugar for an anonymous `ActionListener` (a feature introduced in **Java 8**).
- `getText()` is used to **retrieve** the current text from each `JTextField`.
- Input validation is done via `try-catch` to handle `NumberFormatException` for non-integer inputs.

**Mark Allocation (KTU Examiner Key):**
- Correct imports & class structure тАФ **1 mark**
- Two `JTextField` + 1 `JButton` + `JLabel` setup тАФ **2 marks**
- ActionListener registration тАФ **1 mark**
- `getText()` and `Integer.parseInt` logic тАФ **1 mark**
- Result display + exception handling тАФ **1 mark**
- Event-handling explanation тАФ **1 mark**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**JDBC Architecture (Two-Tier):**

```text
Java Application
       тЖУ (calls)
JDBC API (java.sql.*)
       тЖУ (uses)
JDBC Driver Manager
       тЖУ (loads)
Vendor JDBC Driver
       тЖУ (translates to DB protocol)
Database Server
```

**Difference Table:**

| Feature | `Statement` | `PreparedStatement` |
|---|---|---|
| Precompilation | Not precompiled | Precompiled once |
| Parameters | Hardcoded into SQL string | Uses `?` placeholders |
| SQL Injection | Vulnerable | Safe (parameterized) |
| Reusability | Low | High (execute multiple times) |
| Performance for bulk ops | Slower | Faster |

**Examples:**

```java
// Using Statement
Statement s = con.createStatement();
s.executeUpdate("INSERT INTO students VALUES (1, 'Anu', 90)");

// Using PreparedStatement
PreparedStatement p = con.prepareStatement("INSERT INTO students VALUES (?, ?, ?)");
p.setInt(1, 1);
p.setString(2, "Anu");
p.setInt(3, 90);
p.executeUpdate();
```

**Mark Allocation:**
- Diagram тАФ **2 marks**
- API / Driver Manager / Driver explanation тАФ **2 marks**
- Comparison table тАФ **2 marks**
- Code examples тАФ **1 mark**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice**

**(a)** List and explain the **five fundamental steps of JDBC** with a sample program that connects to a MySQL database and retrieves data from a table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **four types of JDBC drivers** with a diagram. Which one is most widely used and why? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**The Five Steps:**

1. **Load the Driver Class** тАФ `Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver")`
2. **Establish the Connection** тАФ `DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass)`
3. **Create a Statement** тАФ `con.createStatement()` or `con.prepareStatement(sql)`
4. **Execute the Query** тАФ `stmt.executeQuery(sql)` for SELECT, `executeUpdate(sql)` for DML.
5. **Close the Connection** тАФ `con.close()` (preferably in a `finally` block).

**Sample Program:**

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class EmployeeFetch {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");
            Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(
                "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/companydb", "root", "pass");

            Statement stmt = con.createStatement();
            ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT emp_id, emp_name FROM employees");

            while (rs.next()) {
                System.out.println(rs.getInt("emp_id") + " - " + rs.getString("emp_name"));
            }

            rs.close(); stmt.close(); con.close();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println(e);
        }
    }
}
```

**Mark Allocation:**
- Listing 5 steps with one-line explanation тАФ **3 marks**
- Full working code тАФ **3 marks**
- `ResultSet` processing logic тАФ **1 mark**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Four JDBC Driver Types:**

| Type | Description | Drawback |
|---|---|---|
| Type-1: JDBC-ODBC Bridge | Translates JDBC calls to ODBC; needs ODBC installed | Slow; not portable; removed in Java 8 |
| Type-2: Native-API | Uses client-side native libraries provided by vendor | Vendor lock-in; not portable |
| Type-3: Network Protocol | Sends calls to a middleware server, which then talks to DB | Extra middleware cost; latency |
| **Type-4: Thin/Pure Java** | Converts JDBC calls **directly** to DB-specific protocol using pure Java | None significant тАФ used everywhere |

**Why Type-4 is the Industry Standard:**

- **No native libraries** required тЖТ fully portable.
- **No ODBC or middleware** dependency тЖТ faster and simpler deployment.
- **Direct TCP/IP communication** with the database.
- **Examples:** MySQL Connector/J, Oracle Thin Driver, PostgreSQL JDBC Driver.

**Mark Allocation:**
- Table of 4 types тАФ **3 marks**
- Diagram (flowchart showing each type) тАФ **2 marks**
- Reason for Type-4 dominance тАФ **2 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout тАФ Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to close the `Connection`** тЖТ **-1 to -2 marks** deduction. Always close in `finally` or use **try-with-resources** (Java 7+).
> 2. **Using `executeQuery()` for INSERT/UPDATE** тЖТ logical error, full marks lost on execution.
> 3. **Confusing 1-based indexing** in `PreparedStatement.setXxx(index, value)` тАФ many students write `setInt(0, ...)` thinking it's 0-based. It's **1-based**.
> 4. **Wrong URL format** for MySQL: omitting the port `3306` or the database name causes `SQLException`.
> 5. **Forgetting to call `rs.next()`** before reading data тЖТ leads to runtime error or empty output.
> 6. **Mixing up AWT and Swing** in GUI code: avoid using `java.awt.TextField` when the question specifically asks for Swing's `JTextField`.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exams):**

- **JTextField** is a **single-line** Swing text input component from `javax.swing`.
- Key methods: `getText()`, `setText()`, `setEditable()`, `addActionListener()`.
- `JTextField` fires `ActionEvent` on **Enter key** (when focus is in field) or via external triggers like a `JButton`.
- **JDBC** stands for **Java Database Connectivity**, packaged in `java.sql`.
- JDBC provides **database independence** via the **Driver abstraction**.
- The **5 mandatory JDBC steps** are: *Load Driver тЖТ Connect тЖТ Create Statement тЖТ Execute тЖТ Close*.
- Use `executeQuery()` for **SELECT** (returns `ResultSet`); use `executeUpdate()` for **INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE** (returns `int` rows affected).
- **`PreparedStatement`** uses `?` placeholders (1-based indexing) and is **immune to SQL injection**.
- **Type-4 Thin Driver** is the **industry standard** тАФ pure Java, no native libs, direct DB protocol.
- Always **close resources in reverse order**: `ResultSet тЖТ Statement тЖТ Connection`.
- Use `try-catch` for `ClassNotFoundException` and `SQLException` (or the parent `Exception`).
- `ResultSet.next()` is required to advance the cursor to the first row before reading.
- JDBC URL patterns:
  - MySQL: `jdbc:mysql://host:3306/dbname`
  - Oracle: `jdbc:oracle:thin:@host:1521:SID`
- **Delegation Event Model** is the OOP-based event-handling foundation of Swing/AWT.
- Type-1 (JDBC-ODBC Bridge) is **deprecated since Java 8** тАФ never recommend it in exams.
- **Try-with-resources** is the modern best practice for auto-closing JDBC objects.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating and Executing Queries - create table
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creating and Executing Queries - create table

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Java Database Connectivity (JDBC)** under Object Oriented Programming, the **`CREATE TABLE`** statement is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** command in **Structured Query Language (SQL)** used to define a new relation (table) within a relational database management system (RDBMS). It specifies the **table name**, the **column names**, their associated **data types**, **sizes**, and the **integrity constraints** (rules) that govern the data stored within those columns.

Once a `CREATE TABLE` statement is executed and committed, the schema is permanently recorded in the **Data Dictionary** (system catalog) of the database. In Java applications, this DDL command is transmitted to the RDBMS through a `java.sql.Statement` or `java.sql.PreparedStatement` object using the `executeUpdate()` method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> "The `CREATE TABLE` SQL command defines the logical structure of a relation by specifying its attributes, their domains, and the constraints that enforce data integrity. In JDBC, it is executed via the `executeUpdate()` method of the `Statement` interface."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are setting up a **new filing cabinet** in an office for storing student records.

1. **The Cabinet itself** = The **TABLE** (e.g., `STUDENT`).
2. **Each drawer label** = A **COLUMN** (e.g., `roll_no`, `name`, `cgpa`).
3. **The format written on each drawer label** (e.g., "Numbers only", "Max 10 letters") = The **DATA TYPE** and **SIZE**.
4. **The red sticker on the drawer saying "Cannot be empty"** = A **CONSTRAINT** (e.g., `NOT NULL`).
5. **The instruction card taped to the cabinet telling the clerk how to build it** = The **SQL `CREATE TABLE` statement**.

When you "execute" the instruction card, the clerk (the **RDBMS engine**, e.g., MySQL/Oracle) builds the cabinet exactly to your specifications. If you forgot a label or wrote an invalid format, the clerk **refuses** to build it (syntax error / constraint violation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** `CREATE TABLE` does **NOT** add *data* тАФ it builds the *empty structure* that will later hold data. Think "blueprint" rather than "building."

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology to Remember

- **DDL (Data Definition Language)** тАФ Subset of SQL for defining schema (`CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`).
- **DML (Data Manipulation Language)** тАФ Subset for data operations (`INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`).
- **Schema** тАФ The structural blueprint of the database.
- **Tuple / Row / Record** тАФ A single instance in the table.
- **Attribute / Column / Field** тАФ A named property of the relation.
- **Domain** тАФ The set of allowable values for an attribute (enforced by data type + constraints).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the `CREATE TABLE` Statement

The general structure of the command is decomposed into **5 logical blocks**:

1. **`CREATE TABLE` keyword pair** тАФ Announces intent to the RDBMS engine.
2. **Table Name (Identifier)** тАФ A unique name within the schema. KTU rule: Avoid reserved keywords like `SELECT`, `FROM`, `TABLE`.
3. **Column Definitions** тАФ A comma-separated list of `(column_name data_type [size] [constraints])`.
4. **Table-Level Constraints** тАФ Constraints applied across multiple columns (e.g., composite `PRIMARY KEY`, `FOREIGN KEY`).
5. **Storage / Engine Clause (Optional)** тАФ DBMS-specific (e.g., `ENGINE=InnoDB` in MySQL).

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Block

| Block | Purpose | What happens if missing? |
|---|---|---|
| `CREATE TABLE` | Activates DDL parser | RDBMS rejects with syntax error |
| Table name | Registers entry in Data Dictionary | No way to reference the table later |
| Column name | Defines attribute identity | Table cannot store that attribute |
| Data type | Enforces domain of values | Garbage data accepted (e.g., text in numeric field) |
| Constraints | Enforce business rules | Duplicate IDs, orphan rows, null breaches allowed |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet тАФ SQL Data Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **exam-critical**. Memorize the size limits and the appropriate use-case for each.

| Data Type | Storage | Use Case | Example Declaration |
|---|---|---|---|
| `CHAR(n)` | Fixed length `n` | Codes, fixed-length IDs | `CHAR(10)` for Aadhaar |
| `VARCHAR(n)` | Variable up to `n` | Names, addresses, descriptions | `VARCHAR(50)` |
| `INT` | 4 bytes | Whole numbers, counters | `roll_no INT` |
| `BIGINT` | 8 bytes | Large counters (phone, Aadhaar) | `phone BIGINT` |
| `DECIMAL(p,s)` | Exact numeric | Money, CGPA, marks | `cgpa DECIMAL(4,2)` |
| `FLOAT` | 4 bytes (approx.) | Scientific data | `temp FLOAT` |
| `DATE` | 3 bytes | Calendar date `YYYY-MM-DD` | `dob DATE` |
| `TIME` | 3 bytes | Time of day `HH:MM:SS` | `login_time TIME` |
| `DATETIME` | 8 bytes | Date + time combined | `created_at DATETIME` |
| `BOOLEAN` | 1 byte | True/False flags | `is_active BOOLEAN` |
| `TEXT` | Up to 65,535 chars | Long descriptions | `bio TEXT` |
| `BLOB` | Up to 65,535 bytes | Binary (images, files) | `photo BLOB` |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet тАФ Integrity Constraints

| Constraint | Function | Example Syntax |
|---|---|---|
| `PRIMARY KEY` | Unique + Not Null identifier of a row | `roll_no INT PRIMARY KEY` |
| `FOREIGN KEY` | Referential link to another table's PK | `FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES DEPARTMENT(id)` |
| `NOT NULL` | Disallows empty values | `name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL` |
| `UNIQUE` | Disallows duplicate values | `email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE` |
| `CHECK` | Validates a boolean condition | `CHECK (cgpa >= 0 AND cgpa <= 10)` |
| `DEFAULT` | Auto-fills value if none provided | `status VARCHAR(10) DEFAULT 'Active'` |
| `AUTO_INCREMENT` | Auto-generates sequential integers (MySQL) | `id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY` |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems**: `CREATE TABLE` defines `ACCOUNT`, `CUSTOMER`, `TRANSACTION` тАФ the `FOREIGN KEY` enforces that no transaction can reference a non-existent account.
- **E-Commerce**: `PRODUCT` table uses `CHECK (price > 0)` to prevent negative pricing.
- **University ERP (KTU context)**: `STUDENT` and `MARKS` tables use a composite `FOREIGN KEY` to ensure every mark entry belongs to a valid student-subject pair.
- **IoT/Embedded Logging**: `SENSOR_READING` uses `DECIMAL(6,3)` for precise voltage values.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Construction & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Construction Step 1 тАФ Bare-Bones Table (No Constraints)

**Objective:** Create a table to store basic student identity information.

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. The user wants a structure called `STUDENT` (clarity of intent).
2. Each student has a roll number, name, age, and CGPA тЖТ 4 columns needed.
3. `roll_no` should be a whole number тЖТ `INT`.
4. `name` is variable-length text тЖТ `VARCHAR(50)`.
5. `age` is a small whole number тЖТ `INT`.
6. `cgpa` is a precise decimal тЖТ `DECIMAL(4,2)`.

**Resulting SQL:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Student (
    roll_no  INT,
    name     VARCHAR(50),
    age      INT,
    cgpa     DECIMAL(4,2)
);
```

**Engineering reasoning:** Without constraints, this table accepts:
- Multiple students with the same `roll_no` тЖТ bad for unique identification.
- Empty `name` and `cgpa` тЖТ violates real-world expectations.
- CGPA values like `15.50` or `-5.00` тЖТ meaningless data.

## 3.2 Construction Step 2 тАФ Adding Column-Level Constraints

**Objective:** Strengthen the table with business rules.

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. `roll_no` must uniquely identify a student тЖТ add `PRIMARY KEY`.
2. `name` cannot be empty тЖТ add `NOT NULL`.
3. `age` should be realistic (e.g., 16тАУ100) тЖТ add `CHECK`.
4. `cgpa` should default to 0.0 if not entered тЖТ add `DEFAULT 0.00`.
5. `roll_no` should auto-generate тЖТ add `AUTO_INCREMENT` (MySQL).

**Resulting SQL:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Student (
    roll_no  INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    name     VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    age      INT CHECK (age >= 16 AND age <= 100),
    cgpa     DECIMAL(4,2) DEFAULT 0.00
);
```

## 3.3 Construction Step 3 тАФ Multi-Table Design with Foreign Key

**Objective:** Create a `DEPARTMENT` table and link `STUDENT` to it via a foreign key.

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. First, create the parent (referenced) table тЖТ `Department`.
2. Then, create the child (referencing) table тЖТ `Student` with a `FOREIGN KEY` clause.
3. The foreign key column (`dept_id`) must match the data type and size of the parent's `PRIMARY KEY` (`dept_id`).
4. Use `ON DELETE CASCADE` to auto-remove students when their department is deleted (optional, advanced).

**Resulting SQL:**

```sql
-- Step 3a: Parent table
CREATE TABLE Department (
    dept_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE
);

-- Step 3b: Child table with referential integrity
CREATE TABLE Student (
    roll_no  INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    name     VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    age      INT CHECK (age >= 16 AND age <= 100),
    cgpa     DECIMAL(4,2) DEFAULT 0.00,
    dept_id  INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
        ON DELETE CASCADE
        ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
```

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix тАФ Common Errors and Fixes

| Error Code | Meaning | Example Trigger | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ERROR 1064 (42000)` | SQL syntax error | Typo: `CREAT TABLE` | Use exact `CREATE TABLE` keyword |
| `ERROR 1062 (23000)` | Duplicate entry for unique key | Inserting same `roll_no` twice | Rely on `AUTO_INCREMENT` |
| `ERROR 1452 (23000)` | Foreign key constraint fails | Inserting `dept_id = 99` not in `Department` | Insert parent row first |
| `ERROR 1146 (42S02)` | Table does not exist | Referencing non-existent table | Check schema/typo |
| `ERROR 3819 (HY000)` | Invalid `CHECK` constraint | `CHECK (cgpa > 10)` on `DECIMAL(4,2)` | Adjust constraint value |

## 3.5 Mathematical Note тАФ Storage Estimation

The estimated row size for the `Student` table:

$$
\text{RowSize} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sizeof}(\text{column}_i) + \text{overhead}
$$

$$
\text{RowSize}_{Student} = 4_{(INT)} + 52_{(VARCHAR(50)+len)} + 4_{(INT)} + 4_{(DECIMAL)} + 4_{(FK)} + 1_{NULL} = 69\ \text{bytes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This estimation is **non-examinable as a calculation** but is presented to show the engineering principle of schema design тАФ choosing correct data types directly impacts disk I/O performance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logical Block Diagram тАФ Anatomy of a `CREATE TABLE` Statement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CREATE TABLE keyword pair"] --> B["Table Name Identifier"]
    B --> C["Opening Parenthesis"]
    C --> D["Column 1 Definition"]
    C --> E["Column 2 Definition"]
    C --> F["Column N Definition"]
    D --> G["Data Type + Size"]
    D --> H["Column Level Constraints"]
    E --> G
    E --> H
    F --> G
    F --> H
    D --> I["Comma Separator"]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    I --> J["Table Level Constraints"]
    J --> K["Closing Parenthesis"]
    K --> L["Optional Engine Clause"]
    L --> M["Semicolon Terminator"]
    M --> N["RDBMS Parser"]
    N --> O["Schema Created in Data Dictionary"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram тАФ JDBC Execution Flow for `CREATE TABLE` in Java

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Java Application Main Method"] --> B["Load JDBC Driver Class.forName"]
    B --> C["Establish Connection via DriverManager.getConnection"]
    C --> D["Create Statement Object conn.createStatement"]
    D --> E["Build SQL String CREATE TABLE Student..."]
    E --> F["Invoke stmt.executeUpdate sql"]
    F --> G{"RDBMS Validates Query"}
    G -->|"Syntax OK"| H["RDBMS Writes Schema to Data Dictionary"]
    G -->|"Syntax Error"| I["Throws SQLException to Java"]
    H --> J["Returns int rowCount 0 for DDL"]
    I --> K["Catch Block Logs Error"]
    J --> L["Close Statement and Connection"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["End of Execution"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic тАФ Referential Integrity Between Tables

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DEPARTMENT Table"] -->|"PRIMARY KEY dept_id INT"| B["STUDENT Table"]
    B -->|"FOREIGN KEY dept_id INT"| A
    A -->|"1 Department has many Students"| B
    B -->|"1 Student belongs to 1 Department"| A
```

**Interpretation of the schematic:**
- The arrow direction indicates the **referencing direction** (child points to parent).
- The label "1 to Many" reflects the **cardinality** of the relationship.
- The integrity is enforced by the RDBMS engine on every `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` affecting these tables.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`**
> **Define the `CREATE TABLE` command in SQL. List any four data types supported by MySQL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `CREATE TABLE` command is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** statement in SQL used to define a new table and its structure in the database. It specifies the table name, column names, their data types, sizes, and integrity constraints. Once executed, the table's structure is stored in the data dictionary of the RDBMS.

Four common MySQL data types:

1. `INT` тАФ for whole numbers.
2. `VARCHAR(n)` тАФ for variable-length strings up to `n` characters.
3. `DECIMAL(p, s)` тАФ for exact decimal numbers (e.g., currency, CGPA).
4. `DATE` тАФ for calendar dates in `YYYY-MM-DD` format.

> **Q2. `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024, Supplementary]`**
> **Differentiate between `CHAR` and `VARCHAR` data types with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `CHAR(n)` | `VARCHAR(n)` |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | **Fixed length** `n` characters | **Variable length** up to `n` characters |
| Padding | Right-padded with blanks if shorter | No padding |
| Performance | Slightly faster for fixed-size data | Slightly slower due to length tracking |
| Example | `state_code CHAR(2)` for "KL", "TN" | `student_name VARCHAR(50)` for "Rahul" |

`CHAR` is preferred when the value length is predictable (e.g., country codes, gender), whereas `VARCHAR` is preferred for variable-length text (e.g., names, descriptions).

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the general syntax of the SQL `CREATE TABLE` statement with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of `PRIMARY KEY`, `FOREIGN KEY`, `UNIQUE`, and `CHECK` constraints. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Write the SQL `CREATE TABLE` statements for the following scenario. Define the primary keys, foreign keys, and appropriate constraints based on the business rules. **[7 Marks]**

**Scenario:**
A library management system has two tables:
- `BOOK`: `book_id` (auto-increment, primary key), `title` (not null, max 100 chars), `author` (max 50 chars), `price` (decimal, must be > 0), `isbn` (unique, 13 chars).
- `MEMBER`: `member_id` (primary key), `member_name` (not null, max 60 chars), `join_date` (default today's date).
- `ISSUE`: tracks which member issued which book тЖТ `issue_id` (primary key), `book_id` (FK тЖТ BOOK), `member_id` (FK тЖТ MEMBER), `issue_date` (date, not null).

---

### **Model Answer тАФ Question A**

#### (a) Syntax and Constraints [7 Marks]

The general syntax of `CREATE TABLE` is:

```sql
CREATE TABLE table_name (
    column1 datatype [size] [column_constraint],
    column2 datatype [size] [column_constraint],
    ...
    [table_constraint1],
    [table_constraint2],
    ...
);
```

**Role of Constraints:**

1. **`PRIMARY KEY`**: Uniquely identifies each row in a table. Implicitly applies `NOT NULL` and `UNIQUE`. A table can have **only one** primary key.
2. **`FOREIGN KEY`**: Establishes a referential link between a column in the child table and the `PRIMARY KEY` of the parent table. Prevents actions that would destroy the link between tables.
3. **`UNIQUE`**: Ensures that all values in a column are different. A table can have **multiple** `UNIQUE` columns (unlike primary key).
4. **`CHECK`**: Enforces domain integrity by limiting the values accepted by a column based on a boolean expression.

**Neat Diagram:**

```
CREATE TABLE
     |
     +-- table_name
     |
     +-- (
     |     col1  datatype  PRIMARY KEY
     |     col2  datatype  NOT NULL
     |     col3  datatype  UNIQUE
     |     col4  datatype  CHECK (condition)
     |     FOREIGN KEY (col) REFERENCES parent(col)
     |   )
     +-- ;
```

#### (b) SQL Implementation [7 Marks]

```sql
-- Parent Table 1: BOOK
CREATE TABLE BOOK (
    book_id   INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    title     VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    author    VARCHAR(50),
    price     DECIMAL(7,2) CHECK (price > 0),
    isbn      CHAR(13) UNIQUE
);

-- Parent Table 2: MEMBER
CREATE TABLE MEMBER (
    member_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    member_name  VARCHAR(60) NOT NULL,
    join_date    DATE DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE)
);

-- Child Table: ISSUE
CREATE TABLE ISSUE (
    issue_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    book_id     INT NOT NULL,
    member_id   INT NOT NULL,
    issue_date  DATE NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (book_id)   REFERENCES BOOK(book_id)
        ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (member_id) REFERENCES MEMBER(member_id)
        ON DELETE CASCADE
);
```

**Explanation of key choices:**

- `book_id AUTO_INCREMENT` тАФ Auto-generates ID, reducing manual errors.
- `price CHECK (price > 0)` тАФ Prevents negative or zero pricing.
- `isbn CHAR(13) UNIQUE` тАФ Books have fixed-length 13-digit ISBNs and must be unique.
- `join_date DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE)` тАФ Auto-fills with current date if not provided.
- `FOREIGN KEY ... ON DELETE CASCADE` тАФ Deleting a book/member auto-removes related issue records, maintaining referential integrity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the various integrity constraints available in SQL `CREATE TABLE` statement. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Design a database schema for a **Hospital Management System** with the following tables: `DOCTOR`, `PATIENT`, and `APPOINTMENT`. Write complete `CREATE TABLE` statements with appropriate primary keys, foreign keys, and constraints. State clearly any business rules you are enforcing. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Answer тАФ Question B**

#### (a) Integrity Constraints with Example [7 Marks]

| # | Constraint | Purpose | SQL Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `NOT NULL` | Disallows empty values | `name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL` |
| 2 | `UNIQUE` | Disallows duplicate values | `email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE` |
| 3 | `PRIMARY KEY` | Unique row identifier (NOT NULL + UNIQUE) | `id INT PRIMARY KEY` |
| 4 | `FOREIGN KEY` | Enforces referential integrity | `FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES DEPT(id)` |
| 5 | `CHECK` | Validates value range | `salary DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary >= 0)` |
| 6 | `DEFAULT` | Auto-fills default value | `status VARCHAR(10) DEFAULT 'Active'` |

**Combined Example:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Employee (
    emp_id     INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    emp_name   VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    email      VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE,
    salary     DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary >= 0),
    dept_id    INT,
    join_date  DATE DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
);
```

#### (b) Hospital Management System Schema [7 Marks]

**Business rules enforced:**
- A doctor has a unique `doctor_id`, name, specialization, and consultation fee (must be positive).
- A patient has a unique `patient_id`, name, age (must be тЙе 0), and phone number.
- An appointment links one patient to one doctor on a specific date and time.

```sql
-- DOCTOR Table
CREATE TABLE DOCTOR (
    doctor_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    doctor_name    VARCHAR(60) NOT NULL,
    specialization VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    fee            DECIMAL(8,2) CHECK (fee > 0)
);

-- PATIENT Table
CREATE TABLE PATIENT (
    patient_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    patient_name  VARCHAR(60) NOT NULL,
    age           INT CHECK (age >= 0),
    phone         VARCHAR(15) UNIQUE
);

-- APPOINTMENT Table
CREATE TABLE APPOINTMENT (
    appointment_id  INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    doctor_id       INT NOT NULL,
    patient_id      INT NOT NULL,
    appoint_date    DATE NOT NULL,
    appoint_time    TIME NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (doctor_id)  REFERENCES DOCTOR(doctor_id)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    FOREIGN KEY (patient_id) REFERENCES PATIENT(patient_id)
        ON DELETE CASCADE
);
```

**Business rule enforcement summary:**

- `CHECK (fee > 0)` тАФ Doctors cannot have zero or negative fees.
- `CHECK (age >= 0)` тАФ Prevents data entry errors in patient age.
- `ON DELETE RESTRICT` for doctor тАФ Prevents deleting a doctor who has appointments.
- `ON DELETE CASCADE` for patient тАФ Auto-deletes appointment history when a patient record is removed.
- `UNIQUE phone` тАФ Two patients cannot share the same phone number.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `NOT NULL` on mandatory fields** тАФ Examiner deducts 1 mark per missing critical constraint.
> 2. **Foreign key data-type mismatch** тАФ The child column's data type **must exactly match** the parent column's data type. A common error is using `VARCHAR` in child and `INT` in parent.
> 3. **Defining foreign key before parent table exists** тАФ `CREATE TABLE` will fail with "Referenced table not found." Always create parent tables first.
> 4. **Using `PRIMARY KEY` more than once per table** тАФ Only **one** primary key is allowed (though it can be composite).
> 5. **Missing `AUTO_INCREMENT` on the PK** тАФ Without it, the application code must manually generate unique IDs, which is error-prone.
> 6. **Forgetting the semicolon `;`** тАФ In multi-statement scripts, missing semicolons cause partial execution and cryptic errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`CREATE TABLE` is a DDL command** that defines **structure**, not data.
- The statement has **3 mandatory parts**: keyword pair, table name, and at least one column definition.
- **Column-level constraints** are written immediately after the column definition.
- **Table-level constraints** (composite PK, FK) are written after all column definitions.
- **`PRIMARY KEY` = `NOT NULL` + `UNIQUE` + index** (implicitly).
- **A table can have only ONE primary key** but **multiple UNIQUE constraints**.
- **`FOREIGN KEY` requires the parent table and column to exist** тАФ create parent first.
- **Always use appropriate data types** тАФ `DECIMAL` for money, `VARCHAR` for variable text, `CHAR` for fixed codes.
- **Naming conventions**: Use lowercase or `snake_case` (e.g., `student_name`, not `StudentName`) тАФ KTU prefers clarity over style debates.
- **For JDBC in Java**, the `CREATE TABLE` statement is executed via `stmt.executeUpdate(sql)`, which returns `0` for successful DDL execution.
- **`CHAR` vs `VARCHAR`**: `CHAR` pads unused space; `VARCHAR` saves space тАФ choose based on data predictability.
- **`ON DELETE` options**: `CASCADE` (auto-delete children), `RESTRICT` (block delete), `SET NULL` (orphan the FK).
- **Always commit DDL explicitly** in transactional databases (e.g., `conn.commit()` in JDBC) for changes to persist.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: delete
insert
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT416 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Insert Operations in Java Collections Framework

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Java Collections Framework (JCF)**, an **Insert Operation** refers to the act of adding, appending, or placing one or more elements into a data structure (Collection) at a specified position. The Java Collections Framework provides a unified architecture (`java.util` package) for representing and manipulating collections of objects, enabling them to be manipulated independently of the implementation details.

The insert operation is formally defined through the contract specified in the `Collection<E>` root interface and its sub-interfaces (`List<E>`, `Set<E>`, `Map<K,V>`, `Queue<E>`). Each sub-interface overrides the insertion contract based on its underlying data structure semantics (e.g., ordered insertion in `List`, unique insertion in `Set`, key-value pair insertion in `Map`).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The `Collection` interface is the root of the collection hierarchy. The two primary sub-branches are `Collection<E>` (for `List`, `Set`, `Queue`) and `Map<K,V>` (which is a **separate top-level interface** and does NOT extend `Collection`).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Library Bookshelf System**:

- **`List` (e.g., `ArrayList`)** = A bookshelf where books are placed **in a sequence**. You can add a new book at the end, or **squeeze it in between** two existing books (insertion at a specific index).
- **`Set` (e.g., `HashSet`)** = A bookshelf with **strict librarian rules** тАФ only one unique copy of each book title is allowed. If you try to insert a duplicate, the system silently rejects it.
- **`Map` (e.g., `HashMap`)** = A **catalogue system** where every book has a unique shelf-number **key** and the book itself is the **value**. You insert by providing both the key and the value.
- **`Queue` (e.g., `PriorityQueue`)** = A **billing counter queue** тАФ elements are inserted at the **rear (tail)** and processed from the **front (head)**, following FIFO or priority-based insertion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** Every insert operation in Java Collections returns a `boolean` value indicating success (`true`) or failure (`false`), EXCEPT `List.add(int index, E element)` which returns `void` because the operation is **position-based**, not conditional.

### 1.3 Standard Insertion Methods Overview

| Interface | Primary Insert Method | Return Type | Position Flexibility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Collection` | `boolean add(E e)` | `boolean` | End / Natural Order |
| `List` | `void add(int index, E e)` | `void` | **Index-Based** |
| `List` | `boolean addAll(int index, Collection c)` | `boolean` | **Index-Based (Bulk)** |
| `Set` | `boolean add(E e)` | `boolean` | Unique only |
| `Map` | `V put(K key, V value)` | `V` (old value) | Key-Based |
| `Map` | `void putAll(Map m)` | `void` | Key-Based (Bulk) |
| `Queue` | `boolean offer(E e)` | `boolean` | Rear (Tail) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Insert Operations

**Why do we need multiple insert methods?**
Different data structures serve different purposes. A `List` preserves insertion order, a `Set` enforces uniqueness, and a `Map` enforces key-value pairing. The JCF provides **polymorphic methods** so client code can operate on any collection abstraction without knowing its concrete implementation.

**How does the insertion work internally?**

1. **Validation Phase:** The JVM checks for `null` (if the collection disallows it, e.g., `TreeMap` keys), capacity, and type-safety (via **Generics**).
2. **Modification Phase:** The element is stored. For `ArrayList`, this may trigger an internal **array resizing** (growth factor = **1.5x** for JDK 8+).
3. **Index Shift Phase (List only):** If insertion is at an index `< size`, all elements from that index onward are **shifted right by one position**.
4. **Return Phase:** The method returns a status flag or the displaced old value.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Data Structure | Insert Method | Time Complexity (Avg) | Time Complexity (Worst) | Triggers Resize? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `ArrayList` | `add(E e)` | **O(1)** amortized | O(n) тАФ when resize | Yes, when `size == capacity` |
| `ArrayList` | `add(int i, E e)` | **O(n)** | O(n) | Yes, sometimes |
| `LinkedList` | `add(E e)` | **O(1)** | O(1) | No |
| `LinkedList` | `add(int i, E e)` | **O(n)** | O(n) тАФ traversal | No |
| `HashSet` | `add(E e)` | **O(1)** | O(n) тАФ hash collisions | Yes (bucket resize) |
| `TreeSet` | `add(E e)` | **O(log n)** | O(log n) | No |
| `HashMap` | `put(K, V)` | **O(1)** | O(n) | Yes (bucket resize) |
| `TreeMap` | `put(K, V)` | **O(log n)** | O(log n) | No |
| `PriorityQueue` | `offer(E e)` | **O(log n)** | O(log n) | Yes (heapify up) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacity Growth Formula for ArrayList:**
>
> $$newCapacity = oldCapacity + \frac{oldCapacity}{2} = 1.5 \times oldCapacity$$
>
> The default initial capacity is **10**. The size grows by **50%** each time the internal array is full.

### 2.3 The Index-Shift Formula (List Insertion)

When an element is inserted at index `i` in a list of size `n`:

$$\text{Number of shifts} = n - i$$

For example, inserting at index `0` of a list with 100 elements requires **shifting 100 elements one position to the right**. Inserting at the end (index `n`) requires **0 shifts**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Systems:** `B-Tree` insertions (analogous to `TreeMap.put`) form the backbone of indexed database storage.
- **Caching (LRU Cache):** Insert operations in `LinkedHashMap` with `accessOrder=true` are used to build Least Recently Used cache eviction policies.
- **Message Brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ):** `PriorityQueue.offer` operations enable priority-based message delivery.
- **Compilers & Symbol Tables:** `HashMap.put` is used to store variable names and their type/scope information during compilation.
- **Operating Systems:** Process scheduling uses `Queue` insertions for ready-queue management.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: ArrayList Insertion with Trace

**Problem:** Trace the following code and determine the final state of the `ArrayList`.

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.Arrays;

public class InsertTrace {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList("A", "B", "C"));
        
        // Step 1: Append "D"
        list.add("D");
        
        // Step 2: Insert "X" at index 1
        list.add(1, "X");
        
        // Step 3: Insert collection at index 0
        list.addAll(0, Arrays.asList("Y", "Z"));
        
        // Step 4: Check return value of set operation
        String old = list.set(2, "W");
        
        System.out.println("List: " + list);
        System.out.println("Old value replaced at index 2: " + old);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Execution Trace:**

| Step | Operation | Code Line | State After Operation | Index Shifts |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 0 | Initial | `asList("A","B","C")` | `["A", "B", "C"]` | тАФ |
| 1 | Append | `list.add("D")` | `["A", "B", "C", "D"]` | 0 |
| 2 | Insert @ idx 1 | `list.add(1, "X")` | `["A", "X", "B", "C", "D"]` | 3 |
| 3 | Bulk Insert @ idx 0 | `list.addAll(0, ["Y","Z"])` | `["Y", "Z", "A", "X", "B", "C", "D"]` | 5 |
| 4 | Replace (not insert) | `list.set(2, "W")` | `["Y", "Z", "W", "X", "B", "C", "D"]` | 0 |

**Expected Output:**

```
List: [Y, Z, W, X, B, C, D]
Old value replaced at index 2: A
```

**Mathematical Verification of Shifts in Step 2:**

$$\text{elements\_before\_index} = 1 \quad (\text{"A" at index 0})$$
$$\text{elements\_from\_index\_1\_onwards} = n - i = 4 - 1 = 3 \quad (\text{"B", "C", "D"})$$

$${\text{shifts} = 3 \text{ positions to the right}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: HashMap Insertion with Collision Handling

**Problem:** Demonstrate how `HashMap` handles insertion with duplicate keys.

```java
import java.util.HashMap;

public class MapInsert {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        HashMap<Integer, String> map = new HashMap<>();
        
        // First insertion: returns null (no prior value)
        String r1 = map.put(101, "Alice");
        
        // Duplicate key: returns OLD value "Alice"
        String r2 = map.put(101, "Bob");
        
        // Bulk insertion from another map
        map.putAll(java.util.Map.of(102, "Charlie", 103, "Diana"));
        
        System.out.println("First put returned: " + r1);
        System.out.println("Duplicate put returned: " + r2);
        System.out.println("Final map: " + map);
        System.out.println("Size: " + map.size());
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
First put returned: null
Duplicate put returned: Alice
Final map: {101=Bob, 102=Charlie, 103=Diana}
Size: 3
```

**Key Insight:**

$$map.size() = 3 \quad \text{(NOT 4 тАФ duplicate key REPLACED, did not insert)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The `put(K, V)` method's return value is the **previous value associated with the key**, or `null` if there was no mapping. This is a critical KTU exam distinction.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: LinkedList Insertion at Front and Rear

```java
import java.util.LinkedList;

public class LinkedListInsert {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LinkedList<Integer> ll = new LinkedList<>();
        
        ll.add(10);         // append to tail
        ll.addFirst(5);     // insert at head тАФ O(1)
        ll.addLast(20);     // insert at tail тАФ O(1)
        ll.offer(25);       // Queue-style offer тАФ O(1)
        
        System.out.println(ll);
    }
}
```

**Linked List Internal State (Node Diagram):**

```
HEAD -> [5] <-> [10] <-> [20] <-> [25] <- TAIL
```

$$Time = O(1) + O(1) + O(1) + O(1) = O(1) \text{ (constant)}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scenario | Best Structure | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Frequent insert at **end only** | `ArrayList` | Amortized **O(1)** |
| Frequent insert at **beginning** | `LinkedList` | Always **O(1)** |
| Insert with **no duplicates** | `HashSet` | Built-in uniqueness check |
| Insert with **sorted order** | `TreeSet` | Auto-sorts on insert (**O(log n)**) |
| Insert with **key-value** | `HashMap` | **O(1)** average lookup |
| Insert with **priority** | `PriorityQueue` | Heap-based, **O(log n)** |
